review:
Literally Unplayable 

I heard that this game had some pc port and performance issues, but by God I did not think it could ever be this bad. 
No other game could somehow crash like 9 times in less than two hours of me playing it, ON LOW SETTINGS WITH A MODERN PC. 

I'm not gonna sit here and say I have the best specs, but you'd think that with all current gen parts, and 2000$ the game should run fine on low settings, but no. The optimization is some of the worst in the field, and their "anti-cheat" (for a PvE game btw) literally contributes to around 35-40% of that.

14hrs to complete Low Rank story (Credits roll.) 86 hours total to get 100% achievements. I personally didn't have any performance issues other than some graphical pop in.

Not a huge fan of open world games as they feel like big empty space. Not a huge fan of the mounts although I understand they are needed to fix the issue of big open space. I prefer the older games combat system (without focus mode) as it feels much more rewarding to move around and get into better positioning.

I think this game is better than base game Rise but not better than Sunbreak. World + Iceborne are still the best MH games on PC (If you prefer slower more methodical gameplay.)

8/10

This is more of a early review. Sharing my thoughts and what is stayed with me. Since this is a Monster Hunter game, it needs whole lot more of gameplay hours for a *proper* review. And unfortunately there is no middle ground so...
-I heard it had lots of technical issues but right now, mid to high it works really good. For me anyway. Fans go crazy though... And the usual Capcom protections...
-Whole story... They wanted to make it more cinematic and it bogs down the telling/pacing compare to old games. And right now I am not impressed. Nor have I feel *connected* to any NPCs. Especially the kid. If they thought I would feel *soft* and *accepting* just because they added a kid, they are so wrong. Other NPCs that are MC's companions are also feeling off. New handler a little better than The World one but her personality still get on my nerves. Smith girl also made me dislike her a bit... don't know why. Not a good group of cast in my eyes.
-MC talks... at least our character is not a moron or a cactus which is a big plus. They added *fake* dialogue choices. More like adding more lore to the situation that is all. And Palicos talk in human language... feels weird. You can switch to usual language if you want to at least.
-The new mount system... it is a matter of taste actually. It is good enough for me. A portable tent in a way. Switch to your second weapon you pick, your temporary items store in the pack, it moves *automatically* most of the time which helps to your sharpen... but I don't know why it moves on its own sometimes even when I push the disable auto move... That is annoying.
-Focus mode is Really good. It helps you with just that, you keep attacking the correct place, the place your reticle is aiming. I think this is a big improvement. Only tested with sword and shield for now.
-New animations are nice. Much better movement and reaction. Lots of details, along with some new moves to the weapons. I love it actually. Body reaction to terrain is better than previous games.
-More things become portable and on site, I'm not sure about it for now. It is kind of good. 
-Wound system is a double edge sword. It is logical and good but yes it also makes it a bit more easier. But when you look at it this way, now you feel like you are *actually* hurting the monster. They react more, you bleed them etc... It is good in a way. Maybe they will improve some things on the expansion?

For now, it looks like there are some good ideas in it. They are experimenting with this I think. Bold move but we all will see what Capcom store for us in the future. But seriously, the way story is going is... not helping to the game's case at all. Buy it on heavy discount if possible. Don't pay full price!

I've been a Monster Hunter fan for years, and I'm honestly loving Wilds. The new mechanics are great, the combat feels amazing, and I've been having a blast.

My only disappointment is the state of AAA optimization in 2026. It feels like Frame Generation is slowly becoming a requirement instead of an optional feature. On my high-end PC, I get around 120FPS in Forest Biome(in hub 160FPS+) on Ultra with Ray Tracing and High textures (using the Highest texture setting just doesn't make sense) only if Frame Generation is enabled. Without it, my FPS drops to around 60–90.

That said, the recent updates have definitely improved the game's performance, and it's in a much better state than it was at launch. I think Frame Generation is an awesome technology, but it should be a bonus, not something you have to rely on.

I still recommend playing the game, especially now after the latest updates. It really deserves your time.

After completing the base game (more or less) and seeing the progression of this game since release I think it's a good time to write a review. I wanna note that I played this game mostly solo.

Overall I give this game a thumbs up. Don't get me wrong this game has many flaws but I think its strengths outweight it's weaknesses.

+ Gameplay: Best gameplay in the monster hunter series. Every weapon feels satisfying to use and offers a nice kit to explore. Some are easier to learn or harder to master but this is totally fine. They offer a deep variety and there should be something for everyone. I like that you can one trick a weapon if you want. And the monsters themselves offer unique challenges at various difficulties (i.e. stars). I grew very fond of the focus system.

+ Monsters: Simply amazing. The design of all of them are great. No misses. The way they behave, move and react feels just right. The apex monsters are accompanied with memorable tracks and while it does have less monsters than it's predecessors the quality of the monsters is the highest it's ever been.

+ Animations: Deserves a separate mention. The animations make most other games look clunky and unrefined. No matter if it's the monsters, the hunter or the weapons. Everything moves just how you expect them to. Big hits and blocking big attacks have power behind them. I also like the small things like how seikret's and NPC's behave in camps or how the hunter reacts with the environment while they're moving. 

+ Graphics: I might be in the minority on this but the game looks good. Out of the box the game has flat colors which I personally don't like but this can easily be fixed with mods such as "Disable Post Processing Effects" or "RenoDX - HDR and SDR Fix".

+ Story mode: I like the cinematic experience the game offers you at the beginning. The story itself is serviceable at best but good enough for one fun play-through before you focus on high-rank.

+ Linux compatibility: This game runs great on Linux. Nothing more to say. Some workarounds are needed if you wanna play in HDR.

- Performance: The performance is still bad. It's totally "playable" on my main system but on my secondary system with a RX 6600, I3-12100F it's borderline unplayable. 8GB GPU's and sub 6 cores CPU will simply struggle with this game even on low settings and the game starts to look really bad if you do that.

- UX/UI: The menus in this game are horrendous. So many tabs and options, some of them in places you don't expect. Everything feels clunky to use even after you get used to them. I think the worst offender is the fact that there are multiple ways to do the same things (like playing together, accessing your tent/builds, communication etc.). It's just too much.

- Co-op experience: I specifically talk about playing the story mode together (i.e. low-rank). It's such a downgrade from previous titles. I refrain from listing all the issues because there are too many. I have no idea why it's so hard to provide an easy way to play the story mode with your friends.

- Artian weapon system: I like the concept but I hate that they make the traditional monster weapons obsolete. The option to create just the weapon you want is really cool. Having RNG mechanics might be a bit frustrating but I think it could have a place next to a more traditional system. The monster weapons need a lot of love in the expansion.

I love the game, just finished the campaign around a week ago, but when I got my friends to join, the co-op and how it was handled really just sucks, I don't like saying that, but it does.

The campaign is your intro to the game, so being able to enjoy your first couple of hours with others is to be expected, but it is overall one clunky experience: from those on the same quest having to leave their own instance to join whoever finished their cutscene first, forcing them to either sync up their experience artificially or just join at odd times at each of their own ends of the cutscenes, each discrepancy to their pace further pulling their time in the fights apart, to some just skipping and abandoning it so they can spectate and join their other friend who hasn't yet(a function I didn't even know was possible).

To how I had to ascertain all of this over the course of nearly my first hour of playtime with my friends, making their initial experience a not so nice one...this game has the quality, and this problem only plagues their first couple of sessions before the training wheels come off, but not only did it cause a bug where one of them couldn't progress their quest, while the other moved on ahead, leaving me stuck trying to troubleshoot, but it took so much time, including the character creation to no fault of the game of course, but still in the end left the actual amount of game-time spent with my friends, minimal to the amount of work put in just to have that time...

For the love of god, please fix this, make environmental link also base the story quests scenes from the hosts state, make it so those on the "same" beat of the quest don't have any issues playing with one another, making the transition and actual flow of the game for groups more enjoyable.

I feel this is so necessary for those who want a good group experience in this game in its first steps, and it pushes them away from having a genuine good time, I don't want that for myself & my group, or this game.

Low/High rank in MH has never been terribly difficult, but the developers of this game openly stated they deliberately made MH:Wilds easier than prior entries to the series, in an effort to retain players. In my opinion, it actually made the game less appealing.

I love monster hunter, so I've been pretty excited to finally grab this when it went on sale. I heard there was performance issues, but I expected maybe some stuttering or the like... unfortunately, it's actually unplayable because it keeps crashing... I had to restart the same start of a mission 2-3 times (tutorial mision, by the way), and when it finally did let me kill the monster, it crashed again before saving lol

TLDR: Unplayable from crashes. Clunky movement and gameplay that will hopefully be resolved with third party anti-cheat software removed, but I'll unfortunately have to wait and see. Recommend playing Monster Hunter World in the meantime.

Cons:
-Your character talks now. I'm personally not a fan because it adds a personality type I'm not too fond of, but if you don't mind that then it's no problem.
-Player Character voices are limited. There are 6 types, 3 for "male" and "female" respectively. you can change the pitch, but it gets a very hard to ignore robotic voice changer quality that drove me up a wall. 
-If you played Monster Hunter World, there is some getting used to with weapon feel. Maybe it was just an insect glaive issue, but it's clunky where it used to feel smooth, and I'm not sure what the intent was.

Either way, a very fun game to jump into that is harmed by homogenisation amidst a fantastic, detailed world.

I waited a bit before to develop my full opinion on this game. 
I've been with the series since mh4u and with that said, this game is great. However, it feels like a downgrade in some areas of core content, gameplay design, and user interface functionality.
This is a great entry for new comers in the series and a lot of those good reasons are it's drawback for returning players or in my own opinion. It's too easy; I'm not speaking in its difficulty.

At launch this game was abysmal to play, and horribly optimized even if you are packing a elite rig, but over-time optimization fixed, patches its great that it caused me to return to playing and adding more hours weekly into game. 

Environments are beautiful and filled with life like in monster hunter world, but they traded complexity of depth and intricate networking of paths for Auto running Seikrets that take you anywhere on the map faster while adding autonomy to its gameplay. "Yes you can just not use seikret" Tough luck getting to certain locked out areas that only seikret can travel through where monsters are often are.

 Such complexities were compromised with seikrets and guiding scout flie That it almost feels like you are speed running every hunt while doom scrolling on your phone. 
Part of Monster Hunter has always been the hunt itself, and that feeling has been gradually diminished since World; In world you still needed to gather tracks and clues to build up your Scoutfly research level. Eventually, the Scoutflies would guide you directly to the monster on future hunts, but at least there was a sense of discovery and investigation. You felt like a hunter.
In this game, monster is already marked, fly to a close camp, seikret auto takes you to the fight. 

I will admit, it saves time when farming but surely something i missed in older games having no clue where the monster is, sometimes being 10 mins into a hunt before you find the monster and having to paint ball them in order to track them down. New players won't know that feeling, but hey I'm just oldy here.

Game is easy to pick up and play, at first difficulty that monsters were to easy always a complaint in new main tittles releases mainly that people forget that Low - high rank is VERY Easy for returning players. Once rank 8+ monsters were added. I stopped hearing those complaints fact I'm thrilled that challenge has finally arrived until g-rank "Master rank" releases next year.

My last bit, is the UI
Monster Hunter has never been known for having a perfect UI, but that doesn't mean it should be exempt from criticism. There are menus inside menus inside menus when interacting with settings, inventory management, equipment, forging, and other systems.
Even after 300 hours, I still accidentally open the wrong menu, get funneled into unintended screens due to a misinput, or forget that the same button performs different actions depending on the menu.

Overall This game is great, If you can run it on pc get a copy.

Have very complicated feelings on this game.
performance was ♥♥♥♥ on launch and has only gotten marginally better over time. 

Odd shift towards telling a cohesive narrative that started in world is amplified here, with long stretches of gameplay in low and early high rank where you must simply sit and listen to characters speak while riding  your mount on rails. Being forced to listen to plot i don't care about, spouted from characters i don't care about after every single hunt was misery. 

The mount itself and the open world are incredibly bland. The mount is near impossible to control in any meaningful sense, if you are doing anything other than circling the monster, its better to leave it to the autopilot which also frequently makes mistakes in pathing. The open world is essentially not real and very lazy. Areas are gated by tiny hallways and you will likely never traverse them on foot after the story.

The hunts are piss easy, until you get to title update hunts where there is a noticeable and appreciated return to the classic difficulty. This does not mean that past changes i hate such as restocking items mid hunt, and not actually having to hunt the monster anymore (it is simply present at all times on your map) are here and actively degrade the stakes, the challenge and the enjoyment of the hunts. 

The grind has been made noticeably worse now. Most armor sets are gated behind star rank or monster specific tickets, with one required for each piece, which means monster parts are now worthless entirely, since they shower you with them constantly. The only thing preventing you from getting a given set or weapon is capcom telling you you havent killed it enough times. This is essentially the same as the old way of grinding for a rath gem for example. But it does worsen the flavor and essence of monhun by attaching these asinine tickets to everything to let you know that the monster parts you worked hard for are now worthless.

The wound system is just overpowered. The only bad thing about it is how it creates the infinite monster parts problem, and the stupid red glow to make sure that even a potato hooked up to electrodes could hit a wound. other than that i think wounds are a cool concept and the slow battle weathering of the monsters is sick.

I dont really know how to feel, if there was a mid option id pick that, but as it stands, while this is certainly a monster hunter game, it feels as if all of the character and soul that made monhun what it is is being sanded off or sucked out. Going from rise (even though rise also has its major flaws)  to this feels like going from a nostalgic apple pie from granny, to a school lunch served with a broken femur. It may sound like i hate this game, but i dont. moreso i hate the direction my beloved series seems to be taking with its mainline entries. I will eagerly await the dlc to see if any of these complaints are addressed. I will more eagerly await the next portable entry in the series as i have a sinking feeling that is where the true soul of monhun will live going forward.

Maybe it comes from a place of bitterness, or exhaustion, but the fact that Wilds does not demand every moment of your time and perfection of play, means that jumping in and feeling like you are 'having fun' is much more suitable for the modern day. Which many games take too far, or neglect too impotently - I think Wilds balance is a sweet spot that I hope it can maintain going foward.

I will say though, take views with a grain of salt, experiences vary wildly for this one, and while simplicity works well for an adult after the 2020s, not everyone is looking for that.
Paywalling character customisation sucks arse cheeks, and players are evidently having real issues with performance too.

If you’re getting this fantastic game now at a steep discount, with all content and some performance enhancements, and can hit at least 30 FPS, [i]I envy you[/i].

Prologue: Fool that I am, I bought the game on launch with a bunch of my other guild friends. My old laptop had me fighting Nu Udra at 24 film standard FPS. I was almost in 4U monster spawning or Apex Zinogre territory with seeing frames. They released the game early without a final boss, like MHRise, but this time [i]without a gathering hub*[/i], too. The game’s difficulty is on an exponential curve, but because High Rank was cut short on launch, the final mission barely scraped the spike. The game truly was too easy for a series veteran. I’m not someone that defines a game by difficulty, but besides Tempered Arkveld and one returning baby dearest, the option to get our world rocked just [i]wasn’t there.[/i]

[b]This is now my favorite Monster Hunter base game[/b]. I’ve been playing since 3U.

Yes, even before the Title Updates when fighting at a whole 19-24 FPS and folding high rank monsters in 3 minutes with my friends, I have had a blast this whole time. It's only gotten better from there.

✅ Quick Pros
[list]
[*]Love the new investigation system and how easily your team can jump into a multiplayer hunt if you find something cool on your map.
[*]Super useful being able to substitute empty hunter slots with a support hunter if our team has less than four people**.
[*]Fun and shiny new weapon abilities for this generation: Perfect Guard, Perfect Dodge, Guard Counter, and Offset
[*]Possibly an unpopular opinion, but I even love the weapon decoration system. I can carry two different elements easily and a lot of weapon class skills are interchangeable.
[*][b]FASHION![/b] 💃 No more gender locked armor. You can have a colorable modesty shirt and tights underneath. The layered is automatically unlocked when forging the real piece! I’ve always been a fashion hunter, scrolling Athena’s ASS for hours trying to get the cutest sets for my GQ grinds. I’ve been unleashed.
[*]It's not really open world, but I like how base camps and "safe zones" have been merged with their respective maps. The areas feel more alive.
[/list]

❌  Quick Cons
[list]
[*] Low Rank is barely a Rank. You'll be in walking simulator cutscene hell for most of it and it does [i]not[/i] help the monsters keel over and die for you in minutes. Trust me or the friends that told you to get the game - the training wheels come off in High Rank!
[*] Seikret-based exploration. I am NOT gonna be the guy that complains [i]"I never learned the map because the Seikret takes me everywhere"[/i]. Consider steering the Seikret yourself. [i]I'm[/i] complaining about the amount of times I'm walking and have to get on my Seikret to make a jump.
[*]You will be fighting convoluted UI. Multiplayer can be a pain. Even in a voice chat Environment Link is useless to us.
[*]I really wish we had a room to decorate and the ability to display Endemic Life like World :(
[*]I hate DPS checks. There's only two, but that's already too much for High Rank.
[*]Don't like the post processing and end result of the art direction - it's more inline with the melancholy MH1 than my oversaturated 4U, but it's too much (little?) for me. Too milky, too low contrast, not enough saturation. Fallow season looks awful and god help you if you gotta turn the graphics down like I did. Speaking of...
[*]The performance, of course.
[/list]

The performance improvements were serviceable. Nothing crazy, still pretty damn disappointing. It’s the title updates that truly shine. Spring '25 and Summer '25 felt like [i]“IOU the rest of this game”[/i] updates, but Fall '25, Winter '25, and Spring '26 truly put Wilds a step above other High Ranks I’ve camped in.

We’ve received confirmation that the original “final mission” from launch is the only one needed to unlock the Master Rank expansion. Everything released after is optional ‼️ with the aforementioned exponential difficulty curve of how quickly you turn into pavement. It RULES. I was dodging Savage Omega flamethrowers in my dreams. The true final high rank boss is an absolute treat and I’m pretty content with the direction they took the fight in***.

The endgame grind is good enough. Better than Kulve. I'm just playing to have fun.

🚫 Still... It didn't feel good watching Capcom start to post hundreds of dollars worth of microtransactions when my damn game was sub 30 FPS and we couldn't even sit in a Gathering Hub or rematch the Low Rank final boss : )

As a first-time Monster Hunter player, I honestly had a great experience with Monster Hunter Wilds. The combat is deep, rewarding, and definitely made for players who enjoy a challenge. Learning different weapons and creating loadouts kept the gameplay fresh, and the character customization is one of the best I've seen.

I personally had zero performance issues. No crashes, no stuttering, and no major FPS drops throughout my 100+ hours of play. I know some people have reported technical problems, but with a decent PC and the game installed on an NVMe SSD, my experience was smooth from start to finish.

The Palico quickly became one of my favorite parts of the game. It reminded me so much of my real-life pet cat that I even gave it the same name: Rabbit. That small personal touch made the journey even more enjoyable.

The main story is fairly simple, but the real game begins after the campaign. The Grand Hub, event quests, side activities, and the nighttime music festival give the game so much life. I found myself spending hours there between hunts.

The monster designs are incredible, and every hunt feels unique. So far, Omega has been the toughest monster I've fought, and finally defeating it was one of the most satisfying moments in the game.

If you're looking for an easy, casual game, this probably isn't it. But if you enjoy challenging combat, mastering different weapons, and hunting amazing monsters with friends or solo, Monster Hunter Wilds is absolutely worth playing.

Just make sure your PC is up to the task. This isn't a game for low-end hardware. Install it on an NVMe SSD and, with good specs, you'll likely have a much smoother experience.

This game was gifted to me by a very generous friend of mine. However, it does not change my opinion of the game, but I think it is only fair to be transparent. There is this underlying issue that not a lot of people seem to be addressing.

I am a longtime fan of Monster Hunter since Freedom Unite, which is probably why this entry disappointed me more than I am afraid to admit to. My biggest issue is not the gameplay itself, but most of the time it is how the Monster Hunter language has been treated. It is technically still there in the background with some NPCs, but your hunter does not speak it anymore & neither do the cats meowing like they should do & the in-universe people just understands them like the Chewbaccas they are. This one specifically particular change completely changes the experience for me.

The Monster Hunter language & its weird noises & sounds is what gave the series its unique flair. Those distinctive voices made the world feel outstanding instead of like every other medieval or caveman fantasy game. It was never just a gimmick, it helped create an atmosphere that immediately felt like Monster Hunter. Replacing it with just the standardized "normie" voice acting takes it away for me & a huge part of that charm too.

It feels like Capcom wanted a more cinematic presentation, but that has never been what drew me to this series. Ironically, they already tried turning Monster Hunter into a movie & we all know how that turned out. Personally, I never wanted the games to head in that direction.

I am aware that there a lot of people are hating on some of the characters or even felt hard to tolerate them & I genuinely feel that if they had spoken in the Monster Hunter language instead of the fully voiced dialogue, they would have been far less irritating. This new approach that puts much more emphasis on the writing & voice delivery in my honest opinion, does not always work.

What disappoints me the most is how Capcom concede to such a major change. It comes across as another attempt to make the series appeal to a more broader audience while overlooking something that some longtime fans genuinely appreciated. I have asked around some people about their own opinions, searched the internet on the matter. Some of them seem to celebrate the removal of the Monster Hunter language & it is honestly frustrating because from my perspective, the series has lost a piece of what made it special.

The gameplay & the environment is still somewhat enjoyable sometimes, do not get me wrong & I will not pretend otherwise. There are plenty of things in the game that reminded me of why I fell in love with Monster Hunter as its own universe in the first place. Even so, I cannot shake the feeling that something important is missing. Monster Hunter language was not just a small feature to the overall experience of the game, it was part of the franchise's identity. Without it, the experience feels incomplete & it genuinely affects how I enjoy this game.

I am grateful to my friend for buying it for me & I am glad I got the chance to play it. Unfortunately, as someone who has followed this series for years, this is the first Monster Hunter game that has left me feeling like it had walked away from one of the things that made the franchise truly unique.

I agree with peoples complaints about performance, it's really bad, but bearable on DLSS. Graphics, don't get me started on this. This game is the WORST looking out of World and Rise, even with Ultra + High Res + Ray Tracing turned on (I had DLSS turned off and it somehow looked abysmal than it turned on). This game apparently looks better on 4k monitors, but I am not spending that much to get decent graphics flashed on my screen. Worlds still is their best looking 'realistic' MH game BY FAR. I still can't believe how the graphics look this bad with the crappy performance. 

So if you're wondering why I recommend this, it's the combat gameplay. If I were to describe it, it's like Worlds and Rise had a baby prodigy in combat. This game re-ignited my will to re-learn and master Monster Hunter weapons because each one of them has been reworked and made better (I rejoice as an Insect Glaive and Bow main hunter). Istg, it's like they balanced out the satisfying punch of World weapons to Rise's fluid fast paced combat. I've been having such a blast going around fighting and terrorizing monsters, although I have to say their list of monsters are still not up there with World's sheer number and variety of monsters (I'm so happy they brought back my bby Congalala in here whom I played with so much in MHFU). 

I also love the open-world aspect they introduced in Wilds, it definitely felt like it filled something missing for me as an open-world lover, it's nice how you can just hunt monsters any time in the map if you so wish to do so, or just explore with no loading screen hindering your game. Alma is such a huge help too, along with your Palico, they hunt/gather with you. If you also join a lobby, you'll get matched with hunters on your level so you can see them around the map. There's also a guild system in game. 

Character customization is what I adore about the game too, I spent almost an hour making mine and she definitely looked great (and looked quite Targaryen if you ask me). But, it's nice. The armor and weapon sets also look decent, I'm glad armor layers and such are not just DLC exclusive. 

In short, I don't like the performance and graphics but the negatives of those don't really outweigh the fun I have playing the game. Can't wait to spend another hundred hour on this.

This game is essentially Monster Hunter World. It looks the same and still has all the same problems (along with its good sides).

First off, the game is bloated to hell. Much like Tekken, this game carries with it all sorts of mechanics, items and weapons which seem to get more complicated with each iteration. But instead of dropping the bloat, it just keeps piling more stuff on with every iteration of the game. It also refuses to explain much of it. Newcomers should expect to Google most of the "tutorial" themselves.

And this - let's call it "uncommon but possibly intended" instead of "outdated and shortsighted" - direction of design decision is something you will find over and over in this game at every turn.

A few examples:

You start a mission and suddenly you're fighting 3 monsters at the same time in a game with combat that's so slow, unresponsive and clunky that it puts your average Unity fan attempt at a "Souls-like" to shame. 

A squeaky and annoying voice tells you something which you can't hear because the 3 monsters are all roaring and the screen shaking and flashing with all sorts of effects while several notifcations on screen have all sorts of small text you can barely read on your 4k screen. Each of them not getting to the point but elaborately writing something. Most of it completely unrelated to what's going on, of course. But a textbox on the bottom right of your giant screen has the key info: You should have brought literal feces with you to this mission. 

Oh, you didn't? Well, notice the flashing N on the other side of the screen? Probably not because you're being assaulted by 3 giant monsters in a game you can't pause. But let's assume you did: That means you have to get on your mount (that freqently doesn't respond to its button), press N to enter a menu and then click the item there. Then, enter a few different menus to assign that to the radial menu - you got 8 of those by default by the way - and then fire that at the monsters. 

The genius designers thought that was the best way to approach a mission where you're thrown into a sensory overload of 3 giant monsters fighting you at the same time. If you Google this, there are tons and tons of discussions and people asking for help - because that was the obvious and inevitable outcome of a design decision like that. You don't need to be a highly skilled game developer to see this coming. I wager an 8 year old can predict this.

And that's the tragedy of this game over and over again: An 8 year old could have seen the obvious problems inevitably resulting from all the incredibly dumb decisions that resulted in this game.

The mission right after: You're now fighting 2 monsters at the same time. You didn't know this before we tossed you into that mission. Oh, sucks that you brought a fire weapon! Well, you can't leave this mission now, you're stuck in it. You can exit to menu or exit the game but next time, we'll load you right back into that mission with your fire weapon against fire monsters.

If you Google how to exit missions, you'll find plenty of people stuck in this exact mission and wanting to exit it for various reasons which - again - any 8 year old could have explained to the developers as soon as they outlined their idea on the design. It's frustrating that the developers were unable to predict these would happen. This complete lack of foresight is shown everywhere.

There are menus upon menus and submenus, another horribly bloated system. They also expect you to menu around quite a bit in fights to craft ammo and such. Any game created in this century would instead have you able to carry around enough ammo to complete a mission instead. And they'd either reduce the idiotic amount of menuing you have to do or at least pause the game while you're struggling to remember what function is hidden in which menu's submenu.

Also, this game seems to look exactly like Monster Hunter Worlds did, ages ago. Only, it performs much, much worse than that. It performs quite okay on my hardware but if we consider what's going on in the game at any given time and how it looks graphically, it should perform much, much better if it was in any way optimized properly.

This is probably in no small part Denuvo's fault because every Denuvo game performs badly and crashes that I ever heard of. Of course, you can play this for free already since Denuvo can't protect anything and we got day 1 piracy back (just Google it) but if there ever was a clean crack to remove Denuvo instead of just bypassing it, we'll probably see the usual lack of crashes and much better performance again.

If you pick "Single Player", guess what the game does right after. It connects to some servers, of course. For the third time since starting it up in order to play some single player. Each time, you have to wait.

The story is also all over the place. 20 hours in, I still don't know what we're doing. There are constantly cutscenes with characters which are mostly annoying, many of them gender confused, dumping their trauma, personal problems and tribal "culture" on me that I really don't care about. This game's title is Monster Hunter. Instead, I feel like a school counselor most of the time. At least the characters are not all repulsively ugly for a change. In decades of gaming, this is probably the first game where I skip dialogue I haven't heard before because without exception, all the dialogue so far was either boring, annoying or both.

And finally, bring a controller. The game's keyboard layout is still hilariously bad. You can change the buttons, but honestly I wouldn't even know where to begin. By default, expect to press 5-6 buttons at the same time - 3 on your mouse simultaneously sometimes - and then mixing in Q, E, 1, 3, N, F, G, R for all the menuing you must do mid-fight. I can't imagine how they managed to fit all of this stuff onto a controller honestly.

Overall, I like this game, even if it is as infuriating and riddled with janky, dumb design decisions. But Steam wants to know if I recommend it. 

To old MH players? No, I don't recommend it. It's easier than MH World was and doesn't really do anything that game didn't do already for cheaper with much more content.

To new MH players? No, this game is way too bloated and outdated. It doesn't want to be an experience to be played or enjoyed, it wants to be an obstacle that must be overcome.

Originally, this review was negative because the game would crash in every hunt. I was able to fix this problem by downloading the STUDIO DRIVER (uninstall drivers using DDU first and make sure PHYSX is checked in options and CLEAN SHADERS) instead of GAME DRIVERS for nvidia gpu. Used RAMMAP to clear STANDBY CACHE for the ram sticks and lowered ram MHz slightly in BIO. Also set display to 144 fps on nvidia control panel. I have never played a game that so aggressively disrespects the player's time. The grind here is not just tedious; it is outright insulting. You will spend dozens of hours toiling away for microscopic increments of progress, only to look at others and realize you are still eons behind the "big league" players. The gap is insurmountable, and the game seems designed to keep you perpetually chasing a carrot that is constantly yanked just out of reach.

And here is the cruelest joke of all: even when you think you have finally "made it", when you’ve assembled what looks like a competitive setup, you quickly discover you haven't. The goalposts move. The meta shifts. Or, worse, you realize your "perfect" loadout is fundamentally flawed because the game's mechanics are a shallow, punishing mess.

To the modders: I don't blame you. If you are the type to use mods to bypass the slog, go for it. You are not cheating; you are fixing a broken system. Because doing things the "normal" way is a fool's errand. It takes far too long, and the game rewards your effort with crumbs.

Ultimately, you will never experience the satisfaction of crafting the perfect build. The game ensures that feeling remains a phantom, always teased, never achieved. This isn't a game; it's a second job with no paycheck.

Do yourself a favor: Skip this. Buy World. Walk away.

I see a lot of weird negative reviews so let me throw my thoughts at you and let you decide if it's for you or not. 

To start, people keep comparing this game to MHW. I get it, that game was amazing. However I think some of the issues they are talking about just aren't fair or even logical to complain about. For example, I see many people upset that you don't have to track monster steps to find the monster. It's cool that you had to actuall track the monster, but we also are living in a different time. People don't wanna spend 30-40 mins in 1 hunt because they are struggling to find a monster. The ability to go in and fight the monster without much hassle is a GOOD change, contrary to what others will tell you. This is monster HUNTER, not monster tracker. 

Second, along the same lines, is people complaining about the Seikret auto movement feature. Now this feature can be annoying to players who enjoy learning map terrarin themselves, I get it. During the story you are basically hand held and forced to walk on auto pilot listening to NPC's talk. Not exactly a great story experience, I'll be honest. However, during the hunts themselves, you CAN TURN THE AUTO MOVE OFF. I don't understand how people are going to complain about a mechanic YOU DO NOT NEED TO USE. If it was always on, then I get it. But you can turn it off. People who don't want it don't have to have it on and yet they will complain and leave a negative review? That's illogical, it makes no sense. If you don't like it, turn it off. 

Next I want to talk about the combat. The combat feels good. I see people complaining about how the combat is easy and they ruined the game. However as someone who played worlds, my biggest gripe was that the control I had over my character was ass. I had to run in circles just to reposition. I spent more time fighting my own movement than I did fighting the monster I was hunting. The ability to use focus mode and aim to change camera, especially mid attack like during the 2 hit attack from GS, AMAZING change. People who say otherwise are just wrong. Also, here's something else. YOU DO NOT NEED TO USE IT. Focus mode has 2 uses. 1) Re-aim. 2) Break wounds. You can use it only to break wounds and not use it any other time. This keeps the world style attack while still having the benefit for new players or players who WANT to re-aim. So people complaining about this are also illogical. Just don't use focus mode until you wanna break a part. My god, some people. 

I think this game is good. It looks amazing. Contrary to what others will say, the game has a lot of detail in each of it's worlds and I think it's beautiful to walk around in. I love it. I don't know why people are complaining about what it looks like, maybe it looks worse on the lowest settings but my goodness it looks good. 

Now despite thinking the game is good, I do recognize some issues. The game can randomly crash. I had a shader cache corruption earlier and needed to delete my cache on my computer to reinstall it and fix the corruption. Easy fix, but shouldn't have to do it in the first place. I also have a higher end PC, not the absolute best but it's definitely up there. So I can run the game on high settings and not have performance issues or overloading GPU/CPU usage. I will say that if you have a lower end computer, this game won't run as well. However it does run great on console so if you are looking at this and own a console, get it on there. It's crossplay so you aren't missing much. 

The other issue I have is it's lack of proper explanation. It has streamlined the game quite well and made it easier for new players to understand but there are certain parts the game wants you to do something but doesn't actually tell you how to do it which can be frustrating. I hope in the DLC they add a more detailed tutorial for new mechanics so players don't have to google stuff, it breaks immersion. 

Overall, I think the game is amazing. If your PC can handle it, it's worth picking up. I'll list my pros and cons for easier so it's easier to read without the details of my reasoning. 

PROS:
-Looks Beautiful
-More beginner friendly
-Useful and OPTIONAL mechanics that benefit new and veteran players alike
-Absolutely awesome new monsters
-Classic Monster Hunter vibes. 

CONS:
-Need higher end computer to run smoothly
-Still crashes, but usually easily fixable and not very common

Hopefully someone finds this useful and hopefully more people get it and leave a positive review. This game doesn't deserve the Mixed review tag any more. It's a good game.

tldr: Monster Hunter Wilds is now a well running game (at least for me it is) that is very enjoyable all the way through to the end with great combat, monsters, and locales, but has issues with difficulty scaling, rng systems, a certain feeling that existed in older Monster Hunters. Despite being the newest mainline game, Wilds feels more like playing Rise and Sunbreak than something like World.

My background with Monster Hunter includes extensive time with Generations Ultimate, World and Iceborne, and Rise and Sunbreak, but I also have some playtime with 4 Ultimate, 3 Ultimate, and Freedom Unite. Not to say that I have more justification or authority with my review because of that, but just that's the expectations and point of view I was going into Wilds with.

Despite thoroughly enjoying Rise and Generations, going back to somewhat more grounded combat is great. Each weapon does get some new flashy moves, but overall the combat is just more fluid without being high octane. I've heard some criticism of focus mode and being able to aim attacks too much, but I think it was a good inclusion. In my experience, it doesn't remove the need for good positioning or some forethought with where you're trying to attack the monster, it makes it so you didn't miss an attack because of a slight misread on an angle or from the monster toppling/flinching in a bad moment. I don't experience this with my main weapons, but I've heard it also helps with landing attacks on downed monsters with some weird weapon hit boxes. Wounds now exist. It's not overly intrusive, but you can't opt out of using wounds really because wounded parts still take more damage even if you don't focus strike them. It's okay in theory and very cool visually, but it's just too easy to create wounds on monsters and they make the monsters stagger and flinch too much should you strike at them. This could probably be remedied by just making it harder to create wounds which I believe happened with later added and higher star quests. Overall, the combat is amazing.

The locales are very pretty and have good variety in terms of different area feelings in them that allow for a large variety of monsters in just one of the locations. The inter-connectivity of the areas is even better than World in my opinion and having the base camps and villages be seamlessly added to the locations just takes you into the world a lot more. Each space in the world is filled with a great deal of decor and set dressing, but it doesn't feel overly cluttered or in the way. There's a very good variety of monsters in Wilds which also lends to a believable food chain and environment. I believe it to be one of the best rosters across any of the games for being only low and high rank at the time of writing. Getting to have so many monsters out in one map is also so cool and has been something that wished to happen in one of the games for so long and now its finally here. But the game doesn't do much to let you interact with all of this. In streamlining the game more, much of the old feel and grinding of previous Monster Hunter titles was taken out. Weapons and armors now have very generous crafting requirements that don't require many hunts and you are likely to get all of the materials you need if you only need one or two pieces of gear in one hunt up to a certain point.

Grinding and preparation just might be gone for the most part. This is why I say despite being a new mainline entry, it feels more like Rise or Sunbreak. It's easy to come across monster and item materials, you don't need to fight much to get most of what you want, and the farm stand in also provides with a good amount of items. Despite being back, it's very easy to negate the effects of heat and cold just with beetles around those locations and you don't need anything more than basic healing items for most monsters. It's also very east to just gather anything you might need in the maps, no need for even going back to the base camp. The seikret can just lead you to the monster you're hunting faster than you can run and you don't even need to track the monsters as they're always marked on your map at all times, much like Rise. You don't need to craft layered armors and weapons separately from the main equipment anymore. I don't believe all of this is necessarily a bad thing, though I do dislike the implementation of the seikret. I highly enjoyed the old grind filled titles of Monster Hunter, especially before World, but I can't deny that less of a grind has somewhat been helpful now that I have a lot less time on my hands as an adult in college. It's somewhat hard to find a good middle ground I think, as I don't believe anyone should be essentially heavily time gated out of the series, but I also empathize with the people who miss the old feel of the series.

Artian weapons also don't help with this as while monster weapons aren't bad at all, Artian weapons and especially Gogma Artian weapons will just always be better. I am here to hunt monsters, not Artian materials, but the game would much rather me do the latter. Between the rng nature of acquiring Artian weapons, getting the right bonuses and skills on Gogma Artian weapons, and then having BOTH rng decorations and talismans. so much of the end game just feels bottle necked into grind about 4-ish quests depending on what you want just to have the monsters themselves not even matter. This is especially upsetting because the other weapons ARE interesting, they perform well by themselves, but between getting extra bonuses and even armor skills and then also better decoration slots, Artian weapons will always be better. It's just not fun to roll constantly for everything. The only thing I'll give Artian weapons as a positive is that they can fill certain element/status gaps some weapon types might have.

Something good with gear, however, is that all weapon lines and armors can be upgraded to end game viability, much like with Rise and Sunbreak. Transcendence not only gives lower rank armor more defense, but also more slots so you can fit niche pieces into your sets and have more variety in terms of what parts can be used to get your set of skills just right. Hunter Tickets, gained from end game quests, also allow all weapons to be boosted up to end game stats, whether you're using Chatacabra weapons or Rathalos weapons.

Difficulty is also a hot topic with Wilds. I don't really care about the game being that much easier since it's still fun to me, and I do think the end game quests and 9/10 star quests provide a good challenge, but the main gripe I have is the curve into this difficulty. The game goes from being a breeze in my opinion up until late high rank, and even then its still relatively easy compared to older Monster Hunter games in my opinion. But then you go from 8 star to 9 star quests and there's a big jump in challenge there. It just kind of comes out of no where, and while I can handle that as I was wanting more challenging quests, I can imagine that it might be somewhat jarring and unfair feeling to someone who was more used to the difficulty leading up to it.

Wilds is pretty different even from a game like World, but I think a lot of the changes are good or at least there's a reasonable idea for trying to add them. It's a very good action game, but if you're searching for an experience like 4U or even World, it might not be exactly what you're wanting. I think people saying that the game has no challenge are just flat out wrong, but it will take some time for you to get there. But Gemma's hot so what more do you really need?

When I think about Monster Hunter, I think of having to explore a map, learning the layout, find and keep track of the monster I need to fight, being careful about my positioning and commitment to moves, and having to fight a monster more than once or twice to finally get the equipment pieces I want or need.

This game does nothing of the sort, and what is still present here, is heavily disincentivized.

Maps are uninspired, uninteresting, just a blur of a single color, nothing striking, nothing cool.
Which would be an issue if you had to learn them, but "luckily" mr. Capcom is here to save you from a problem they themselves created: here is a mount that will automatically Uber you to your destination, no need to find the monster, it does that for you, no need to stop to gather materials, you can do that from range by selecting what you want from a list of nearby sources, you dont even need to aim at them you just need to press a single button.

Combat is laughably easy at the core, are you using a weapon that can block? You now have perfect blocks, and even the strongest attacks can be nullified completely that way, no need to get points in guard or guard up skills.
Attacks can be redirected and corrected, even mid-swing in some cases, you don't need to position yourself, to consider what the monster is doing or could be doing, the game simplifies everything for you.

This time, as the awful gimmick that every monhun game must have, we've got Wounds.
You hit a part enough time and it gets wounded, which makes it take more damage until the wound is destroyed, making the monster fall down or flinch. The issue is, you can just stunlock a monster to death with this, from the weakest of them to the final monster of high rank.
But hey at least there's no wirebugs.

All in all this is just a mediocre game, but just that would be not enough for me to just tell people to not play it, these are my thoughts, other people might like the extreme streamlining of the game.

What definitively tips the scales to a "don't bother just grab World+Iceborne its cheaper and better" is how the story is done and the complete removal of flavor from the game:
The game is on rails for quite a long time, you are very often stuck walking or riding along your marvel quips-dialogue riddled npc characters, and you cannot skip these sequences that last for a relatively long time.
And they must've thought they really were cooking for this one because they removed the monhun language so you get to experience all of it in an actual language of your choosing.

Flavor text from the npcs in quest boards? gone.
A village like in every single other game? hope you weren't expecting Pokke or Seliana, gone.

The music is nice but few tracks manage to stand out, the rest is okay, not bad but nothing that makes you go WOW or look it up on youtube.

It really gives the feeling of shareholders meddling after seeing that MHW was their best selling game ever, and devs somehow being confused and maybe scared about what to do with this one.

It's not awful, it just feels flavorless, there's no seasoning. It just feels corporate.
Your money is better spent on Monster Hunter World + Iceborne, or if you already own that, just go back to it, there is a reason that game still has as many active players as this one does.

The most casual MH I've played so far and I honestly love it for that. 

Things that stand out, for better or for worse:

-Sometimes the controls are just... unresponsive. Not as in disconnected, but as in "I'm pushing this button for three seconds and nothing's happening because the game decided it wasn't going to happen".

-My least favorite Palico of the three MH games I've played. I get having the AI adjusted when joining a game with another player, but having my Palico floating around in a mini-airship while not actually firing it instead of knocking me out of my sleep dance when I'm playing solo certainly raises a few hackles.

-I don't have a sense of direction, so the auto travel is great.

-The fights feel shorter as long as you're properly prepared instead of designed to be drawn out for no reason at all. This is good. I like fitting in a bunch of hunts when I play.

-I have a decent enough PC and so I've had no problem with performance. But honestly, I feel for a lot of people who have performance issues. I don't think the game looks good enough to warrant whatever the heck is going on for most people. The lighting, especially, is inconsistent and kinda bad in a lot of places. On the other hand, when it looks good, it looks really good.

-500+ USD worth of DLC already? Why tho? That said, Alma in those Cammy pants...

It's a 7/10 for me, which is 2 points above my baseline of 5 as being average. So it's very good. I still like Rise better and think World looks better by way of having consistent visual fidelity. As always with these games: Much, MUCH more fun with friends.

I really, REALLY wanted to like this game. I have been playing the Monster Hunter series since the 3DS days. Wilds has some fantastic monster designs, smooth and satisfying combat, and a decent story. Unfortunately the gameplay loop outside the combat encounters is where it falls flat for me. 

I am not a fan of how automated the movement and gathering mechanics are with the Seikret. In the other titles you are rewarded for learning the different maps. You find shortcuts, areas with more materials and spots to lure the monster you are hunting. In Wilds since movement between areas is automated you never really learn the maps. In my 40 hours of playing I never once became familiar with the map layouts because I never needed to.

I know in future updates they increased the difficulty of the monsters, but playing through the campaign and then some post-game I really was never challenged. I died a few times here and there, but most of the time I could tank a half-dozen hits before it became a problem. 

The optimization for this game is astoundingly bad. I really do not know what they were thinking when they released the game in the state it was at launch. They kept promising updates and improvements, but it took over a year for the game to get into a 'fine' state. Even now it still struggles and has tons of crashes. I cannot see how this will improve further as it seems like a limitation of the engine used to make it.

I really want to like Wilds but there are so many things that I cannot get over when playing it. I hope its newly announced expansion, Ascendance, fixes a lot of the core issues this game has. I probably wont be picking up Ascendance unless they show that the performance has vastly improved.

Oh boy, the game was a hot mess when it released... So i waited until their final update before the expansion to get back into it.

First of all: lots of weapons got balanced, and i feel in a good way.
Quite a few monsters were brought back from previous games, at varying degrees of success.

But, and it's the main problem with the game in its actual state: the endgame is absolute garbage.
Apart from farming the top tier weapons from the meta, there's nothing to do.
Top of that, ALL those weapons are the same variation: the gogmartian.
Let's summarize how Gogmartian farm is working:
-First, you have to farm your artian parts, 3 needed by weapon, all with matching element, of course randomly obtained.
-Then you upgrade them to gogmartian with 3 attack gogmazios relics, the two others not being relevant.
-Now, you upgrade the gogmartian to unlock 2 random perks that can be rerolled.
-And finally you reroll all your random stats to get the bonuses you need.
You end up farming the absolute mess that is the gogmazios fight (3 phases, all gimmick-y with the messiest battle in P3 from all the MH series, and i'm a veteran from the PS2 game...) and killing loads and loads of Arkveld to get the stats reroll. Absolutely no variety in endgame arsenal, all gogmartian with the right bonuses are the top tier for each element. And it's full gacha.

Add on that the UI that is the most confusing and complicated I've ever seen. (and i'm a Black Desert Online player...)
How do you need three types of invites to play with someone? Why do you get disconnected when your host is switching biomes while environment linked? Why can't i check my hunting horn melodies without pressing START, CHECK EQUIPMENT, pressing RT TWO TIMES and then MIXING THE MELODIES BECAUSE THE INTERFACE ISN'T CLEAR AT ALL, all of this WITH AN APEX PREDATOR TRYING TO EAT ME??
Best MH, i came from MH generation, Freedom, Rise/sunbreak and world!

MH Wilds is a weird game with high highs and really low lows :
The pros :

-The game is beautiful, the prettiest monster hunter game we ever had 

-The character editor is the best we ever had for a monster hunter game

-Some weapons got complete reworks making them way funnier than ever

-Most of the new monsters are really cool and returning ones are better than ever. They're overall more agressive than before


The cons :
-Awful performances even with a good computer the game can randomly crash

-Several bugs especially Network bugs still here since release, making it impossible to play online sometimes because you get disconnected from the quest even with a good connection

-The engame gameplay loop is just not fun, instead of just grinding for the mats you need to craft a weapon like in previous games you basically have to play a gacha using Gog's mats, you're just farming this single quest to be able to have your weapon, it's just frustrating and not fun and feels more like a job than a game

-The Gogmazios quest, this quest is just not well designed phase 1 and 2 are okay but the 3rd one is a real mess, by wanting to give us a cinematic experience they gave us a visual mess. This quest isn't really hard (at least the 9* one) it's just messy and most of the time frustrating. The fact this is the main quest you'll do if you want a good end game build makes it worse and will make you hate this monster because this quest is the same thing over and over

-Having to pay if you want to change completely the appearance of your character

There's a lot to say about Wilds. It's Monster Hunter. It's the 6th mainline installment. It had really bad performance issues at launch, which has plagued its reputation for its whole lifetime. The user interface is incredibly difficult. That's really the worst part about the game to me. Eventually you get used to it, but you'll be confused for a while. Also, you have to manually configure many of the control settings, particularly for seikret riding and focus mode.

The weapons' movesets have been updated, and while I haven't played them all, the weapons I use have all received vast improvements. Long sword has a two-button rotation issue, but I personally don't mind that so much. The fun of playing long sword is lining up counters and timing them, not performing your dps rotation. As a keyboard/mouse player, I adore focus mode. Trying to rotate my hunter was always annoying in World.

The monster lineup is pretty cool. I love the return of Nerscylla and Lagiacrus, but I think the new monsters are the real highlight. Rey Dau is one of the most hype monsters since Nergigante, and Arkveld surpassed even that. Both of their theme songs and the ice cliff theme are top tier.

Overall, I think Wilds is the best modern Monster Hunter, even though it has its issues. I predict Wilds will get the same resurgence in players that World is currently experiencing when everyone realizes how good it is. I suppose we'll only find out with Ascendance.

Personally, I am in love with Monster Hunter Wilds. It's a phenomenal game with fun monsters. I'd say the worst part about it is, of course, the performance. In order to make the game run smoothly on my laptop, I had to go into an INI file in the game's folder and bump up my CPU thread count.

Additionally, even with the high resolution texture pack, the game's textures are severely lacking and you can very often see the pixels clear as day, though this is mostly only evident during cutscenes. Outside of texture issues, however, I think the game generally looks pretty alright.

I'd say the game is a solid 7.5/10, if you can get it running. Great gameplay, alright story, and with Ascendance coming out soon, I am eager to see what's next

After the performance fixes, this is easily the best base monster hunter game in the whole franchise. Even though the story is mediocre, the whole narrative of the game feels like a throwback to Monster Hunter 4 with subtle hints of it all over the place with the additions of gore magala, frenzy virus and gogmazios with the final update. The game was criticized heavily for it being too easy on launch and after several patches and with the addition of 9 and 10 star monsters, the game difficulty is perfect and is as hard as any other monster hunter games in the past. Focus mode is also a great addition in my opinion as it just makes more sense being able to move and reposition mid combos rather than commiting to a certain direction like older monster hunter games, and with the monsters being actually hard now after the patches, this makes the game alot more enjoyable as you have alot more offense and dosen't feel overpowered like during the launch. I also like the fact that there is only two elder dragons in the game and apex monsters are the norm for challenge as opposed to previous games, as the problem was usually in endgame you usually hunt down elder dragons and it gets boring soon, whereas every monster can feel as powerful depending on the star level and elder dragons are now more depicted as calamitous force than before, due to its rarity and the quality of those fights represents it really well. The way layered armor and weapons work is also well implemented compared to previous games. the only downsides i have with the game is the way artian/ gogma artian system works, other than that the game is amazing.

I'm a big fan of the Monster Hunter series and really enjoyed both World and Rise.

Unfortunately, I haven't been able to get this game running reliably. It crashes constantly, usually near the end of hunts, which is incredibly frustrating after spending so much time on a fight. I've tried all the suggested fixes, but nothing has resolved the issue.

I only picked it up recently during a sale, and given how long the game has been out, I expected these problems to have been addressed by now. Because of the ongoing stability issues, I can't recommend it in its current state.

I love the Monster Hunter games, but this one is disappointing. It's hard for me to say that, because the story isn't bad. The gameplay is good, and there is tons I actually do like about the game. 

The problem is - the game has to work. The performance issues are frustrating, to say the least.

I already went through a large chunk of the campaign once, got close to HR50 - had to restart the game because my save data got corrupted. It got so bad that the game wouldn't even launch into the main menu, and I'd get a wall of text of errors. Bummer, because I was on par with the people I was playing with - and now I have a baby character. No biggie, rush through the campaign again. Get further than I was, start building my stuff back up. 

Only for the game to completely crash my whole system, and won't launch of start up. Again. 

I want to continue playing, but when it does this crap - I'm not sure if I want to invest more hours into the same content I've already had to repeat. I'm not sure at the moment if my save data is corrupted, as I'm still trying to fix the problems. But I'm not sure if I'm willing on restarting a brand new character for the third time...which sucks because I was really excited to play this game, and was enjoying what I had so far. 

Disheartend and dissapointed. But also, not surprised considering I went through something similar with MH: RIse and save data.

Edit 1: First crash was due to save cloud data being corrupted, so that was turned off. This crash was due to my AMD drivers all of a sudden being null and void, and the game is working again after reinstalling the drivers, and re-enabling the device. Will update in the future if there are future crashes.  I hope to be able to change this review to a good one at some point.

I don't understand a lot of the complaints about this game. I think it's fun and spectacular which is what Capcom was probably going for with this. They make Devil May Cry, of course they're focused on spectacle that's accessible over anything else. When you just try to enjoy the game on those merits, of spectacle, it makes a lot more sense why things are they way they are.

Frankly, I was surprised there's hyper armor again and the massive QOL changes and features there are in this. Not to mention that this is probably the best Fashion Hunter game in the entire series with the incredibly customizable layered armor and layered weapons plus weapon charms plus color customizable chicken mount plus layered palico weapons and armor plus the wacky collaboration features that we're hopefully getting even more of in future plus... you get the point.

It runs okay on my PC but on Steam Deck it has to be a blurry upscaled mess on all minimum settings, and there's STILL massive frame drops. Please gods please optimize it for Steam Deck if nothing else. The gameplay on Deck is great but the graphics are awful.

Monster hunter requires a certain type of person to enjoy the content. I am not that person. Take this review with a grain of salt. 

Maybe it is just me. But if you are a fan of a: 
[list]
[*] Kinda Dry Story 
[*] Annoying characters 
[*] Quite Challenging Boss fights that dont really explain how to deal with them
[*] Hidden Mechanics within the weapon system (Example: [spoiler] Power Struggles, Moves within the weapons, mounting, etc) [/spoiler]
[*] A company that is quite stuck in their ways 
[*] A grind at the end of the game that requires you running the same content over and over and over. 
[/list]
Then this game is for you! 

Now you may be saying: 
[list]
[*] "But Mr. Frog, you have 45+ hours of this game. Why play it if you dont really enjoy it?" [spoiler] Friends played it and wanted to spend time with them. Continued to play because I dont want to lose them [/spoiler]
[*] "well you must have enjoyed parts of it to keep playing.... what parts are those." [spoiler] Fishing and gathering are always fun. The customization of things that you can do also help quite a bit. (Camps, armour, etc). [/spoiler]
[*] "If there was a fight/fights that turned you off from the game or made you lose your interest, what were they?" [spoiler] Omega, Apex predators, any content above a 7★ [/spoiler]
[*] "If there was something that capcom could change to bring you back, what would it be?" [spoiler] Similar to other games, a difficulty setting would be nice. The game is quite hard but for some, that is what attracts them to it. That is fine, but there should be an option to make it easier for people who simply want to enjoy the finer things that this game has to offer. Easy mode, as it were. [/spoiler]
[/list]
Again, take this review with a grain of salt. Perhaps you will find it more enjoyable than I did.

I'll try and write this without mentioning performance issues, because while important don't have anything to do with the actual game itself.

This game is very much an evolution of the complete rebrand the series underwent with World, taking that blueprint while streamlining some of the more superficial gameplay elements to make it the most accessible Monster Hunter game to date. It gets you right into the fights without much of the fluff, while also being one of the first MonHun games to actually have a good story attached to it. 

That streamlining I think leads to its detriment, as some of the older clunk that made the older Monster Hunter games more unique is now lost. I do like some of the changes, such as the Focus Mode so I no longer feel shame about whiffing on ever SAED on charge blade because the monster moved three feet to the left mid-move. The Wound system is also miles better than the Clutch Claw in World/Iceborne, as it still feels impactful to the gameplay flow without it being absolutely game-breaking to said flow. The change that really doesn't get me is getting to the hunt as well. There's no longer any pre-preparation and now you can just press a button and have your Seikret mount move immediately wherever the monster you're hunting after is going to. That, in my opinion, takes out part of the "hunting" part of Monster Hunter, which I know has turned away some older fans for being too "easy". 

All in all, it's still a Monster Hunter game at its core. There are still interesting monsters to fight and the DNA of the original games that had people hooked on the series are still here. The game will likely get better once the expansion comes out so we can compare it to the games of the past, but as is the game is a complete one. It's not a perfect game but it's a great one nonetheless.
Love all the new monsters and weapon changes. The combat feels more refined than World which is incredibly impressive and overall is a cohesive and accessible monster hunter experience.

Don't get your hopes up for this game. It's a long shot that it'll be as good as MHW. If you're going to buy it, wait for a VERY GOOD discount.

The main story is simply unbearable. The characters have the charisma of a door.

The game has a weird pace. You don't get to know the areas very well because everything feels way too rushed.

There is practically no grinding in this game. If you kill a monster twice, you'll likely have its full armor set and weapon already. That may sound like a good thing, but because of that, you never really get familiar with the environment. Gear recipes also rarely require parts from small monsters, which makes you completely ignore their existence.

The same goes for the Seikret. It may seem like a good addition, but it's introduced at the very beginning of the game, which encourages you to just let it guide you straight to the large monsters while completely ignoring the environment. The Scoutflies already know the monsters' scent without you having to interact with any tracks, even though that tracking mechanic still exists for some reason.

I kinda liked the combat changes. The wound mechanic is really satisfying, but all the large monsters feel like punching bags. None of them gave me the same rush I felt when I first fought a Barioth or Tigrex.

The world itself doesn't feel alive like MHW's. It feels like the game was made by people who don't really love the franchise. For example, cooking is no longer a fun animation of Palicoes working hard to prepare a huge feast for you. Instead, dude cooks a damn CABBAGE and eats it like it's the greatest meal ever created. Excuse me???
Yes, the hunter it the cook.

Honestly, this game is disappointing in 99% of its aspects. I'm definitely not buying the DLC.

P.S.: I've been playing Monster Hunter since the PSP games.

Monster Hunter Wilds is an absolutely beautiful game. 

The character models, the lighting, the weather, and the monsters themselves look stunning; it really shows how a strong art direction and a cohesive vision can carry an experience to new heights. Performance is a hot topic for this title, but I had zero issues running it on high settings with a great framerate. The game is clearly CPU heavy, but it runs beautifully if you have a good one.

This is my very first Monster Hunter game. It took me a while to get used to the strange control scheme after playing so many souls-like games, but the combat felt incredible once the mechanics clicked. Every monster presents a unique puzzle of openings and punishes; it almost plays like a fighting game at times. You create your own character and watch them develop through the gear you craft, the builds you design, and your own skill as a player.

The story is also highly engaging. You get to discover a completely new region in a believable way alongside a guild and companions that create a genuine sense of camaraderie and partnership. Everyone is supportive, making the entire journey feel like a massive group effort where you explore different cultures that feel diverse and thematic to the setting. 

All around spectacular game.

Monster Hunter Wilds is honestly a blast to play. The combat is fun, the monsters are awesome to fight, and there's something incredibly satisfying about hunting a giant creature, crafting better gear, and then going after something even bigger. Once the gameplay loop clicks, it's really hard to stop playing.

The world is also impressive, and the new features make the experience feel smoother and more accessible without losing what makes Monster Hunter special. Whether you're playing solo or with friends, there's always something to do.

That said... the optimization is rough.

I don't know what happened, but this game has some serious rendering issues. Sometimes I'll fast travel somewhere and the characters look like they came straight out of a game from the early 2000s. Textures take forever to load, models randomly lose detail, and it can completely kill the immersion for a moment. It's honestly hilarious when it happens, but it definitely shouldn't be happening this often.

The performance problems are probably my biggest complaint. Underneath all that, there's an amazing game, but the technical issues hold it back more than they should.

Overall, Monster Hunter Wilds is incredibly fun and one of those games that can easily keep you hooked for dozens of hours. I just wish the optimization matched the quality of the gameplay, because seeing a monster rendered perfectly while a character looks like they escaped from a PS2 game is a bit wild.

Still, despite the technical issues, I had a great time with it and would definitely recommend it if you're a fan of action RPGs and giant monster fights.
