I originally tried Monster Hunter Wilds for one simple reason: I wanted to see whether my PC could run it well. Surprisingly, the game would not let me simply walk into the first area and test performance. Instead, I was forced through a lengthy series of tutorials, scripted sequences, explanations, and mandatory mechanics before I could finally do the one thing I had launched the game to do.

[b]That experience ended up reflecting my biggest problem with Monster Hunter Wilds as a whole: the game feels determined to automate as much of the hunting experience as possible.[/b]

Whether it's the Seikret acting as an autotaxi that automatically takes you to your destination, the heavily guided progression, or the numerous wound-bursting finishers and cinematic combat actions, I often felt like the game was playing itself for me. Older Monster Hunter titles made me feel like I was actively tracking, learning, and hunting monsters. Wilds frequently feels more like I'm triggering automated systems and watching flashy animations play out.

[b]My second major issue is the image quality.[/b]

Despite being the most technically advanced Monster Hunter game to date, Wilds often looks blurrier than both Monster Hunter World: Iceborne and Monster Hunter Rise: Sunbreak. Yes, the models have more polygons. Yes, the animations are more detailed. Yes, the environments contain more visual detail. But during actual gameplay, much of that work is hidden behind a layer of blur.

Before anyone suggests changing settings, I already disabled Motion Blur, Depth of Field, and every other blur-related option available. I also tested the game at native 1080p with DLSS completely disabled. The image still looked noticeably blurry.

The only thing that significantly improved image clarity was increasing the render scale to 200%. At that point the game finally looked sharp, but performance dropped dramatically. Frame Generation helped recover some of the lost framerate, but needing 200% render scale just to achieve a clean image is not a realistic solution for most players.

I often hear people criticize Monster Hunter Rise: Sunbreak for looking too colorful or too much like a "children's game." While Rise may not push cutting-edge graphics, one thing it does not suffer from is excessive blur. In motion, I frequently find Rise to be the cleaner and more visually appealing game simply because I can actually see the image clearly.

Even in the limited time I spent with Wilds, I got the impression that Capcom was focused on making the game as accessible as possible in order to attract the largest possible audience. There is nothing wrong with making a game more approachable, but I feel that some of the player agency, challenge, and sense of hunting that defined previous Monster Hunter games has been lost in the process.

[b]Should you buy Monster Hunter Wilds?[/b]

If you're already a huge Monster Hunter fan, you've probably purchased it regardless of what I write here, especially considering the game didn't just release yesterday.

For everyone else, I would strongly recommend trying Monster Hunter World: Iceborne and/or Monster Hunter Rise: Sunbreak first when they inevitably appear on one of their frequent deep sales. Both games feel much closer to what is considered a classic Monster Hunter experience, while Wilds feels like a streamlined and heavily automated version designed primarily to appeal to newcomers.

I've played Monster Hunter since the Monster Hunter Freedom on PSP and have basically played every game since. The pacing of this game makes it very difficult for me to get into it as a long-time fan. There are very frustrating moments in the very early game where you want to go off and explore, but you're denied, because you're roped into a quest that asks you to follow character X in story moment Y. And it's all bland and boring. 

Let me explore. 
Let me go enjoy the game. 

It's very discouraging that I don't even want to progress the story because it doesn't feel like the monster hunter game has even begun. 

The game difficulty is also very easy. Which I expected, but in the setting I'm in it's further discouraging. 

I've been told the game opens up once you get through the first 3 chapters, but that will take 10+ more hours of gameplay, and honestly there are so many games to play, it's tough to justify staying engaged with the game. It sucks. 

I'm leaving this review in hopes that this tells the developers that their longtime fans (who loved World) would like them to pare back some of the changes they've made in the hopes of finding a larger audience.

Completed a large portion of high-rank content and felt it was time for me to leave a review. My initial thoughts when I started the game were that it was far too easy. A lot of quest items were sort of just handed to you and environmental effects such as heat and cold were constantly not a problem due to the existence of items and consumables readily handed to you. As of the time of writing, this is still the case as most of these environmental effects are basically non-issues. 

As for monster difficulty, early on monsters die very fast and by the time the fight feels like it's really getting going. It's already over. This isn't to say I didn't have fun, but it does lose some of the joy of hunting a monster for 25 to 30 minutes when they're dying in as little as five, making the gameplay feel very fast early on where the game kind of just hands you everything and lets you win. Because of this early on I was going to write a negative review but decided to hold off. And I'm glad I did because the game definitely picks up difficulty, just more gradually than previous monster hunters. As you delve into high rank and deal with tempered monsters, the difficulty of the previous games is back and even with some extra flavor and some more dynamic combat. The wound system is very fun and works far better on tempered monsters where it allows you to sort of force yourself to have a bit of a breather as a desperate maneuver to stun the monster for a brief second. 

Progression also feels better into the high ranks early on. Early on you get so many items and drops that grinding monsters is also something that felt like it was originally pushed to the wayside. However, as if my current review and experiencing the later game of high rank, I can safely say the grind is there and it's very enjoyable and feels incredibly rewarding. The story is more interesting than previous entries, although I didn't quite enjoy it as much as I enjoyed Monster Hunter 4 ultimate, but I think it's unfair to put that game in this equation as that was probably the Pinnacle of the Monster Hunter series and I don't think any game is ever going to hit quite the highs that monster Hunter 4 ultimate did. Speaking of Monster Hunter 4 Ultimate Gore Magala is back and even a pinnacle part of the plot in chapter 5. The buildup is nice and the battle itself is extremely fun and honestly a bit refreshing to have Gore Magala back in this capacity and with a proper title of Demi elder dragon. Here's hoping that the DLC brings about Shigeru Magala. 

All this being said, I do recommend this game if you're a Monster Hunter fan. Just know you're going to have to get through some easy early game content to get to that monster Hunter excitement you're probably looking for. Mounts like in monster Hunter world are available from very early on and do change the way you move around the map. Unfortunately paint pods are rather useless. They are infinite but that doesn't really matter when they don't really serve any sort of purpose at this point. Paint pods no longer assist in tracking a monster as the flies you carry on you reveal all monster locations on the map which makes me wish that there was some sort of weather effect that would maybe Dull The watcher flies effectiveness to incentivize the use of paint pods again. Part of the problem I think is that as many people have said this game feels more like monster fighter rather than monster Hunter. And part of that is because of the fact that it just shows you where the monster is and you just constantly mollywop it. There is no proper tracking. At least world had tracking where you had to find the monster first, in this game, the days of tracking basically don't exist and that does greatly disappoint me. 
However, Gogmagzios is back so instant 10/10

Monster Hunter Wilds is way worse than Monster Hunter World in almost every way.

The combat is good when you actually get to do it but the game constantly interrupts it. You’ll fight a monster for a few minutes, start having fun and then get thrown into long stretches of dialogue and story.

The story and characters just don’t work. Most of the characters are forgettable, the dialogue drags on and it feels like the game is more interested in talking at you than letting you play.

The bigger issue is that the game feels split in two. The hunting and combat feel like classic Monster Hunter but everything around it the story, characters, and auto-pathing feels like it belongs to a different kind of game.

TL;DR: The hunting and combat are basically Monster Hunter World but a bit worse because hunts are shorter and easier. Everything outside of that (story, characters, auto-tracking) feels like a different design direction that doesn’t fit and it constantly gets in the way of the gameplay.

Overall 7/10

Wilds is a weird game for me. There's a lot of things I really like compared to World, and there's a lot of things I really dislike compared to World. But overall I'd said it's a marginal improvement.

The good:
- Better combat, weapon movesets, depth.
- Much better QOL features with a healthy blend of exploring the map and rushing the monsters thanks to the mount + grapple
- Better tutorial features (the tutorial is still not exactly good but things are a bit less vague atleast)
- The new drip is immaculate

The bad:
- The story sucks. Monster Hunter never had a "good" story but here it feels extermely forced and does a poor job of making me care about Nata and his village.
- The performance isn't great on most hardware, and isn't as good as it should be even on high end hardware.
- The art style generally makes everything look washed out and bland most of the time.
- The gimmick of guardian monsters being constant explosions is not partiacularly interesting or fun to fight against.
- Low Rank is a bit slow to get through

Everyone focuses on performance and how bad it is, so I'm not going to. The game itself until the endgame is actually pretty good. The difficulty curve is smooth, the environments/weather are nice, and the monsters are generally pretty cool to fight. This all falls apart at the endgame.

The endgame grind isn't even the problem, it's how ridiculous they made the monsters. You can't get hit or you lose 80%+ HP. Which means you can just randomly get 100-0'd from attacks that really don't even look like they should be doing much damage, or you can get hit once and then combo'd before you can even get up to move out of the way.

I run LBG, which apparently makes you take up to 40% more damage for some reason, which means that defense is LITERALLY useless. I had near max defense on my armor (All Rarity 8 pieces, 2 pieces transcended and fully upgraded, 3 upgraded 1-10 randomly), lvl 3 divine blessing (which never activates when you'd want it to), armor charm, food defense buff, and armor dust (forget the name). Trying to do the gore magala quest with garbage Nadia and he's literally 100-0'ing me with random little baby attacks. It's actually unplayable.

So you might think that LBG/HBG/Bow is the problem, right? Just stay away from those and you'll not only do way more damage but defense will actually do something right? Wrong. I've been in games with people who have fully upgraded/transcended armor getting hit with nothing-attacks for 70%+ of their HP as well. I've never seen one get 100-0'd like a gunner, but they still take WAY TOO MUCH DAMAGE.

This games endgame can be summed up like this: Try to dodge attack, sometimes fail to dodge attack, hope you can get up before it combos you dead, drink all the way back up to 100% to avoid a one shot, 5 minutes later start doing damage again. It's just awful.

On to the review.

Monster hunter Wilds does things right and things wrong (in my opinion) regarding the latest installement in the mainline MH franchise.

I recently picked the game up and had been waiting for a nice sale due to the rumours of lack of content/monsters and then i went directly from monster hunter World: Iceborne.

I wanted to make my own opinion and didn't look anything up regarding it's content except from the initial complaints regarding PC optimization and that the content felt short when it comes to the story and the lack of monsters.

So... after clearing the mainstory and reached HR 100+ (for those who don't know HR refers to Hunter rank. Higher the rank, the more difficult missions unlocks basically) what is my take/experience?

I haven't experience any crashes and the game seems to run well on my system so no complains there.

They've added quite a lot of Quality of life features like if your inventory is full, any excessive material you gather get sent to your stash/item box, so no more dilemmas regarding what material to abondon and what to keep, making farming crafting materials easier when roaming the different places.

The gameplay has improved for some weapons, some has been changed how their unique mechanic works, some for the better some for the worse (looking at you Heavy bowgun.... where's my wyvernsnipe??).

One change i just feel that they unneccessary made more complicated than it was in MH World is simply joining a friends lobby in-game, when my friend joined me to play together he joined my lobby but he seemed to be in another instance(?) of said lobby. 
Another change i noticed was that your mount doesn't really feel responsive in a way i would expect/prefer (so this is my own opinion, i just feel that it's weird sometimes).

The campsite that you unlock just doesn't feel as earned as it was in MH World. In World you would need to find it and then gather required materials for it to be unlocked.

The different areas is a downgrade when it comes to immersive exploration, there's no true hidden parts that you need to find and move branches/roots that's in the way to unlock a new cave/path/section which is a shame.

I see what they were trying to do with the different areas  but some feels unispired but what they all have in common is that the roaming monsters looks more that the area is "bloated" you can clearly see each monster and where they are as soon as you arrive, field research is no longer a thing you seem to just by hunting learn and know everything after a few encounters.

When it comes to story and progression... the sotry is short... i mean really short and this is a comparison with Monster hunter World base game and Monster hunter world Iceborne seperately.

Felt like i barely started the game before i reached the credits and all story beats felt rushed. Without spoiling to much i would warn monster hunter world players to not even remotely expect epic hunt like the scenario where you finally face zogmagdaros that actually feels epic (untill you need to farm for materials).

Another thing that i think harms the game more than doing it any favors is that there's basically no hunts that truly feels challenging, most of the story related hunts are total pushovers so there's no monster you encounter a long the way that acts as a skillcheck where you are expected to learn and maybe also change up your gear to target it's weaknesses.
And then another thing that i think doesn't do the game any favors is how easy it is to level you hunter rank and tempered monsters basically are available way early in my opinion since the game doesn't really give you any time to learn or rather incentivises you with learning the game mechanics properly.

There's only 3 hunts that has any kind of mechanic that you actually need to learn how to do the fight. one of them isn't enjoyable (in my opinion) and SOS flares can really screw you on that one so either dedicated team or solo is my suggestion, you'll know which one i'm referring to when you encounter it.

The end-game right know is basically famring RNG materials to make powerful gear which i find is quite a dumb game design decision since you can become fatigue doing the same fight over and over.

The game is rather barebone when it comes to encounters but the gameplay feels a better although jumping from MH world there's some things that you might need to get used to, for exampel Hammer is no longer as simple that it is in World.

This is not a fullprice worthy game at all, if you want to get it, wait for a huge sale

This was my introduction to the series, from which I played 4U afterwards. 
Without getting into many details here is the short review the game.

Story - not very good (Maybe Lore Masters would enjoy, but no one else)

Gameplay - Very good! Love focus mode, epically after playing older titles such as Rise demo and 4U, the positioning felt awkward and clunky rather than anything particularly well designed but needing practice and skill.
Weapons were all a blast to try out, I thought all of them had fun uses and found my darling in the hunting horn.
Difficulty had a clear distinction, easy story mode, escalating difficulty in high rank, I was satisfied in the end.
I also greatly enjoyed fashion hunting, it is one of my favorite past times in the game.

Complaints and Issues - My biggest gripe with the game is that how repetitive it felt. Don't misunderstand, I know the game is all about hunting monsters and I had fun in the process, but it felt strangely empty doing so at points. 
I feel as if the game needs more ways to interact with monsters, the environment, and hunting. The game almost felt small once I beat it because all I found to do was beat up the same monsters in the same environment. 
The Strategy involved in making builds with armor and weapons is nice, but the game isn't difficult enough to really demand it, as proper skill and practice can overcome most fights as long as the gear you use isn't obviously lacking.

Overall - It was a fun game, around 7 - 8 / 10, Gameplay was excellent but could use variety, you shouldn't be here for the story, I am looking forward to the exspansion, and hope it can raise my score up some points.

It feels redundant to come on here and complain about the optimization. But I'm seriously demoralized and there's this goblin in the back of my head telling me that feedback will actually fix this somehow. Most of my hours on Monster Hunter Wilds (MHw) were put in before TU1, the game ran smoothly at the highest graphics settings without a single issue. TU1 brought a couple issues that seemed to have been fixed but after TU2 it took a nose dive, bargaining graphics, crashes every other hunt and it started to feel like too much effort for how little fun I was having. I skipped TU3 and 4 but when the ascendance trailer dropped I couldn't help but feel the need to catch up and make some builds for the expansion, after a smooth crashless couple Lagiacrus hunts I was completely hooked on the game again and hopped into Omega and Gog as soon as possible. Except the game crashed every hunt, and eventually started crashing when I would check my inventory or even on the main menu. I did everything, turned off framegen, locked my files to read only, ditched graphics entirely making it look like a projector over mashed potatoes, I reset my drivers, I reset my shaders cache, fully factory reset my pc and set launch option to -dx11. I did so much to try to make this work, my friend with the same exact specs as me and my friend with a crappy prebuilt from 2019 could run this game just fine but for some reason I'm excluded. The worst part is MHw bricked my pc, putting me in an infinite bluescreen loop only giving me the option to reset my pc again or blue screen again. So here I am 266 hours about 120 or so spent fighting this game's ♥♥♥♥♥♥ optimization with nothing to show for it.

Yet I can't bring myself to leave a bad review. If you're lucky enough to avoid the optimization demon you'll be met with a really fun experience and one of the best monster hunter communities (Nothing will ever beat world's community (ROCKET POWERED GREATSWORD!!!!!)) and if you can't avoid the optimization demons then I don't think it's unreasonable to say that they'll eventually fix it. I can't help but have faith that eventually I won't want to rip my hair out and break my nearest window when I play this game.

It has been 45 hours I've played this game. Seriously nowhere near the end of the game and exploring everything the game has to offer, but I think I've seen enough to put up a review.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a game I really like for reasons people don't. It's simple, fast, and has really ironed out all the clunkiness that the old players for some reason love, and for me? That's more than enough. I just want to fight monsters, get cool gear, explore the flora and fauna and find materials, and repeat again and again without having to deal with old clunkiness that people just can't seem to let go. The fights are intense, the weapons and armor are very good looking, and oh my god don't get me started on the character creation. You can get stuck there for hours and I wish I'm kidding.

The story is so-so and while I don't really resonate with some of the characters, I think for a game that is known for its combat and gameplay loop, it's serviceable. The open world part of the game is very underdeveloped and I wish they do more with it, although the maps are varied enough and big enough to enjoy and explore. Really wish they'd do more with the open-world aspect, seriously. There is a lot of monsters here to enjoy, and the poster boy fight is so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ fun.

If this is your first Monster hunter game and you are discouraged by the mixed review from people saying the game is bad and is too simple and everything, you honestly should pay them no mind. I think this game, and World (obviously), is the best starting point for newcomers like me who come from modern era of Monster Hunters.

Do keep in mind about the performance issues though, you can always buy, jump in, mess with the settings, and if doesn't meet your standard just refund, although you will be missing out on one of the best gaming experience.

I give MH:Wilds a solid 8,5/10, could be an easy 10/10 with better performance and more interaction with the open world, but aside from that, awesome game.

This game is a truly great experience. All the haters are just salty, nostalgia‑driven old heads. They’d also completely talk down the new Final Fantasy VII remakes, even though those too are absolute masterpieces of gaming. Some people simply can’t grow up and don’t adapt well, so they get frustrated when things don’t work exactly the way they’re used to. Sadly, there are far too many of these “gamers” these days.

Back to the actual topic — the game itself.
Your first playthrough will be an absolute blast, especially with friends. Here’s one of my only real downsides, though: the pairing system and the entire co‑op aspect are poorly done and could’ve made this game shine to an extreme degree if implemented properly. I really hope the next installment addresses this according to modern standards. There’s simply no good reason for it to be so restrictive and clunky. When it does work as intended — even with the restrictions — it’s an absolute blast.

My wife and I loved it. Her sisters played with us too, and that’s where most of our problems happened when linking up. I don’t know if it’s because we’re on PC and they’re on PlayStation, but while it worked, it had issues and required way too much reconnecting.

The endgame could also be much more fleshed out, which I’m hoping the new expansion will take care of. Overall, I think the combat has improved greatly, feels very cinematic at times, and is extremely satisfying.

Now to the so‑called performance issues.
The game runs perfectly fine for me — no problems whatsoever — and also on my wife’s PC, which has much lower specs. What most people fail to realize is that poor performance is often not solely hardware‑related, but depends heavily on how the system is set up and optimized by the user. People just slap their parts together, turn it on, and send it. It’s only a matter of time before something causes issues.

A true PC gamer knows their machine and how to operate it properly. PCPartPicker helps with compatibility, but you still need to check and adjust BIOS settings, GPU software settings, redundant Windows settings, and so on. It all depends on the hardware and software you’re running. If you know what you’re doing — which is a must when owning a PC, and honestly not that hard once you’ve done it a few times — you’ll always have a good time with maximum graphical fidelity, great performance, and almost no crashes.

If everything is set up correctly and the very rare occasion happens where something does go awry, it’s usually because the game just released and they obviously can’t test for every single unique machine configuration. That’s why it’s so important to know what you’re running and act accordingly.

Long story short: this game runs great and looks great. If you’re having issues, check Reddit or, even better, proper PC forums — there are always knowledgeable people willing to share their experience and help you solve your issue. That’s how I learned most things.

So if you like Monster Hunter, cinematic skill‑based boss fights, and cute cats that act as your squires and cook you delicious home‑made meals, look no further. In my humble opinion, this is one of the best in the franchise. I, for one, am absolutely psyched for when Ascendance drops. I’m also getting Granblue Fantasy: Relink Ragnarok and the final installment of the Final Fantasy VII Remake. What a time to be alive.

Have fun, fellow gamers, and don’t let the naysayers sway your opinion or keep you from enjoying yourself. As always with anything on the internet, you’ve got to learn to sift through the sea of nonsense to find the gold. Have a good one!

there's a sickness out there, hidden behind each engine, each share, each polygon and pixel. It permeates all and everyone suffers for it, the player, the developer. Wilds is sick, but so is everything else. The twisted and gnarled limbs of the sickness choking out hope and hammering down on belief. But wilds is still here, still fighting, and I watched, even knowing the sickness would come for it.

I am not an authority, I am nothing, I did not load those zones or play when the story was simpiler.

But I do like monster hunter, I like it a lot. I think the hunger is still there, the ache and twang, sitting just below that heart. There's a tendency to auteur, to attribute the work of many to the vision of one, but monster hunter is too many things to be one, it is too many things to be enjoyed by everyone in equal amounts, but it is my belief that it still is what they said it once was. When the grit made you sweat and the friction gave the victory a different kind of meaning. I think it's still there, but you wouldn't be wrong to rebuke me, because what I heard and what I shared tapping into the adventure was never going to be the same thing as you.

And perhaps we will die fighting this war, who's right and who's wrong, when the franchise will die and we will no longer have a home, but this game is still so honest, so absurd and so confident, where the technique and skill could topple giants and the flames underfoot could be danced away if you knew the right steps.

The truth is, I have nothing logical to say to you, to convince you this is worth it, but my heart soars every time I take a swing, nock an arrow, or charge a move. There is a valiant effort behind this game, I think it's worth it.

Co-op is a lot of fun, but the endgame is one of the worst progression systems I've seen in a game.

The problem is that maximizing your weapon is almost entirely dependent on RNG. The endgame isn't really just about mastering the game or overcoming difficult challenges—it's about rolling weapons over and over until you eventually get the one you want. Once you finally get that perfect roll, you've essentially reached your peak, and the journey to get there is largely determined by luck rather than skill.

What makes it even worse is that weapon stats are tied to a hidden table. The possible outcomes are fixed, but the game doesn't tell you where you are in the sequence. One player might get the weapon they want in five rolls, while another might need hundreds or even thousands. There's no way to know. You just keep grinding and hoping the next roll is the one.

A better system would be RNG on weapon part drops while allowing players to work toward the stats they want through gameplay or remove the hidden table it's dumb. At least then your effort would feel rewarded. As it stands, the system feels arbitrary and unrewarding.

My kids initially loved the game, but this mechanic completely killed their interest. They don't play anymore, and neither do their friends. The core gameplay is fun, which makes it even more frustrating that the endgame revolves around a system that feels designed to waste your time rather than reward your progress.

The armor system is another disappointment.

In most RPGs and action RPGs, getting better armor noticeably increases your survivability. In Wilds, that doesn't seem to be the case. Going from Rarity 6 to fully upgraded Rarity 8 armor made almost no noticeable difference in my ability to survive endgame hunts. I've eaten meals, used buffs, guarded with a shield, and still been one-shot by 8-star tempered monsters.

At that point, it starts to feel like armor values are largely meaningless and that only armor skills matter. I've tried multiple recommended Charge Blade builds, fully upgraded my gear, and still get deleted by certain attacks. If the difference between mid-tier armor and endgame armor isn't noticeable in actual gameplay, then what's the point of upgrading it?

I've played a lot of games, and it's hard to think of another one where you're wearing fully upgraded endgame gear and can still be routinely one-shot. Endgame monsters should be dangerous, but character progression should also feel meaningful. In Wilds, the armor progression often feels disconnected from survivability, which makes upgrading gear far less satisfying than it should be.

The game has a fantastic foundation and excellent co-op gameplay, but the endgame progression systems undermine what could have been a truly great experience.

-You remember bounties? They're gone.

-Handing in requests to unlock things? That's gone.

-Canteens? Which have been a thing since the PS2 era? Gone until a simplified 6-item menu in the post-game.

-Turf Wars? Actually they're still here, but they're so rare you have a genuine chance to never see it happen (or if it does, you will just get a turf war message in the chat from out in the open world whilst you're in a hub).

-Perhaps you are hoping Capcom's collision detection has improved since Megaman on the NES. No, it hasn't. Enemies doing forward facing attacks can poison you from 3 meters behind their body, and no collider lines up with its visuals.

-Hey, maybe you liked part-breaking, one of the most satisfying parts of the middle of a fight; severing tails, breaking heads and backs. Yup that was extremely streamlined, you can't even break the head on a Rathalos anymore. In its place is a nice but pales-in-comparison wounds system. You will rarely ever be breaking parts off anymore and don't even think about toppling enemies with greatswords, even if you manage it they get up after four seconds. The few places where you can break/cut-off parts now takes ages.

-Every other new monster is "What if we did a tentacle thing that was black and gooey." and by the time you reach chapter 3 (Still Low Rank) they are repeating enemies as variants in the base game (At least MH World mostly waited for its expansion to start repeating outside of arch-tempered fights). 

-Optional quests are so completely hollowed out that there is a couple of "kill the random small creatures" quests and then the rest are literally just being allowed to re-fight anything you have killed before. Investigations? Double fights? Why would you need that? 

-You remember how much the clutch claw ruined MH World? Remember how they added that monsters would take this gigantic step back if stunned as part of it so that if you don't use clutch claw they get free time to recover? Well the clutch claw is removed but many monsters still just do the step back because screw you they want monsters to recover for free.

- I like toppling monsters. Do you like toppling monsters? When constant barrages from a hammer or greatsword to the legs could topple almost anything with enough skill and persistence? Yeah that's almost completely gone, it only happens one time when breaking the leg which as mentioned is extremely annoying to do now. I don't think tackle-knockdown are even in the game.

-It looks like they go to finally abandon roar-spam at the start, but it's just a calm before the storm, and past a certain point in the game (And I mean maybe 2 hours in) every single enemy roar-spams. There's no earplugs skill in low rank so you just get to suffer.

-Most enemies become intensely overtuned, where even if you learn their moves you actually still cannot counter them because the only way to do the counter system (the 'offset' attacks) is to be psychic and know what attack they will do. If you wait for a single visual cue, you're already too late. Seregios is a perfect example of this, where you cannot even unsheathe a greatsword before he does a full fly-back sweeping attack on you and thus cannot be countered unless you are just randomly swinging offset moves for no reason.

-You wanted larger regions, right? Well you're in luck, they're five times bigger now and you must use the new Seikret or you'll get nowhere. The catch? Now you'll spend 40% of your gameplay on autopilot with the seikret mount as it sat-navs you to your destinations. There's no point trying to remember the maps, half the passages are seikret-only anyway. You can literally just play Rise or Gen-Ultimate on a Nintendo Switch whilst waiting for the Seikret to take you to the next part of a fight.

- The new herd system exacerbates a historical problem where monsters get a dung-pod to their face, leave to a new spot and immediately choose to walk back to you. Now there's more enemies to randomly choose to walk or fly straight back to your position. (Shoutouts to whoever came up with Large Dung Pods being mandatory just to waste an additional inventory slot)

-Bonus that High Rank is a convoluted mess. You are not allowed to quest-hunt certain monsters (99% of them) until you reach high enough hunter rank, but you can quest-hunt their tempered versions. So you're considered not high enough HR at lv20 to hunt a Congalala but a tempered Congalala is fine...

I really hope future Monster Hunter games put more effort into the writing and cast.

I was skeptical about getting this game for a long while. I loved Worlds and Rise, but the ratings on Wilds still sits around "Mixed".  It seems that many players still complain of frame jitters and instability, but otherwise, there's a solid gaming experience obscured behind the poor PC graphical optimization.

Wilds went on sale, so I took the plunge, as did my husband. His PC is fully AMD and runs Wilds just fine. No frame issues, solid 60 FPS  with High graphics settings and without RT. My PC (Intel/Nvidia) had the stuttering frames. It wasn't too distracting, but it was just enough of an issue that I began digging for solutions. Finally, I got the suggestion to switch off DLSS. I did so, and everything was fixed. I've been playing for 10 hours since doing so, and I still have solid frames WITH RT on.  GPU temp is 64-68c while running the game if anyone was curious. =]

Graphics aside, the game is much like Worlds, but with a bit more heart so far. I've been enjoying the ride thoroughly.

Picked it up in sale. For context, I've played (almost) every single monster hunter since the MH1 demo on PS2. 

So far, I'm not really sure why it's mixed. Sure, it's not as good as world, but it also isn't as bad a rise. I'm playing it on a mid-range laptop right now and while it gets hot, I've not noticed any issues or frame drops. I've also not had any crashes that people are complaining about; I have however been disconnected from the servers a couple times which is annoying but not the end of the world. 

The game itself is pretty good, much better than I assumed it would be. It feels like MH:World and nothing stands out with the actual combat itself. The one thing I am absolutely hating though is that until High Rank, you are forced to do story mission after story mission. It's boring, it's a lot of just walking and talking followed by a cutscene. Then you have a fight, kill the monster and then you have to do another story mission which follows the exact same premise. I wish you could just skip all the story stuff; I don't care for the story, I much preferred the older monster hunter titles that were like "Go kill some ♥♥♥♥ and prove you can handle higher tier quests". 

Overall, I'd probably rate it 7.5/10 so far. Definitely not as good as world but no way as bad as people have made it out to be.

This is, tied with MH Tri, my favorite monster hunter game. 

The story is adorable and sincere (if series-normatively sparse). Every character in it is aesthetically and emotionally enjoyable and, while I miss the artistic value of the older games idiosyncrasies, I can sincerely say that at least in the case of Wilds the progressive trend towards engaging and fun gameplay breaks cleanly above "worth the trade off". 

Additionally, as an LBGunner I adore the drive towards more dynamic and engaging moment to moment gameplay (though I do miss the crushing necessity of prep time) and agree with a lot of the post release balancing. 

A lot of people say it is unstable or difficult to run but that hasn't been my experience at all - I'm on fedora with a weird GPU but that should theoretically count against it not for it and I don't think its crashed on me once.

My friends game crashes often, he has a 5070 and some AMD CPU (third-string company) - out of principle I cannot recommended Wilds.

World was incredible and Rise didn't try to hide what it was. Wilds made genuine improvements to World in meaningful areas, and is an enjoyable game, but is seemingly missing a layer of complexity that World had, particularly in endgame content, that is ultimately a shallower experience.

I would still say buy it. But I would never abandon a friend - I'd stand beside that man until the end.

Monster Hunter Wilds is turning out to be an incredible experience. It's fast, beautiful, and completely action-packed. The combat feels so much tighter and more fluid than what I'm used to, especially with the new Focus Mode letting me target monster wounds directly to get rid of that classic stiffness. I really appreciate the streamlined direction too; letting the mount automatically navigate the map keeps my momentum high and skips the tedious tracking. Plus, the dynamic weather transitions are awesome to watch, making the open world feel genuinely alive whenever I catch the monsters interacting and fighting with each other.

Having spent time with Dauntless and MH Freedom Unite, and having just finished the ‘main-stories’ for Wild Hearts and base Rise, I've played some fun titles in this genre. I'm actually playing through the Sunbreak story on the Switch right now as my portable Monster Hunter option, and ‘Monster Hunter Now’ is my cell phone quick fix when no other consoles are around. MH Wilds had been on my wishlist ever since the beta, but I finally pulled the trigger and bought it now after the expansion announcement since it was at a good discount price. Out of everything, Wilds is easily my absolute favorite so far.

I just finished Chapter 2 after finally beating the "Nu Udra" urgent quest in the Oilwell Basin, which puts me at about 35% to 40% of the way through the story based on what I’ve read. That intro cutscene where it wrangles multiple massive Ajarakan at once had me in awe and perfectly showed how the power scaling works between the monsters.

Performance-wise, I remember how rough things were when I played the free beta, and the initial launch on PC was notoriously rocky for players who bought it at release. However, the massive updates this past year have completely turned things around for me and many others. I play on controller, have DLSS and Frame Gen active, and it runs great.

With the Ascendance expansion just announced, I'm just really hyped for where this game is going. Out of everything I've played, no other game right now stands out quite like this.

Something about this game has felt an absolute chore to playthrough. If it wasn't for the love of the franchise and the desire for them to carry on I wouldn't have bothered. It took me a year after buying to finally bite the bullet and see past the performance issues too BTW.

Absolutely loved MH:W put in a good 100+ hours.

This game just seems to fall short in every area by comparison.

Story - Weak, cliche, horrendous character designs/identities, Repetitive.
Monsters - Easy, bland, lack of variety.
Environments - Ugly, uninspired.
Graphics - I'd love to join the hate train but quite frankly it's just the same victim of modern rendering techniques e.g. patchy blurry overly reliant on upscaling/frame gen  tripe. The game looks messy at full specs and the performance for what you're seeing just doesn't seem to make sense. To say it doesn't look much better than World but requires significantly more power is absurd.

Gameplay: In terms of combat its reasonable, it's the traversing that bothers me. 
Unless i'm being absolutely dense aswell the lack of insect farm/herb farms like in World just for me absolutely cripples the game. I used to craft intensely, I can honestly say I barely craft in this because they've just made it so hard to obtain the ingredients.
The armour aswell seems to be quite uninspired, the amount of slots for decorations etc seems to be nerfed IMO.

It's not an UNPLAYABLE game by a long stretch, but if this was my first experience of Monster Hunter I likely wouldn't stay on for future itterations.

Monster Hunter Wilds is easily one of my favorite games. From the moment I stepped into its world, I felt pulled into a living, breathing wilderness filled with danger, beauty, and adventure. The environments feel alive, the monsters are incredible, and every hunt feels exciting because you never know exactly how things will play out.

What I love most is how the game makes you feel like a true hunter. Tracking monsters, preparing your gear, learning attack patterns, riding your Seikret, and finally taking down a powerful creature gives such a satisfying feeling. The combat feels intense and rewarding, and Focus Mode makes battles feel more precise and cinematic.

I also really enjoy how seamless the world feels. The changing weather, the monster behavior, and the way the environment reacts around you make the game feel bigger and more immersive than ever. Whether I’m hunting solo or playing with others through crossplay, the experience is always fun and memorable.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a beautiful, action-packed game that respects what fans love about the series while also making everything feel fresh and more alive. It is the kind of game I can keep coming back to because every hunt feels like a new adventure. If you enjoy epic battles, amazing creatures, exploration, teamwork, and building stronger gear, this game is absolutely worth playing.

Infinite content

As a monster hunter fan, this game is packed full of contents, the combat is extremely fun, easy to to learn, the environments are full of life and I had fun exploring and fishing all over the places. 

However, upon release, the the game was horrendously optimized, and the developers prioritized Nvidia frame generation technologies and it completely ruined the immersiveness. The game is grey, dark and blurry most of the time, and sometime the fps dropped, dipping below 30. 

Had the development teams focus more on hardware configurations and optimizations, which they were known for (Monster Hunter World was famous for its beauty and less demanding hardware) I think Monster Hunter Wilds would have been the game of the year.

I don't recommend this game to newcomers, especially those whose PC specs are weak. But I hope they will focus on this main prominent issue within the community.

I want to preface the praise I'm about to give this game by stating a couple obvious things. First and foremost, the state the game launched in was appalling. Poor performance across the board, numerous bugs, lack of content, and missing feature were all things that plagued the game upon arrival, which is why I held off on purchasing the game until well after I knew these things had been resolved. Next up, it is absolutely worth mentioning that the game is still not very well optimized. Though playable and mostly smooth, I still experience graphical issues and occasional frame drops, even on my very current hardware. That's not to mention that I can hear my device chugging along whenever I run the game--an issue I have with nothing else I regular play on it. Finally, while I have always been an enjoyer of Monster Hunter campaigns and feel that Wilds is in contention for the very best on that front, the way in which the game railroads you into experiencing that story is heavy-handed to the point of genuine irritation. Again, I did find the story quite compelling, and was hooked for almost every minute of it, but the fact that the game is so restricted until you clear the low rank village questline is absurd. It was frustrating to think about how many people would be put off by an otherwise great game because of how it locks you into experiencing it in one specific way. New players quite literally cannot create an informed opinion on the majority of the game and decide whether or not to refund it because the opening two hours of it are bloated with exposition and tutorials, arguably even more than World was. Additionally, Field Surveys, which are maybe my favorite feature in the game and really allow the world to come to life, are arguably at odds with the campaign. There's so little you can do with them until you've reached the final boss, and you can't meaningfully progress your hunter through them either. Maybe I'm just mourning the old village-hub split, but this game could absolutely benefit from being able to engage with its world, gameplay, and mechanics without being forced into experiencing a slew of story missions which, arguably, don't show this game at its best. Setting aside those blanket criticisms though, I have absolutely adored this game. The new additions, such as seasons, packfights, and seamless traversal between prep and hunting, are the beating heart of this game. Never before has a Monster Hunter world felt so real, so explorable. While I certainly wish there were more reasons to engage with these sprawling natural expanses outside of just hunting, I've found myself so in love with the locals that, more often than not, I'll walk to a destination rather than use the Seikret (this game's equivalent to Rise's palamutes). It feels like a natural evolution of old Monster Hunter's thesis of area design, where each part of a locale has a backdrop which foreshadows the areas to come. For example, the Windward Plains is home to the Wind's Gasp, a gigantic structure which dominates the skyline from almost every vantage point. True to form, rather than simply having this act as set dressing--a skybox which wows the player but offers little more--Wilds allows you to actually travel all the way inside it, just as we once ascended the Ruined Pinnacle in GU or the Ancient Tree in World. Wilds in general has every other series title beat in terms of aesthetics, as even on its lowest settings, this is the best Monster Hunter has ever looked. While I can't say I love how the series has steadily forgone a unique art style in favor of pursuing photorealism, it's hard to deny that the result of that hasn't led to a game wherein every frame is a painting. Even just wandering over a hilltop or quietly observing the endemic life feel breathtaking in a way that is at once bombastic and yet incredibly understated. Everything in this game fills me with a sense of wonder that I struggle to describe, even, or perhaps especially, the mundane. It's a feeling akin to that first time you reach the edge of BoTW's Great Plateau, and are awash with the realization that the giant tutorial area you've just explored, which seemed so expansive from that sweeping title card shot, is actually just a small fraction of the game's world. If I had no care for the health or longevity of my PC, this is a game I would spend countless hours just letting run, checking in every now and again just to glimpse the beautiful world that exists at the players fingertips. Pivoting away from the look of the game, I want to touch on the combat. It is, undeniably, too easy--at least in low rank. While I'm not going to sit here and tell you that low rank Anjanath or Zamtrios were actually secret super bosses in their respective games, I feel that the games which they existed within were simply less streamlined and forgiving. The monster themselves are, across the board, just as challenging, if not more, than they've always been. The drop off in difficulty is instead a result of mechanics like weapon storage, wounds, Seikret fall, and more. Wounds are really the big issue here, as while they make combat undeniably enjoyable, in a sort of glue-eating, braindead way, they cause monsters to crumble like paper, which is a shame considering just how well made almost all of them are. On it's face, I don't think it's a mechanic that's impossible to fix like Iceborne's Clutch Claw was, but I worry that instead of rebalancing the system, the solution to introduce greater difficulty into the game's inevitable Master Rank expansion will be to just overinflate monster healthpools, just like Iceborne did. Regardless of my issues though, I cannot deny just how good combat feels, even with the lack of difficulty. My weapon of choice, hammer, feels even better than it did in World, which for years now has been my gold standard for how the weapon should play and feel. Considering it was maybe the worst weapon to play both in the beta and at launch, this is HUGE praise. I've taken the liberty of trying out some of my other favorites, such as dual blades, Switch Axe, and Light Bow Gun, all of which feel fantastic to play. As mentioned before, the monster are no slouch either, with this game being home to some of the very best fights new gen has to offer, with Rey Dau, Zoh Shia, and Lala Barina being some personal standouts. Ultimately, this game as of writing this review, is a sort of 2 steps forward, 1 step back situation. It introduces a lot of great things to the series, but it almost always comes at the cost of something great, or the introduction of some new, seemingly pointless obstacle. While it's a net positive in terms of the direction it's headed, the many pitfalls it has hold it back from really soaring.

[h3]9/10[/h3]

Monster Hunter Wilds delivers what is easily the best gameplay in the franchise and stands as one of the best action games available today.

The story is surprisingly cinematic by Monster Hunter standards, with a solid 18-20 hour campaign that is well paced and easy to follow. It's still not a story-driven masterpiece, but it's easily among the strongest narratives the series has offered. The cast is as charismatic as ever, although Nata feels underdeveloped and his character arc never fully lands.

Combat is where Wilds truly shines. Every weapon feels balanced, unique, and rewarding to master, with enough depth to spend dozens of hours learning each one. The pacing is excellent, animations are incredibly smooth, and the hitboxes are arguably the best the series has ever had. The Seikret is a fantastic addition that makes exploration and traversal much more enjoyable, while the ability to carry two weapons feels largely unnecessary and underutilized.

The soundtrack is phenomenal. Arkveld's theme is a standout, and hearing classic Monster Hunter tracks return never fails to create memorable moments.

Difficulty is a tale of two halves. The main story is surprisingly easy, but the post-launch monsters and Arch Tempered hunts significantly raise the challenge, creating some of the most exciting fights in the game. Rey Dau and Arkveld are particular highlights.

My biggest issue is the endgame progression. Almost every meaningful upgrade is tied to RNG, decorations, talismans, Artian weapon parts, weapon upgrades, and bonus rerolls. It often feels less like Monster Hunter and more like a free-to-play loot grinder gacha game, with very few ways to reduce the randomness.

The other major weakness is performance. While the art direction is excellent, the technical side never reaches the same level. Optimization remains disappointing, texture quality is often shockingly poor (looking like a PS3 game texture), frame pacing can be inconsistent, and the visual fidelity doesn't justify the performance cost. The colors are so washed out, I had to install ReShade to bring the game to life. Even after multiple updates, it's still one of the game's biggest flaws.

Despite those issues, Wilds is an incredible game and one of the best entry points the franchise has ever had. Whether you're a veteran hunter or a complete newcomer, it's easy to lose hundreds of hours chasing new builds, mastering weapons, and hunting monsters with friends.

Monster Hunter continues its streak of having no bad games, and Wilds is another excellent addition to the series.

NPCs will tell you the game is "fixed" because it runs slightly less ass and monsters don't trip constantly in the last 5% of quests, as if that excuses the rest of the game being complete cheeks.

It feels like this was made with investors in mind first, shoving in an "open world", detailed environmental interactions, and ecosystems that do nothing but dramatically increase the minimum system requirements, while trying to appeal to an even wider modern audience once again, even after World already successfully got so many new people interested in the series. 

If you want to get to the decent parts of the game, you have to sit through a boring, poorly written story that treats you like a child. CONSTANTLY you are put on walk&talk sections with slow NPCs that are unlikable or have no personality, up until you find and slay a monster that hardly ever fights back because it trips constantly even when going solo and disabling a partner cat. There is no interesting lore, character, story event, or mystery ever, its painfully boring and impossible to care about anything going on. If you try to have fun by slaying a monster you haven't seen yet on the map, or deviate from a boring walk&talk section, the game cries and tells you to stop. You can't even make gear for that monster you slain early until you unlock its quest later either. The only redeeming part of it are cutscenes showing monsters being monsters. And you have to sit though this ♥♥♥♥ for 20+ hours. When even modern braindead game journalists are claiming its too mindless and boring, you know its bad.

There are little positives to the more open, interconnected locales. After the awful story line that keeps you on a leash, you get a bit more freedom and can actually spend way more time exploring the maps. But since the game has had you on rails for so long, you're left with little knowledge on the actual layout of the locales. They were aware of this, so the game lets you call a Seikret that works as a taxi, driving you to whatever you select on the map automatically with one button, letting people scroll on their phones during that downtime. Why even learn or explore the locales at all? Hidden areas offer nothing of value other than the rare vantage point letting you take in the games awful visuals, and they don't offer a shortcut to areas, the Seikret already finds the optimal routes for you. This leaves the locales being extremely forgettable and lame, with the openness and ecosystem details of them feeling completely pointless. Nobody is going to care that some small little lizard takes a fat dump at a specific spot after eating a bug.

Focus mode making positioning matter even less, wounds making monsters trip and stop moving constantly, and Seikrets being able to pick you up after an attack and avoid 99% of monsters by just circling while you sharpen/heal, makes hunts feel so much less engaging and unique than before. Only a few monsters at the end of the postgame feel like they were balanced around it. I don't think the combat is bad yet, there's still some fun to be had with it if you don't try to compare it to the other games, but this is easily the lowest point in the series that leaves me with little hope for the expansion.

Update: I finally figured out what was causing the crashes on my system.

The game would crash constantly while smithing, opening the map, starting/finishing quests, etc. After a lot of troubleshooting, I found that enabling NVIDIA Frame Generation was the culprit.

Switching to AMD FSR Frame Generation completely stopped the crashes for me.

I'm leaving the review up because the crashing issue was real and made the game nearly unplayable, but at least I now have a workaround. If you're experiencing similar crashes, try disabling NVIDIA Frame Generation and use AMD FSR and see if it helps.

TL:DR it's a great Monster Hunter that is more streamlined in terms of hunting the monster. Optimization is miles better now, and has (in my opinion) the best combat of any Monster Hunter game. Solid 9/10 game for me. The story is mid (like most Monster Hunters) but fighting monsters has never felt better.

 I will start by saying I love Monster Hunter Wilds, as someone who played the original on PS2 and then hopped back in to Monster Hunter when World came out since I didn't have most of the Nintendo consoles that the prior entries were on. I've put over 1100 hours in World, significantly less in Rise but that is split across PC and Switch, and now over 300 in Wilds. It is a great game with a ton to do, my personal favorite combat of any Monster Hunter, and great new monsters/monster designs. Capcom has regularly released content as well as optimization fixes (albiet they took their sweet time on the later) and it each update has felt great.

I understand the complaints involving optimization, the game's launch was very buggy, with poor performance, many crashes, and even people with moderate or good pc specs had very unstable gameplay.  I was struggling to get 50 fps at launch, now with some settings tweaks I can get 120 fps easily, with Ray Tracing enabled. I do play on 1080p because I haven't really desired to drop the money for 4k hardware, but the game looks beautiful to me and runs well now. Where improvements like this feel like they should have been available from the start, I still do appreciate when devs don't abandon their games like so many big companies do.

Beyond core gameplay and optimization, I do see a lot of negative reviews for the game not feeling like prior entries. I get that to a degree, and every one is entitled to their own opinion, but I like going in to new series entries with a fresh perspective. They have 100% streamlined monster hunter and made it much less about 'hunting' the mosnter and more about getting straight to the fight. I do miss some of the gathering tracks and the hunts feeling like actual hunts that MH World had, and I think it had a superior design in getting to know your environment while hunting. That being said, there is no denying that the scale and combat in this game is superior, even if I still think of World as my favorite Monster Hunter. It is incredibly addicting to get back to grinding for weapons/armor and playing around with builds. The new updates also took care of difficulty that people complained about and now there's that challenge that World had again. It has me and my friends failing, going back with new builds, and seeing what works again, which is my favorite thing about Monster Hunter games.

Overall, I'd give it a 9/10. It's an excellent addition to the Monster Hunter franchise that just had a rocky start, and has a somewhat mid story, but to be fair, no one plays mainline Monster Hunters for the story.

I highly recommend avoiding Monster Hunter Wilds in its current state. Do not let the brand name fool you; this game is fundamentally broken due to atrocious optimization and technical failures.

My PC hardware actually surpasses the developer's recommended specifications, yet the game is completely unplayable. I am constantly bombarded with severe "Fatal D3D Errors" and mid-game GPU crashes.

I have gone to absolute extremes to fix this on my own: I have tested 5 different graphic configurations, disabled upscaling features, and continuously lowered the in-game quality to the absolute minimum. Despite all these efforts, the Fatal D3D crashes persist without fail.

To make matters worse, because this game relies heavily on manual saving, every single crash completely wipes out hours of my actual gameplay progress. I have spent more time troubleshooting and losing progress than actually enjoying the game.

I even reached out to Capcom's official support team, and they were unable to provide a working solution. Even worse, Steam refused to refund the game because my "playtime" exceeded 2 hours—ironically, that time was spent entirely on the main menu trying to fix their broken software.

Do not waste your hard-earned money on a misleading product that punishes you for having high-end hardware. Save your money and your sanity. Avoid this game until Capcom actually releases a massive stability patch.

installing ver. R mods that make my hunter's butt look bigger, and opting to travel by foot instead of seikret so that i can stare at her butt for longer unironically tripled the enjoyment of this game for me. it also solves the issue of seikret auto-travel depriving you of player autonomy, and gives you more time to really take in how beautiful and intricate the environments are. i would recommend everyone try this at least once.

jokes aside, now that all of the title updates for the base game are out, i think wilds is a solid 8 out of 10 experience. i think focus mode was a good addition to get more people accustomed to how MH plays, and though risebreak still has my favorite iteration of my main weapon (insect glaive), i still find it fun to use in this game. i also really like how varied the monster roster is. i will say that i found the low rank and high rank story quests to be a breeze overall, but that's to be expected since i clocked hundreds of hours into risebreak. the difficulty balance with the endgame monsters (9-10* tempereds and ATs) could've been handled more smoothly, but they're at a level that i find pretty decently engaging without completely jumping the shark (save for 10* gog and savage omega planetes, dear god these DPS checks are a nightmare). my biggest wish for ascendance is to make the weapons even crazier in terms of movesets and capabilities, which seems like will be the case with the boost bracer.

something to note is that the equipment balance has two examples of what i really like, and what i don't like. on the one hand, i love the gogmazios armor sets and transcendence mechanic, as they give you really strong endgame armor while still making it viable to combine their pieces with other armors for different group skills. on the other hand, i cannot stand artian weapons and gogma artian weapons, as it feels like there is too much randomization involved in crafting them optimally, and it devalues investing in the existing monster weapon trees. i would prefer if ascendance leaned more into what gogma armor does well, and less into what gogma weapons do poorly.

i do think wilds would've fared better at launch if it had the amount of content it did around title update 1 (mizutsune, HR [spoiler]zoh shia[/spoiler], AT rey dau). the performance issues taking as long as they did to iron out wasn't great either. also, while i think the inclemencies are really cool for what they add to the living breathing ecosystem of the forbidden lands, there are some areas in which they either don't change much to be noticeable (iceshard cliffs), or are just really ugly to look at (scarlet forest). i feel bad for uth duna because so much of its first impressions are from fighting it in muddy water, even though it's actually quite pretty in the plenty. i hope ascendance (and future MH games) give us much more vibrant locales to look at.

in general, this is a solid entry that had a lot of issues at first, but i have faith that they will continue to be ironed out and improved upon with time. with how much sunbreak helped me to love rise even more, i remain eager yet cautiously optimistic for how ascendance will elevate wilds from its current state.

also, can we please stop charging for character edit vouchers...? if there's always going to be so much cosmetic DLC, it would be nice if we could have more leeway to see how well they suit our hunters.

230 hours later, and I’m still finding reasons to come back.

Monster Hunter Wilds has somehow been both one of the easiest and hardest games for me to get into. It’s easy because you really don’t need to min-max your character, create a specialized loadout for every monster, or understand every hidden system to have a good time.

It’s hard because once I realized how much depth was there, I wanted to understand all of it.

That usually meant a ton of in-game reading, testing different builds, and the occasional trip to the wiki to figure out why one skill, weapon interaction, or piece of equipment worked the way it did. The game never forced me to go that deep, but it made me want to, which is probably the biggest compliment I can give it.

Most of my time has been spent playing with friends. The multiplayer isn’t exactly seamless, and there’s definitely an initial ritual involved in figuring out how the lobby, link party, and quest systems work. Once everyone understands it, though, it generally works with minimal issues. The way hunts scale for multiplayer is also excellent. They remain challenging and exciting without becoming frustrating or feeling completely trivial with a full group.

I’m not a fan of the number of microtransactions, but I do appreciate that they are entirely cosmetic. There’s no pay-to-win advantage, and you can completely ignore them without losing any gameplay content. That being said, the Gemma outfits have absolutely added to my personal enjoyment of the game, so I would be lying if I pretended I hadn’t bought any.

Wilds is a game that lets you engage with it at whatever level you want. You can pick up a weapon, hunt some monsters, and have a great time, or you can disappear into builds, skills, decorations, weapon mechanics, and highly specific loadouts for hundreds of hours.

Apparently, I chose the second option.

Highly recommended, especially with friends.

This is a long review, but in short:

Absolutely amazing game that I would recommend to anyone who has the proper hardware to play comfortably.

Monster Hunter has never been more beginner friendly to play which is both a positive and detriment. You may find themselves bombarded with advice and constant handholding in the beginning, with tutorials and walk and talks happening quite often. But I promise you, the combat and dynamics of hunting in Monster Hunter is unlike any other game and is so, so worth it from the amount of fun you can derive from incredible amount of diversity you can find in this game's monster roster.

 The visuals are breathtaking, probably the best Monster Hunter has looked on a graphical level (though I do prefer some of the map designs in older games like in Heaven's Mount, The Ancestral Steppes, The misty Peaks, etc.) The level of detail given to the ecology of the locales you visit is incerdibly detailed and awesome to me as a fan of specualtive biology. Having played all the Monster Hunter Games from 4U to Wilds, I can say Wilds has in my opinion the best base game experince, with hunts  in High Rank comparable to hunts in Master Rank in terms of difficulty and enjoyment.

 The story for a Monster Hunter game is solid, with some charming characters (Nadia, Erik, Rove), some returning from previous games, though the story's execution is somewhat falwed (with Nata's whole arc feeling somewhat unresolved as the Hunter has resolved most of his issues for him), the many other features of the game far outweigh the negatives of the game as a whole. Although Monster Hunter's elements as a 'Monster Hunter' has been viewed more as a 'Monster Fighter' in recent times, I still enjoy many of the features that makes Monster Hunter distinct from any other action game from breaking monster parts, using endemic life, choosing specific meals, recognising specific patterns of a certain monster type and much more.

The performances issues have been somewhat resolved but the game still is quite performance intensive. I myself have only experienced a few crashes in the beginning of Monster Hunter Wilds' launch and none since. I recommend downloading some mods to help with performance and graphical adjustments, though it is not mandatory, please play the game how you wish and achieve fun your own way without it being at the detriment of others.

Please bring back Shagaru, Valstrax and Dalamdur Capcom!) and highly recommend getting the game if you like cool monster designs, awesome fights, beautiful locales and hunting monsters!

[h1] The Sad End Of An Era [/h1]

I never thought I would be writing a negative review for a Monster Hunter game seeing as it was and still kind of is one of my favorite video game franchises, but here we are. In my opinion, Capcom has completely and utterly dropped the ball on this game and ruined what was once an incredibly compelling and deep gaming experience. In this review, I will illustrate my thoughts on what went wrong and why this game should be avoided for the time being.

[h2] The Gameplay [/h2]

Since the release of [i]Monster Hunter: World[/i] there has been a slow shift to make the series more accessible to newcomers with the introduction of streamlined mechanics. As someone who started with World and went back to play older games, I can say that most of these changes were welcome additions, moving the series forward from its slow and sometimes clunky roots. Wilds tries to continue this trend, but I feel as though it streamlined some mechanics too far, trivializing the experience. 

Take the new Seikret for example; what should be a fun tool that makes map traversal easier is immediately ruined when you realize that this creature knows where the monster is on the map at all times and is able to automatically take you to its location with very little input from the player. At that point, it really doesn't feel like you are hunting the monster anymore and it gives the player very little incentive to explore the map or study monster behavior which used to be a core mechanic of older games that was beloved by fans.

Another drastic change that’s been made was the decision to split defensive and offensive skills between armor and weapons respectively. I am really not sure what Capcom hoped to accomplish with this choice, because all it really did was make builds way more rigid and took away a lot of freedom when it comes to armor skills. This has resulted in an endgame build that basically fits every weapon with not a lot of deviation which feels very boring.

Speaking of endgame, that is another thing that Capcom has completely neutered. I’m not going to beat around the bush here and say that this game was unfinished at launch. [i]Monster Hunter[/i] has always been a series praised for its deep and complex endgame grind, but there has been a concerning trend that started with Rise where they release the game with only a couple of monsters to hunt for end game gear, and slowly add more monsters with content updates. This trend has continued with Wilds where, at launch, this game had 2 endgame monsters to grind. There is also the aspect of the special weapons that players are able to make from grinding these endgame monsters, which are also incredibly flawed. Basically, you get these weapon pieces from high powered endgame monsters and can build weapons out of them. You can then “reinforce” them which is basically rolling augments for more buffs. Here’s the thing though: if you don’t get the right stats for these weapons, you have to [i]Disassemble[/i] the weapon, losing those parts that you worked hard to grind for. That is an insanely unsatisfying and unrewarding system that is basically just straight up gambling for the right augments. I know the Monster Hunter series is known for its RNG-based systems, but this is getting ridiculous at this point.

[h2] The Story [/h2]

[i]Monster Hunter[/i] has never been a series known for its riveting plot lines or deep character arcs but over time it seems that Capcom has been wanting to remedy this. In World we got fully animated and voice acted cutscenes which deepend our connections to the world and characters. Then there was Rise which gave all of the NPC characters names rather than just their job title which I thought was nice. But then we come to Wilds, where the team seemingly decided to put even more emphasis on the story while simultaneously not putting any effort into making these characters memorable or have any sort of character arcs. This is the first time where I would say that the story of a Monster Hunter game actively got in the way and hindered my enjoyment. First of all, the story overall felt very rushed. You are moving from one locale to another at a breakneck pace with barely any time to get familiarized with the towns and locals. But at the same time, your crew of NPC friends that you’re on this journey with are constantly gathering with you for mandatory “yap sessions” where you all stand in a circle and talk about what has already happened in the story so far. It is so beyond frustrating because I want to take my time and get to know the locations and monsters of the area, but it feels like the game is shaking me by the shoulders and yelling at me to just get on with it. The story is basically the same as every other entry in the series, but I really feel like the developers are trying too hard to make me care about these plot points and characters when that was never really why I played these games to begin with. It makes the whole experience feel like it was born out of corporate greed rather than passion.

[h2] The Performance [/h2]

I will include my PC specs in this review to show that I’m not working with the best or worst hardware here but also to illustrate that the PC launch of this game was [i]rough.[/i] From day one, I have experienced several stability issues that have made playing this game quite frustrating to play. Frequent crashes, long load times, and graphical bugs have made me dread booting up this game because it just becomes a production. Not to mention it has that classic modern gaming touch of having to preload shaders before it can actually start. This is super frustrating, especially if I haven’t touched the game in a while because it will now take 20-30 minutes to actually load into this game. I don’t know if the RE engine is just bad when it comes to PC gaming, but so many important aspects of this game's performance just don’t work. The worst part is that this is all for a game that doesn’t even look that much better than [i]World.[/i] Now, credit where credit is due: CAPCOM have released several stability updates that have improved performance significantly. But the damage has been done and the game still sometimes has stability issues. It’s a real shame because up until now Monster Hunter games have had pretty rock solid performance. I just hate to see one of my favorite series in such a state.

[h2] Conclusion [/h2]
Like I said at the beginning: I don’t like having to make a review like this. I spent about 5 years playing these games religiously. There really is no other franchise that even compares to this unique living world that the team has created. So it breaks my heart to see it like this. I have watched this series slowly but surely fall into the trappings of greedy modern gaming practices that it has become a shadow of its former self. Fortunately or unfortunately depending on how you look at it; a lot of these issues will be fixed with the DLC expansion that was recently announced. It’s just a shame that we will have to pay extra to get the “full” experience. You know, there’s a part of me that wishes this series never expanded, that it never broke free of its slower, more methodical if not clunky roots and stayed a niche series. But Monster Hunter is a series that loves to experiment; that’s something I love about it. Wilds, however, has been a clear failure.
