NOTE: Original review 6/9/26

# Context
I've played Monster Hunter for a while: the original Monster Hunter on PS2, Monster Hunter Freedom: Unite on PSP, Monster Hunter Tri on Wii, Monster Hunter Tri Ultimate on Nintendo 3DS, Monster Hunter 4 Ultimate, Monster Hunter World+Iceborne, Monster Hunter Rise (did not like so did not get Sunbreak), Monster Hunter Now (mobile game), and finally I get to try Monster Hunter Wilds.

Despite my experience, I'm only an average player in terms of skill. I still chug potions and get my butt kicked from time-to-time. Here are my reviews so far: 

I generally enjoy slower-paced souls-like games and dislike faster action games like Devil May Cry. Therefore, if your tastes don't align with mine, feel free to move on from this review, as it may not apply to you.

# Focus System
So I had got to play the demo and originally found the wound system very clunky and awkward. It felt weird to try to focus on the enemy. Now that I've bought the game and played it a while, I have changed my mind. Unlike in World/Iceborne where the clutch claw forced you to pause your flow and get in melee range to wall-bash the enemy, focus attacks can be integrated into your existing combo. The focus system feels fun and rewarding compared to the old clutch claw mechanics, and I really love it.

# Map
The map is very cool! Monster Hunter Wilds has the first system where all the worlds are interconnected -- this has been somewhat of a dream of mine. You can literally walk from the volcano level to the dessert to the forest. It continues the system from World/Iceborne where you can randomly find monsters in the field on a stroll without being on a quest and just decide to ruin its day. It continues to have all the little critters you can capture, fish in ponds you can fish out, and large monsters still leave trails you can investigate. It definitely feels like a sequel to World/Iceborne. 

# Campaign
I love the single-player campaign! It's no longer just "enter a quest and see a cutscene". They literally walk you through the whole world, with a Pokemon Snap type of rails where you can just pull out your clutch claw and it guides you on a tour while you catch ingredients or fauna. I personally get a little anxious when I'm unable to control my character or I'm forced to walk at a slow stroll, but it helps that I can catch little stuff to distract myself.
So far, the story itself seems fine. I don't like the little boy character, but the others are entertaining. It's fine -- more story than any other Monster Hunter game or any souls-like games I've played.

# Combat
So World/Iceborne had a slower-paced combat, and Rise had a fast-paced combat. They've thankfully gone back to the slower-paced that I enjoyed. I liked the weight of my weapons to feel heavy and impactful, and having to consider whether I should take a second swing versus prepare to dodge/block similar to souls games. Rise had removed that deliberation needed by adding emergency buttons to fly out of the way, and that has thankfully been removed. 

# Weapons

## Lance
So I've used lance in literally every other Monster Hunter game starting with the PS2 one where you could literally lock down Rathian with head stabs. It's a safe and comfortable weapon, and it was improved greatly with MH Tri where they added the counter stab. It was improved further in World where they added power guard (at least I think that's when it was added). I hated lance in Rise because a slow, tanky weapon feels awful in a fast-paced environment. In Wilds, in the demo, it felt weird. My hunter wasn't changing directions to face the monster. And then I found out I have to have the focus mode *toggled* to stay on, and then now my hunter always faces the camera and I wreck face. They kind of ruined the flow by adding a counter thrust automatically when you press O (circle) three times followed by another circle (without holding R2). This forces you into the counter thrust stance, which I hate, because I'm spamming circle until I get three thrusts normally, and then press R2 + O only if the monster is winding up an attack; otherwise, I'd just hop and attack again. So this new flow ruins me if I spam circle. I could of course just not spam buttons, but I don't really like timing my attack presses. Oh well. It's still fun.
Oh, and in Rise, they nerfed offensive guard -- it couldn't be procced by counter thrust, and that ruined the game for me. I have no idea in Wilds if it works, as I don't have access to the skill yet.
Focus mode works seamlessly into the combo, as it's just an extra few stabs. You won't notice the difference in your combos.

## Bow
I only played a little of this in the demo and training, and it felt kind of weak, but Focus mode works at range, so it also shouldn't ruin your flow too much. It otherwise plays just like how it did in World, and it feels the same.

## Chargeblade
I wanted to try learning a new weapon, so I tried this weapon. I dabled in it in World because it looked cool, and then I mained it in Monster Hunter Now, and it's a fun weapon in this game. They made it easier to convert your phial energy into filled vials. I think they also tweaked it so that axe mode has a chainsaw mode that is kind of fun. And whenever you do a Focus attack, you thrust your chainsaw into the monster until it explodes -- it's really awesome. For the Focus attack on chargeblade alone being an amazing and satisfying chainsaw thing alone, I've switched to trying to main chargeblade in Wilds, and it's crazy fun. Highly recommend!
My only gripe is that triangle + circle + R2 should really pull out your shield, but it doesn't. That sucks. 

# Multi-player
Alright, so I've only played single-player online exclusively because you get freaking amazing NPC hunters!!! 
You can se up an option to *always* start the hunt with 3 other NPCs, and if they die, they don't take away from your retries, so you can last forever! And they're amazing! They stun the monster, buff you, and it lets me play aggressively with higher commitment combos. I don't know if I actually will bother with real people because I love the NPCs. If you're anti-social and feel bad whenever you cart, you'll love this!

# Monsters
I haven't played enough to see all the monsters, but I'm loving them so far. Their designs are interesting, and their moves are fairly telegraphed so far. They have some returning monsters, but most I've encountered are completely new.

# Bugs/Performance
My computer has to work a bit harder on the CPU side -- the game seems more CPU intensive for me, but my GPU is a bit stronger than my CPU, so that's understandable. But I guess because the world map is massive and interconnected, and it has to take into account all the little wildlife, all the large monsters in all the zones, etc. That being said, I can run in 60 FPS high graphics and it's like 20-30% GPU usage and like 50-70% CPU usage
My game crashed only once the entire time I played, and it was during a campaign cutscene.

# Overall
I'm loving the game so far. If you loved World/Iceborne, I highly recommend giving it a try!

--- 

Update 6/11/29: So I just beat the story campaign in about 18 hours logged (I was AFK a lot of that time, so it was probably less). It's a short campaign. Monster Hunter is known for having an extensive endgame/postgame, but keep it in mind in case that's a turn-off for you. I did enjoy the campaign, and I thought it's totally worth the sale price of about $30. I stand by what I said for everything else: the gameplay is fun, the monsters are fun, and it just feels like a good follow-up to Monster Hunter World/Iceborne.

After 200 hours I haven't had any performance issues, I don't know how the other players with much better spces than me leave negative reviews talking about poor performance, the game works completely fine for me. I've only had some connection issues but that gets fixed just by updating my dirvers so I wouldn't say I've had any perfomance issues so far.

The combat is really fun and the monster fights are great, there are many mechanics that I like for example being able to aim your melee attacks to target a specific part of a monster and it complements the wound mechanic perfectly. You can also use the environment at your advantage by creating hazards to trap or damage the monsters and use the endemic life in your favor in order to heal or inflict status ailments to the monsters.

There are many weapons to choose from and everyone plays very differently so there is a weapon for any playstyle; one thing that I really like about it is that no matter if you choose a simple or a very complicated weapon because once you master it any weapon could be as good as the next in my opinion.

The bosses are well designed and are very distinct from one another so there will be a lot of variety in the many different hunts that you can take once you hit High Rank. The main story can feel easy at the beginning for some players but the late game bosses have more advanced patterns of attacks and it could get really dangerous if you mess up.

The developers clearly wanted to make a fast paced and action packed entry on the series; while MH WORLD has a more slow and meticulous approach that a lot of people liked becuse they felt like actual hunters WILDS gives you a bird like dinosaur that automatically rides you through the map so you can focus only on fighting and so removing the elements of exploration and monster tracking that makes World so appealing for most people.
I think that it is a valid criticism and I actually agree with the players who feel that way but I also feel that they are too harsh on this game just because is different while it clearly has no intentions on being like it's predecessor.

In conclusion, old school Monster Hunter players may not like the newer games and specially this one either for the easier difficulty or the quality of life that removed some elements form previous entries but every Monter Hunter game is very different from one another and everyone of them are able to excel on their own despite their differences.

Honestly this game is a 10/10 for me and I highly recommend it to any one who likes action/adventure games and to anyone who wants to get into playing Monster Hunter.

Monster Hunter Wilds is exactly the kind of evolution I wanted from the series. The combat feels weighty, satisfying, and rewarding, while the world feels much more alive and connected than before. Every hunt feels like an actual expedition instead of just loading into an arena and chasing a monster.

The monsters are easily the highlight. Their animations, behavior, and presence make them feel dangerous and believable. Fights can get chaotic, but in the best way, especially when the environment, weather, and other monsters start affecting the hunt.

The weapon gameplay is still addictive. Learning a weapon, improving your timing, crafting better gear, and slowly mastering each monster gives the game that classic Monster Hunter loop that keeps pulling you back in for “just one more hunt.”

That said, the game is not perfect. Performance can be inconsistent at times, and some parts may feel overwhelming if you are new to the series. But once it clicks, it becomes one of the most satisfying action RPG experiences out there.

If you enjoy deep combat, huge monsters, grinding for better gear, and slowly improving your skill, Monster Hunter Wilds is absolutely worth playing.

Recommended.

The game itself is relatively fine, my gripe is with the Co-op.

My friend group saw it was a 4 player game and thought it would be a cool new one to try out (we've never played a game like this one before). Come to find out the process of playing together is so obnoxiously convoluted that it's not even worth playing.

You can't do actual missions together, you have to do the dialogue and npc following by yourself, then once there's a boss fight, it'll send an auto invite to anyone in your party to join (which isn't instantaneous btw, you'll have like 30-60 seconds of just you fighting by yourself at the very least; PLUS, it spawns your teammates at the base, not at the boss fight, so they have to run all the way to the fight, wherever it may be)

Oh, did you think you were gonna be able to just run around in the open environment together by just simply being in a party? You'd be absolutely right if it were any other game. This game requires you to be in a "link party" first, then be sure you've progressed at least an hour and a half through the game by yourselves, then you can make an "environment party", where you can just run around the open world together and fight basic monsters, no quest-related things, though.

I've never been so irritated trying to figure out how to simply play with my friends, and we would have all just returned the game but by the time we were able to finally get far enough to make it work, the 2 hour limit for returns was over.

If you're in my shoes wondering if you and your friends should get this game to play together, look elsewhere, it's genuinely not worth the headache to do barely anything with each other anyway.

I've been seeing a lot of reviews with both positives and negatives. Some criticisms are fair, but I feel like some takes go a little too far. That doesn't make the game any less fun for me.

For context, I've played MH Rise, MH4 Ultimate (3DS), and Monster Hunter Stories 2.

The game had a rough launch, which I think was the main reason behind many of the negative performance reviews. It's still a fairly demanding game, but after a few updates it runs fine for me. It definitely took some time to find the right settings. I've never experienced any crashes, except when I had the Medal overlay enabled and tried switching FSR on and off.

I have what I'd consider a mid-range PC and play on a 3440×1440 ultrawide monitor, which makes the game even more demanding. Even so, I can maintain around 60 FPS with upscaling enabled and decent settings.

Throughout the game, I really enjoyed myself. The world feels much more alive compared to previous titles, and I liked the simple story. The combat is a lot of fun, especially the wound system (as a Dual Blades mainit keeps me feeling like Levi Ackerman from Attack on Titan).

I do miss the wirebug mechanics from MH Rise, as well as being able to directly control monsters while mounting them, since they added a lot of variety and mobility to hunts.

Overall, I'd recommend the game if you have okay hardware. Despite its technical issues, I've had a great time with it and think the game has largely redeemed itself after its rough launch.

Coming from Monster Hunter World, MH Wilds feels like a side-grade instead of a successor. Graphically this game feels like a downgrade compared to MH world. While the lighting might be better in this game everything feels muddy and blurry, so any higher details that might be present in this game is lost. Performance is also not that great considering what is on screen. 
Crashes are also very common. Going through a playthrough with a friend, it was a gamble on how many missions we could do in one sitting before one of us crashes. 
Playing with friends is also painful especially when trying to progress through story missions together. For some god awful reason you cant join someone else's quest until you've finished all the dialog/cutscenes, so you end up having to start the mission yourself and spend a couple minutes watching cutscenes just to watch them again while you're playing with someone else. This was also an issue with MH world but at least the game did not crash forcing you to do missions all over again.
You would think by now over a year after release they could figure out how to not have the game crash every 20-30 minutes, or fixing how god awful the experience is trying to play with with a friend. As of right now I can not recommend this to anyone unless they make the game stable enough to play, or if you get a deep discount off the price. If you're thinking of buying this I would suggest getting Monster Hunter World instead as it is a much better experience.

Back when Monster Hunter 4 Ultimate came out, I recommended it to people who were curious about Monster Hunter because it explained the ways in which the game's esoteric and kind of eccentric systems worked. And it was a huge success. 4 million copies in the west. Huge at the time for the franchise. When I said I wanted Monster Hunter to simplify itself for a bigger audience, that's really what I meant. Wilds is a game that holds your hand, assumes you're too stupid to keep an eye on how sharp your weapon is or whether or not you've killed any small monsters in the area or cut a tail, and I spent a really long time in the menu trying to turn these things off, but there doesn't seem to be a way to do it. I'm sure there are mods out there, but I shouldn't have to mod the game to tell Alma that the sword mode of the Switch Axe has inherent Mind's Eye and it'll be just another 10 seconds before the monster departs so I can sharpen strategically.

Is this a good action game? Well, probably. But I don't think it's a very good Monster Hunter game. The game hasn't really made me feel like I've hunted anything. All the "hunting" is automatically done for you by the admittedly cool-looking bird mount that auto-steers if you let it be for more than 2 seconds. I had similar complaints about the Scoutflies in World, but I'd take Scoutflies with no auto-tracking any day. I guess I can see why people don't like the whole paintball thing, but I liked it. My list of issues with this game:

1. At the time of release, content was lacking. It took them a lot of time to add enough to the game so that it was actually acceptable for a MH game. Examples: lack of replayable Zoh Shia mission, lack of a Hunter Hub, etc. The experience across the lifecycle of the game was kinda poor overall. Endgame is somewhat enjoyable, though.

2. Poor performance even after the last updates (some areas of the map still at 50-ish FPS on 1440p, specific monster attacks make my GPU choke for long enough to be disrupting to gameplay, etc.). We waited A LOT until things improved, and even then, the improvements were modest. This new engine (RE Engine) is cursed.

3. Atrocious multiplayer model that gives cryptic errors every mission or another, since release, and to this day. They can appear anywhere: during initial game loading, during login, before and during missions, etc. Sometimes I can't even login or post missions in a lobby, whereas I can create/join World and Rise multiplayer with no issues whatsoever. I suspect Crossplay is the culprit, but I have no idea anymore. Also, having only 4 players like in Rise is not enough, but I don't know if having up to 100 players in a single lobby is the best idea. Note that the errors are generally server-side ones, as far as I can tell from my research. My network is configured as Capcom recommends. Disabling Crossplay or chainging regions on Steam improve stability, but eventually, they happen again.

4. The amount of RNG in this game is insane too, and I refuse to save-scum or use mods. I'd prefer if they took an approach of farming for specific items to create equipment (even if only to increase chances), instead of depending too much on lootbox mechanics. This is not a gacha game.

5. Expanding on the previous point: I dislike and disengage from the Artian endgame whenever possible, for the reasons already stated.

6. Overtuned difficulty in the endgame. The impression I have is that adjustments were a knee-jerk reaction to criticism of the game being "too easy." It seems difficulty is just more moster HP + more damage + one-hit KOs, which is just a band-aid over an issue that's deeper than just minor tweaks. It seems something in the gameplay makes it hard to balance hunter's abilities and monster's challenge. Too many mechanics, perhaps?

7. Can we consider Gogmazios a siege fight? If so, that's not what I was expecting. I wish we have old Siege fights like before, with a shared goal across missions. Also, I dislike Gogmazios' fight: too much spectacle, too many huge attacks, area-of-denial everywhere, and too much craziness going on. Multiplayer is unstable for me, so sometimes I can't even complete missions before some random error pops up. If you bring Support Hunters, they completely steal the moment of the fight, and when you least expect, Proof of a Hero is playing and you're not even in the arena. Luckily, I'm not farming Artian, so I can just ignore Gogmazios altogether.

8. Excessive skills and mechanics. I have the impression that the base game has too much "stuff" going on, which maybe shows a lack of focus? Most of the systems and skills should have been introduced on G-rank instead.

9. Minor pet-peeve: The lack of a truly viable Health Augment mechanic in the endgame is also something that disappoints me. PLEASE, Capcom, bring it back on the expansion. I don't care if it's a bit less effective than in World, just bring it back.

I hope they improve things for G-rank, in which case I'll revise this review, but so far, I'm not holding my breath. I'll wait some time, and also for reviews, before buying the expansion. I must confess that, even though I'm disappointed with Wilds, I'm still hopeful the team can turn things around, but I can't give anything but a negative review in good/honest conscience.

Currently have fought every monster in the finished base game. In the current state that the game is in, I do believe that Wilds is a better game than World was at the end of its base game title update cycle. Keep in mind that I say this as a person whose favorite game is World: Iceborne.

Starting with arguably the most important of any monster hunter game: the monsters. World ended its base game with 35 monsters. Wilds is sitting at 34 monsters. The monster roster is, at least I think, inarguably higher quality than World's roster. The variety in the roster is exceptional: brute wyverns, fanged beasts, leviathans, bird wyverns, leviathans, cephalopods, temnocerans; its got a remarkably even spread of all them. The copy cats are few in number (guardian dosha, rathalos, and arkveld) whereas world is known for it's lacking variety (lots of flying and brute wyverns and several copy cats: azure and pink, radobaan, Jyuratodus, and lunastra). And there aren't any standout stinkers. At worst, kut ku is mildly annoying but charming and gypceros is a little underwhelming, but also with some charm of his own in the right moments. World, on the other hand, had some very notably unenjoyable monsters. Jyuratodus, Lavasioth, Zorah Magdoros, Leshen, Uragaan, and Lunastra are all known for being particularly frustrating, boring, or both. I actually like Lunastra but many do not, so I thought I'd mention her.

Performance is far far better than the state it was in at release. I consistently maintain 90-120 fps on max settings with no framegen, only dropping below 80 when I'm in a black loading screen switching areas. I know that others have reported issues with GPU crashes, and that sucks, but, if it means anything, I've never experienced any such crashes myself. I know that my specs aren't cheap, but they're also not top of the line either and the game runs like butter for me while looking absolutely gorgeous. I don't know what people are on about when they say that World looks better. Wilds is honestly one of the most graphically impressive and vibrant games I've ever played, and I much prefer it to World's rather muted and washed out palette.

My two complaints with wilds are with the story and the difficulty. The story is buns. I didn't give a ♥♥♥♥ about it, and it felt like a slog just trying to get to the next thing while mashing through dialogue and waiting through on rails sections that I didn't care for. And I get Nata's a child, but he seemed laughably naive to me ("Arkveld and I are same." wtf?) Monster hunter has never been franchise known for its gripping narrative. A problem shows up. You kill the problem. A new problem shows up. Rinse and repeat. But the on rails sections and dialogue about sparing monster lives just made it a little more mind numbing for me. The difficulty is also far too easy during the story. Like, laughably so. I played solo for the almost the entirety of it and I never lost a single hunt until far into high rank. Wounds are far to prolific and completely cripple any monster when taken advantage of by someone with more than half a brain. Fortunately, later into high rank, the difficulty kicks up considerably to a level that I want in a monster hunter title (I now consistently lose certain hunts), but I shouldn't have to make it that far to encounter any resistance. Real shame.

Overall Wilds is great. If you've got an at least decent pc, go ahead give it a shot. If things don't work for ya, then you can refund if any issues discovered within 2 hours. Can't wait for ascendance.

The game itself looks good and runs well at 144fps at 2k on my higher end specs, however very dull looking in terms of colors. The combat is fun and fluid, with the focus mode allowing for much more freedom in the combat, though it makes the game a lot easier. The weapons are all pretty fun, with the dual blades, my main weapon, probably being in the best state its ever been. The game itself is easyish, with none of the low rank monsters making me cart once, but I feel as if its been the same for rise and world, where their difficulty started spiking especially after the expansion DLC.
For the rest of the game, the story is actually on the higher end for the stories in monster hunter games. The open world is beautiful, but the seikrets really trivialize the exploration, so you cant really "feel" that its an open world. The hunt itself has become the easiest its ever been, with no real investigations in the game. The monster designs are probably at the best theyve ever been, with all of the fights being pretty fun. 

Overall 8/10

Don't let the mixed reviews discourage you from trying out wilds. I also had the GPU crashing issue for a while. My particular (and unexpected) solution involved moving the game files onto a separate hard disk from my videos that I generally play on my other monitor while gaming, and in Properties>General in the launch options, i put in -dx11 to force the game to start with DirectX11. I've had multiple multi hour game sessions since doing this, and so far, I've not had a single crash. It still runs on the disk drive as I don't currently have a large enough SSD.

That out of the way, Monster Hunter Wilds is a fantastic addition to the series. All the weapons got quality of life updates from previous games, popping off wounds on monsters is never not a satisfying feeling. The ability for mounts to glide is a lot of fun to exploit by diving in from above and raining hellfire down on a sleeping monster. Whereas historically in Rise/World I had a lot of trouble finding the decorations I want for my stuff, in Wilds, I'm basically drowning in options with some exceptions. A good problem to be had for sure.

The largest criticism I've got is the aforementioned technical issues. MH Wilds doesn't seem to be the best optimized game regardless of whether it's on an SSD or an HDD and appears to have some memory leak issues. With it being over a year since release, I'm not the most optimistic about a quality of life update, but I hope nonetheless we get one before the new expansion drops next year. The story is also, at least IMO having started with MH4U, nothing super remarkable. You're not going to lose a lot if you decide to skip everything. 

in conclusion: If you're not sure you can deal with the GPU crashes with your PC, get it on consoles,

12 hours of gameplay at the time of review. 11 of those hours have been messing with settings, getting Vortex for the FPS boost mod, and looking things up as well as installing new drivers and updates and doing literally anything possible to get the FPS past 50. 

It's not even the graphics, it's the insanely laggy and horrible optimization of the game that drags it down. It's such a hardcore CPU bottleneck because of all the unnecessary ♥♥♥♥ happening in the background that you can't see lol. 

Edit: If you change your graphics from 1920 to 1280 and the FPS doesn't increase, it's a dead giveaway for being a CPU bottleneck as I mentioned above. Meaning that the graphics ARE NOT the things responsible for your frames. Look through settings for anything with a CPU rating on it. I lowered render distance, reduced animations, lowered endemic life count, reduced mesh detail (kept texture quality at high) and reduced foliage quality and swaying to the minimum. All this and I was still capped at 55fps. NVIDIA AI frame generation is a godsend.

Features one of the better stories i.m.o. for a Monster Hunter title. Something something ecosystem isn't in balance, go fix.

Combat is more fluid as compared to World, but that also makes it more forgiving, would have loved more challenge in the early game.  Later, you'll have the option to challenge Arch-Tempered versions of previous monster, which did kick my butt. Moreover, I personally wasn't a big fan of some monster species--without spoiling much, I would've loved more 'four legged kind'.

Base game MH:Wilds has two good maps, being the steppes and the forest--however, the two other maps are primarly underground and feel bland and not lived in. MH:World had more variety, each having a unique local and living fauna.

The visuals look good, but the performance--even after all the performance patches--is not good. I would've been contempt with stable 60fps, but even then it dips at some intense scenes. I understand (?) the engine wasn't meant for the big open world areas, but I expected more from a mainline series from CAPCOM. Performance issue DID tire me, to the point I didn't feel connected with the game.

As it stands now, I'll give it a 6,7/10--in particular it's (1) the performance and (2) the maps that don't do it well enough for me.

In many ways, Wilds manages to be a lesser "hunting" experience than the series' own turn-based RPG spin-off franchise, Monster Hunter Stories, and I can't help but feel that with each new installment, Monster Hunter loses a piece of what made it into the quinessential hunting-action franchise in the first place.

If you can plod through an overly-streamlined story that has you progressing on rails for the first 10 hours, as well as a VERY slow difficulty curve that will leave most genre veterans wanting, you'll find that a delightfully challenging, albeit grindy, endgame awaits you in Wilds. However, I don't believe this game's approach of saving the best for last was worth the tradeoff this time, and unlike series classics such as Monster Hunter 4 Ultimate or Monster Hunter World: Iceborne, I can't see myself itching to boot up a brand new save file on Wilds anytime soon.

But alas, it's still Monster Hunter, so I'll recommend it, only with the caveat that you should save this one for last if you're a newcomer. Capcom's strange reluctance to port any of the series' older titles to modern hardware puts fans in a frustrating spot right now, so if you're unwilling to resort to emulation to play the classics, I'd at least recommend starting with Monster Hunter World. You'll thank yourself later.

As much as I like this game, it does have some glaring issues that get somewhat hard to ignore after playing for 60 hours. I'll list some of the pros and cons below.

The pros include:
- Fun, fast paced combat with a variety of unique weapons.
- Each monster feels unique to fight and are very well designed.
- Due to the amount of variety in different weapons, armor sets and skills, the opportunities for builds are almost limitless.
- The environments look good (most of the time), and each area really feels different than the last, though color grading can make them look bland sometimes.
- The character creator is fun to toy around with, and I usually spend a lot of time fine-tuning the way my character looks.

And for the cons:
- The performance is still rough, though infinitely better than how it was when the game had first released, but it is choppy at times, even on a higher end PC.
- The multiplayer is convoluted and needlessly annoying to set up, there was no reason to change how it was in some of the previous games like Rise or World.
- The story is only okay, nothing special compared to some of the other games CAPCOM has put out recently like Pragmata or Resident Evil: Requiem. 
- Nata is a character that exists.

If I could give games mixed reviews, this would be a case where I would, it has a lot of good things about it, but lots of flaws to go with it. I'd say it's a 7/10 at the moment, though I'm hoping we'll get some news on the DLC sooner rather than later and maybe that can improve on the issues the game has.

I only this year finished deco mantles in world. As for my thoughts on game it's fine atm, good basis but it's kinda missing in in content with harder fights. Base roster is pretty easy. Besides AT Arkveld and Omega none of the fights are actually that difficult move wise. They can make seregios one shot you and have bazillion hp as the "difficulty" they added seregios still has a simple to learn moveset and nothing will change that. I've been getting 60 fps most of the time with upscaling everything at max with dips to like round 45 fps on more graphiclly intense areas like the desert in plenty or the rainstorms in jungle. 
Now the main reason I'm putting the game as not recomend is pretty simple. My friend has a 6700 XT which is one of the most standard budget gpus. He is a huge monster hunter fan and pre ordered the game. He has been having crashing issues all the time. Is the main reason I waited till game is round 30€ to grab coz if a popular gpu as that is having problems what will my B580 perform like which is like a card that less people have than there is hunting horn players. To my surprise I only had two crashes so far and both were the ones where I entered a menu to fast. Friend decided to play the game with me after I got it in december and he found a "fix" where if he lowers the power of his gpu in AMD software it crashes less. Wyveria is still crash prone so we basiclly were doing speedrun mode when we had to fight AT Arkveld for his gear. He crashed once during those but I just waited for him to reconnect so we can trap Arkveld for gear drops. So with that in mind I can't recomend a game that just might not work for you.
Second issue I have with game is Gogma artian weapons. One of reasons I like mon hun is when you get at end you got all this list of weapons where you can look at raw, crit, sharpness, deco slots, element and decide which one would be good for weapon type or build you are using. Gogma artian makes all that moot. It is the best weapon type period and you can RNG roll armor set bonuses on it. When people decide to do spreadsheets instead of proper farming coz it's too RNG you know you got a problem.
Also to add to RNG I got 282 hours in game. I'm mostly a SnS and Lance player. I still didn't get a single Offensive Guard/Guard, Guard Up/Guard decorations dropped. So yea it can get bad.
Personal pet peeve is also that game looks really bad color wise. I recently booted up world to finish all my achivo hunting in that and that game just has way better artistic direction. Wilds can look good but for majority of game you are stuck in the weather effect mode which just ruins the game both with how it looks and how much you can see. I legit couldn't see Gore Magala during the quest I hunted it in icepeak  coz blizzard covered it up. Like I'd just see claw swipes come out of the storm. It can get pretty absurd.
Also why the ♥♥♥♥ did that jin dahaad has a armor that is focused on power clashes but no Lance weapon? Who designed this garbage? Clearly the worst offender in the entire game.
Anyway once Monster Hunter Wilds Unleashed drops and me and friend can beat Dragontorch Magala flagship without crashing I'll change this review to positive. I just hope they don't somehow make the game even less stable with the expansion.

Been passionately playing monster hunter since MH3U (hunting horn main).
First, let me explain that this game had a ROUGH launch for pc! Not only the optimization, it was the weapon tuning, input delays, difficulty, lack of monsters within the open world and less content compared to every other monster hunter titles. The game felt barren after you were shotgun through low rank/storyline into high rank. You were forced into the storyline until it was finished. Before the title updates, the game didn't have much to do afterwards and left a deep scar into Wilds from previous monster hunter communities.

   After playing from launch to title update 4, I believe Capcom has done what I felt to be a validated come back from these title updates. They fixed their optimization issues, added more event quests, tuned up weapons, added more monsters and scaled up late high-rank difficulties (You will face challenges).
The dev team did their best to listen to the Wilds community from the rough launch and they did deliver. Their efforts were genuine and I fully hope the DLC is to be something that the community is all hoping for.

Do not buy this game. Capcom has been playing fast and loose with performance for a while, but the game will actually be unplayable after 5 hours on any AMD graphics cards. Capcom knows about this, and their solution is to recommend year old AMD drivers that do not solve the problem. I played 90 hours on a 2070, then upgraded to AMD, only to require my graphics to be lower than my 2070 could run with frequent crashes. Look into any other RE Engine games and you will see that almost all of them cause "fatal d3d error 24 dxgi_error_device_removed" with AMD cards. People will tell you to undervolt your card, reduce frequency, turn off upscaling techniques, clear your shader caches, etc. The only solution that buys you time is deleting your shader cache files, for me specifically I have to delete DXCCache every time I launch the game to play a single hunt. Capcom does not care about you or your crashes. They got you already: they gave you a look at their game, dropped a title update, bricked it for many people and locked the door. These crashes conveniently can be attributed to hardware malfunction or software corruption, and Capcom wields that excuse like an axe. In fact, you might find most people on the internet describe this as a client-side problem; it isn't. This game worked before they "fixed performance."

wilds' story though is so... i don't even know. i think id feel better about it if it WERE awful, because at least theres some entertainment value in that, but its just annoying at best. playing with other people is a task and a half, because not only did they split multiplayer between like 80 different equally annoying systems, but this game continues worlds' trend of not being able to do multiplayer unless both of you have seen the same cutscenes.

which is fine, it was an inconvenience in world but there werent that many cutscenes to get around, and world had more Hunting than Story. but this game has a cutscene literally every 2 seconds for things that /dont even justify cutscenes!!!/ 

like wow, yes, the 50th scene where i cant control my character on the mount where we do nothing but stare at the environment while we zoom in for 20 seconds on ANOTHER wildlife creature while alma talks my ears off about how Valuable the Earth Is. its done in /every/ new area without fail, and it sucks, because if you thought the handler in world was annoying like i did, alma is much better! except they shove so many annoying arbitrary cutscenes down your throat with her in them that as time goes on her enthusiasm for learning just becomes obnoxious!

plus, and what really gets me about the mount system, is that i do LIKE the convenience of Having a mount. i dont like that it gets rid of one of my favorite game aspects from world, which is learning the terrain and the environment and how monsters Work. part of the fun for me is seeing a monster get injured and running back to its nest and you having to look around and figure out where to go and how to get to its nest, if there are any environmental traps you could use to slow down its path there, etc etc.

in wilds there is none of that, because the mount auto tracks anything you target. you start fighting a monster and it runs off? hop back on the mount, scroll your phone for a minute or 5, check back into the fight, rinse and repeat. you dont interact with the environment as much because everything is pointed out or handed to you. "fire your claw to knock over x hazard!" is a popup you get during almost every fight. you dont have to manually chase down monsters or figure out where their returning point is because thats what the mounts for. 

that all said -

to lay off the complaining a bit, i DO really enjoy the combat. its a lot more fluid than it is in rise or world, even though i think the controls (if youre playing keyboard & mouse) are convoluted, its easy to learn what works for you combat wise and what doesnt. i dont use the lock on camera, for instance, because of motion sickness and the fact it disorients me, and i can still beat things Fine.

the game IS very pretty environment wise & character wise. im not going to say anything in the game is ugly because that would be a lie, and if you liked worlds environments youll like this one. you do have to go out of your way to interact with it, where in world it was more integrated into the game experience, but it is worth it.

when the game isnt trying to Force you to care about the characters, theyre actually very lovely. i like that the handler(s) have passion for things Other than being a handler, and i like that the other hunting team you work with throughout the story is made of competent people. natas story as far as things go is Understandable when the game isnt Telling You Explicitly to feel bad for him. 

so all of that said, where does the game stand for me?

as i said in the tldr, its alright. it has QOL over world i appreciate a lot but i think capcom DESPERATELY needs to learn what makes a story good if MH is going to take the direction of a story focused series from now on, and if youre GOING to buy this game, buy it on sale. otherwise id recommend just playing world instead

I have played MH since Freedom Unite. That is my credentials not that it matters. But anyone who says "Just don't use x system, it's optional" are the same reddit-tard normies who leech onto people's favorite franchises (CoD and Halo come to mind as prime examples) and MURDER beloved game series. And this might be controversial but just because you like something doesn't make it good or peak - and Wilds isn't bad, but there are some problems that, for me, keep it from getting past "i like it" and to "This is the best MH since (enter your favorite one here)". I will try to explain what I mean. I'm only a woman, so my opinions largely don't matter anyways. It's not that serious.

I actually liked the wound system, I felt like it was a better implementation than the clutch claw from World even though I liked CC there too. In World the need to use the CC was more overt and required you latch onto the monster - if you didn't, it turned a 10 minute hunt into a 20 minute hunt (approximately, but you get the picture). The way that it is used to grab environmental aids, pods, use the net, resources is a natural progression that I think is overlooked sometimes. The wound system didn't require you to latch on like a screaming banshee, it shows you weak points and gives you opportunities based on weapon type. Good. Natural progression. Solid 8/10 experience.

Now, I will preface this by saying I still have trouble navigating the maps, but I do like that it seems pretty straightforward to find items. Though, items are so prolific, it kind of feels moot to actually prepare for a quest outside upgrading my weapons/armor because of the sheer amount of items/endemic life. There's just a lot of it, but I do think it's good in a pinch - especially in higher difficulty quests. In higher rank quests, gets a 6/10.

And why do I have to cook my own food? Where are the cats that magically tap the pot and BOOM there's a bunch of fried rice. Sure, I still get the same effect. I still get the buffs. But when I order a steak and they bring me a cow and a grill, I have to ask: "Why am I paying $60 to catch and cook my own food when I could have gone to Kroger and bought a yellow sticker ribeye to grill at home and still have money leftover for jalapeño poppers?" It just makes no sense. 4/10. I want full service Chandler's, not self service gas station hotdogs.

Why is the guild now SO POOR that we are living in a 3rd world tent with a blacksmith that only has the grip strength to hold a hammer? How did we go from "over four generations of hunters coming to research this phenomenon, we have built this incredible forest base that has a restaurant, a fully stocked smithy, a floating gathering hub (that was accessible from the start), three types of living quarters for our top hunters, full staffed biology/botanical research, and an outpost base with at least half of that and additional things like a melder and safari for your fuzzy buddies" or the Hoarfrost segment of "Lets build a completely functional, isolated base in less than a month in the bitter cold and a hot spring gathering hub for our elite hunters AND give them a McMansion with two ponds and a hot spring with a license for house pets" all the way down to "We've been here for x amount of time and only managed to post up a bunch of tents, no kitchen, a primitive smithy, a small stockpile, and you get a private tent but that's all you get". sure, you dont get spoiled with living spaces in older games and its just a world thing - BUT RIDDLE ME THIS, BATMAN: If I can capture lil guys in the wild, where the heck am I supposed to put them?! 3/10. Makes no sense

Werner did nothing wrong. He fixed the World Spine, told them that the energy built up was too much and the forge would blow. They ignored him and turned it on anyways. Then they cried about it and Gemma says "use your heads, guys" and, magically, they guy who'd lived there his whole life remembered there was a second forge. They use that one and call Gemma a hero and Werner gets to sulk. Bull. Story, based on that and several cringey Nata moments, and a stupid amount of "women are better leaders than men", gets a genuine 2/10. I am not here for boobs or POC celebration or story. I'm here to hunt monsters. MH4U did the story balancing the best. It's like when you watch a Godzilla movie and out of like 2 hours, you only get like 20 minutes of Godzilla. Some bull. I do feel like Wilds is missing a lot of that cartoonish charm from the older games, it leans a bit too hard into environmentalism and, where World got praise for it, I feel like Wilds missed the mark in a way that makes it less even about the environment and more about the NPCs. I think they had a huge missed opportunity with the addition of Arkveld and how it was a artificially created - if they wanted their modern tie, THERE IT WAS! It was right fricken there and it felt like they half-assed it. Like: "Oh yeah, btw, there's a franken-monster that is trying to figure out where it stands in the ecosystem" - which, to be fair, is kind of a jurassic world rip off but its also very topical because one of the BIGGEST themes in monster hunter is HUMAN MEDDLING or HUMAN'S GOING TO FAR. The Equal Dragon Weapon and that giant tower cemented together by a metric crap load of Kushala are two of the most grievous atrocities cited by the guild were major causes of the Great Dragon War that NUKED mankind back to the beginning, scrabbling together rudimentary tools to start the perilous climb back up the mountain. But the genetically created monsters felt kind of like the bug subplot in Jurassic World: Dominion, it felt like nonsense instead of a plot point of any real consequence. But the idea was there, the execution was kind of poor with the guardian monsters. 5/10. much room for improvement.

Alright, this review is very Love/Hate. I got this game at launch. I was looking forward to it as a long time Hunter on various gens/consoles. But Day1, the performance issues were BAD. Clipping/freezing/glitching. Tried various settings changes. I could play, but it wasn't a great experience. Lots of people had issues. Then update/patch came out. Somehow made it worse. Couldn't even walk around base camp, let alone hunt. Completely unplayable. Uninstalled. Super Unhappy

>>> Fast Forward. Over a year goes by and i start seeing it popping up on online feeds again. So i decided to redownload it and start over. NOW its playable. 

Most of the hate for this game comes from the original launch issues and I was one of those people. But now that I've been able and played to the credits rolling, I'll say the game is decent. The story is pretty short and simple, but the characters aren't terrible mindless zombies. The first credit-roll, before going HighRanking, with all my main weapons and armor crafted took about 20-25 hrs. Some veteran Hunters are upset that they made hunts a lot easier with Seikret-Riding and Focus Mode. Frankly, it makes it easier for new players to hop on and learn. You can also put 2 weapon on your load-out so if you want to try different playstyles on one play through, its much easier. 

All-in-All my rating would be much higher if the game didn't have such bad performance issues at launch. Most of that isn't a problem NOW. I occasionally have online connection issues, but the performance has drastically changed for the better even at Ultra-high settings. I'd say, if you're a Veteran Hunter, you can still have fun on this game. I have. If some new mechanics are "too easy".... just don't use them..  As a Newbie or someone wanting to try MH for the first time, i would say this game was built for you.

If I could review a game neutrally I would. 

For starters, this game is a beast to run as well as you'd like, and even after the optimization update I still get crashes on AMD hardware. Crashes that report as an overheat error.. at 50c. lol. Somehow has a lower power draw than World on my machine in spite of this so I guess that's a plus? 😭

Anyways, onto the actual game. 
Wilds streamlines a TON of mechanics, even beyond that of 5th Gen. The game is a mixed bag for me in that regard. To get complaints out of the way: 
Your supply chest is always with you, and even then in hot/cold areas there are bugs whose only purpose is to save you from the horror of using an item the game gives you. You have zero cooldown wirefalling onto your bird, who is a non-combatant in this game unlike Palamutes and thus has no other actions to queue beyond bailing you. You have an aimbot toggle with no downsides. The team seemingly balances monster power based on how much people complain about difficulty, leading to a very sharp uptick in stats in the endgame, practically out of nowhere. (Though, it is nowhere near the insanity of some past games, lol.) Weapons are seemingly designed to funnel you into your (usually new) big finisher more or less across the board. Pre-TU this was.. rather obnoxious, more so than now. Some weapons are still rather strange. I main Insect Glaive in games that have it, and otherwise Longsword/Charge Blade/Switch Axe/Sword&Shield. Glaive is on of my least played weapons in the game because of how weird they made it in this game. And when they're not weird, they're bugged. IG and LS both have ~7 frames (at 60fps) of input delay on their ROLL. This includes when the weapon is sheathed, because these weapons committed the sin of having a multi-action button that uses the evade button somewhere in their kit. In a past Title Update they fixed it for Longsword's most precise counter, and then just.. didn't fix it for the [b]DODGE BUTTON[/b]. Items practically spawn in your inventory for very little due diligence. Seriously, I have never ran out of materials less in any Monster Hunter, and I claim DLC starter packs in oldgen games. I cannot cause myself to go below 50,000 Points when actively trying to while playing. Wholly unrestricted Seikret autopilot saves you from the horror of truly learning the layout of any given map. Many strong mechanics skew the Monster-Hunter relationship towards the Hunter, and in all but the hardest fights you can kind of just.. not care about what they're doing. The endgame largely boils down to hunting one guy, or if you let yourself dabble into 10*, like.. 6 guys to subject yourself to RNG. This is a stark contrast from the pre-TU4's 9 Star system where you could grind gear with practically any monster in the game. 
Something simple that was earnable in World, and $2 in Rise, the Wyverian Ear(rings), is locked behind a bundle that will run you ONE HUNDRED AND TEN UNITED STATES DOLLARS when it's not on sale. Thank you Capcom. May you find new sales management forthwith. 
And on a minor nitpick, like half the monsters in the game being weak to fire is just kind of goofy. 

As for positives. The game is REALLY fun to play. All of your weapons are flashy as ♥♥♥♥, and a lot of their guard rails have just straight up been removed, or at least replaced. Perfect dodges and instant blocks and offsets, while briefly complained about prior, let you outright manipulate the pace of a hunt. While I have issues with this on a design level, I cannot deny that it feels awesome as hell to do. Some weapons especially, like (imo) gunlance are pretty big winners in this game that I have discovered a love for in ways I didn't expect. Some weapons have become more granular, like Dual Blades' Demon Flurry/Blade Dance combos being multi-step and weavable. The overworld system is a significantly less clunky version of expeditions / expedition tours from 5th gen, which is extremely welcome. While not quite fully open world, the staging of the environments is quite excellent and fun to navigate. Monsters are FAR more agile, expecting more of your immediate attention due to Focus Mode (having no downsides lol). Returning fights are reimagined in quite entertaining ways; the Rath families in particular expanded on their already great reinterpretation from Rise into something truly special. Gogmazios (when not farmed excessively like the game wants LMAO), is a true marvel of spectacle for a siege fight. Arkveld in all its forms is one of the most fun monsters to fight in the series. Its design is also phenomenal, same with other original monsters like the apexes (especially Rey Dau) and Zoh Shia. Despite how intricately crafted it is to fit into its environment, I frequently forget Ajarakan is a newcomer to this game.  The models in this game really are something else. The level of detail and care put into every aspect of the world is impossible to not be impressed by. The ecology system is by far the best this series has seen in ways that make the world truly feel alive. It's easy to get used to, but monsters being able to form packs is such a unique introduction to the series that I hope they explore further in the future in Master Rank. The main pause menu is super customizable, even if kinda really weird, but the actual navigation of item boxes and loadouts is the most painless it has been in the series. 

On the topic, the customization in this game demands a ton of attention to itself. This game has one of the best character creators I have ever seen. Seeing that I could blend face part shapes the first time blew my mind, lol. By nature of the endgame progression being tied heavily to RNG, this also comes with extremely high degrees of customization in your weapons' builds. There's quite a lot of unique off-meta skills that you can play with as well; a determined mind will struggle to run out of things to try in this game. (even if this aspect is not exactly exclusive to this game in particular) Its Layered Armor system is the most in-depth the series has ever been, removing gender locks and all. Fashion is a near endless facet of Wilds' endgame. 

There are things I'm forgetting, but any more and I'll hit the character limit. Seriously. This review reaches it. Oops. 
All to say: This game reignited my passion for the series after a long time of not touching it. That said, after revisiting older games (even as new as 5th gen..) and coming back to Wilds, I find it lacking in fulfillment. I still play the game and find enjoyment in doing so, but. A big part of Monster Hunter for me is the sense of fulfillment from succeeding in spite of its intentional restriction of instant gratification. In simplifying the game for broader appeal, I can't help but accept that some of that is lost. Not to say that it's worse than before per se, it's just different. Very different, but still. I play this game to scratch an altogether different itch in my brain than every other game in the series. 

Wilds, in all, is very worth your time if you meet it halfway and accept it for what it is, through its strong positives and strong negatives. If you want a starting point to the series, I will still generally point people at Generation 5, solely because of how jarring coming to older games can be from this one. But you can't really go wrong with it. This game is the "english teacher being pregnant" of going on sale, and its expansion is getting news imminently as of the writing of this review. If you're on the fence, consider giving it a shot when it goes on sale, especially if you're a newcomer to the series. Most of the downsides discussed REALLY only apply if you aren't a newcomer. People have very strong opinions and camps for those opinions about this game especially online. This series is a beautiful modern masterpiece, even (and arguably especially) in its imperfect forms. Don't let the noise of our modern cultural landscape rob you from experiencing it.

What makes Monster Hunter a Monster Hunter?

1. Consumable items, when used, should and will always stay and flex for like 240 frames.

2. Every map should and will always be tons of loading screen. 15 sections of 1 region map should take 10 mins only for the loading screen.

3. Walking, sprinting, walking, sprinting for 5 mins to encounter the quest objectives only if you already know where the objectives are. The core gameplay loop is still there, and it's still fun; other things are not as good compared to rise/world.
The game looks both good and bad. Most monsters look great. Maps are fifty fifty (the stone formations on the desert are horrible imo). Then there are weird visual effects like heavy contrast/saturation changes (again on the desert). Many textures look pretty bad, even on high settings (maybe they downgraded them due to performance reasons). Npc design is okay. There are lots of armor options, which is cool for people who care about layered armor like me.
Performance is bad. I admit that with framegen on it runs fine for me, though, and I don't really notice it.
In terms of gameplay, I feel like it's a bit too streamlined. The seikret auto-pathing is very annoying and aggressive, even when you learn how to toggle it on/off. Focus mode is whatever; I like being able to change directions mid-combo, but it's way too forgiving (you can just turn 180º during a true charged slash) and there are no downsides to having it permanently on. The wound system I also don't care much about (some weapons have cool animations or combos when breaking wounds, but you can't even rely on them because they're shared on multiplayer). Some weapons feel way worse to play than in other games (glaive), while others are very satisfying (hunting horn).
I don't like some of the default visual effects (glowing character and a jarring outline when your char is not visible). At least you can toggle them off.
The story is very boring. Story has never been a strong point of the series, but at least they didn't focus much on it, meanwhile this one is a drag.
Multiplayer UI/UX remains stupidly obfuscated, as per usual (lobby, link party, environment link party, etc.). Hard to simply progress the story with friends while seeing each other in the overworld.

I think it’s time to add a more grounded perspective to the discourse around Monster Hunter Wilds. Some of the criticism it’s getting is fair—but a lot of it feels like long-time players complaining about changes that don’t meaningfully harm the experience and, in many cases, actively improve it.

For context, I’m not a diehard fan of the series. I played Monster Hunter: World, skipped Monster Hunter Rise, and came back for Wilds specifically because it addressed some of the friction points I’ve always had with the series.

Focus Mode:

Focus Mode doesn’t “ruin” the game—it’s optional. If you don’t like it, don’t use it. That’s the simplest reality.

For many players, especially newer ones, it improves responsiveness and reduces the feeling that you’re fighting your own character instead of the monster. There’s a recurring mindset in parts of the gaming community that clunkiness equals skill expression. It doesn’t. Sometimes something just feels bad—and smoothing that out doesn’t dilute the experience, it refines it.

If someone finds themselves using Focus Mode despite claiming to hate it, that says more about how it improves gameplay feel than anything else.

Wound System:

If there’s a system that actually impacts gameplay balance, it’s this one.

The Wound System was over tuned at launch and, even after adjustments, still leans toward being too strong. It can trivialize encounters by enabling repeated staggers and high-damage windows with minimal risk. As a result, fights often feel shorter and less dynamic than they should, with monsters going down in just a few minutes.

This is where criticism should be focused; not on optional quality-of-life features, but on mechanics that meaningfully affect combat depth.

Mounts and Travel:

Auto-travel isn’t particularly exciting, and it does feel like a convenience feature rather than a meaningful addition. That said, the argument that it “removes the hunting from Monster Hunter” doesn’t really hold up.

The series has never truly been about hunting in a simulation sense. Monsters are marked almost immediately, and the gameplay loop has always been about engaging them in combat rather than tracking them in a systemic or emergent way. There’s no real risk of losing a trail, no deep ecology systems to learn, and no meaningful punishment for poor tracking.

At its core, Monster 'Hunter' is more-so just a name than it is a suggestion of its gameplay loop and has always been closer to an arena-based action game than a true hunting simulator. Certainly not like something even somewhat controversial in hunting communities, like theHunter: Call of the Wild.

Where its seen as a watered down hunting game, this one doesn't even barely begin to reach the basic foundations to be really considered one. You're chasing after a monster to fight it, you aren't exactly hunting it when there's nothing but surface level flavor text outside of missions to give an illusion of that.

Alongside that, I highly doubt that a large portion of players are sitting here taking in the vistas. Monster Hunters in any form aren't exactly pushing the graphical boundries and while the set pieces of their world can be cool to see, like any game's environment, it's guaranteed to get old for the vast majority of players by even the 5th time going through it, so I don't feel like this is any better of a complaint. 

There are few if any exploration rewards or reasons to spend a load of time in these zones beyond fighting the monster or grinding ingredients to fight the monsters and the zones themselves aren't to the quality or beauty of something like RDR2, Dark Souls titles, Crimson Desert, and more which all focus more on the exploration quality than this series ever did and likely ever will.

These maps are just that: Set pieces to an epic battle between some giant beast and a human. That's why most players got into this series and most players will keep playing it, not because the tiny map you can run around in 15 minutes looks nice.

Grind:

Complaining that the game doesn’t demand excessive grinding is hard to take seriously.

Reducing artificial time sinks isn’t a flaw. If players can build viable gear without repeating the same hunt dozens (or hundreds) of times, that’s respecting their time. Players who enjoy grinding can still do so voluntarily—but designing progression around extreme repetition shouldn’t be the baseline expectation.

A better direction would be adding meaningful challenges—harder variants, unique encounters, or skill-based rewards—rather than padding playtime through low drop rates.

Broader Issues:

Where Wilds, and frankly, World before it actually stumbles is in content and post-launch structure.

Both games launched feeling incomplete, relying heavily on updates and expansions to reach their full potential. There’s also a noticeable reliance on reusing monsters to stretch content. This pattern suggests a broader issue with how Capcom is managing the series, rather than any single gameplay mechanic.

Final Thoughts:

A lot of the backlash toward Wilds feels less like thoughtful critique and more like resistance to change—especially from players whose experience and identity are tied to mastering older, clunkier systems.

But improvements to responsiveness, accessibility, and pacing don’t invalidate the series—they modernize it. And if those changes are optional, then they’re not even taking anything away.

Criticism is important, but it should be aimed at systems that actually affect the game’s depth and longevity—not features that simply make it feel better to play.

Great game. Finally, an enjoyable Monster Hunter game that made you want to do the optional hunts. I finished the game at HR50 (the second Zoh Shia), considering that the end (I don't plan on grinding to HR100), but I'll definitely return to the game when the DLC and Master Rank arrive.

Changes from previous games:
- The fights are finally spectacular, with perfectly matched music, fight locations, and monster selection. My favorite fights were the two with Rey Dau. I replayed each of them, so I fought him four times, and I think this fight is outstanding, monster, his moveset, location, music, in my opinion the best of the World/Rise/Wilds trio. And there are more fights like this that are simply enjoyable.

- Many small changes that improved the game, including:
1. Adding a player silhouette that can be seen under the monster
2. Automatically driving to the target, so you can do other things while driving
3. The character lying down after a knockdown now has iFrames, so the opponent can't combo with your corpse, which is a great change
4. Doing everything seems faster - drinking potions, eating things, using items - which makes the game run smoother


- Difficulty level - If I had to rate it, I think Wilds and Rise are similar in difficulty, and World is the hardest, so (IN MY OPINION) the difficulty level has been maintained; I didn't use help from anyone else once while playing Wilds. I did all the fights solo, just like in Rise. Almost the entire World expansion was so difficult that I was constantly begging for help.

- FINALLY you can pause a game, if you're in "Online Single Player" (which is offline mode that I used all the time), you can pause a game all the time, even during hunts, this is a huge change as you can just pause hunt and do something else, it was impossible in World/Rise, I used it a lot of times in Wilds

- Main character finally talks, I realized how important it is only when he started, we had mute protagonist in World and Rise and it is a good change that main chracter finally speaks, it adds a lot to the game

- The characters and storyline – it's still not top-notch (and no one expects it), but I liked them and it was fun to work with them.

My only real downside to the entire game – the two fights with JIn Dahaad – I'd be happier if that fight wasn't in the game at all. Not only is JIn Dahaad an HP sponge, but he also has a one-shot, one-kill attack. At a certain point, he gathers energy and attacks the entire location. To defend against it, you have to throw down a layer of ice from above and hide behind it. When I tried to prepare this beforehand, he destroyed it, and when I threw down the ice as he was starting his attack, it was a 50/50 decision whether I could do it all. Additionally, this entire sequence is bugged. A few times I was standing under the ice and couldn't get it off, so I got a free faint that I didn't deserve. I generally repeated this fight many times. Generally, I haven't had any failed quests in this game, and this one at least 5. When I finally defeated him, he was 0.5 seconds away from nuking me, I was on my last HP. He did this attack with about 500 HP. I almost lost this fight when he was at the end of his life. I probably would have uninstalled this game.

This game is fun when you treat it like a standalone entry, but it's a whole different game from its predecessors.

As a spectacle fighter, it's fun. You have a lot of combos. The movement is clunkier than most spectacle fighters, but far from aggravating either. It nails the spectacle of the environment and the monster designs as the series always have. The performance is demanding, but you can get a stable 45 fps with mid-low end rig like mine at medium graphic settings. Stable 60 fps if you crank everything down. 

However, as I said, it's a spectacle fighter, not a Monster Hunter game. If you ever heard about the controversial change between Bioshock 1/2 and Bioshock Infinite, this is more or less the same case. Capcom removed any semblance of mandatory player agency this time around. The preparation and tracking side of the game are entirely omitted. 

You can resupply anywhere with fast travel, removing the need of limited inventory system (yet it is still there for some reason). Items are available in abundance, and consumables are pre-crafted for you. Everything is highly automated, so much so it surprises me that the combat remains manual. Even disengaging from monsters is now assisted extensively by the addition of all-purpose bail out vehicle.

There is no tracking anymore aside from some interactive cutscene. The contrast is like The Hunter hunting sim series and Duck Hunt arcade. The old tracking method of using paintballs gives you extra incentive to memorize monster habitats and their routine. Whether or not you enjoy this extra layer of gameplay depends on your stance towards immersive tediums. If you came from combat focused genre, MH will understandably feel like a clunkier version of your favorite game, with or without the tracking system.

However, Monster Hunter doesn't have to be another spectacle fighter or souls-like (as much as i love dark souls). Monster Hunter is always its own thing with its proudly distinct "prep-track-hunt" gameplay loop. I hope Capcom understand this.

Absolutely incredible game, instantly propelled the Monster Hunter series to one of my favorites ever and also got me to buy World and Rise after finishing it.

Combat is fast paced and centered around the counter mechanics that pretty much every weapon has now. Focus mode serves this combat style very well, since it allows for instant reorientation of your character mid-combo. Compared to World's much slower pace (at least before Iceborne) and much higher focus on positioning, both games have a distinct gameplay identity that ensures one game is still worth experiencing even if you've played the other.

Early game monster quality and variety is superb, but the fights themselves are quite easy. I don't mind this since it eases players in and there's a lot of challenging fights later, mostly in the endgame. Farming for gear (not including the endgame Gogma Artian weapons) also feels much faster than World.

My main complaint is the Gogma Artian weapons and the insane amount of RNG you need to get a good one. It's not a massive problem for me, since we're bound to get a new (and hopefully better) system in the future DLC and personally, I just ignore the set bonuses and craft the weapons just for the increased stats while still not feeling underpowered even in the highest level quests.

In terms of performance, before the last 3 major optimization updates (which started in December of last year), I had to run the game on the lowest graphic settings with frame generation and DLSS on Ultra Performance and would get about 40 FPS on average. Now, after the big optimizations, I've increased the quality settings on important things like mesh and fur quality to high (I also increased most other graphic settings by 1 tier at least), turned off frame generation and set DLSS to Performance. I now get around 70-80 FPS with no stutters and no crashes, and I'm perfectly satisfied with that.

But after final update (TU4) the game is now optimized and OK to play for most players (Though you still require a high-performance PC to fully appreciate the game). I gave it 5/10 (minus 5 for I must go through all those optimization shenanigans). 

Story: Quite enjoyable, even though I did not expect anything from Monster Hunter in storytelling. Wilds is quite good, not as good as story driven games but good nonetheless. I gave it 7/10. 

Gameplay mechanics: The main selling point of the series, and I have to say it is the best of the best. Every mechanic, every detail, from weapon to weapon is meticulously built to perfection. I can’t say for other weapons as I main only bow and long sword, these weapons are very satisfying to play. Bow perfect dodge big attack is exhilarating (timing your dodge carefully or kart). Long sword Iai spirit slash feels so good when it connects. I still miss Wirebug from Rise though. 10/10 for me.

It has really interesting lore that the plot railroads and rushes you through.

It has a rich ecosystem that you are never required to engage with.

Once the game opens up in High Rank, the lore gets to shine more.
Once the later-game monsters become more resistant to wounds, the rest of the combat system fits into place.
Once you fiddle with the settings and disable Seikret auto-movement, the world is actually a nice place to wander and gather in.

The game is constantly in tension with what it wants to be, but most of the friction that comes from attempting to appeal to newer players is temporary or opt-out, and it rewards you sticking with it. I firmly believe late-game Wilds is better than World, and the Wilds expansion has the potential to be the best Monster Hunter has ever been.

[h3]It's... different.[/h3]
I genuinely do not know how they managed to make MONSTER HUNTER of all things an [b]uninteresting[/b] game. Simplified and trivialised to the point it's not even challenging for the most part. It's like the game has lost it's soul. I have over 500h in MHW:I meanwhile here I'm just staring at screen contemplating whether to launch the game or not. It's by no means a bad game, just not as appealing as it's predecessor, what a shame.

[h3]Performance[/h3]
I barely see any difference in graphics compared to MH World. No upscaling, no framegen and I get stable 110 fps on medium/high compared to [i]smooth[/i] 165 fps in previous game. Performance wise game is like 6/10, not tragic yet could be better when it doesn't really look all that different.

[h3]The good[/h3]
[list]
[*]Game is improving over time (slowly)
[*]Cutscenes are skippable.
[*]Some cutscenes are nice enough to not skip.
[*]Sharpening now recovers sharpness with every pass instead of animation locking.
[*]Solo hunts are faster.
[/list]

[h3]The bad[/h3]
[list]
[*]Calling the chicken (Seikret) is your panic button for each and every inconvenience. Why?
[*]I don't like the story. It's generic and boring (to me). Period.
[*]Focus mode is supposed to be an [u]addition[/u]. Yeah, it's active for just about 90% of the fight.
[/list]

[h3]Glaive rant🔥[/h3]
[olist]
[*]Glaive got changed into very one-directional playstyle. Currently it has an identity crisis as [b]Airborne[/b] moveset got essentially nuked. It sits as something Longsword-like but bootleg. You either trigger RSS one after another or get stuck with awkward suboptimal combos. The kinsect powders and RSS are [u]mutually exclusive[/u] mechanics, why? The very foundations and identity of the weapon got messed up making most of what you've learned in previous games to be thrown out the window.
[*]Kinsects got completely cast on the side. While they were noticeable in World with powders or [b]kinsect drill[/b], here they just exist as extract harvesters.
[*]Focus mode [u]at all times[/u] or no damage + trash moveset. BAD DESIGN.
[/olist]

[h3]Summary[/h3]
Currently I'm waiting for interesting changes. Maybe the DLC will introduce something nice, we will see. All I know is that I'm not the target audience (despite sinking hundreds of hours into MHW:I) but oh well, what can you do; the experts at capcom clearly know better [i]right?[/i]

Best described by the phrase “A good game but…”
If only Steam let me mark a review as mixed.

In some areas, Wilds excels at what many love this series for; the incredible creature design and their fantastical yet grounded ecology/biology, the goofy and playful charm present in various animations/cutscenes etc., the excellent music and sound design and more.

In other areas however, it feels at odds with both previous MonHun games and even itself. The aggressive streamlining/handholding and even outright removal of certain mechanics prevents a lot of experiences you could have in the series’ past and clashes with the theming and presentation of the game; the titular “Wilds”. Most notably the lack of wonder and discovery due to many UI elements and railroaded progression amongst other things as well as the simplification of combat with systems like focus mode and the difficulty balancing overall being extremely easy for most of the game (both in terms of the players' capabilities and the monsters').

While the difficulty complaints (amongst other things) have been for the most part addressed through title updates, the deeper issues with mechanics like focus mode will likely remain unchanged throughout the game’s lifespan.

I’ve gotten a lot of hours out of this game, the majority of which I’ve enjoyed, which is why the review is still ultimately marked as positive. It has a lot of great stuff in it that’s just mired in some of the questionable design choices that feel like regressions to the general formula Capcom has worked on since 2004. (Coming from someone who is kind of an RE6 apologist) I hope Wilds ends up being the Resident Evil 6 of MonHun and kickstarts a reset of the series. While that is unlikely to happen with the next portable game, I will be placing my hopium onto the mainline game of 7th gen.
