The real review must wait until the inevitable G-rank. But here are current thoughts:

WAY too much talking and attempt to lore dump. I don't mind the optional talking to NPCs, but the story is a slog when you have to walk so much and look at things. If the developers really wanted players to look at the world, then bring back actually tracking monsters and make the mounts slower. There is no reason to look at the world when every monster (including supposed never seen before by any human in hundreds of years) is tagged and geolocated at all times and your mount zooms straight to target.

The game is on the easier sides of MonHuns. Not quite Generate Ultimate (or XX in Japan) with the instant dodge ability, but it does have counters that are relatively easy to time and allow to completely block an attack and counter. That the game still gives 60 minutes for most hunts is funny when you have to be not playing the game to get past 10 minutes and most hunters will be sub 5 minutes in hunts.

Performance has been mixed, but has gotten better. I started in Windows 10 (same hardware listed in the attached PC Specs) and the game never ran horribly like many reported. When I switched to Linux, the game had the stutters. Recent updates fixed those and the game runs like it did when I was using Windows 10.

--EDIT--

Got some more help with settings and optimization, and now the game runs pretty well but will still crash occasionally. Still do not recommend a purchase, especially if you are using AMD 6000 series cards as apparently they have the worst time running this game.

Every quest:
Hunter: Can I kill this monster?

Alma: The guild authorizes you to KILL that monster for ecosystem stability and research purposes!

It's Monster Hunter, and even my crap computer can run it now. It does have issues, but if I didn't have a ♥♥♥♥ HP 2060 prebuilt with exactly one stock fan it'd be different. And even then, I run consistently at 60 FPS, albeit with lower graphical settings to not trip my pitiful VRAM limit.

That aside, it's a good time to play now because there's tons of content and I'm only so inundated with playtime. I still haven't gotten to my favorite monster, Lagi, but lord am I close. And it's fun, I play Gunlance/Hunting Horn and both feel super tight, with a very good flow. I'm not too GOOD at HH, but thiat's beside the point (and if anything, another good point to the game if I can have fun while bad).

My one gripe is the monsters are a little frail because barring obnoxious delays by outside forces, like 3/4ths of my hunts end in 10 minutes, and the rest in 15 (but this can also be a plus, and I haven't gotten close to super endgame yet). 

I guess the Seikret does automate things a little much, but these are hardly reasons to skip a game that you can ABSOLUTELY sink your teeth into. Plus, as is customary with MH an expansion will be coming, so while things are calm for now, there'll be more in the not-so-far future.

Point being, play this game if you like MH or have seen it and like what you see; it's going to last a good while regardless of whatever gripes there are. And there's some fun monsters too!

Edit: I'm at about 150 hours now so I thought I'd say more: Lagi's an incredible monster in this game. I got to trying dual blades and they're obscenely good, especially if you like caffeine-addled gameplay. my main issue is my dumbass computer can't cool itself right rather than anything about the game running. Disclaimer: I only tried Wilds AFTER all of the title updates were done hence my review will be based on the current version of the game as of May 2026.

TLDR: I understand everyone has their gripes with the game from the perspective of diehard MH fans but it is just so wrong to call this game bad. There is yet to be an actual bad MH game, maybe a bad entry but overall all games are insanely good and are worth the time. What you used to love about the combat may be lost but the new direction has a good idea just a slight execution problem that will maybe be fixed in the upcoming DLC. 

If you are a veteran of the franchise (played before MH World) then this game will feel like a massive step in a different direction with the increase of reactionary combat instead of position-based and slow combat. Benefit of focus mode is that the MH dev team can give us insanely mobile monsters that abandon the traditional moveset standards of MH games such as Arkveld or Rey Dau without the problem of players going "tis monster making me unable to play too fast, oh my gog!!!". Bad side of focus mode is the abandoment of positioning for more reactionary based combat with the parry and offset mechanic. This feels extremely satisfying to pull off but at some point i questioned is it even okay to just parry everything with the SnS shield like its nothing. Me personally, love the new combat since I play weapons that have completely different playstyles from each other, but if you only play shielded weapons you'll see how parry intensive the game gets. Also as for Omega and Gog, my gog, the spectacle of those fights. Awesome job by the devs. 

As for the ecology... oh man do people love dumping on it. Let's say right away, ECOLOGY IS NEEDED. It was always a part of the series. Did they do it well this time around? Ughhh... maybe? Scarlet Forest by all means feels like the best executed environment in the game, it does feel alive in each season. Plains are strange, some parts of them feels extremely lush, while others feel like very dull and boring (the rock canyon where Chatacabra is), Rey Dau's nest looks amazing during the Inclemency. Which brings me to the main ecology point... Seasons!!! They are an awesome addition that is sadly butchered by a different mechanic, resting. If the season system makes a return i hope we have to endure them and that they have higher effects on the player besides "pop a cold drink". Rest system on the other hand needs to go ASAP. Or just nerf it by making it only change the day night cycle and not the season. Abusing it to get the perfect investigations on the map is just stupid. 

Next part, the story.... it's okay? Nata has some of the most inconsistent writing yeah but overall low rank story as a whole is pretty decent. Guild trying to tie in with the local villages is a cool thing. Artificial lifeform made by a civilization eradicated from a bygone era is cool too. People forget how fantasy this IP can get (look at Castle Schrade that's 1000 years old).  Where I think the story just kind of vanishes is the frenzy. What was the point of it besides that one bit of it infecting the forbidden lands just for it to suddenly gain immunity with some vaccine or whatever. That part is actually borderline funny how fast it gets solved. I hope the DLC fixes and brings frenzy to a greater importance than it was here given Nadia's quest. 

Random thoughts section GO: Endgame is serviceable for a base game, I hate Artian weapons but they make a decent grind for people till DLC, DO NOT BRING THEM BACK PLEASE. Event quests were okay too. DLC cosmetics this time around were way too predatory. Some in dlc packs, some not, some are just event cosmetics, its just stupid but I get the higher ups pushed this.

Lot's of good stuff here to digest as a MH fan, what I think the community did is hyperfocus on the things done poorly or mediocre and started dumping on it for the longest time even to this day. It is not the best game objectively because all of them are fantastic in their own way. If you crave the latest entry this is a good game. Be open about the shortcomings of the game and you will enjoy it way more.

Monster Hunter Wilds is honestly a great game at its core. The combat feels good, the monsters are incredible, the environments look amazing, and when the multiplayer actually works the way you expect, it’s a blast.

But for a game that constantly pushes multiplayer and co-op, the actual system feels weirdly restrictive and overcomplicated.

You can be in the same Link Party, on the same quest, and still not even see each other because the game keeps separating players into single-player instances unless you jump through extra hoops. Then when you finally do get together for a hunt, you often have to reinvite people manually, and once the hunt ends, the whole thing basically resets and sends everyone back into menus again.

If you want to casually roam and farm monsters together, you have to use Environment Link mode, but then story progression gets disabled. So the game creates this constant split between “play the story” and “actually play together.”

It honestly feels less like a true co-op Monster Hunter experience and more like a single-player game where you occasionally summon friends for boss fights before everyone gets kicked back into menu management again.

The frustrating part is that none of these restrictions feel necessary. The game already has the systems to let people seamlessly stay together — it just keeps putting barriers in the way. A lot of the multiplayer flow feels designed around menus, sessions, and re-invites instead of simply letting friends stay grouped and hunt continuously.

I love the actual game itself, which is why this stands out so much. The core gameplay is fantastic. But the multiplayer structure feels unnecessarily gated for a series that’s supposed to shine when hunting together.

As someone who hadn't played a Monster Hunter game prior to World, I didn't come out of World with the best experience. There was a lot of foreign mechanics and features that eluded me. But eventually I got the grasp on *some* of them.
Going into Wilds I had a much clearer idea of what to expect and how things worked. That helped me to find this entry more enjoyable. However there are still some things that are better or worse in certain ways between the two titles. 

I like that there appears to be a lot more visual variety in the monsters we hunt in this game, for all it has, World I feel suffers from a lot of repetition, fighting another monster that looks like a dragon or giant lizard of some sort. Sure the monsters have different moves but there is only so much that could be done for the nth lizard monster to differentiate it from the last few. Same thing I feel happens in a game like Elden Ring, lots of bosses sure, but much of it feels a bit shallow. Even if it can be enjoyable it must be noted how it felt stale after a while. Wilds has simply presented me with more variety: spiders, dragons, worm like creatures, cephalopods, amphibians, birds, mammals, etc.

The world feels more connected between zones. Going back to a souls reference, World kind of felt like Dark Souls II while Wilds felt more like Dark Souls. Though, there are some things about how the game tells its story and how that connects with the world that I found a bit jarring or strange for the game. Traversal of the world feels about the same as World, the addition of the Seikret was I think nice, besides the fact that I love them and they're adorable, I don't think it changes much for traversal. The ability to hook items in from a distance and while on your Seikret is much appreciated. Having the Seikret auto follow can be a bit cumbersome at times, when I just want to hop on and gather stuff around me while he tries to dart to the monster immediately, its a minor annoyance.

Regarding the story, I genuinely don't know what the story of World was supposed to be. It all felt so irrelevant and that is something that I felt made me less interested in the game because I like investing myself in a story and World just didn't really provide much. So we travel to the new world to do something? then all of sudden are interrupted by Zorah Magdaros and then were just doing stuff. It felt very reactionary and like there wasn't much of a centralized reason or way in which everything connected back to itself besides "Monster is ruining ecology, kill it" Now with Wilds I was more invested in the story. Trying to find Nata's tribe and along the way discovering cultures and the monsters, introducing characters and how the world is changing. Now something that was a bit jarring about how Wilds handled its story is that it all felt very much like a gallery, or I guess like going from one thing to the next, back to back to back and a bit predictable, both in narrative and gameplay. So like cutscene, hunt, cutscene, return to base, cutscene, hunt, and so on. It felt like there wasn't much room to breathe. Now In the later game where cutscenes are less frequent it felt a little better and its still delivering its narrative rather effectively. Anyways, that was the bit I mentioned earlier about it being jarring. I think I understand how a Monster Hunter fan would be more interested in a hunt and the challenge as opposed to more story being at the forefront, so I suppose finding a better middle ground would be ideal because its true that I found this game more enjoyable overall I still think the transition between gameplay and story throughout could use some tuning.

Speaking of challenge, I can agree that the monsters in this game appear to go down easier than they did in World, however. Thinking back on my experience with both I think some monsters could just use a little tuning being slightly more health or dealing more damage to keep the player moving. I don't think it need much more than that. In World, monsters felt like they were taking a long time to deal with and most importantly the game just felt clunkier than Wilds does. I think that's the gist of it, Wilds takes out some of the jank that makes monsters *feel* more frustrating than they actually could have been. Like swinging my weapon towards where my character is facing instead of my camera or getting stuck in terrain, or hitting the environment instead of the monster, or having the monster just be so big it makes my camera bug out while it tries to track it or as it charges at me and traps me between (or under) it and the environment. Some things are still there in Wilds but to a lesser extent, though it also introduces other things that can be annoying, like being stuck in a long attack animation while the monster has already moved or repositioned. 

Some things I miss from World though, the way investigations used to be, just made it clearer for me to understand and to have them on hand. Same goes for the bounties and with bounties I could pick the ones I wanted to focus on rather than being forced to do the ones in rotation or wait until they expire. The food system seems interesting in Wilds, more so than in World but I miss having that be part of the preparation before heading into a quest than doing it while on the quest. I do like that it lasts longer than a single quest though and that the benefits don't appear to be taken away after fainting. Speaking to the whole meal and preparation before a quest. Wilds does feel like it tries to incorporate a sudo open world or more seamless experience by having a cutscene between quests and it transitions into them and to a hunt, and being able to cook while in the world rather than have to go back to base and just having base be a part of the world rather than having to travel to it making it like a never ending expedition if that's what you're looking for. But yeah, some things suffer from the change. 

Performance is okay, even now after the patches its still just fine. I get between 50-70 fps with DLSS off, which isn't great but it holds steady most of the time and with DLSS on I get about 120. (Oh, on medium to high settings I think I have it at.) 

I feel like I had more to say but honestly I forgot and or I can't think of it right now. Overall though, I think its a good game. I'm still making my way through it and am trying to take part in as much as I can.

The "not recommended" comes not from any issues with the game, combat design, or performance. I quite love offset attacks and wounds, I love the additions to the movesets of many weapons, not particularly bothered by the self-driving mounts, don't really have performance issues either. 
My specs (as seen below) surprisingly get me a fairly stable 60 fps (with like 50 fps lows at worst) most of the time at 1440p ultrawide on mostly max settings (no raytracing, FSR upscaling, no framegen, but lowered volumetrics and foliage).   
The game looks pretty enough (though not quite deserving of the hardware requirements), and the fashion is probably the best I've experienced in the games I've played (world and sunbreak).   

My issue comes entirely from the GPU crashing that many people also experience. 
I've tried every single solution proposed (except for fully reinstalling windows, I'm not doing that for certain reasons), and nothing has been able to remedy the issue. At best I've obtained slightly more stability, but it's hard to estimate, as the game will sometimes crash every 30-50 minutes, or I might go an entire day with no issues.   

Going through a harder hunt, only for the game to crash just as the monster is about to die, or worse - on the reward screen right before the game saves, is really off-putting. And when you need to grind a fair amount, especially if you want to play a variety of weapons, the frustration adds up really quickly.   

I've at least gotten my money's worth, but I cannot recommend the game when there's a decent chance of having an unsolvable crashing issue.

to start of with the good from my point of view so far:
- fun combat, i stuck with dual blades up until the endgame, but ive started playing around with multiple weapons and none of it is "bad" in my opinion, love the combos, interesting mechanics, perfect guards, offsets, wound system, its all fun.
- music is GREAT, it took me a while to finally sit down and enjoy it, but thats only because i loved it the entire way through. 
- ecology and interactions are crazy cool, love seeing monsters interact with their respective locals.
- all the monsters are good, some are definately a step above the others, and vice versa
   - yian-kut-ku is annoying, but not a terrible monster
   - the guardians for the most part are just worse than their counterparts. especially G. doshaguma, it doesnt have its signature pack or fling smaller monsters. G. rathalos is the only one thats not strictly "worse" than base rathalos, its just somewhat different in that it spends more time on the ground which is nice. 
- i havent noticed any bugs so far, or crashes, from what i can tell AMD GPUs are a match made in hell for this, but im hoping capcom does fix that before the expansion does drop since im planning on forcing my friends into playing it with me. 

BAD:
- graphics could be a bit more optimized, using base settings i was getting around 30 fps and it didnt look too great, but after a bit of messing around with settings and prioritizing what i need/dont need, want/dont care about, i managed to make the game look pretty good while running around 60-90 fps. 
- online multiplayer can be a bit annoying at times, ive had instances where im hunting a harder monster, send SOS flare and the people who join either are just terrible at the game, or legitimately throwing. 
- joining SOS missions can be a bit annoying since there are sessions that are set to manual accepting, i think the system is great, but it could be a bit more visually indicated that the session is set to manual accept instead of it just being a line in the sessions settings. it has annoyed me multiple times joining a hunt with rewards i want and then seeing its manual accept, waiting 1-2 minutes to be accepted and never being accepted. 
- artian weapons are kinda boring in my opinion, from the people ive interacted with and looking at all guides online it seems to be the best weapon endgame, and with it being RNG based with bonuses i dont love it. i would love to see some cases where the normal way of crafting weapons ends up being the best, i know that in like TU2 the levin acrus was the BEST choice for thunder dual blades, which was great, but then gogmazios artians dropped and instantly became better. one type of weapon just being clearly superior is boring imo.
- making a new character save to experience a new weapon from the start is annoying as all hell. you are forced to play through the tutorial, sit through dialogue and cutscenes, its all just down time that i dont appreciate on my second playthrough. on my first playthrough this wasnt an issue since it was interesting for me and i do genuienly care about story. but for a second or third playthrough, hell no.

overall i love the game, i dont see myself quitting for a good while, i still have lots of endgame content to grind through, all the AT apexes, proof of a hero. MASSIVE amounts of replayability with learning all the weapon styles.

It has something special behind it with the amount of attempts to defeat or redo a boss because it's difficult and the satisfaction you earn once you defeated it.

Also the graphics is insanely good!

Alongside all of this is an egregious amount of microtransactions (although at this point there is nothing 'micro' about them) that will inevitably be followed by a G-rank expansion and its own tranche of microtransactions. 

The greatest sin that this game commits though; is that there is no Meowscular chef. 

All in all, I cannot recommend this game.

Wilds is part of the reason I got myself a beefier PC. On max settings with 2k, the game still looks muddy, grainy and flat. 

Allow me to be unc for a minute. MH's vibrancy is a part of the series identity. These games used to be so colorful, so JUICY, albeit graphically unimpressive in the technical sense. The environments used to be inspiring and whimsical, the kind of thing you want to stop and take in. Here, they feel like duller, lesser imitations of what we saw in previous entries. Generic. It's odd to feel that way about a series that is otherwise overflowing with personality through its design.

Essentially:
Art direction > fidelity
Performance > fidelity

They bet on 'fidelity' and somehow made a game that not only runs like utter **** on the vast majority of systems, but is uglier than ever and therefore not even worth the performance trade-off. 

My first 20 hours of Wilds was experienced on my dated system, and it was perhaps the most torturous gaming experience I have ever had. I can finally give the game a real go with my new beast, but I will always be bitter about the lag fest Capcom made me go through just to get a taste of their latest hotdog water. We deserve better.

I feel less inclined to touch on gameplay, because my gripes are mainly with what I mentioned above. But, judging from the relatively small amount of time I committed to the game so far, I can confidently say I felt understimulated. I rarely ever got carted, and the monster was dead hilariously fast.

I’m not sure there’s much here for vets with hundreds of hours in the other games other than a time sink and a participation badge. MHGU is forever superior. And for an experience that is smooth and pretty, there’s MH World.

Genuinely a fantastic game and as always Capcom delivered. If you like story rich games with deep lore then this is a game for you but if you like action packed fighting and hunting down monsters then this is also a game for.

It used to run so horribly on open beta and at launch but now runs perfectly with my setup using FSR4 Native AA, although you still need a few mods to make the game not look so blurry

as for gameplay, the weapons feel incredible to use and the pacing of the fights are tense and extremely fun, although compared to monster hunter world, where your attacks had to be more methodical and committed, cancel timings for blocking and rolls in wilds are far more forgiving, and the seikret mount makes things like healing and using your whetstone way less punishing since youre not so vulnerable, you dont even have to search for the monsters in this game, they are always on the map, which could be upsetting but honestly in world it can become kinda tedious just running around the map not even being able to find tracks for whatever you are hunting, its alot of small changes to streamline the experience and get you to fights alot sooner, making this a very accessible monster hunter game to get into, but could make older games harder to get into because of that.

TLDR: game runs pretty good now but still needs mods to make it look clear, gameplay has been streamlines even further than previous titles but still ends up being extremely fun to play, with an unfortunately short campaign but still plenty of side missions and event quests to play

I dont recommend this game at full price but at a discount absolutely, though Monster hunter world or rise with their DLCs could be bought for as low as $10, are complete games with more content, and still have players on PC so it might be worth waiting for the expansion for this game to come out or at least wait for a release date to get into it before the expansion launches

The highest graphics setting (Frame gen disabled) can manage a stable 144FPS on overkill hardware. It comes with some technical issues for half of the people, for example, game crash on startup unless ran in compatibility mode for windows 8, so you most likely will have to make some tweaks in order to run the game, which is mildly infuriating because a consumer shouldn't have to fix a dev's game for them. If you haven't sacrificed a kidney for an overpriced computer, then treat this review as a 5/10 rating for the game.

Graphics themselves are good but inconsistent. Some textures look really good while others look 480p.
Pixel clarity is abhorrent, which is a given for today's industry as nobody puts any care or effort into creative solutions and just slaps on the same sloppy, blurry, and eye-melting grainy filter to cut down on costs. Monster Hunter World has better pixel clarity than this game.

Combat is good, once you get used to the rhythm of attacks and evading the game comes somewhat close to feeling like DMC5, which is honestly a great achievement. Some hitboxes feel iffy, and the combat starts to feel really terrible when your ping starts going above 100+ so if you are playing with overseas friends, keep that in mind.

The controls are optimized for controller, so naturally the keybinds are abhorrent for Mouse and Keyboard. Start changing keybinds immediately. The game also constantly restricts your controls during story quests in a manner that feels like someone is grabbing you by the jaw and repeatedly going "Hey look at this cool fkn plant bro look bro its got a stem and holy sh*t it has some petals too" like yeah, you pointed that out 3 times 40 seconds ago, what do you want me to do about it?

The UI is overwhelming at first but you get used to it pretty quick and it starts having its unique benefits. Honestly though, this is just a matter of personal preference, it will probably be a hit or miss for most people.

The art direction and monster designs are some of the best and pairs nicely with the rest of the game's world, which is full of life, and every creature, down to the smallest critter, feel like they serve a purpose in the ecosystem. You will, often probably, break away from the main quest and go your own way because exploring the world is just that fun.

Worth $45 out of $70

Played every game since MH3, with World being my favorite. I was ecstatic about Wilds being essentially World 2.0, but it has been largely a disappointment. Unfortunately, there's too many glaring issues to recommend the game.

My gripes have nothing to do with performance. I had around 60 fps on launch, and after toying around/light modding to get DLSS and frame gen to work with my GPU, I had a consistent 80-120 at all times, which I was fine with.

My disappointments were mainly with how much Capcom has dumbed down the game. They started adding QoL in World and Rise, and while some vets complained, most welcomed them. They saw the success those games brought and pumped that QoL up to 1000 for this game. Focus mode completely erases the methodical nature of MH combat, gathering or even grinding for monster parts (outside of ultra endgame gear) is a thing of the past, getting hit goes unpunished if you call your loyal steed to swoop in and save you, and my personal least favorite - the beautiful, sprawling maps the series has come to be known by have turned into glorified cutscenes.

I wasn't a fan of the mounts in Rise for a similar reason, but at least you were [i] looking at the screen [/i] while riding the palamute. Now when the monster goes to a new area, you [b] literally [/b] press tab and pick up your phone, alt tab, do WHATEVER YOU WANT, because the "hunting" part of monster hunter is a thing of the past. Why do monsters even change locations anymore? Or why not just fight everything in an arena and load in instantly? Some of these maps are gorgeous but I couldn't tell you a single thing about most of them.

Besides that, the endgame Artian weapon system (that they double downed on with TU4) is so mind-numbingly boring to interact with that I haven't bothered. I lost all interest when I saw multiple endgame build guides recommending savescumming to get the desired pieces. The game has a lot of great stuff- in particular I loved the ambitious approach to the MSQ. While it could've been much better, I've never been a huge fan of the "do this list of instanced quests" approach MH has done until now, and appreciated them trying something new. Furthermore, it has a great roster of beautiful monsters to fight, and the maps are on par with World for the most part. But too many poor decisions took away from the magic of MH and make it a chore to play.

This game is literally a downgrade from World. The only redeeming qualities are the fun combat and the new monsters (Xu Wu, Nu Udra, Rey Dau, Arkie), but that's not enough for a game that costs 70 €.

Amongst the bad:
- Had to wait until the LAST TITLE UPDATE for the game to be playable in PC. Even in high end machines. This is just plain incompetence.
- The "seamless game experience" they talked so much about in the trailers, its nonexistent. Same as any other MH game, the only thing that changes is that you can jump straight to a quest from an expedition, but you still have to travel between biomes via world map, and you have a loading screen on all the normal quests.
- Pack monsters? Turd bomb. Nonexistent pack mechanics. Also featured in most of the marketing.
- Difficulty? Cakewalk. With all the new mechanics, like wounds, perfect guarding and offset attacks, they really should've pumped up the difficulty. There is an exception, but the fight is meant to be played in a very specific way, so I'm not counting it.
- RNG decorations or RNG talismans? Don't worry, you dont have to pick, no both are RNG! Artificial way to inflate the gametime. Also ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Haven't read about it much, but I'm sure the talismans have tables decided on a new save.
- Artians and the upgrade are a mistake, and they invalidate making and picking weapons from monster parts. They should've never ever existed. I really hate how they try to make this game a gacha with some disgusting predatory mechanic.
- The story is bland and nonsensical. Unrelatable characters, a plot that doesnt hold itself and doesn't take itself seriously enough. Unironically, the franchise had better story when it wasn't focused on the campaign. Some cool scenes, but thats all.
- I really, really love the Seikret, but as it is, it makes monster running away pointless, and you barely get to see the map unless you explore it by yourself. World did it better, where you had to unlock the Raider Ride to be able to auto track the monster. Maybe the auto follow should be a sidequest to make your Seikret learn how to navigate each locale, instead of having it unlocked at the start.
- And many more I'm probably forgetting.

The difficulty on this game lies in trying to ignore all the bad qualities it has. As it stands now, even after its last update, it doesn't cost its full price. Get it at -50% at least if you really really wanna play it, otherwise skip it. I have no hopes for the DLC whatsoever.

Just know that if we keep enabling Capcom by buying their games when they're clearly not worth their price, in a couple of generations we're gonna be paying 120 € for a game with 10 monsters that requires a quantum computer to run.

Now that all title updates are out, I feel that I can properly express my feelings about this game. It started out pretty rough, but REALLY picked up through the title updates. I started with Generations Ultimate and played every game since its release.

Pros:
- Incredible feeling combat. Intense + fun. Aiming attacks was a great addition.
- Very addicting gameplay loop.
- Almost perfect monster roster. There aren't really any "annoying" monsters this time around.
- Beautiful armor and weapon sets. Fashion hunting is fantastic in this game.
- Great soundtrack that feels intense and immersive.
- Gathering feels so much better. It's quick and you can grab from a distance. 
- Decent story for a monster hunter game. Story is actually presented like an RPG and feels much more cinematic compared to previous entries. Lore has very interesting implications for the future of the franchise.
- Title update monsters were all amazing. Also, increased challenge in updates were great!
- Now runs great on my system after recent updates!
- Visually stunning. Locales and monsters are excellently designed.
- Offsets and counters are very satisfying to land.
- Reduced grind compared to previous games.
- Amazing character creator. Lots of settings to truly create whoever you want.
- Lots of content. You can easily sink lots of hours into the game.

Cons:
- Artian weapons are a horrible idea. They completely negate monster weapons.
- Visually, the game always looks a bit blurry, even with DLSS and frame-gen off?
- Bowguns feel odd in this game. They feel very clunky in a game were every other weapon was updated to feel smoother. Seregios guns are the only exception in my opinion.
- Insanely easy difficulty until the later title updates. I understand that i'm a long time fan, but killing a new monster before you've even seen all thier attacks for the first time is kinda crazy.
- Insect glaive has extremely complicated controls this time around. I usually main this weapon in every game, but this was the first time I was slightly turned off from keeping it as my favorite (I still enjoy it though).
- The Seikret completely destroys the feeling of exploring and immersing yourself in the maps. It automatically brings you to every monster. You never really get to take in the layouts of the maps. With the almost 300 hours ive played, without the Seikret, I dont think I could navigate some of the later maps naturally.

Despite the flaws, this is one of my favorite entries in the franchise and I highly recommend playing it. I truly believe that with time, these flaws will be improved upon and it can grow into the BEST Monster Hunter game.

One of my favorite games, period. Character creation is really robust, the story is relatively simple but well-written and solid (I actually CARE about these characters and care about solving the mystery), and of course the stars of the show, the monsters, are as awesome as ever. A lot of my grievances from World were answered by this game (the option to choose non-sexualizing armor for fem hunters, much better character creation, the handler isn't irritating as hell, etc.). I especially appreciate that the major recurring characters, except for Nata, are all women. And they're *characters*. Simple characters, yes, but they have personalities and feel like they actually had thought put into them. Gemma isn't just "the smithy", she feels like a character I've actually bonded with over the course of the story. I love whenever Olivia rejoins the picture and helps on a hunt.

I love how much screentime the hunter gets in cinematics, and I'm constantly finding myself taking screenshots. Really my only complaints with this game are the sheer size (over a 100 GB... surely it could've been optimized a LITTLE more?), the ever-present cosmetic DLC, the fact that even the buffest fem hunter body is still pitifully small, and the weird limitation on adjusting character appearance after creation. Yeah, you get two free hunter re-customization vouchers, but you have to pay for more beyond that, and why lock character re-customization behind a barrier like that anyway?

Overall, an extremely enjoyable game.

Acceptable amount of content, lacking performance, solid weapon moves, solid monsters (no really fun and replayable endgame monster like Alatreon or Fatalis in MH: World), much faster hunts than MH World. 

Performance is a major question for most so with medium-high settings at native resolution I get 100% usage of CPU and GPU at 80 fps. My PC build is linked for reference. 

Overall really solid game. 

I don't like the Siekret ♥♥♥♥, I don't like having to call for a horse multiple times mid mission just to catch up to monsters that are running away, I don't know the solution but this isn't it. 

It feels like capcom doesn't want us talking to other hunters and overburdens my chat screen with random messages that I don't care about making me miss text chats that my quest/squadmates are trying to send me. You also can't receive text chats if you're in your item box, tent, or if you're talking to an NPC/forging, which I don't like. 

Also I'm not crazy about the default "accept and depart" option because they give extra rewards if you're part of the squad when the mission starts and almost every single time squadmates or lobby members launch a quest, it's already started by the time you've joined. 

Obligatory "I shouldn't have to pay for character or palico edits" inclusion, It's happened before where I made an edit and then afterwards realized it also changed my voice to the default voice which is annoying. 

I still love the game but these are literally the easiest problems to solve.

จากใจคนที่พรีออเดอร์วันแรกรู้สึกผิดหวังมากๆ
● จุดเด่นที่ดีที่ชอบคือ
1. ระบบมีบอทเข้ามีช่วยในปาตี้ ทำให้ไม่เหงาเหมือน 2 เรากับแมวในภาคก่อนๆ
2. ระบบนิเวศ ภาพสวยขึ้นกว่าภาคก่อนๆ
3. แผลของมอนเตอร์ อันนี้ดี ทำให้การเล่นดูสมจริงยิ่งขึ้น
4. กับดักธรรมชาติ เถาวัลย์ หินงอก หินย้อย อันนี้ดี

● จุดที่ไม่ชอบเลยคือ
1. เนื้อเรื่อง การเล่าเรื่อง แย่
2. ท่าเคาเตอร์ของอาวุธต่างๆที่ดูเวอร์เกินไปไม่สมจริงออกไปแนวแฟนตาซีซะมาก
3. ระบบแกะรอย ดูพฤติกรรมมอนเตอร์ก็ไม่มี (ส่วนตัวชอบแบบนี้มันคือความท้าทายอย่างนึงในตามหา ตามล่า มอนเตอร์ไม่ใช่ขี่สัตว์แล้ววิ่งไปหาได้เลย)
4. แผนที่ต่างๆไม่ค่อยมีความอยากน่าสำรวจเลยถ้าเทียบกับภาค  World ที่ดูซับซ้อนกว่าเช่น Ancient Forest (การหลงป่าคือเสน์ห์และความท้าท้ายอย่างนึง)
5. ปัจจัยต่างๆที่เอื้อผู้เล่นมากเกินไป ดูสะดวกสบายไปเสียหมด ไม่ว่ามีสัตว์ให้ขี่ แคมป์ที่ตั้งได้ทั่วแผนที่และอื่นๆยิบย่อยอีกมากมาย
6. ระบบสุ่มอาวุธ จริงๆผมไม่ชอบตั้งแต่ภาค world ที่สุ่มจากการตีแพะทอง อาวุธหรือชุดเกาะควรได้จากการแล่เนื้อมอนเตอร์ หรือ เดินหาวัตุดิบในแผนที่มากกว่าเพื่อนำมาอัพเกรด/สร้าง

สรุปภาคนี้ ส่วนตัวผมมองว่าแย่กว่าภาค World ครับ กลิ่นไอของนักล่ามอนเตอร์ยุคดั้งเดิมหายไปแทบหมดเหมือนคนละเกม ความท้าทายไม่ใช่แค่การที่ใส่มอนเตอร์ยากๆมาในเกมเพียงอย่างเดียวเพราะมันทำให้ผู้เล่นบางกลุ่มเบื่อง่าย (เช่นตัวผม) อยากให้ตัวเกมกลับมาแนวเดิม เน้นผจญภัย สำรวจ ฟาร์มวัตถุดิบในแผนที่และการเอาตัวรอด แบบนายพรานที่ต้องวางแผนก่อนออกไปล่ามอนเตอร์เป็นหลัก อยากแก้ไขพัฒนาในภาคต่อๆไปนะครับ

monster hunter wild makes my life fun and exiting again, everyday looking forward to hunt together with new friends/nnew hunter, and so much posibilities inside the game, despite the ♥♥♥♥ optimization of game itself, I will give it a  10 out of 10.

Yes, there are fun moments.  Yes, the combat feels good.  Yes the environments look neat.

But this game requires way too much to run as well as it SHOULD.  It's also another game Capcom put on the sacrificial alter to experiment what the RE engine is capable of doing.  (DD2 honestly refused to learn at all so it really doesn't count).  But there's so much that's neutered in this game when compared to Rise and World, and I'm sure the really old heads would agree even THOSE are lacking a lot of the OG MH games.

This boils down to an experience that feels like Capcom is trying to cater to everyone, and in doing so, they cater to no one.

No one really wanted the exact same monsters mid and late game.
No one wanted an auto-pilot dog mount.
No one wanted a free-aim button for combat that's meant to be clunky and tough to aim.
No one wanted their hand held.

I do love the game, I hope it can be turned around somehow, but no way will I ever suggest another MH game for a while.

I play monster hunter from the first series, played through the MH dos in japanese on PS2 with friends and translate each characters from japanese to english to notes that we carry all around. Played all the series except the MHO (chinese release).

As a fan there are things that i love on MH wilds but much more things that i am disappointed with, even after the latest patch.

What i love :
1. This game gives me the nostalgic feelings of MH4u and MH dos (in a way).
2. The animation, and combats are awesome especially offset and perfect guard.
3. Cooking, fishing, mining, endemic and details (corpses, mining minerals, etc) are amazingly done.
4. Armor and weapon design are better than MH world and it brings back some old design.
5. Cinematic cutscene of monsters introduction.
6. AI support hunter makes it great alternative for the solo hunter to fight siege or combating apex with helps.

What i am disappointed with ;
1. I was expecting more immersion with the seasonal change system like what you did with MH dos. The monsters migrated and changes not only in numbers but some species and resources.
2. Talking about the season, immersion wise, i was expecting to have to wait for the season to change before you can take specific quest on specific season, this will give limitation but better complexity in game that brings up immersion.
3. Lack of small monsters variant and alpha variant of small monsters. Even herd of large monsters are rarely appear. I was expecting alpha balahara, alpha hirabami and other variant carnivorous small monsters.
4. I was expecting more requests and more side quests from villagers and help development of each village and the villager to progress through the story, IMO it will gives the end game much more rewarding and meaningfull.
5. The story almost all the time rush you towards completing it, it is not encouraging any exploration, finding new things, and appreciate the ecosystem of the world.
6. Poor optimization on performace and visual, most texture on armor and other things look worse than the MH world.
7. Most items that supposedly help the hunt are useless or easily ignored. Mostly only use flashpod, large dung pod and traps for hunts.

I love MH series, but for me personally MH wilds doesn't lived up to the hype and expectations of some old fans. I hope once the G rank expansion out they will fix most of the problem and brings out new experience.

I don't really recommend the game with their current state.
And at the moment i am going back to MH world.

In 43 hours, I only enjoyed 3 hours. The game is so boring; it’s literally the same gameplay: fight the same monster with a different skin and loot, and repeat the same thing again and again and again.
The online in this game is so bad we couldn't handle it. One of you starts the quest and others join; you can't roam around with your friends without a mission, and it always returns you to your world after you finish or lose it. Then, you have to go through the loading screens again to rejoin your friend.
Gameplay isn't fun when you repeat it. The fight is: go to the monster, hit it a billion times, then it runs away; follow it, repeat that two times, and there you go—that’s the entire game. You can cook and capture monsters, sure, but you paid what, $60–$70 for that?
You might ask, "If the game isn't fun, why did I keep playing after the first 3 hours?" Because after level 100, there are new hunts, builds, and stuff. I thought that was going to be fun, but no, it was still the same thing—repeat again and again and again. A truly awful and unbearably disgusting repetition.
And before you say anything about me, I did play Warframe for around 700+ hours; I don't mind repeating stuff if it is actually fun to do. The story, as usual, is trash—nothing special. Some characters are hella annoying and make no sense at all, while others are amazing, but that depends on your rating.
All of that aside, you can see my specs in the comments. I literally needed another software to shut down my second screen and everything else because the optimization is crappy. I can barely play at 80 FPS with AMD Frame Gen on the lowest settings for everything. I really wish I could refund this game because it was a waste of money and time. I wish I had spent my time reading a book or counting rice instead of playing this. I got it on sale for $30, and I still regret it so much.

Online: 3/10 (it doesn't lag usually, but it crashes sometimes without reason, even with a good network and host)
Optimization: 1/10
People say Monster Hunter: World is better, but I won't try it myself because I’m not risking my money on that again.

Still hard to recommend after a full year. Performance is better now, but overall the game just suffers from trying to be like an mmo with side quests, their prereqs and the copious amount of on-rails pseudo cutscenes that seem to have replaced the unskippable cutscenes from World. For a start, I keep it simple: Yes! I can recommend Monster Hunter Wilds!

Now to the why:

I never really had any performance issues besides very few crashes right after release. I also know that my rig is non-standard, so take it with a grain of salt.

Story:
The Story in MH Wilds actually has some weight, which is kind of new to MH. It is not the most heartwarming or faszinating story, but it gets you interested in the lore and world of MH.

Soundtrack:
The music is beautifully crafted. The main theme gave me chills and every monster gets its own little theme, matching the musical style with the monster's type.

Visuals:
I think the game looks beautiful. Capcom does rely a bit too much on upscaling and frame generation, yes. But especially after a turbulant year they managed to bring it to a really good state.

Gameplay:
I fell in love with the complexity of MH combat. Being able to do so many different moves with each of the 14 available weapon types is amazing! Not just heavy and light attack spam. And they improves the combat even fuirther in Wilds. You are more agile, movement feels smooth, attacks can alter with directional input and you are less locked in place like before. I actually really appreciate the new focus mode system. It allows you to play more with camera orientation, and for some weapons it trivializes the execution of some moves that used to be much more difficult. You are not dependent on focus mode, and can absolutely play without it or in an on-and-off way.
I also appreciate the new moves and updates each weapon received!

Difficulty:
It is true, the first Monster Hunter you play is the most difficult. I believe, that MH Wilds is the best newcomer entry of the franchise. For someone who is a veteran or already experienced in MH, the game is not really a big challenge. Later on, in upper high rank, the difficulty takes a strong leap. I think this enables most new players to actually get a hang of the game before getting too frustrated. So when they then reach high rank and endgame, they are not overwhelmed with the actual difficulty. We are also aware that the upcoming G-rank expansion (Master Rank) will be a slap in our faces regarding difficulty.

Conclusion:
MH Wilds is not a perfect game, but in the current state it is absolutely fun and challenging. Also, fashion hunting was never that good! (we know this is the TRUE endgame :D)

Getting more sales than MHWorld in the initial days only to taper off after the issues made themselves apparent would make you think that Capcom would try to fix the game to keep their sales from slowing down, HOWEVER DESPITE the massive influx of cash this game brought in they don't seem to care that the game is in an unplayable state for someone that has a mid-tier gaming pc, mind you this is a 70€ game so they make more money from less copies sold.

Capcom should focus less on collaboration content and invest more of that marketing money into actually fixing this tech demo they dare to call a game.

As my game hours portray, I quite enjoy Wilds. So far it has done a wonderful job of blending a new story with a love letter to 4U. The story can go quite fast if you do minimal stuff aside from it, so it's definitely a game where you need to stop and smell the roses. I love the story, this is one of the most story-driven MH games I've ever seen (have yet to play World, but compared to 4U, GenU, and Rise, it's got an advantage).
Pros:
- Devs have actually been listening to feedback. Some weapons got nerfed, but then got buffed again to better balanced their playstyles
- Aiming your weapon doesn't involve planning your attacks out by 5 business days, Focus Mode lets you redirect
- A lot of weapons imo got improvements

Cons:
- Weapon changes are a classic MH thing and some are great, but some I think were downgraded with boring combos
- Seikrets are WONDERFUL, BUT they seem to have some issues with movement sometimes. They like to move when and where you don't want them to
- It can start out really easy. That's fine if you're a beginner, I do think Wilds is quite beginner-friendly. That being said, it does ramp up in difficulty really fast in the late/post game. Nice for veterans, bad for beginners

Ultimately I don't have any major complaints anymore now that they've done some optimization for PC (it was so bad for a while that I genuinely could not play the game). Great game, lots of fun if you like running around and Legally Bothering Monsters

As a newcomer, I can't suggest this game to other new players.
Maybe it's just me, but each time I give it a try, I end up ALT+F4'ing after a couple of minutes.

TL;DR
I completed the game, not all the side tasks, but most of them, using the greatsword, and maybe this is what ruined my experience. I didn't like most aspects of the game and found hunting monsters too repetitive once I reached the endgame.
I think it's a game more suited for players who have been playing MH for a long time rather than newcomers.
If I'm not wrong, Wilds is the third installment aimed at a wider audience, since most of the previous ones were released on portable consoles and Wii.
I think it failed to go beyond its own fanbase, and I don't see a reason why someone new should play it instead of a Souls game or even a DMC, which is developed by the same publisher.
Those two examples share some core elements, and despite their differences from Wilds, their gameplay is more enjoyable.
It's not about difficulty, it's about structure.
MH's structure isn't bad or poorly developed. It's just harder to enjoy.
The learning curve requires so much effort that it feels exaggerated for a game.

Last thing: as I said, my experience was probably ruined by myself.
I'm not saying it's a bad game, I can't say that after all.
I'm just not recommending it to newcomers.

Below is what I didn't like.

Side note: everyone mentions technical issues with the game.
As I played, I found no major problems besides the CPU running at 100% constantly on a high-end build.
I had a crash once after exiting the game properly, but nothing more than that.

What I didn't like:

a) 14 weapons to learn seems like a good starting point, for those who have time to sit for a couple of hours per session to learn combos and then apply them against monsters.
It's something I can't do.
I picked the GS because it's my favorite weapon style, and it was very disappointing.

b) The game does a horrible job of helping the player learn.
Most of the time it's better to watch a guide on YouTube.
The dummy room is kind of useless, since you cannot spawn monsters and learn their patterns. You can only practice with a cat that throws barrels or hits you with two useless attacks that don't help in any way, since monster attack patterns vary a lot.

c) The Arena would be good content for this. Too bad you're forced to use premade loadouts depending on the challenge you pick, so you cannot use it to train properly.
It may work for something, it's not all bad.

d) Another thing that annoyed me the most is that monsters have the habit of running away for miles, so you have to chase them down.
It's interesting the first couple of times, but then it falls into a constant loop that breaks the pacing.

e) Monster and player animations are exceptional… if I were watching them in a cutscene.
Player animations have long animation locks for everything, rolling, dive bombing, even calling the mount. That prevents reactive gameplay.
Some movesets are predictable, in a way, but most of the time I felt like I had to roll the fortune wheel and hope for the best.

Side note:
I started to think that part of the CPU usage is used to calculate and predict player input. I hope this is just my impression, because having a machine that reacts in milliseconds is bad design in my opinion.
There was this Congala, and it felt like it was reading my inputs.
It's bad luck or bad timing when it happens once or twice, but it happened on almost every input. It felt very unnatural.

f) Game progression is another thing I didn't enjoy.
The game throws hunt after hunt at you without giving you time to learn the previous ones.
In a couple of hours, the player has already faced 4 or 5 new monsters while progressing through the story.
I get that it's "Monster Hunter" and the goal is to hunt, but from a new player perspective, this fast progression doesn't help in understanding how the game should be played, especially when using a GS.

g) I played some MH World before Wilds, and one thing I liked about World was that I had to find the monster first, following tracks, etc.
Wilds just spawns monsters directly. And sometimes 3 or 4 of them are in the same area.
I can use dung to make them go away, that's fine, but it adds more tediousness when you're chasing a monster and it runs into another one. Using dung makes both run away, and the chase starts again.
After the third time, I ALT+F4'd. I thought it was Monster Hunter, not Chicken Chase.

h) Mount auto-run.
It felt good at the start, then it turned into a fight against my own character. I hate it now.
First, it defeats the purpose of exploring. You open the map, set a pin, and the mount leads you there. Good at the start, then just annoying, especially when it starts moving without input as soon as you mount.
Even the ability to pick up items while riding is something I ended up disliking. I spent more time looking at the left side of the screen than at the world.
At first glance, it seems like good QoL, but after 100+ hours I felt like I lost the chance to explore the world while trying to enjoy the game.
Even if I don't like the gameplay, I don't mind playing a "walking sim" if the world is beautiful and worth exploring.

i) Itemization seems well expanded, but the UI, organization, and decoration names are a total mess.
It made me quit very quickly and just go for brute force instead.
Armor stats are quite confusing.
I did the Omega fight with 300 defense and then with 480 and didn't notice any difference, especially on charged attacks.
I started questioning the usefulness of upgrading gear.

j) Grinding.
It's fine to grind for better gear and upgrades, but not when it takes on average 10–15 minutes per hunt just to drop armor sphere IV.
Grinding should be quicker, so you can move on to tougher fights.

k) Omega fight.
As a XIV player with a couple of hours in that MMO, I really disliked that fight.
It felt like CAPCOM tried to add MMO elements into an environment that isn't built for it.
I suspect they tried to emulate the "two-hit kill" style of XIV raids.
XIV raids are a dance, you learn the patterns and can adjust quickly.
You can't do the same in MH. The design is completely different.
In XIV I know when I can maintain uptime because I know the timing and what comes next. Omega acts randomly, like any other MH monster.
There are patterns, but most of my deaths were due to a triple charge into beam (a beam that can only be tanked by shield users, which is weird since GS can tank every other beam in the game and is supposed to emulate DRK, a tank job in that fight).
Plus, NPCs don't maintain aggro, and for some reason when playing with them, Omega often targets the player.
In a two-hit scenario, this is bad design.

The same thing was done by SE with Rathalos back in SB—it wasn't a good fight for the same reason.
Arkveld, on the other hand, is a much better fight because SE used their standard gameplay design for that encounter.

Those are the main points I really disliked of the game. 
There are some thing better than others, like CC it's well designed and offers many options for the customization of your character. 
The dubbed voice of you character is another thing I appreciate. For once my hero wasn't  silent  and it felt they had some agency in the story. 
Story wise, I liked the first half, the second is just repeating the same thing quicker. CAPCOM could save some budget on that, imho. 
Monsters animation as well character's are well made, too bad the game doesn't emphatize them during combat sessions. 
I liked the fact that there are four seasons i believe which rotate along the night/day cycle but only the forest area cought my attention. 
The 3D map is...something. Cool at the first glance, than you just want the standard 2D version. 
Anyways, for 30 euros I don't regret the purchase, just the time spent.

Assuming you've played a Monster Hunter game before, this is more of the same with a few mechanical differences sprinkled about including mounts, finer precision control and some great looking weather effects. There are, however, a few glaring issues.

Firstly, the story is an absolute load of dry wank. It attempts to be incredibly melodramatic yet the characters are two dimensional at best, and barely receive any coherent development. This is, of course, only a problem pre-endgame (where the majority of the game is to be had), but even then it is an annoying chore to sit through.

Secondly, the game is pretty easy up until the very endgame, and even then it doesn't offer the kind of challenge more seasoned players will be looking for. I'm personally not very good at any of the MH games but even I found the start of the game to be pretty unengaging. I was well into High Rank before I carted for the first time.

Lastly, the performance and optimization is hot poo on toast. Frame drops are frequent even on acceptable hardware, and crashes are frequent enough to become annoying enough to cause me to write this review at 3 in the morning after the 3rd crash in the last 2 hours.

At this point, if you're new to the MH franchise then I'd consider picking up one of the other options available on the platform (World+Iceborne if you're after something of a more traditional MH game, or possibly Rise+Sunbreak if you fancy something a bit more action-y to sink your time into). Both are considerably more stable, challenging and feature complete than Wilds in it's current state.

Bad story. Good gameplay. Infuriating grind.

"This review assumes you're already somewhat familiar with the MH formula, especially Monster Hunter World"

0. The Performance

9070XT AMD GPU and not a single crash in about 60 hours of gameplay, which is probably lucky considering the amount of players reporting severe issues on that front. Otherwise, framerate is pretty good, about 95-100 FPS 1440p high settings with FSR4 enabled (and about 70-80 FPS without upscaling).

1. The Story

Absolutely miserable to go through. If you hate annoying characters, especially arrogant little kids (think Ava from Borderlands 3), this game's story is not for you. I'll avoid spoilers as much as I can but the events of the main story are centered around a little boy that is treated like the most precious, most important little king of the world by everyone who interacts with him (even by your voiced player character). It's insufferable. You're supposed to be part of a guild of monster hunters and you're all beholden to the whims and feelings of a small child. I've played a ton of games, experienced so many stories, and this is the only one where the dialogues made me physically cringe.

2. The Gameplay

The combat is really good, with many fun additions to the classic weapons movesets. Focus mode is great (especially on mouse and keyboard), and it makes fighting so much more comfortable than in previous entries.

3. The Grind

They made the endgame gacha. The only way to get to the final meta builds is to farm a set of materials that you can roll for a 0.05 chance to get a good weapon which completely warps your build as it massively synergises with your armor sets and their bonuses. It's so good that you can't really swap to another non-gacha one (with a different element for example) on the fly during a hunt because your build would collapse. If you like switching it up, playing different elements, or even different weapon types sometimes, you will always be weaker and feel weaker than on your main build.

And the only way to get the materials to craft these weapons is to farm for days on end what is probably the most badly designed and frustrating monster fight in the whole game. Examples of this dreadful design are :

- 400k HP, and you're expected to deal most of that damage with a special type of slinger ammo and environmental hazards.
- The monster is absolutely massive. If you want to go melee, you will be mostly blind to his movements as he will tower over you and obstruct your vision. He's also extremely fast and mobile for his size so you'll need to chase after him more than you can actually hit him.
- The monster can fly and can shoot lasers while flying, forcing you to look up in order to dodge these one-shot rays... except he will also throw a ton of explosive blobs on the ground that you can step on, that will also take 90% of your health bar. You're supposed to look up and down and the same time, constantly under pressure to survive, but you also need to damage him or he will launch a special attack that will instantly wipe you and your whole party.

This is not Monster Hunter. Half of the damage you deal doesn't even come from your weapon. This is a brutal gimmick fight that you need to repeat over and over to farm the endgame weapons.

Of course, you can skip all this and just not bother. Play suboptimally, play for fun, whatever. But it's still frustrating to finish your time with a Monster Hunter game still rocking a so-called "budget" or "poverty" build.

I'll ultimately recommend you play Monster Hunter Wilds as it is still a good game overall, and maybe the endgame can be fixed in the upcoming major expansion ? Story's unfixable though, good luck with that.

This isn’t exactly an easy review to write, admittedly. As of the time of this review, Monster Hunter Wilds is currently at the end of its base game update cycle. The game’s optimization has improved significantly over time, which makes recommending it far easier than it used to be. 

Monster Hunter Wilds is the latest installment of the Monster Hunter series. Its shining (and now decently optimized) visuals are complemented by a series of quality of life features that fundamentally change the way the game plays compared to previous titles. The first notable change lies in Focus Mode. 

Focus Mode allows players to hold down the L2 button to aim their attacks with infinitely more control than ever before. Greatsword users are no longer committed to minute movement while building up a True Charged Slash. As a Sword and Shield user, I practically held down the Focus Mode button for 90% of the fights. The control of movement is truly insane.

The next notable change involves the inclusion of the Seikret, which is functionally similar to the Palamute from MH Rise. However, the Seikret does not engage in combat. But it will get the player out of risky positions. Did you get hit and need to heal safely? Call the Seikret. Need to sharpen your weapon while not staying in place? Call the Seikret. Don’t know where the monster is? Call the Seikret. Actually, that one is kind of a falsehood. You’ll never not know where the monster is. It’s tracked at all times. You start a hunt and immediately know where your target is. Moreover, the Seikret will automatically move you to the monster’s position without any further input from you. You’re given an autopilot that removes the joys and pains of exploration and discovery. And that doesn’t feel right.

I say this because I’ve been playing the older games in the series lately. Specifically, I’ve been playing MH2, a game that seems to actively loathe the player. You actually have to find the monster (a quality of most Monster Hunter games, admittedly). Smaller monsters will incessantly interrupt you as you fight the main monster. Paintball markers conveniently fade away the moment a monster decides to move to another zone, causing you to run all over the map if you don’t already know the monster’s movement habits. (That one isn’t actually a design flaw, I think my luck is just awful. Speaking of which…) Drop rates feel brutal, especially after slaying 55 Congalala and getting only one Vibrant Pelt, which allegedly has a 21% drop chance upon breaking the head. Upgrades are remarkably costly. Hitboxes are nonsensical. Plesioth exists.

And I love it.

(The game, not Plesioth).

Playing MH2 so close to MH Wilds shows the wide disparity between the two titles. But is that such a bad thing? Is convenience such a damning quality? Is a series’s design supposed to be set in stone? 

Probably not. Kind of. And definitely not. 

Long before I even started playing these games, a discussion has raged on regarding the difficulty of the series diminishing over time. I’m contributing to this discussion by bringing up MH2 in a discussion of MH Wilds. And I still don’t have a conclusive feeling about it, ultimately. MH Wilds feels easy to me, but I’ve definitely gotten better at the games with the hours I’ve put into each game. The quality of life features merely exacerbate the sentiment of “this game is too easy.” 

I’ve seen some people online praising this game’s story. I’m grateful that it was so pleasing to those people, but I didn’t care for it much. Cutscenes were barriers that held me back from slaying monsters. Fortunately, those cutscenes are skippable. And you can hammer the confirm button through every dialogue section if you are a bloodthirsty freak like me. The characters might be good. I wouldn’t really know because I was much more concerned with the hunts than the characterization of the NPCs. Shrug. 

I wish there were more monsters in the game, but I also recognize the painstaking labor involved with modeling and programming these high quality models. The new monsters are pretty great. Arkveld rules and is a really fun monster to fight. Bullying Chatacabra will always be fun. Rey Dau is also plenty of fun.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a fun game, admittedly. I enjoyed my time with it. Offset Attacks and Guard Clashes are hilariously effective. Hunting Horn is ludicrously good. The soundtrack is better than solid. The graphics look great, but I’m the kind of guy who is still satisfied by the graphical fidelity of a PS2 game. So, you know, make your own aesthetic determinations for yourself. 

If you haven’t played a Monster Hunter game, you might really enjoy this game. The quality of life features won’t feel as incongruent with the philosophy of the series when you have no frame of reference to compare to. Maybe you’ll really like the characters. Maybe Artian Weapons will look aesthetically pleasing to you. But if they don’t, Layered Equipment will let you change the appearance of your equipment so you can be stylish without losing your precious equipment skills.

If you’re curious, I would recommend this game. But I’d be more inclined to recommend some of the earlier titles before recommending this one wholeheartedly. MH2 is brutal, but insanely satisfying as a result. MHGU is flashy, fun, and challenging (eventually). MH4U is wonderfully fun and charming, only held back by it being a 3DS exclusive. I didn’t like MH Rise when I started playing it, but I grew to like it more and more as I played it.

So, sure. Play Monster Hunter Wilds. You might just like it. And don’t be afraid to play with seasoned veterans. Generally speaking, Monster Hunter veterans tend to be so happy to help out newcomers. You’d be hard-pressed to find someone with more patience for rookie mistakes than a Rank 999 hunter. 

Maybe you’ll find yourself engrossed in the same obsession that already lies in the heart of so many Monster Hunter players.

I've been sitting on this opinion for awhile, and finally felt like doing it now for some reason. Basically, this game is mid in the purest sense. It's definitely not good, but if it was exceptionally horrible it would at least be something. I was lucky enough to not be hit by the horrible optimization, and I do have some compliments. I like how diverse they tried (emphasis on tried) to make the monsters, and even added cephalopods! It also has some of the theoretically funnest weapon kits so far. Other than that, this game is disappointing. I want to go over every point I have, for no other reason than that I wanna. I'll also be comparing it to World, as that is the best good monster hunter game to compare it to. It's not trying to be Old Gen, and Rise (which I actually like) is trying to be a modernized portable entry and not a spectacle like wilds is. 

POINT 1: ART & ENVIRONMENT
While the monster designs are generally okay, they're also rather boring. I'm happy they're adding new skeletons, but very few of the monsters are memorable. They also kinda forget to add all but the baseline ecological implications. While World's designs were often called boring, there was also a delicious amount of care put into their biology and ecology. It gave a sense of pseudo-realism to the monsters that gave even boring designs a little bit of edge. Wilds only cares about the superficial idea of ecology. Like, Great Jagras was a pack hunting secondary consumer that was a predator of Aptonoth while also prey to tertiary consumers like Anjanath and Rathalos. Chatacabra is just kinda... there. It's also confusing why an organism in a dry savannah wastes so much water on saliva production. The environments themselves are also just boring. Everything is just muddled browns, greens, blues, and greys with no distinct flow. The palatte itself is also oddly muted, and half the monsters kinda blend into the environment. The maps are bigger, but there is less in them. There is just zero personality or direction to the environment. There's also no ecology in the environment itself, especially compared to World. World took immense care on how the environment works, and that purposefulness makes both the gameplay and design feel a lot deeper. WIlds tries to mimic this with it's seasons, but all that system does is make farming more annoying. None of the species use the inclemency in an interesting way, other than the apex coming out. Even without the Seikret (we'll get there), the environments don't have nearly as much care put into them.

POINT 2: GAMEPLAY & COMBAT
Combat is too easy. While I said the weapon movesets were theoretically really good, it's held back by the monsters folding too fast 90% of the time to need any skill. The focus system is also horrible. There's no reason to use it aside from wound breaking, which just autostuns the monster and does way too much damage. There's also the fact only one player can hit them at a time, and some wound attacks are way better than others which leads to fighting over them. Guarding also feels way more OP with a giant deflect window that negates the main drawback of shields and power clash can free knockdown. As long as the monster isn't twoshotting you for daring to not have a perfect set you spent 400 hours making max level, you are never at risk. There's also "take two weapons at once" thing, which is kinda useless except for the need to carry two elements at once. Speaking of carrying, the Seikret is the worst. As alluded to, the environment is built to force you to spend all non-combat time on it. This makes the already empty and boring environments feel even emptier as you are automatically brought to the monster. Tracking had it's annoying moments, but it made you engage with the environment more and felt like, y'know, monster hunting. Every aspect of non-fighting has been made so frictionless you start to forget the environment around you even exists. Then there's the split between weapon and armor skills, which is super frustrating. The game is also super grindy in an unfun way. Having literally everything cost those stupid hunting tickets that are way too rare for something literally every endgame weapon costs. In world, my main limit was a rare material from the monster I was hunting (which could also be gotten for melding tickets if I was bored). In Wilds, I'm constantly missing that one item that is just a chore to get. This compounds with the fact you can't just do quests anymore. Unless it's in optional quests, you just have to wait for the monster you want to show up. Monsters also just drop less resources to account for the gear from wound breaks, but that just makes grinding even more of an RNGfest. It also has both randomized talismans and decoration, which weren't exactly popular features when separated. There's also the fashion game, which forces you to get every piece of armor. It also brings back the issue world had with most weapon designs being boring, but at least World had the benefit of the barebones design adding weapon gameplay variety. Each weapon in Wilds only has like half of the monsters for design options, and the armor is really boring. I really like how they removed gender lock, but having only like 3 sets that are interesting looking makes it a moot point. Imagine if we had Namielle, Savage Jho, Kulve Taroth, or the entire Rise roster. So the gameplay is more unfun, more annoying, and more randomized for less drip.

POINT 3: STORY & CHARACTERS
The fact they call this a story is cute. It's trying out some sort of serious environmentalist narrative, that is at odds with the fact you are mass hunting rare species. This wouldn't be a problem if it wasn't so focused on having the narrative take center stage. World's (and literally every game before it) story was unabashedly an excuse plot to explore and hunt. Wilds wants to be serious and thoughtful, but is also terrified of doing everything even remotely controversial. It brings back the cut lore of the ancient civilization, but also doesn't bring in any implication of humans being in the wrong. It wants to have unique human native cultures, but none of them do anything and are somehow ignorant over basic facts of the world. Azuz not knowing about Nu Udra makes about as much sense of a Canadian not knowing what a bear is. It's commitment to it's tone also just takes away the silliness that has always been apart of the series. We don't even get poogie or funny palico animations. This wouldn't be too bad if the story was any good. Can anyone remember anything that actually happened in it, even with how much they shove it down your throat? It's just thin pseudo-philisophical ramblings even a 14-year-old could see through. Then there's the characters. Oh, the characters. They're extremely safe and inoffensive, but are under the delusion that they're somehow deep. I'll say it: I liked handler from the very start. Sure, she's a lil stupid, but at least she had personality and free will. Alma feels like a robot, always praising you, supporting you, and making sure to never get in your way. It's like they saw a handful of losers complaining about handler, and needed to "fix" her. Were people really so angry that handler saying "we did it" when she does all of the research, paperwork, scouting, and maintenance for you? Nata is also an absolute nothingburger who contributes nothing to the story despite being a main character. Gemma is also just a pair of tits, and nothing more. It's not that I want to feel that way about her, but she does literally nothing and has no personality. I don't wanna sound like I think World had perfect characters, but at least they weren't so pretentious about it.

Everything can be summarized as this series trying to be "Safe." No vision, no plan, just corporate metrics. Unless they do something crazy for the DLC, this game is dead.
