Monster Hunter Wilds is a game that was unfortunately rushed before it was ready at its initial release ultimately launching with a slew of technical and performance issues, however many of these issues have since been fixed and/or improved (not all of them mind you) which isn't an excuse but is welcomed. With technical things out of the way the actual gameplay as a complete package is very good, there are areas I personally think could be improved a lot however.

Starting with the story, it felt very "anti-Monster Hunter" in its pacing meaning there was little reason to ever engage with the main gameplay loop of Monster Hunter until the story was over as you could typically craft a weapon and armor after one hunt which was more than enough to carry you forward. This left the story feeling more like a linear railroad then a Monster Hunter game, the game does go back to a more traditional gameplay loop after the story though so it wasn't a large issue for me ultimately I just wish the story was more harmonious to how Monster Hunter is typically. The actual story itself was enjoyable and I thoroughly liked the exploration into the worlds ancient history I have no notes on the writing.

My next issue with Wilds is how it's leaning into the trend of completely removing friction from anything that isn't directly hunting a monster, this was also an issue I had with Rise but it was leaned into a bit differently in that game. This lack of friction effectively removes an entire quarter if not more of the franchise that I have personally come to actually look forward to quite a lot. Without the friction I no longer have the quiet downtime moments in between hunts of peacefully running around a locale admiring the amazing amount of detail that was thoughtfully put into it while gathering ore, bones, or other materials, I haven't had to do this more than probably 10 times in my nearly 200 hours of gameplay in Wilds compared to once every few hours in World, GU, 4U or 3U. Gathering isn't the only place friction has been removed though, Wilds showers you in monster parts to the point that when Gogmazios was added I was able to make his entire set of armor after 2 hunts with parts to spare. Additionally Wilds also completely removed the joy of getting a rare gem or plate from a monster as you will very regularly get investigations that give you a guaranteed rare item. This all leads to crafting armor and weapons becoming more of a checklist than a chase which is very unsatisfying. Friction isn't an inherently bad thing in a video game if it's added with intention it can add a lot of value and satisfaction to an experience and I hope some of these things get reevaluated in the future.

Now for a small list of more minor gripes I personally have with wilds.
- Monster Hunter games feel best with a lot of hit stop on powerful attacks, both Wilds and Rise have a minor amount of hitstop which makes every hit feel less satisfying but its lack hurts especially on things like True Charged Slash on Greatsword, look at World as a baseline for satisfying hitstop.
- Insect Glaive does not play well with charging based mechanics, it makes the weapon feel stiff, slow and immobile which is the antithesis of what Insect glaive is in every other game. (it also hurt my hand trying to play Insect glaive optimally against things like the Arch Tempered monsters)
- No Gammoth????
- Artian weapons as well as Gogmazios weapons are the worst type of grind with astronomical luck required to get just ONE weapon the way you want it, an endgame grind should take effort but it should be actually feasible with a clear end in sight.

I don't want to leave this review only talking about things I don't like so to finish off here's a list of the standout things I like about Wilds.
- The artistry in the map design is absolutely GORGEOUS and awe inspiring, it brings me back to looking into the distance in 4U or GU seeing a giant massive world that couldn't really be explored except this time you actually can explore it pretty thoroughly. I also love how every locale is interconnected and you can run from the Windward Plains all the way to Suja.
- I'm a huge fan of how special Wilds makes Elder Dragons feel by making the couple that are there such a huge deal
- The new monsters are all excellent and a couple have taken spots in my top 10 favorites.
- Nata lets you know he heard from the others (very useful).
- Wilds added the foundations for underwater combat genuinely returning to the series which gives me a great deal of excitement for the future.

Ultimately I have personally enjoyed Wilds despite its flaws. As a final rating I give it an 8.2/10 its a solid and enjoyable video game and when it does something well it does it VERY well but probably wait for a sale because of the technical issues its had.

After playing it for 31 hours at time of writing I can whole heartedly reccomend this game, at its heart its still much the same as worlds but with a few key differences that I've noticed which I will now list.

Number one, there is no more clutch claw, well... not exactly anyways. whilst you cannot grapple onto the enemy anymore you can still use it to do things like grab a material from afar.

Number two, the Palamute from rise makes a triumphant return in what I can only really describe as a chocobo like creature called a Seikret, and man is this trusty and noble stead a MASSIVE help, from giving you quick terrain traversal from riding upon its back, your mobile supply box (not item box :c) and carrying a second weapon for you to use, this noble beast is gonna be one of your best friends in these wild and crazy lands.

Number three, a more personal asside I'm sure but I find your palico friend is much more helpful in this game than wilds. They have a wide range of abilities to use ranging from being able to deploy a cage of flashflies, deploying shock traps, status effect clearing abilites, even their vigorwasp game is more on point in my opinion.

Number four? I don;t expilicitly remember this being a thing in any MH game before, being able to actually aim your melee strikes. Just simply hold down LT (seriously play this game on a gamepad, you'll thank me later) and this little reticle pops up and where that reticle is-is where the general spot yer swing is gonna go.

Number five, wounding has changed a bit. you still hit the same spot many times and it generates a wound, but now rather than it being just a temporary damage boost to hitting that same spot more, wounding a target generates a spot for a focus strike that when you hit it with a focus strike it instantly breaks that wound but for a massive hit that will even sometimes ground the target.

it is for these reasons and differences to what I;ve played before in other MH game that I can confidenly give this one a solid

"After the sandstorm comes the new spring, when life is restored to all the earth."

If Worlds didn't click with you, you might actually love Wilds for whatever reason.

Forget the optimization and hardware requirements. The first 5 hours of this game will have this terrible washed out look no matter what you do.

However, once you get past the desert, a luscious spring of evergreen awaits.

When a monster starts running away, it's no longer frustrating. Now, you hop on your stallion and herd it into a corner. You feel so powerful when chasing the injured beast, like "where do you think you're going?"

If World is the Sherlock Holmes of Monster Hunter, Wilds is the John Wick.

The Chocobo in this game is like that moment in Halo: Reach where you get ambushed by Elites and Carter says "Five and Six, clear the hole" and Jorge closes the door behind you guys as if saying "We're not trapped in here with you, you're trapped in here with us."

They say Worlds takes 50 hours to get good, but I've been having a blast with Wilds since the moment I started.

Also Laura Post is goated as usual.

I have been unable to consistently play this game due to the constant crashing on AMD drivers for over 8 months now, but I finally found a fix online that worked at fixing the game. I don't know if this fix will work for everyone but I haven't had it crash since I tried this fix! I cannot and will not troubleshoot your issues on the game so don't ask, but here is the fix that worked for me:

TL;DR AMD Drivers and this game don't like each other, game keeps crashing, here is what worked for me:

1) Open RegistryEditor on your pc
2) Go to  Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers
3) In the GraphicsDrivers folder (where you can see default, DxgKrnlVer..., MinDxgKrn..., PlatformSupport, and UnsupportedMo...) right click
4) Right click in the open space area where the files inside GraphicsDriver are located
5) Click on New > DWORD 32-Bit Value
6) Set the name to TdrDelay
7) Change value to decimal and set it to 10

This changes the amount of time your pc allows the graphics card to fix itself after a freeze or pause from 2 or 3 seconds to 10. In game this change is not noticeable but it is the reason your game crashes suddenly and should fix the game from crashing, or at least that's what helped me!

I left the initial complaints below for context.

[hr][/hr]
Insane how a game with graphical fidelity this low, can be so demanding. The post processing makes it look like a dull eyesore, the textures are laughably low quality to the effect that it almost looks like an asset flip, the game feels blurry af even with anti aliasing, upscaling, and motion blur disabled, The only thing I found to combat the awful visuals was forcing HDR on and doing some heavy tweaking, and using FSR native AA to make smaller detail look presentable.

I already paid for the damn game, why are you trying to sell me such a basic feature multiple times??? Same vibe as the BMW heated seat subscription.

Great game, not for me. At its core this game is a Monster Hunter game. The fantasy is that you are an underpowered fighter taking on larger than life monsters that are very dangerous and lifelike. The primary progression paths are skill, preparation, and pattern recognition. You memorize the monster's attacks, when it's vulnerable, the type of damage it does, what it's weak to, which tools to bring, when to heal, when to sharpen, etc. Equipment/armor and weapon stats matter as a type of baseline for the fight but they won't compensate for a lack of fight skill and knowledge. My issue with the game is with that premise. I was wanting to, at some point, overpower the monsters with gear and weapon stats over time with grinding to over-level and trivialize the fight but that's not the fantasy the game is selling. While it's the easiest in the franchise until later ranks it's still Monster Hunter fantasy. If the genre and the premise mentioned above interests you, this might be the game that gets you hooked and trying out the older entries. If, like me, you're into more of a power fantasy or non skill-based progression, this game isn't going to scratch that itch. Enemies are stat scaled so that you are almost always 2-3 hits away from death and the fight timing depends on your own skill at the fight and knowledge of the monster.

To address performance, this game performed terribly on launch. I've included my specs but as of 04/2026, on my specs, performance isn't much of an issue anymore. If you have an AMD GPU, double check which driver the devs recommend. I had to downgrade my driver by a few releases to I believe the December 2025 driver to stop crashing. This driver may change to a more recent one so I recommend taking a look at forums or discussions to see if there's a new recommended one if you're running into this issue.

Monster Hunter Wilds nails a lot of what I loved about Monster Hunter World.
The gameplay feels familiar in a good way — heavy, satisfying combat with smooth weapon flow. The visuals are gorgeous, and the atmosphere genuinely feels alive thanks to dynamic weather and reactive environments.
But it’s not all perfect.
The story feels forced, like it’s pushing itself forward instead of unfolding naturally. Progression also feels rushed, moving you through gear and story beats before you’ve had time to settle in. And the rideable animal, while convenient, actually hurts exploration. It makes it feel like the game wants you to sprint past the world instead of experiencing it.
Overall:
A beautiful, lively world with great gameplay, held back by pacing issues and design choices that discourage exploration.

One step forward yet one step backward when compared to World and Rise. However, it is the best base game experience as a package compared to the 5th gen games. The difficulty progression of the game is quite questionable as the game is very easy through it's main story, but quite hard for it's endgame quests. The difficulty progression made a lot of monsters that are not the apexes of the areas nor other high-tier monsters (i.e. TU monsters) a bit forgettable unless fought in their event quests. Wilds' version of Rathalos is the best as he now has an exclusive song with the Forest and Hills motif of the old-gen games.

Focus mode is the better implementation of the clutch claw and tenderizing mechanic in MHWorld Iceborne but with how the main story is balanced, trivializes a lot of early game monsters. This is better compared to Iceborne's addition and implementation of clutch claw which affected the base game though most beginner players in World will still need to learn how to use it first. The wounding mechanic feels the most balanced and the best at the higher level quests i.e. 9-10 star quests. 
 
Overall, not my favorite Monster Hunter experience as the Sunbreak DLC is still my personal favorite, with Iceborne as my second. Will update this review when I have played the DLC in the future.

Monster Hunter Wilds absolutely blew me away. From the moment I stepped into its massive, living ecosystems, it was clear this game is something special. The world feels alive in a way the series has never pulled off before—dynamic weather, shifting environments, and monsters that behave like real creatures instead of scripted encounters.

Combat is smoother and more responsive than ever, with every weapon feeling powerful and satisfying. The new mechanics add depth without overwhelming longtime fans, and the learning curve feels perfectly tuned for newcomers too.

What really hooked me is how immersive the hunts feel. Every encounter tells its own story—monsters fighting each other, storms rolling in mid‑battle, and the environment constantly changing the flow of the hunt. It’s the most cinematic the series has ever been, without sacrificing the challenge that makes Monster Hunter so addictive.

Visually, it’s stunning. The attention to detail in the monsters, the landscapes, and even the small environmental interactions makes every moment screenshot‑worthy.

If you’re a Monster Hunter fan, this is a dream come true. If you’re new, this is the perfect place to start. Monster Hunter Wilds is a masterpiece—an evolution of everything that makes the series great.

Monster Hunter Wilds is an ambitious addition to the Monster Hunter franchise. The inclusion of mounts, melee aiming, and more make this game very accessible for people new to the series.

Just like the previous mainline release, Monster Hunter: World, the game had performance issues making it difficult to run smoothly on most PCs. At the time of this review, there have been several patches to resolve this and performance has improved, but not to the point of perfection.

Overall, the game is probably one of the best base games to the Monster Hunter series due to its varied roster of monsters to hunt, good quality of life features, and challenging quests. Whether you are new to the series or a longtime fan, Monster Hunter Wilds is a great mainline game in the series to experience.

Overall I find Wilds to be very enjoyable! I know for a lot of people they had issues with bad performance but thankfully I have been sparred from that, running crisp and stable at 3440x1400 ultra wide. 

The good: The movement and weapons all feel great, buttery but not drifty. Compared to world its a whole new world, and the weapons all having unique models is amazing. The monsters are fun to fight, missing some of my favorites but I heard the ones we have are treats for the veterans. The Mount/ Siekret is the best single addition to the game. 

The Bad: The old investigation system from world was just flat out better, most hunts people run these are not even investigations so its a miss. The food system was a big complaint but that's been somewhat mitigated by the new hub but I dislike needing ticket. So far the only true end game farms are Archvield and Gogmazios, I get they wanted to move away from elder dragons but we need more variety in the end game. It seems like nothing is coming until the DLC

That all being said 8/10 game.

Monster Hunter Wilds looks like a confident and exciting evolution of the franchise. The game introduces more dynamic, living environments where weather and ecosystem changes actively influence hunts, adding a new layer of strategy and immersion.

Combat appears smoother and more responsive, with refined weapon handling that rewards both skill and adaptability. At the same time, monsters feel more alive than ever, showing more natural behaviors that make each encounter less predictable and more engaging.

Visually, the game is especially impressive running in 4K on maxed-out settings, it looks stunning, with highly detailed environments and beautifully designed creatures. Combined with strong sound design, it creates a truly immersive experience.

Honestly i think this game is better than all the old games, which is definintly an unpopular opinion but im having more fun with this than literally anything else respectively.. Well except rise. Which probably says alot about me.

Performance:
I've never had issues with crashing etc, even when it first launched so i cant complain there - tho i know some others do struggle with that which is surely a ashame. ( i do play on epic graphic settings etc)

Difficulty:
I DON'T think this game is too easy. For veterans that have beat all the games with no armor, one potion per battle, no dodging, no walking, no sprinting, no atacking, no weapons however? yeah probably. I didnt have much problems with low and high rank (except gogmazios) and honestly? i dont think that, that is a bad thing. This makes it easier to introduce friends into monster hunter that never played it before. And i myself hate having to try and kill something and then fail for days on end, which was (in my opinion) an issue in the early games. I genuienly do not like playing in multiplayer in most games, and i don't enjoy it either. I have played most of this game solo since i just dont like people and have issues regarding social things such as games and other due to some disabilities. Mentioning this because i have played with friends in the past and its easier with people than solo. i can understand why some are frustrated with the game being easier, as the old games were harder..  I mean i have played frontiers, that does not mean i wanna torture myself thru this one tho lmao. Grinding has been made easier which i dont mind that much. I think its a bit too easy however, and wish the gem drops didnt just.. Appear. I wish the investigation gem rates were lower and in general a gem is barely worth anything anymore.

Story: 
story wise? i think the story (even if its a little corny) is good. i like nata which alot of people dont, these people dont realize hes a stupid kid and him making mistakes and being traumatized is normal. (or yk, they just find him annoying lol) i personally think its good to have him in the story and i hope to see more of him. i also wish he could join you in hunts as more of an on-looker then helper, just for him to feel like hes there. Arkveld did have a good built up and so did zoh-shia. Gogmazios? not so much. his built up was barely if anything, existant. was he a good fight regardless? definintly. i enjoyed his fight and the replayability is better than for example gaismagorm in sunbreak. I knew he and zoh-shia were supposed to be end bosses. unlike by shara-ishvalda in worldborne for some reason. i didnt know she was the end Boss till like a Year ago. ( i didn't continue playing after her because i completely forgot about world. I do NOT like world.) I think they coulve done more with the title update monsters, but they barely ever do. Wouldve still been cool tho. The story is NOT the best from the whole game series but it's still good in my opinion, especially because everything is voice acted, and the hunter has stopped yelling every few seconds (looking at rise oh MY GOD.) I still think there should be missions where the hunter actively has to help some monsters, as that would make the whole aspect of us not just being mindless murderers more appearant. We genuienly look like we just kill monsters because we can, Sometimes the whole 'we gotta protect ourselves or others' is thrown in but it just doesnt feel good enough. If there was a story line where we would actively have to help the monsters itd be great. I like that other civilisations are shown in this. Especially ones that are very strong and capeable of Reconstructing elder dragons / black dragons as a construct. I think the constructs were a GOOD idea, and i like the guardians alot. I wish some of the monsters had more of an spot light tho. 

Exploration:
The Seikret auto walking SUCKS. it's one of the worst things in this game. Knowing where every monster is, is also awful not just for the exploration aspect but also the emersion aspect. This already annoyed me in rise, why have a mount when youre not gonna walk around anyway? Genuienly the worst thing. I LOVE the seikret, but every aspect that could be fun with it was taken away. The searching by tracks from world etc would be way more enjoyable with the seikrets, and it would add so much. Yet, it's completely useless as you just let the game do everything for you. Regarding optional missions and grinding it doesn't safe time anyway, youll remember where the monster is after one hunt anyway. Exploration isn't as prominent in the game as for older games. (not including rise. jesus rise.) which is just sad. Id rather have world exploration back without the seikrets than this. And yes, also without the tailriders cuz they genuienly suck. 

Artian weapons:
Eh. they are alright. Not good, not bad. just there. I barely use them anymore as they arent the best thing on for example the hunting horn. But genuienly master rank isn't even out yet. HOW is an Great sword hitting 1k dmg already?? 

The monster roster:

Ok except who let Congalala and Gypceros back in.. I don't wish alot of the old Monsters back. (cough, cough, Rajang) I do however kinda want Forokururu back, which i know, its an frontier Monster, but i can hope. I just think it would work in the scarlet Forest. I think Zinogre, Astalos and Glavenus are missing however. 

Overall:
The people that say this game is the downfall of Monster hunter are the same people that have played since Monster hunter - Monster hunter freedom. Which will obviously have an high impact on your skill level and other stuff! I genuienly don't think this game is bad and it is better than alot of older games for obvious reasons. Are people allowed to critizise and wish more of the game? Absolutely. This is NOT a Master piece and may never will be. however, people are overreacting regarding the games way of being. Of course, if you have performance issues, that is not your fault and fully valid if youre frustrated or don't like the game because of it, especially if you payed for it. find the game too easy or the exploration too lacking? also good point. But for people that for example have just started the series, this game is great for them, and the game is fun regardless, and it's by far not the worst Monster Hunter game there is. (The only thing that carried Generations was Generations Ultimate. The base game was bad in my opinion.)

Monster Hunter Wilds Review (a.k.a. “I Came, I Saw, I Got Sat On by a Lizard”) 

So I booted up Monster Hunter Wilds thinking, “Yeah, I’m the apex predator now.”
Five minutes later, I was getting folded like laundry by something that looks like a dragon crossed with a bulldozer and anger issues.

Welcome to Monster Hunter.

First off, the world? Absolutely gorgeous. The environments are so detailed that you almost forget you’re about to be emotionally destroyed by wildlife. One second you're admiring the scenery, the next you're being chased by a creature that clearly skipped breakfast and chose you as brunch.

Combat is back and just as satisfying—and by satisfying, I mean:

You spend 20 minutes tracking a monster
10 minutes fighting it
30 seconds getting absolutely obliterated because you got greedy

Classic.

The weapons feel amazing though. Whether you're swinging a sword the size of a small car or bonking monsters with a hammer like you're late for work, everything has that chef’s kiss impact. You don’t just hit monsters—you emotionally damage them. Until they emotionally damage you back harder.

Also, the monsters? They don’t just exist—they have personalities.
Some are chill.
Some are aggressive.
Some wake up every day and choose violence specifically against you.

Multiplayer is where the real chaos begins. Nothing builds friendship like:

Screaming at your friend to heal you
Watching them run the wrong direction
Then both of you getting flattened simultaneously

10/10 bonding experience.

Now let’s talk difficulty. This game doesn’t hold your hand. It slaps your hand away and says, “Figure it out, champ.” And honestly? Respect.

Final Verdict:
If you enjoy:

Getting bullied by dinosaurs
Crafting armor out of your enemies (petty and fashionable)
Yelling “WHY WON’T YOU DIE?!” at your screen

Then congratulations, this game owns your soul now.

Overall: Recommended To Try (8/10)

Graphics (7/10): Decent graphics, but falls short of modern UE5 engine standards, due to capcom's own in house old engine. The game does not look like the store page screenshots, often looking muddy and washed out. Running at the highest settings and high texture pack, it still looks blurry. The world appears best in sunny light and forest biomes. The monsters and weapons shine, as they are detailed, terrifying, and have incredible effects. Having joined the game late, I have not encountered any optimization or technical issues; they seem to have been fully smoothed out. 

Combat (9/10): The main strength (or weakness) of the game. The weapons are extremely unique, with vastly different playstyles and mechanics, and you can switch between them easily. They have a high mastery ceiling, with varying degrees of difficulty to pick up. As you progress, reaching key understanding moments of your weapon is especially satisfying. This means those who are impatient and expect flowery combat spam akin to korean rpgs, will have their asses clapped and face frustration. Understanding when to use certain moves, positions, as well as learning each monsters' moves are key factors to whether you will enjoy Monster Hunter or not. 

UI/Controls: (1/10): A major player friction point. The menus are convoluted, many gaming standard controls are difficult (like drinking a simple health potion), sheathing, mounting, weapon swapping, interacting with objects, are all clunky and require multiple clicks that are unnecessary. These small frustrations add up and are blood boiling annoying as you would encounter it endlessly (accessing inventory, viewing equipment, talking to any NPC, map, the list goes on). Coop multiplayer play is straight up bizarre but manageable once you get used to it. Simply start a party and you can play together. You must start all missions individually, finish the cutscenes, then leave your own game to join onto your friend's. There is no seemless coop mission sync. 

Gamplay Progression (7/10): The story and monster progression is fast paced. It is more accessible than previous titles and is welcoming to new players. There is a freedom to try different weapons until you find your calling. Endgame will have several walls, forcing players to re-evaluate their armor goal and improve their skills/gear. Grinding consists of armor spheres to upgrade gear, rerolling your artian weapons, combining armor and jewels to create set bonuses, and farming talismans with rng bonuses. Key motivators seem to focus around rng farming for min/maxing and rare material. Simple endgame but it has good replayability if you just enjoy killing big monsters and need an excuse to do so. The game has received its final update 4 and new content will come in the next expansion. 

P2W/Fomo: (9/10): Apart from light dailies and logins, the game is great for casuals and stay away from typical predatory asian game practices. Additional dlcs are cheap and add no gameplay advantage, focusing on skins, stickers, emojis, etc. Only one outfit is for sale, with most of the cosmetics coming from in game play and achievement. 

Fashion: (8/10): Transmog is free. It is locked behind the highest tier of any gear. Character customization is free. Dyes and dyeing are free. Fashion is heavily influenced by the monster parts you have slain, which helps immersion. The only downside is there are not a lot of fashion to choose from. 

Life Skills: (3/10): Fishing is fun but is useless and does not tie into the game. Mining/harvesting is fun but useless as the materials are not needed in crafting. Farming has been dropped and streamlined by just clicking on an NPC to collect. Pet system is useless, they are just an automated AI partner (but are very cute). 

Story/Missions: (4/10): Negligeable and can be safely ignored. Recommend to skip lengthy generic dialogue, but watch the cutscenes that introduce the monsters. They are badass, imposing, and help you learn about their features. Side missions give abysmal rewards. Focus on the palico cat ones and the five farming ones. Extra Missions are later updates that add new monsters. 

World (7/10): The world size is pretty small, but it makes up for it in detail and verticality. Each biome makes you feel its weather and bring the monsters to life. There are environmental interactables spread out that spice things up and are crucial for better hunts. Smaller creatures and vegetation take a back seat and are mostly set decor. The world feels alive as there is weather, day/night cycle, time schedules, monster roaming, and random events. Monsters eat, travel, and move through the world and interact with other creatures as if in real nature. 

Conclusion: Overall a solid game that is great for people new to the series. Once you get over the hurdles of the UI and clunky combat, it becomes very rewarding. There is no wasting time on boring grinds or small adds. The game focuses on the meat: giant monsters boss fights. The community is friendly and the game is most fun with a group of 4.

This review is not about the performance, too many are. While those are relevant complaints, there is always an underlying "this is a great game but it runs like sh*t therefore not recommended". This isn't a great game, this is an above average action game and a sorry excuse for a monster hunter. I am a MH veteran, and I can safely say this game strays way too much from what made the series fun for me. 

"Wait, what do you mean, isn't that just hunting monsters!?" - no. Well, yes, but also, no. It is about hunting, but in a haulistic sense. It's everything that goes into a hunt that made this series what it is. Learning about a monster, what it does, what it is weak to, what items to use against it, and strategically making decisions on what to bring against it, how to approach it.

Older games were simple, more limiting and also kicked your ass if you didn't engage with its mechanics properly. Every action was commital and carried its weight. Even a weapon like Dual Blades asked you to be incredibly careful when and where you attack. Monsters weren't just your glorified punching bags that keeled over if you so much as looked at them menacingly, and while updates have gone a fair way in making more difficult monsters, other games didn't go from 0 to ♥♥♥♥ in the endgame. I know, other base games are also easy, wait for G rank! Is what people will likely say. Which is also innacurate, because other base games still had you foraging for resources and managing them like a professional hunter bloody would. There was an ebb and flow, it felt like you were just a hunter out on your own living life in this world helping out your community but ultimately just being independent. Want to make sure you got the mega potions and items ready for next hunt? Go out and forage for it, it serves as a nice break between really intense hunts. How many hunts in a row can you go without needing to do that? What about locating the monster and being aware of the maps and your enviornment? 

You could claim this isn't challenge or difficulty or fun, it's just tedium, and if you feel that way, that is your prerogative. To me, it helps with immersion in the world and creates a nice pace. Maybe you just finished an intense 40 minute hunt or multiple hunts that really stressed you out, now you can go and forage some ores or pick some bees or get some honey or herbs to just relax a little before your next hunt. Go to your farm, fry some steak, relax. Talk to the NPCs, get absorbed into the world, sit, have a drink. It could be the coziest and most stressful game in the world, and both halves to this whole helped to create it for what it is. 
Not to mention even low ranked require you to upgrade your gear, sharpen your weapons correctly and learn the monsters. After reaching 90HR in Wilds, I went back to 3U and to my surprise low rank Barroth carted me. I was careless and didn't respect the game. 4U is even more fu*ked up than that, 4U low rank makes the high rank of Wilds look like a joke.

If there was a maybe option instead of yes or no to recommending, I'd pick maybe. Basically, if you have AMD, go play World until they finally actually fix it the crashing issue. I'll change my review to a yes (if I can) once it's fixed. And... if I remember.

I've tried to not make this a not recommend but three crashes in a row because they haven't fixed the issue with AMD. I've tried all sorts of fixes from all sorts of posts online and it just... it's too frustrating. If you don't use an AMD, go for it. Otherwise... just go play World. My pc more than meets the recommended specs, I don't have it at the highest settings... I'm tired, boss. Alright, let's lock in for an essay on this game vs World.

I was introduced to MH with World. I loved seeing monsters interact with the environment and each other. The turf wars were amazing and so were pair interactions such as Lunastra and Teostra. I loved hunting down endemic life and getting to display what I caught, having a private room to decorate and make cozy for my endemic life. I'd often be midhunt with my bf and sit there theorizing about the biology of the monsters because World was so immersive. We'd be getting punched by Brachy and I'd be sitting there wondering about what causes the slime to explode faster when it's enraged. I loved the monsters that were chill with you until you attacked, it was fun just following Tobi-Kadachi around and seeing it just interact with the environment and leave different tracks. I loved Grammeowster and Meowscular chef.

Wilds does simplify things so it's easier to play. No need to gather tracks, no need to worry about having a monster at level 3 so you know when it's near death for a capture (I try to be capture only). Dual blades have never been more fun since I can just back and forth beyblade on a Jin Dahaad like no tomorrow and it's satisfying as everything. It feels like there's less environment interaction and less immersion though. Not nearly as much endemic life as there is in World, though I did lose my entire mind seeing a village filled with Downy Crakes. One of the things I loved about World was how each locale also interacted with each other in a lore sense - the dead things from the Coral Highlands ends up in the Rotten Vale, for example. I'm sad that I no longer have cats cooking for me, though the animation of the three kitties with ENORMOUS picnic baskets is cute! If you're used to just quickly paying and getting through a meal in the canteen or gathering hub, you now require vouchers. You can cook your own meals now though after getting ingredients from gathering points, endemic life, trades with NPCs, and the ingredients cat (love you Waffles). The Diva is a great provider of buffs in what is essentially the new gathering hub and her songs are great. 

Monsters cycle in and out a little quicker than in World and you can see a general forecast of what monsters are coming up next along with a general time amount. If you didn't like the handler of World, you'll love Alma and Erik, who becomes a possible handler after a while and completing a certain quest for a certain monster capture. 

There is no private room or way to pick endemic life to display, so while it's nice fishing or getting the rare ones, there's really no point other than getting trade items sometimes when capturing them. In the tent in the main camp in Windward Plains with the three dudes who give you quests to capture endemic life, sometimes the terrestrial ones will switch out to have one on display randomly. No way of displaying the aquatic ones. Turf wars aren't as common. No clutch claw anymore, but in its place is the ability to create wounds which you can do special attacks on to cause big damage and get a material. The locales are far bigger than the ones in World. Your hunter is now voiced and there's a lot of customizing you can do.

... the cat can talk now too, but there's a setting you can do to bring it back to regular meowing. I miss Grammeow chef.

Having given it some thought, and having 100%'d it, Monster Hunter Wilds might be my least favorite game.

I'm an old hunter. I started with Monster Hunter Tri back on the Wii and I love that game to bits. But I'm not some jaded old gen guy who hates everything new; Monster Hunter World is another of my favorite games, and though it had its issues, namely the uninteresting story and AWFUL endgame content, the game was still Monster Hunter at its core and I still enjoy playing World to this day. I'm saying this as context when I say that Wilds was one of the worst gaming experiences I've ever had.

Almost nothing about this game works. I say almost because there are legitimate improvements in Wilds worth recognizing; the ability to use your binoculars to check the sizes of monsters is something that should have been in the series years ago, as is the ability to skip the 60 second end of quest timer. These are good changes. Unfortunately, they're subsumed by a cavalcade of baffling game design choices.

Let's start with the least of my issues; the pacing. To the game's credit, the pacing of the game is, at first, pretty good! Monster Hunter has always had issues with getting players into fighting big monsters and this one does it the fastest, and after the intro you're already fighting a boss. However, afterwards the game's pace rams straight into a brick wall and doesn't pick up again until the third act.

Until then, you are railroaded on these truly - and I do mean this - TRULY boring walk and talk scenes where nothing happens and the characters talk about the most boring things imaginable. If these dialogue segments were written well, I wouldn't have minded so much, but almost every bit of dialogue in this game is stilted and uninteresting to listen to, much like the story. It's not the absolute worst story I've ever had the displeasure of sitting through, but it is still a boring, melodramatic mess that cuts into time I could have been spending slaughtering the local ecosystem for new boots, and while yes, the cutscenes are skippable, the frequent walk and talk scenes are not and you have to sit there on your mount as the characters around you drone on about nature or whatever. Have fun!

Let's get into why this game really grinds my gears. The gameplay of Wilds was gutted from previous games. Hard. Monster Hunter's combat has always been about proper positioning; knowing where you should be to hit a monster's weakspots while not getting killed yourself. It was simple, it was engaging, it was unique and it was fun. Monster Hunter Wilds introduces new mechanics that remove all of that; Focus Mode and the wound system can effectively stunlock monsters to the point where they are a non-threat and almost laughably easy to kill. You don't need proper positioning when the answer is always aim for a wound and pop it with focus mode, which means you can basically just wail on a monster and kill it in five minutes. What made Monster Hunter's combat so unique has been replaced by hack and slash gameplay and I think that is unbelievably disappointing.

Continuing from monsters being a non-threat in this game, returning monsters from older games have been slowed down significantly or gutted of their moves to the point where they're hardly the same monsters anymore. Even new monsters have attacks that just leave them wide open for several seconds, and it comes off as unnatural and video game-y, which is ironic considering the story of Wilds. This is a nitpick, but for as much as folks like to rag on Capcom for reusing assets or recoloring monsters in older titles, this is the game that feels the most like I'm just fighting the same monsters over and over with different body shapes.

Wilds is also painfully easy. I was killed one time during the entire low rank campaign, and it was because of a camera issue. Monsters simply do not do enough damage to you throughout low rank to justify getting new armor past, like, the midway point, it's pathetic. Preparation, a key component in older titles, has been streamlined to the point where it's almost unnecessary. Your cat friend can heal you, buff you and cure you of all statuses the instant you get them, removing any urgency you may have getting poisoned or burned, and unlike previous games, your cat always has access to all of their tools, removing any semblance of customization for them. Luckily the cat can be turned off, but the random endemic life that does the same will always be out in the open for you to use, removing the point of half of this game's items.

Connecting to friends has never been made harder. Instead of the simple act of selecting a lobby and then joining, Capcom thought that process wasn't complicated enough and introduced several friend code and friend list systems that muddle the joining process and make connecting with your friends way more difficult than it needs to be. Even once you're together, God help you if you picked the option "environment link" because now you're all stuck in the Windward Plains with nothing but a Chatacabra, a Balahara and absolutely no way to initiate quests. What the hell was wrong with the old system?

This is the big one: Your mount will automatically take you to your quest target, removing the need to learn your environment and understand a monster's behaviours. This is one of the core components of Monster Hunter and it was completely removed: Actually HUNTING the monsters. Now you just always know where your mark is, you go to it and you hunt it. Yes, it's more convenient, yes, it's easier to get into playing the game but it removes so much nuance and charm and uniqueness the older games provided to the point where Monster Hunter Wilds doesn't even feel like Monster Hunter anymore.

It feels like just another action game, its individuality and personality torn to shreds and its mechanics broadened to make Capcom as much money as possible from as many people as possible. And it worked. Capcom will never learn from this because it sold like hot cakes. They will never make another Monster Hunter game again, but they will keep making games like Wilds devoid of what made Monster Hunter, well... Monster Hunter.

And I think that is a genuine shame.

Having crossed the 100 hour mark, I believe it appropriate to give a review. To get the big question out of the way, the current state of its performance is that my laptop with an Nvidia 3050 RTX GPU and 16 GB RAM can handle this game fine, and I can have the most impactful lighting options set to medium quality and texture/model quality set to high. The main shortcoming of how it downscales is that shadows have noticeable artifacts and noise, it clearly wants raytracing enabled. Not great for screenshots, but is not obvious enough to hamper readability of monster attacks. My experience is that the graphics auto-config tool is good about ensuring your settings will have nice framerate so you don't have to spend 10 minutes wrestling with your game operating at 5 FPS.

I do not use frame-generation, I cannot speak to how that impacts the experience. If you use an AMD video card, you may have more problems than what I experienced.

[b]If you hail from previous Monster Hunter games...[/b]

Monster Hunter Wilds will be crazy easy with your past experience, and Focus mode is an absolutely cracked buff. Once you start getting into HR 7+ the monsters will start pushing back and provide due challenge. Arch-Tempered Arkveld's super-enraged state makes Savage Deviljho sound like the voice of reason by comparison. A lot of the apparent easiness, outside of just the player character's handling being buffed by Focus mode, is the absolutely massive gathering radius a player has courtesy of the hook slinger, and the Seikret mount's auto-pursuit means I can grab stuff like Godbugs and Might Seeds while still moving directly towards the hunt target, resource attrition takes much longer to set in. Compared to World, or even Rise, be prepared to be let down by the environment. It lacks the interesting buffs you can collect in Rise and the act of exploration is nowhere near as "tangible" for lack of a better word, as it is in World.

[b]If you're new to Monster Hunter...[/b]

Ignore anything I said about the game being easy, I'm a salty veteran hailing from Monster Hunter World and Monster Hunter Rise. For anyone else, Monster Hunter's complexity is as monstrous as its literal monsters. A Soulsborne comparison is reasonable for how combat works in Monster Hunter, you press dodge (or block) to not die and hit the monster until they die. The primary difference is that you have basically no i-frames unless you build for it, and you will want to go in thinking of your weapon as basically your character class. There are 3 weapons that provide a weapon and a shield, including classic Sword and Shield, Lance, and "Charge Blade" (it's a sword, and a shield, and an axe? And it actually makes sense visually???) and they play profoundly different from one another despite the superficial similarities.

If I were to suggest anything, once you visit the starting town you'll want to visit the Training Area as soon as possible, if you run around the edges of the town you will find it sooner or later. There you can pick a weapon (the game automatically gives you a level 1 version of every weapon type) from a box and beat on a training dummy which will show damage numbers and trigger mechanics. Press all the buttons and see if there's any combos that aren't being explained to you in the upper-right hand side of the HUD. You can talk to the Palico (cat) in the Training Area to configure the training dummy to actually attack you too, so that way your judgment about what weapon feels right to play with is closer to real combat. Also, don't be afraid to wiki-search how things like "Evade Extender" or "Critical Status" work, or even your own weapon combos. While many work as advertised, for some the English tooltip isn't quite enough to explain the nuances.

The other suggestion I would have for a newcomer is [b]do not under any circumstances ignore Optional Quests.[/b] They quite often have permanent and significant upgrades as rewards for completion.

But of course before you hunt you make a character first. "Wait, there's a GAME attached to this character creator?!" applies to Monster Hunter Wilds. Depending on how into it you get you can easily spend an hour or two just getting your character made to spec. And you have a talking humanoid cat (Palico) who you are expected to customize and name just like a player character!

[b]What both newcomers and veterans can commiserate on...[/b]

The story is probably the biggest weak point of Monster Hunter Wilds as an actual game (not including nitty-gritty balance issues as that is always a moving target), it starts out fine and does its job of introducing the explorable zones in an orderly fashion with a narrative connection between them. Trouble comes when it wants to play up its cutscenes and dialogue, which takes the game hostage like it's a Final Fantasy game but it never really gets past "A problem? What could be behind this? Oh it's a monster! Because this is Monster Hunter and of course it is." Cutscenes are often played right before you fight a 6000kg fire breathing chicken so it necessarily has to be pretty shallow. There is also a notoriously disliked character named Nata, who I half-jokingly refer to as the "plot parasite." I don't hate him like most people do but I don't fault it either. His presence in the plot actually makes a lot of sense in the early to mid game, but without spoilers the most I will say is towards the end of the core storyline he ends up being the victim of some really bizarre characterization that comes off as incredibly unnatural, be it for an adult or youth. However, it does ultimately arc back towards something acceptable, it doesn't stay cringe forever.

[b]The overall takeaway...[/b]

That said Monster Hunter is a game about... hunting monsters, of course. It unquestioningly delivers on that front and has never been so accessible to newcomers. Some parts of that make the game shallow in a way that is casually obvious, the most glaring one being the mount which very much dumbs down the process of exploration and literally auto-moves to the target you have set on your map (and is automatically set to the monster you are hunting!) with full pathfinding. The greatly enhanced mobility of the player character's attacks is much more tastefully done and is the primary strength of Monster Hunter Wilds. Also, once you've completed the core storyline, you get access to a transmogrification system and there is a limited ability to dye armors, Fashion Hunter is the true end-game of Monster Hunter Wilds and if you're big on that you can get 100+ hours out of the game like I have.

I'd rather give this a neutral rating but since I have to decide I'll go with negative. I enjoy the game and will keep playing it but there are some things that just keep bothering me. The expansion may be able to address some of those but not everything can be fixed. I'd say post Title Updates Wilds has the better endgame than the base games of its modern predecessors but that wasn't exactly a high bar to begin with. Is the game worth a purchase? Honestly Rise and Sunbreak together offer all in all the better experience if you don't mind graphics and multiplayer. However if you're looking for multiplayer and your rig can handle it I'd take Wilds over World and Iceborne.

For the things that bother me:
- Graphics: it's true they patched the performance to some extend but there are things they couldn't patch like the blurriness, the fog and the brutal post processing. The game lacks visual clarity and as consequence it can be hard to tell what's going on. Their pinnacle of design is literally an all grey zone with terrible lighting where you fight a grey monster.

- floaty controls: Capcom has multiple motion cap studios and it shows. They felt like they had to animate everything but as consequence your character feels unresponsive thanks to additional intermediate animations. It's especially bad after getting hit when you're on the ground and try to get up just for some struggle animation to play. People would say old generations were clunky and had idiotic extra animations but even with things like the superman pose the controls itself were crisp and not as muddy as they are in Wilds. While it doesn't affect me frame gen will introduce additional input latency making it even worse.

- Weapons: Some are good, some are bad, at least one is so awful you could just remove it. I hated the counter focus in Rise however there you had alternatives. In Wilds they are forced upon you. I don't even get the point of 14 weapons when everything is a counter stick. A lot of people will point out that focus mode killed positioning but as someone who plays a lot solo I never need to position anyway as I'll just wait for the monster to attack me and then counter it. For me the biggest loser is the Insect Glaive even post patch as Capcom doesn't seem to know what it should be. Hammer is now "what if we turned that youtube hammer slowmotion Tigrex video into a weapon" however offsets become pretty awful once the monster have high hp and buildup resistance and outside of them at least slower weapon have a hard time dealing damage. I wouldn't even say hammer is gameplay wise bad cause I enjoy it but all the extra steps you have to do just to get the same damage as other weapons in total is frustrating. All in all if hunters weren't so absurd strong maybe monsters wouldn't require all this extra crap either.

- Seikret: They wanted a combination of Palamutes and Wirefall but made both strictly worse. Palamutes were more interactive and they weren't forced upon you. You could always bench them and use Wirebug movement as alternative. Something similar isn't possible in Wilds at all and maps are clearly designed around the Seikret. The worst is the Seikret pickup is just bad. In order to pick you up the Seikret has to physically run to you from its current random location while having collision with the monster, can be knocked over by monster attacks and will jump over ledges so the pickup will always fail at a ledge. How long this takes and in what direction the journey will go? WELL you will see. It works in the early game but once monster deal damage it can flat out cart you if you accidentally use it.

- Ecology: I don't care about corpses rotting. I don't care about the pack monster interactions that requires you to stalk them for 10 minutes. Endemics were a nice touch in World but you would see them while traversing the map. Captured endemics you could place in your room and interact with. Here the Seikret strikes  again as it walks you just past all of them with the maps not being designed around seeing endemics anyway unless you search for them. You catch them? They are just an entry that's it. I don't want ecology to be removed as whole but it should be a nice to have and not the main focus. The game is called MONSTER HUNTER and not some pseudo realistic bird watching simulation.

- Maps: the desert is unnecessary big with anything other than the north-eastern area being awful to fight in thanks to slopes resulting in sliding and mini jumps when dodging. Forest and Oilwell are OK but Cliffs and Wyveria are two awful maps as they are cramped, visually monotonous and full with ledges, slopes and other crap.

- Too much random grind: this used to be a franchise about killing monsters and using their skin for armour and weapons. The materials being random drops was plenty honestly. Sure some parts were hard to get but hunting the monster again and again was the game. Now? Random decorations, random talisman, random weapon parts, random weapon bonus, random weapon set bonus. You want to try a new weapon? Too bad now go through absurd amounts of randomness cause the crafted weapons cannot compete with the randomised ones. An appealing grind has progress but here you just bash your head against the wall and hope you will eventually get what you want which can be never.

Judge for yourself with this
[h3]Performance[/h3] There has been a noticeable improvement from version 1.0 to 1.041, but it’s still not enough when compared to my experience with World Base, where, by simply meeting the recommended requirements, I could play at high settings with a stable 60 FPS in almost every situation. In Wilds, on the other hand, even when significantly exceeding the requirements, the game only gets close to 60 FPS on high settings, and not consistently across all scenarios, the compromises between engine change, better graphics and multiplatform release are not acceptable if the game is poorly optimized
[h3]Focus mode[/h3] They decided to speed up the combat system and to compensate, the monsters have longer movesets and attack chains, This should further incentivize learning the timing of attacks with defensive tools like dodge> parry>counter rather than slower gameplay focused on positioning and precision. Precision is no longer crucial due to the ability to redirect attacks 180° by moving the camera and execute almost any attack without needing to reposition using the moves designed for that purpose, further diminished by the magnetism of the focus mode that brings you closer to the monster while attacking. Only the monsters from the latest updates need to use focus mode (~30°) to fight them, so I hope they reduce the focus mode rotation angle a lot, balance the latest and future monsters on this change
[h3]Seikret[/h3] It seems designed more for switching weapons within the same hunt rather than between hunts, since in most cases players return to camp to restock anyway. The issue is that most hunts are relatively short, so switching to a weapon that requires resource refilling is rarely worth it. Furthermore, the ability to call Seikret to dodge attacks or use consumables reduces interaction with monster moves and undermines the skill-based systems meant to manage threat. The possibility of not being able to call it during the aggressive state of a monster would be a solution and would increase the usefulness of dedicated tools that allow you to lose aggro without therefore denying the initial function of the mechanic. The autopilot currently makes sense as there is little use in the materials that can be collected by exploring the map. I hope that with a deeper understanding of all the mechanics that require the collection of these materials in the master rank, they will also make it available after to encourage people to explore and collect the materials needed for hunting to avoid increasing the gap between more experienced and new players in the MR
[h3]Sharpness[/h3] The sharpening system has been changed so that each whetstone cycle restores part of the sharpness instead of fully restoring it at the end, a consequence to lead most people to sharpen the weapon even without seikret. This reduces the usefulness of all skills and consumables that decrease sharpening cycles, speed up the animation, or reduce sharpening frequency 
[h3]Monsters[/h3] There is a large variety of monsters focused on different mechanics, such as: poison blight, stench or defense down, the problem is that they are not explained well and are not even overwhelming to have to consider them, but it promises well for the MR if they decide to give it relevance. Apex monsters seem to follow a progression where one element counters the next, but they are communicated to the player only visually, the order is initially mixed up by putting the one with the most recognizable weakness first, and without taking advantage of the numerous materials that are given to you by closing the wounds which should lead to easier crafting of elemental weapons for fighting these monsters, only for the low rank. The game teaches little about elemental weaknesses, risking situations similar to Alatreon where players ignored simple key mechanics. If players are not guided toward understanding these basics, it becomes difficult to introduce more complex mechanics later on, provoking bad experiences in multiplayer.
It seems that for Uth Duna there was the idea of ​​using the wedge beetles to dodge certain moves, the problem is that it were too permissive especially with the waves even in the AT version, leading the community to ignore everything and leading them to think that the hitboxes were wrong, it was a good opportunity to see how the vertical movement was perceived and maybe use it to transfer some more moves from Rise and take advantage of RE ENGINE. Gogmazios’ final phase in particulary has been poorly received by the community especially in the part where you have to throw the pods, probably put because the last time they introduced an elemental DPS check, many people joined without an elemental weapon, contributing 0 in the hunt, so to prevent all this, they put a combat portion that almost completely denied the use of weapons, forcing the use of a tool that allows them to contribute a minimum
[h3]Hunts[/h3] Re-encountering previously hunted monsters has largely been reduced to the simple task of gathering materials, without any real evolution in understanding their attack patterns or improving repositioning strategies. It often boils down to a relentless cycle of attacks, exploiting blind spots to attack without consequence.
Multiplayer hunts have gotten so bad that there is the option to replace people with NPCs, this is a failure for a game that should encourage cooperation with other people, NPCs should be used for introductory missions and to explain mechanics clearly
[h3]Skills[/h3] The amount of useful skills and build variety has increased, but there are also almost completely useless skills because they conflict with the mechanics they are supposed to interact with.
Bladescale Honing could have been a very interesting armor skill to give more space to low-sharpness weapons. With this skill after a dodge, sharpness have a damage multiplier equivalent to purple sharpness for a number of hits, which would make it more effective with weapons that are balanced on sharpness as far from purple as possible, hence many unused monster weapons, while supporting niche playstyles like counter-based or sheath playstyle, and testing how good it to make change in responce in MR. Instead, it has been restricted to a weapon branch that uses white sharpness, limiting its impact with specific moves and making it overall good with the whole moveset, but mediocre if compared with other weapons.
Bludgeoner is designed to be an easier counterpart to these weapons with low blue sharpness that encourages maintenance, so it was a good idea to create weapon branches with the skill already implemented at low rarities. This is also because it isn't necessarily present at the start when trying to obtain the jewel for this skill, and new players might not even notice it. Lastly, there is a jewel that takes up a level 3 slot, but there are no weapons of rarity 7 or higher with the green sharpness, when the level 3 slot is available 
 [h3]Post-game[/h3] Monster difficulty is indicated by the number of stars and depends on the hunter level, progressively increasing the spawning of stronger monsters on the map. The problem is that after a few hours the difficulty stops and reaches its limit with events

It's been a bumpy ride for this game, but it's only aged like wine with the updates. New difficulties(more 8 star monsters, 9 stars, 10 stars, ATs, new tough fights like Omega and Gog) have done a good job with making the game not feel as button mashy as it was originally. I think now it's probably the best base game the series has received, meaning the potential for the expansion is absolutely insane.

Please note that I do really enjoy this game, even with some of the changes in this game compared to other modern MH games. 

However I AM leaving a negative review as the game pretty much requires an SSD to run smoothly, it won't matter what's in your rig. You WILL have "loading periods" where all of the models for the pc, npc, armor, weapons, etc. will be just triangles for a good 2-4 minutes, on the higher side if you're unlucky.
Yes, the game appears to run smoothly with shorter "loading periods" on lower settings (i play on medium as, gameplay > graphics, for me always)- it's not that my pc couldn't handle the highest settings, but this shouldn't be an issue at all, considering the rising SSD prices.

Overall, i'd say if you are a die hard monster hunter fan like me, its worth it. If you are a beginner and you want to get into monster hunter, just go play world.

Edit: It's been many weeks since my last review and I'm still logging in almost daily hoping to see any changes, but the performace is still garbage.

Each new update brought performance issues but were more or less ironed out in the following weeks. The February performance patch, however, completely wrecked the game for me and there hasn't been any more patches for over a month. More than 900 hours invested into the game and it suddenly became a stuttery, unplayable mess.

Writing this having just finished the low rank "campaign" --full disclosure I don't usually play MonHun games but my brother loves them so I got this purely so he and I would have something to play together.

Visually, this game is gorgeous, easily the prettiest game I've played in years. I like the characters, the combat is easier to learn than Rise, which is the only other one I've played at all. I'm getting mid 40s FPS on a 3060, which is fine for my eyes.

I knew going in the story as such wasn't going to be too deep, but it did kind of surprise me that I breezed right through the low rank stuff without needing to get help or really optimize, but the lack of grind that early on was welcome.

The only real complaints I have are:
-Some of the controls in the default mapping are awkward on mouse/kb (R+E for example)
-Driving your mount with separate keys for accel/decel rather than W for go release W to stop is annoying
-Some mechanics exist but aren't introduced well, seeming to expect you to have played the series before

Overall it's pretty fun, but for my taste I'd love to see a game in this style and world with more depthful story (I know MH Stories exists, but they play differently/have different visual style)

I really love this game. So much i even say that it beat World and Rise for me. I just think, same as World, the base game has so little monsters although it's way more than base game World, but if we compare to Rise base game, it has so much more (like Zinogre, Nargacuga, Tigrex, etc. Maybe i'm biased with the flagships lol.)

The best part of Wilds is the open world for me, it's seamless from one quest to another. I just can't stop bouncing from one monster to another, it's so seamless, quick, and fun, with the addition of the pop up camps, just make things way more convenient. I just think the maps are less memorable than World or Rise, but i like the many ideas of the different direction of the usual biomes like ice being like a ruin and the usual volcano being a basin, i really like that idea.

There is certain things that i just don't like about it though. For me the low rank is so redundant. managed to finish low rank by just crafting the Low Rank G Rathalos armor to fight the final boss, and it's like a piece of cake. But after you reach High Rank, the game finally opens up, but at that point the story is already finished.

The story itself is not really that compelling to me, although it's the best out of World or Rise. But again, this is Monster Hunter, i don't expect like a thought provoking story out of it.

Overall i love this game, and definetly worth picking up. And to be clear, i do not counter any crashes, althought the online sometimes could be pain in the ass, like disconects out of nowhere in the middle of a quest which is annoying, but other than that, i do not counter any crashes so for me, the experience is buttery smooth.

This game could have been so much better. I'm by no means a Monster Hunter purist, but the way this game plays has drained all fun for it for me. I even waited until recently before picking it up, because I'm no fan of the drip feeding of content, but in the end I probably will not even experience most of the newer released stuff.

I love Monster Hunter Rise, so unlike some veterans I'm not opposed to more fast and fluid gameplay. Wirebugs are a way superior system than Focus mode though, I felt it was pretty clunky and not much fun at all. Add to this the handholding in the story, the Seikret go-to-target auto mounting, and the way the game handles exploration, I couldn't help but feeling bored after reaching High Rank.

They tried to make the world more detailed, seamless and open world, but it comes of as having way less charm than it's predecessor's maps. This is probably in part due how the gameplay loop consists of pressing up to automatically go to the monster, slay and repeat, so there's no incentive to learn the maps. Due to the auto pathing the maps also feel way more restricted, compared to Rise.

The story is unfortunately also not very inspired and feels more like some boss rush where control is constantly wrestled from the player. I honestly was also not very fond of Monster Hunter: World, so maybe the main line games are just not for me, but I think there definitely was potential that it learned some lessons from Rise to elevate the experience, unfortunately for me that is not the case.

The game looks quite nice in certain parts, and I had no serious performance issues. But I had some issues with texture popping in way too late in some cutscenes making a lot of the more scenic scenes look pretty bad.

All in all a rather disappointing 30 hours for me. Maybe I will come back and the game will click for me all of a sudden, but I don't hold my breath. So if you, like me, loved Rise, I cannot recommend this game.

After hitting the 100-hour mark in Monster Hunter Wilds, I feel like I can finally give a proper opinion on it and honestly, I’ve had a great time.

This was my first Monster Hunter game, and I won’t lie: the combat felt strange at the beginning. It’s not your typical action system everything has weight, timing matters a lot, and you can’t just button mash your way through fights. At first, it felt a bit clunky, but once it clicked, it became incredibly satisfying. Learning monster patterns, positioning, and committing to your attacks makes every hunt feel rewarding.

What really kept me playing is how engaging the gameplay loop is. Hunting, upgrading gear, trying new weapons (even though I think I used the dual blades for around 80 of the 100 hours lol) it never really got old for me. There’s always something to work toward, and each improvement feels earned.

On the technical side, I personally didn’t run into any major issues. No noticeable lag, no game-breaking bugs overall a smooth experience throughout my time with the game.

All in all, Monster Hunter Wilds ended up being a fantastic first introduction to the series for me. It has a learning curve, but if you stick with it, it’s absolutely worth it.

Having played Monster Hunter: World and Monster Hunter: Rise, I had high expectations for Wilds. While it lives up to the Monster Hunter legacy, it feels like it could have been so much more.

The world is mesmerizingly beautiful, with ever-changing weather and diverse regions. Riding my colorful bird and watching my palico’s clumsy antics are fun, but compared to World, Wilds feels like it’s missing some life. I can’t pinpoint exactly what, but the sense of vibrancy isn’t quite there.

Combat, exploration, monsters, wildlife, weather conditions, different regions, mini-games, and side activities are all interesting—but not enough to justify the $70 price tag. At a 30–50% discount (or more), I’d recommend picking it up. For those wondering whether to leave World/Iceborne behind and jump into Wilds: don’t. Wait until bugs are fixed and new content (DLCs) is released.

Pros:

🌍 Beautiful world with dynamic weather and diverse regions

🐦 Fun mounts and palico companions

🐉 Classic Monster Hunter combat and exploration

Cons:

🕳️ Feels less alive compared to World

💸 Not worth full price at launch

🐞 Bugs and lack of content updates (for now)

Overall Recommendation:  
Worth playing eventually, but only at a discount and after future updates. For now, stick with World/Iceborne until Wilds matures.

After 300+ hours, I can safely say this is one of the most addictive entries in the Monster Hunter series.

The core gameplay loop is as satisfying as ever—tracking, learning monster patterns, and finally taking them down never gets old. The combat feels weighty and rewarding, especially once you really start mastering a weapon. There’s a ton of depth here, and the game does a great job of letting you improve naturally over time.

The monsters are a highlight. They’re well-designed, challenging without feeling unfair, and each hunt feels like a proper fight rather than just a damage check. Multiplayer is also a big plus—hunting with friends or randoms adds a lot of replayability.

That said, it’s not perfect. Some grinds can feel a bit excessive, and there are moments where the pacing slows down, especially when farming specific materials. Performance and minor technical issues can also pop up depending on your setup.

Still, if you enjoy action RPGs with deep combat systems and rewarding progression, this is absolutely worth your time.

TL;DR:
Great combat, awesome monsters, tons of content. A bit grindy, but that’s part of the charm.

Monster Hunter Wilds released in an inarguably terrible state. The performance issues were inexcusable, and even on a fairly powerful rig like mine, I still struggled to maintain a decent framerate. Many also experienced consistent crashes, stuttering, low-poly models appearing, bad pop-in issues and basically any performance issue under the sun. To add to this, the title-update model Capcom has been using for the past few games is merely an excuse to release an unfinished product while still charging full price for it. While I enjoyed Wilds at launch, I would not have recommended anyone buy it at that time. However, in its current state, I would recommend you pick up this game: especially if you are looking to get into Monster Hunter for the first time.

Capcom has almost entirely fixed the performance issues that once plagued the game as well as fleshed out the roster, and they have added many amazing hunts. Archtempered Arkveld alone makes Wilds one of the better Monster Hunter games in my opinion. 

There is no better game for new players to enter the series from; Wilds is by far the easiest game in the modern Monster Hunter era. While that is a detractor for veterans, it undoubtedly eases new players in. Couple that with character controls that are crisper than they have ever been, and you have a recipe for clean, fun gameplay people of all skill levels can enjoy. 

If you are still skeptical, wait for a sale before picking up Wilds, however, I believe this already decent game will become much, much better when the expansion releases. If Capcom can release another expansion on the level of World's Iceborne, we are in for a treat. I'd rate Wilds in its current state a solid 7/10.

This review is being given through the lens of someone who already loves Monster Hunter, but waited an entire year for Wilds to receive its final update and get fixed with multiple patches. If I were to leave a review based on my initial experience though, it would have been an easy thumbs down because the game performed horribly and had an incredible lack of content. I'm honestly glad I waited, because it's really good now and if you were on the fence before, it's a great time to hop in.

Capcom should have released the game in this state though, to be honest. There's no denying or forgiving them for that. It's sad that as a huge MH fan, I (and probably many others) had to wait for such a long time to play the "real" game and I feel especially bad for early adopters who brute forced it anyway during launch because it was a lot worse then than it is now. Totally unfair to them, especially for the price.

Even today though, in its "better" state, the game still feels a bit thin and has some performance issues. For example, there's a noticeable lack of end game content, masked by a boring grind that will probably have the gap filled with this game's version of "Iceborne", whenever that comes out, and it definitely still runs pretty bad, even on high-end hardware without the use of several AI tools (DLSS, frame gen, etc).

Despite its flaws though, I think Wilds strikes the perfect balance between Rise and World. Maybe a hot take, but this is my favorite combat in a MH game so far. It's not too fast or too slow, plenty of quality of life changes have removed the "boring" parts of hunting which keeps the pace going strong and I know this is controversial, but the focus/wound system really resonates with me. I enjoy this combat a lot.

Some people say this is the "easiest" MH so far and I agree, to an extent, but I also think a lot of that sentiment comes from experienced MH veterans who already gained enough knowledge from other MH titles to easily succeed in this one. I can definitely agree that Wilds is more accessible to newbies overall, but it still has that classic MH learning curve that many people can't overcome. I wouldn't say it's an "easy" game by any means, it's just that the core MH playerbase has gotten better over the years, so it's hard to make the game feel difficult for them without totally alienating new players at the same time. Every MH game going forward will probably feel "easy" if you've been playing them since 3DS to now.

Overall though, Wilds is an undeniably good game that's unfortunately being managed by a company who doesn't seem to care for it very much, but I am still having a blast. Even almost 150 hours in, I can't seem to put it down and I can't wait to see what the inevitable expansion brings. Remember, World didn't have the greatest reception either until Iceborne dropped and then it really delivered. Same thing with Rise and Sunbreak. People seem to forget that. Capcom has a formula now and they're not changing it with Wilds.

tl:dr
The game was bad, now it's good, but it still has some issues and gaps to fill that will probably be addressed with the upcoming expansion.
