The story is absurd, the characters are insufferable, and the mechanics have been dumbed down to where an IGN game journalist could make progress. Be prepared for railroaded training wheels for the first 6-8 hours before the game allows you to hunt monsters how and when you want to. 

For the core mechanics of the game.... it's Monster Hunter for the low attention span, duel monitor-watch-your-favorite-show-while-you-play. Everything is on auto pilot. Auto movement, auto explore, auto monster tracking, auto crafting, auto material gathering, auto goods harvesting. 

Capcom created a large beautiful words to explore but was so worried your little brains would get bored in the 30 seconds it took to get from point A to B that put in fast travel points everywhere on the map for you to place. The entire game is base on getting you back into the action as fast of possible. 
As an end game mechanic or system, that would be convenient. 
As a core principle of the game, I feel it cheapens the experience. 

All in all, it isn't a horrible game. It's just not a very good monster hunter game. A Monster hunter Lite* or an arcade version of previous monster hunters. It can be fun and I will continue to sink some hours into it but absolutely not worth the $70 asking price.

Monster Hunter Wilds scratches the World itch while adding new features and QoL improvements.

New mechanics such as counters, rushes, and more keep the player engaged and involved in fights better than previous games. Similar great camera work and animation stops on big hits recreate the weightiness of succeeding extensive combos and hitting monsters at just the right time. 

You won't find as many spacing setups as World and even less than Arise, so the name of the game is getting up close and personal for the most part. Wedge beetles exist, but are far less user friendly by being out of sight and in inopportune areas. The camera constantly fighting to stay on the monster doesn't help much either. Air control somehow feels worse than World.

Lance is more pressure based. 
Gunlance now has two wyvern fires and shelling is built more into combos. 
Greatsword now has a parry and counter system, something much needed and rewarding. 
Sword and Shield is still the omnitool. 
Long Sword combines facets of World and Arise with better meter building and leveling combat systems; however, personally I found it less flashy than previous games. Once you learn combo transitioning, it does play smoother than World.

Overall, beyond the lackluster performance on launch, Monster Hunter Wilds is a great successor to World. I do think they need to rerun pretty much all the limited time free challenge quests due to the onset of new players who waited until performance issues were patched.

I bought Wilds because I needed a MH fix before Stories 3 (which I've been playing now and is actually great), and man, honestly? I can't recommend it: between the texture loading issues despite me being in the recommended specs range and trying numerous fixes, the watered down gameplay, that removed even more friction from MH than the last titles did, the maps that while having a certain beauty, you're not incentivized to learn thanks to the mount that brings you everywhere automatically, and an issue I see few people mention, but to me seems extremely important to talk about, the horrendous, hand holdy, low rank. I know what people want to say about that "oh it's just LR, it gets better!" yeah, after 15-20 hours. I am baffled at people being okay with such a boring narrative and easy gameplay being so thoroughly shoved down their throats, those walk and talk sections are horrible, and who are they for? If you are impressed by the environment, and want to explore, guess what? NOPE! Back on the ride boy! You do not get to explore but in a few selected parts or wait for High Rank! You don't care about the world or ecology? Then why would this be interesting in the slightest? Genuinely the worst for both.

If this is your first MH, honestly, go back to Rise and World, they're more feature complete, play better, their narratives aren't as obnoxious, have G-rank, don't cost as much, and seriously, it seems unless you have a REALLY high end rig, you'll have these texture issues that make the game look worse than World anyways.
If you are a MonHun fan, if you thought World and Rise simplified things too much as is, prepare for Wilds going a step above. If you did like World and Rise, I'd still wait for a sale or G-rank. It's a shame, the environments are inspired, so are the monster designs, but everything else is just worse than older titles.

Much better than at launch. Performance has improved a lot—no more texture pop-in on my end, and the game feels significantly smoother overall.

Core mechanics are heavily simplified. Focus Mode is a godsend, especially for Greatsword users, but thankfully it’s optional if you want the classic feel. The changes also make the game more approachable, especially if you’re not as fast as you used to be.

That said, the game is very forgiving in the early to mid game. The real challenge only shows up in the endgame, where things finally start to get intense.

Overall, Monster Hunter Wild is a mix of accessibility and challenge. Easier than older titles, but still has its frustrating moments—and likely more to come with future updates.

The biggest pain point I've had since day 1 is that there seems to be some kind of global cooldown or input delay after performing almost any action. As a result, the game often feels unresponsive, even when using a wired controller in offline mode. What makes this especially frustrating is that inputs are sometimes registered during the cooldown and executed afterward—but not consistently. For example, after sheathing your weapon, there’s a long period during which you can’t do anything except move. If you try to equip slinger ammo during that window, the game usually ignores the input, but occasionally it will equip the ammo after the cooldown, equip and then immediately unequip it, or equip it only for you to accidentally unequip it because the game wasn’t responding when you pressed the button. The whole system feels clunky and unreliable, and you end up overcorrecting for the game’s sluggish response time.

The camera also needs work. When using the auto lock‑on camera, it frequently gets stuck in walls, floors, and environment structures. Using an item from the radial menu while locked on always causes the camera to snap to a random angle and remain unresponsive until the item animation finishes.

Monster Hunter is easily one of my favorite franchises, and I really want to see it succeed; but it feels like this game was rushed to align with the 20th anniversary of the franchise. Capcom has released several titles recently before they were ready, and left customers to languish in bugs and performance problems.

Maybe some of these issues will be addressed in the upcoming expansion, but I’m not optimistic. Given the poor reception Wilds has received so far, I get the impression that Capcom will cut their losses after the expansion releases and move on to the next title.

Monster Hunter Wilds triumphs in its monster fights and combo-heavy gameplay, something that I rather expected after deeply enjoying the hunts from Worlds and Rise. I was a bit bummed when I learned there wasn't a new class in Wilds, but I still enjoyed picking up my comfort picks (SnS and Lance) and trying out the new combos available. The monsters were very well made and mechanically challenging across the board (i'm pretty slow at learning boss patterns). Gore Magala was probably the most insane fight to me, the first one that I had a lot of trouble dealing with. But besides that, the game was overall great. The progression from low to high rank felt good. The jewel system was familiar and fun to min-max. Farming out gear sets felt rewarding and not too tedious. And the glam system was well-made and much appreciated.

On the flip side, I do have my personal complaints about the game. First, I wish Capcom made a bigger leap with multiplayer lobbies and progression. One of my biggest gripes with Worlds and Rise was that multiplayer/coop was NOT seamless, as I need to constantly invite friends and then remove and repeat if we were to progress through the story together. It seems like this style of choppy progression is what stayed in Wilds, and I personally feel it breaking my immersion (especially when a cutscene ends with the new monster aggro'd onto you and THEN the coop flare becomes available ugh). Additionally, despite the game looking visually better, I had a lot of performance issues running this game on a high-spec PC. Even on mid-low settings, I would receive lag spikes and coop displacement, which kind of sucks. I would rather the game look the way it did in World/Rise and run smoother rather than Capcom trying to achieve the highest graphics.

For now, I'll be waiting for the rest of the title updates and expected expansion. There's obviously no real endgame right now, so I haven't spent too much time grinding out jewels and pushing my rank. I hope they can release something repeatable and rewarding like the Guiding Lands from Worlds. But yea, overall great release. Some things could be better but the game does well at what it does, and I thoroughly enjoyed my time with it.

My Thoughts on Monster Hunter Wilds

Monster Hunter Wilds has found a special place in my heart, even if it leaves a slightly sour aftertaste. The main culprit? Its clunky and overly complicated multiplayer system. Playing with friends shouldn’t feel like solving an ancient riddle or requiring divine intervention, yet somehow it does. Half the time you’re struggling to connect, the other half you’re getting disconnected for no clear reason. It can test your patience more than any monster ever could.

That said, the core of the game is something I truly love.

The hunts, the journey, the steady climb from struggle to accomplishment — that’s where the game shines. Every victory feels earned. There’s something incredibly satisfying about taking down a powerful monster after a long, punishing fight. The kind where you’re barely standing, your resources are gone, and your heart is racing.

One of the funniest realizations I had was discovering that having NPCs on your team actually made the game harder. Suddenly everything clicked. “So that’s why it felt like I was playing on nightmare difficulty!” And yet… I kept doing it. Not because it was harder, but because I loved the challenge. That thrill of not knowing whether I’d succeed or end up getting dragged back to camp by my Palico never gets old.

In a strange way, I’ve come to prefer these solo adventures. Just me, my trusty Palico, and companions who struggle alongside me without questioning every decision. Every win feels personal. Every loss feels like a lesson.

Still, I can’t help but wish the multiplayer experience were smoother and more accessible. If they ever simplify it, the game could reach an entirely new level.

Until then, my advice is simple: give it a chance. It’s not as bad as some people say. Just because something is difficult doesn’t mean it’s unbeatable. Every monster can fall — I’m living proof of that. I made it through even the toughest encounters, unknowingly increasing the difficulty, and still came out on top.

And honestly? That makes the journey even better.

[h2]Yikes! That monster hunt was wild, right Alma?[/h2]

Now, I know what you're thinking: what could a man possibly hope to gain by spending so many hours of his short, pathetic life playing a game he doesn't care for in the slightest? Why, nothing, dear reader. Nothing but the satisfaction of knowing his integrity as a hobbyist hater has been preserved. Indeed, I'm not one to simply rag on games - nay! I dedicate my life to playing them, studying them, completing them so I can hate on them more thoroughly! For you see, I fancy myself a bit of an ecologist. Much like substances and the U.S. government, I believe that each and every game franchise must have been put on this Earth by their developers in order to fill their own wonderfully unique niche. Have you an inkling of where I might be going with this? What game but Monster Hunter should I play to feel like a monster hunter? Can't answer? I'll tell you: it isn't Bayonetta.

I get that most people here had never heard of this series before World, and I'll be the first one to admit that the old titles were anything but perfect. I'll also mention that I did actually enjoy my time in the Forbidden Lands somewhat. Call it Stockholm Syndrome if you like, but if only Steam allowed for mixed reviews, I might have been more lenient. That said, the fact that Capcom made me miss Freedom Unite of all games is a matter of slight concern. Maybe I'm getting old, maybe I'm just burnt out on this series… but, by the time I was reaching the end of my High Rank journey, one specific question kept buzzing around my head, inhibiting my enjoyment of the experience like the proverbially ill-placed Vespoid:

[i]If you spend seven years of your life crafting a big, beautiful video game world, then design all the mechanics around having to interact with it as little and as briefly as possible… what is the point of it all?[/i]

To look pretty? Sure, the game looks pretty, at least for the mathematically-minded among us. I'm sure glad I can count each individual pore on Gemma's nose as she stares at me, expressionless, because we blew our entire animation budget on the low rank cinematics. And the voice acting, dear Gog. I ended up switching the audio to Brazilian Portuguese so I could focus on reading the subtitles. I can't quite put my finger on it, but something about this generation doesn't look right to me. It's less tribal fantasy, more Netflix remake. Perhaps Rise had the right idea.

What about the fights? I'll confirm that the combat does feel smooth as butter, as least if you play Greatsword like I do. Focus mode might be a little too forgiving, but the end-game monsters were issued with a crate of compensatory Dash Juice, so it evens out. The other weapons might not have been so lucky in this department, but overall, balancing seems consistent with its previous iteration: Longsword is good at everything, Bow is a legal cheat code, and only friendless nerds play the Light Bowgun. Hammer mains hang somewhere near the corner, doing the Soulja Boy. If it ain't broke, don't fix it, as they say. It would have been nice if the environmental designers followed this same philosophy. Remember all the little slopes and vines and ledges and walls you can run on? When was the last time you pulled off a slide attack? Mine was in 2024.

The levels were a stride and a half in the wrong direction. I can look back to my stay in the Plains with some fondness, though I cannot say the same for the rest of the game's locales, which I found aggressively drab and uninspired. This feeling only doubles when the Fallow season arrives, and triples when I'm forced to hunt in the Oilwell Basin because of yet another giant ape. Man, some of these maps suck. I grit my teeth and shoulder bash through the most boring volcanic area I've ever seen, only to be rewarded with a glorified corridor with a black-and-white filter tacked on top of it. I get it, guys, it's a helix, a DNA strand. Very clever. It's still an awful map. And you know what makes these maps even more awful? The fact that you need your mount to traverse them. Great. We sacrificed the complexity of World's Great Forest for a taxi on legs.

The reason I'm being so harsh isn't that the game is bad, it's because it isn't the game I want it to be, and I bet there's a subset of players out there who share a similar opinion. It's like every single wish we had over the years had been filtered through some weird monkey's paw-shaped self-pleasuring device which run on arcade coins. We wanted our paintballs back, so paintballs are back, but the monster's location is always displayed on your screen, so they're pointless; We wanted a better story, so we got a marginally better story, with high-fidelity cinematics that we still can't experience alongside another person. We wanted our crafted gear to stay relevant, so we got, like, four different gacha roulettes based on killing the same three monsters. The game is an effigy of completely unrelated mechanics in the shape of the old Monster Hunters. Innovation and convenience above all, like watching your favourite flower patch being turned into a parking lot. A game with great fighting and not much else. And where does that leave us, the ones who liked the hunting part of the game? The part where you pack your whetstones, set off into the unknown and get robbed by a bunch of cats? That'd be the door, old timer. And swing by the gift shop, while you're at it.

When I first started the franchise at MHWorlds, and subsequently moving onto MHWilds, the core essence of gameplay was simply about tracking and slaying massive monsters. However, as the hours piled up, I came to a realization. You are moving through an environment that doesn't revolve around you. Adapting rather than controlling, learning rather than conquering. The endless cycle of hunts started to feel less like progress and more like a reflection on effort, failure, and persistence. Somewhere along the way, it slowly and quietly flips the premise on you: The hardest and most destructive of monsters isn't the one roaring in front of you but the one within. Your impatience, arrogance, and your need to dominate in the cloud of frustration as you battle. In that moment, the hunts stop being about victory. But rather, something closer to self-destruction.

Let me start off saying this is not a great Monster Hunter game, as it suffers from a lot of problems. Let me get out of the way what is good...

The combat is fluid and what you would expect from a MH game. It's probably on par with Rise, maybe a little easier because they both simplified things by removing the wirebug, and gave you more opportunities to punish monsters without getting hurt via the wound system. However, I do miss the wirebug...

The game is also beautiful, but it suffers big time from texture issues. Whether that's texture streaming problems that cause hi-res texture pop-in at jarring times, or just simply unacceptable low-res textures when your texture settings are set to high, even with the high res texture pack. There's also strange stuttering issues that I encountered where, while my framerate was high, I was getting these stuttery drops, especially in the forest region. Turns out some people need to disable ReBAR, which is generally a performance enhancing setting. Very annoying.

Moving on from graphics, but related, is the game performance. Yes, the devs have made strides to make the game playable. No, it's still not an easily accessible game without a higher end video card. I'm running a Radeon 9070 and am only getting good framerates on mostly high graphics settings when I run FSR4 quality. If I try to run Native AA it's fairly playable but can get a bit low framerate at times, but at native resolution? No way. Thankfully FSR4 and DLSS4 work well. I'm still rocking a $700 video card whereas most people are not.

Next is the monsters, and unfortunately this is where things start going south. There's a really interesting variety of monsters...in low rank...that the story pushes you to rapidly go through. Wilds has twice as many low rank monsters as Rise or Worlds. While some are very interesting, like Lala (the spider), there's nothing that incentivizes you to explore, take on optional quests, or do anything but rush to high rank. Fair, almost, because high rank is where more of the game content is locked, but please don't rush me. It's irritating.

Once you do get to high rank, suddenly everything is a rehash from Rise, Worlds, or prior iterations, but they're "guardian" versions! Totally different, and not just blue! Sarcasm aside, I was disappointed. Still crashing after a year – unacceptable

I really wanted to love Monster Hunter Wilds. I’ve been a fan since Monster Hunter: World, and I was genuinely excited for this release. But after nearly a year, the game is still in a barely functional state when it comes to performance and stability.

The biggest issue is the constant crashes with the “Fatal D3D error.” This isn’t some rare edge case — it’s a widespread problem affecting many players, including those with high-end PCs. And it’s been around for months with no real fix.

I’ve tried everything:

- Reinstalling the game
- Rolling back to recommended AMD drivers AND updating to the latest ones
- Disabling frame generation, using lossless scaling, running native
- Clearing shader cache and rebuilding it from scratch
- BIOS updates, SSD health checks, RAM tuning (XMP), BAR/SAM checks
- Pagefile adjustments, DDU clean installs for GPU drivers
- Updating chipset drivers
- Lowering VRAM usage in game
- Closing background apps / disabling hardware acceleration

Nothing works. The crashes persist.

At this point, launching the game feels like a gamble. Going into a hunt knowing there’s a high chance it’ll crash and waste 10–20 minutes of progress completely kills the experience. It actively discourages me from playing.

What’s most frustrating is that after a full year of updates, this is the state the game is still in. Yes, there have been “performance improvements,” but if the game still crashes this frequently, then it’s simply not good enough.

I want to support this series. I want to enjoy this game. But right now, it feels like an unfinished product that was pushed out too early and never fully fixed.

At this point, I’ve lost faith — not just in this release, but in the direction of the series as a whole.

Now that all the content updates have been released, meaning the base game is effectively complete, it felt like a good time to revisit my thoughts on Wilds' base game. For some context, I've been playing Monster Hunter games since 3U released in 2013, and the first ~230 hours of my playtime on Wilds were built up in the initial two months after launch.

To quickly summarize my most important thoughts: I think this is the strongest High Rank game we've ever had in the mainline series and one of the strongest High Rank experiences in the franchise. The monster roster is very good and the gameplay feels like a significant improvement from its World/Iceborne roots, which are the two most important parts of a Monster Hunter game. However, even for a High Rank game, it does feel abnormally easy until you start hitting the endgame (HR50+), where it ramps up and starts feeling much better. Performance is acceptable in its current state, though it still leaves a lot to be desired considering the level of fidelity on display. The main story is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, the prodigal child it revolves around is annoying, and there's several lengthy auto-walking segments that always drag the pacing to a crawl - but that all becomes irrelevant once you finish Low Rank (which should take you 15-20 hours). You suffer through it once and then basically never worry about it again.

The game is in a fairly good spot now. While I always hesitate to recommend any $70 game at its full price, I think that most of the potential for buyers' remorse has been eliminated by now, and if you're comfortable paying that much then you won't regret it. If you can get the game on any kind of discount then it's much easier to endorse. Unless these sorts of grind-focused action games just aren't something you get along with, I guess.

I'm eagerly awaiting the G Rank expansion. If Capcom can come back with a roster of additions that's a similar size and quality level to that of the base game, then Wilds will probably dethrone GU as my favorite title in the franchise. But that remains to be seen.

If that's enough information for you, great. If not, here's some additional detail for each of the points I mentioned above. 

[h3]Gameplay[/h3]
This feels like a refined and more responsive version of World/Iceborne, which makes sense as the two entries share a director in Yuya Tokuda. In many ways, Wilds is like a spiritual successor to World. I main Hunting Horn, and in my opinion this is easily the best iteration of the weapon we've ever had. It restores the emphasis on positioning and predictive play that had fallen by the wayside in Rise/Sunbreak, while doing a better job of incentivizing aggressive play than previous entries. I've also logged a few dozen hunts with most of the other weapons, and those too feel like improved versions of their World counterparts. Safe to say that unless the only way you could have fun was spamming SAEDs with Charge Blade, you'll probably be happy with how your main feels right now.

As I said before, I think the monster roster is very strong. That isn't to say that there are no weak links; rather, that even the most forgettable fights are still enjoyable in the moment. There aren't any true stinkers in the game right now, no equivalent to World's Lavasioth or Rise's Almudron. Nothing so unfun or annoying that it makes you want to stop playing for the day after a handful of hunts. For me, the closest thing to that is the returning siege monster you'll find after reaching the endgame. It's another one of the heavily scripted pseudo-raid fights that World became somewhat infamous for, and it just makes me sad. The original version of the fight wasn't exactly exciting either, but loading the cannons and activating various arena traps was still more enjoyable than any of the bespoke mechanics created for Wilds' rendition of it.

Around launch, the game received a lot of criticism for being too easy, and it's not entirely unfounded. Even though this is just a High Rank game and those are always on the easier side, the power delta between your hunter and the various monsters is much higher than it's been in the past. Focus Mode gives you the ability to freely aim all of your attacks with very little downside, meaning that mistakes are punished less harshly. The wound mechanic allows smart players to chain flinches and knockdowns together in ways that we've never been able to before. The effect is frankly comical on some monsters. And wounds are very easy to exploit as you progress through the game, it isn't until the endgame hunts that wounds become noticeably harder to create. I made it all the way to HR50 before failing a quest for the first time, and my weapon has historically been the worst out of all melee options.

Outside of the 9- and 10-star endgame hunts, which are properly hard, the difficulty just isn't going to be there until G Rank comes out. If you want a hard Monster Hunter experience right now, the Risen Elders and high-level Anomaly fights in Sunbreak are your best bet. You could also go load up GU's Hypers or EX Deviants. Hell, if you really get off on putting your genitals in a bench vice then go look up how to play on a Frontier private server. I've done all of those and had a blast in each of them.

The weakest aspect here is how large some of the maps are while not having enough points of interest to justify the size. Just flat open spaces for the sake of encouraging you to ride your mount. It's also annoying that several shortcuts between zones are inaccessible if you're on foot, particularly in the early maps. The game conditions you to enable full self-driving, and that makes it harder for you to feel connected to the world like learning maps in previous games could.

[h3]Story[/h3]
I don't have much more to add here aside from it being ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ and feeling like a slog because of the regular autoscrolling segments. The story in a MH game being bad shouldn't surprise anyone who's played one before, it's always been an afterthought and it wasn't until World that they tried upping the production value, but this one really left a bad taste in my mouth. The prodigal child it's supposed to revolve around is distinctly unlikable and annoying every time he's on screen. The main story beats are nonsensical and the conclusion is very underwhelming. Thankfully you only have to do it once, and it doesn't take very long to get through (despite how it feels at times).

[h3]Performance[/h3]
Most areas of the game run acceptably in their current state. I play at 1440p and average 60-65 FPS on mostly High settings with FSR running in Native Anti-Aliasing mode, however some parts still drop into the low 50s or even mid 40s. One thing to note about my specs is that my 6800XT is overclocked to 2.5GHz, so if you have the same card then your performance may be a bit worse than mine. The heavier parts of the game still seem CPU-bound so it might not make a huge difference either. Fact is, the RE engine doesn't cut it for open-world games, which is something that Capcom has known for some time. They couldn't get the upgraded REX engine ready in time, and having to backport a ton of work to the RE engine was one of the biggest contributors to the game's myriad of issues at release. Hopefully REX will be ready for MH7 (or even Portable 6th).

Even on a mix of High and Highest settings, including the optional high-res texture pack, the game really doesn't look good enough to justify being so hard to run. It's certainly [i]an[/i] upgrade over World, but it's not a huge one in terms of actual visual fidelity. Fur looks much better, textures are noticeably sharper, characters and environments are more detailed... however there's still a certain blurriness to the presentation even with all the usual suspects disabled (upscaling, motion blur, TAA, volumetric fog, DoF, etc). You won't always notice it but it sticks out every time you do.

[h1] Game’s release [/h1]
Bad optimization, no gathering hub, no arenas, we have 4 now, 2 of them being Akuma quests, no final boss refight, horrible weapon balance on release. I could go on, but the point is they released a laughably unfinished game and drip feed us the rest of it for a whole year
World also had TUs, but those were clearly post launch content and not a way to cash in on an early access. Rise at least they had the COVID excuse and actually optimizing RE Engine to run on the switch 
Wilds just doesn’t have that excuse

[h1] Combat [/h1]
We have way too many OP mechanics
Focus mode not only makes positioning, one of the core elements of MH, much less relevant. You even gain reach and magnet to the monster if you’re far away from it.
Wounds are just free damage with CC on top
Offsets are busted too, free damage windows on many weapons, and it completely ruined Greatsword. No old hit and run playstyle, no satisfying True charged slash, it feels like your only option against many endgame monsters is spamming offset attacks and lvl1 and 2 charges
But combat can be fun. The roster is probably the best one a base game has ever had, power clashes are cool, predicting and offsetting a monster with Switch Axe or Hammer feels satisfying, and some weapons like Dual Blades or Lance feel better than ever. 
But for every fun Dual Blades quest you do, you have a bad time playing with the horrible Insect Glaive Control scheme. For every fight where offset feels rewarding, you are forced to play the most unfun Greatsword iteration ever. And while sometimes a mechanic like wounds can work during an endgame hunt due to Capcom making monsters more resistant to them, it completely trivializes every single quest below 8 stars tempered fights

[h1] Non-Combat [/h1]
Just awful. There is no reason to really explore the maps except for setting up camps
Something I really loved about the newer entries is how you could take a break from all the hunting and do some more chill activities, World had canteen ingredients gathering, Tailraiders quests and shortcuts discovery. Rise made use of the wirebug mobility and while that game is more arcadey, they even had some of the Relic records hidden behind some platforming, and both had endemic life collecting
But Wilds has nothing of the sort. Instead they designed maps to be traversed with the Seikret, that are way vertical which makes fast travelling to different camps incredibly annoying,way too narrow which makes fighting there a claustrophobic mess, and don’t require you to learn them since you always know where the monster is
For a mainline entry, which supposedly pushes the original vision of the series such as ecology and immersion instead of focusing solely on combat, has these aspects as barebones and non-existent as they’ve ever been
The game looks appalling. 4 screenshots during the plenty season is not how the game looks most of the time. There are seasons that make the game look like different shades of blue or brown or will make the performance fall off a cliff
I don’t care if they’re going for a more realistic color palette or if it’s the way they want the game to look. It looks horrible. I’ve had to play with a Reshade preset since almost the beginning of the game, and the one time I had to deactivate it for TU3 just cemented my opinion. The game looks washed, ugly and the graphics don’t justify the terrible performance, even 1 full year after release

[h1] Progression [/h1]
Easiest Monster Hunter I’ve played by a mile while going through the story. That in itself is not a negative per se, Rise’s village had significantly easier monsters than in the Guild Hall quests, while ramping up the challenge in High Rank (Tobi Kadachi, Mizutsune and Apex Arzuros). I can excuse the opening hours of the game being less challenging to make it more accessible to new players, and I can forgive the story being streamlined so those new players stick with the game until the ending -That is if there is more to do after that’s over
Old gen games weren’t designed in a way to keep new players engaged until they saw the ending credits. The progression had much more friction, you hit walls and had to stop and farm a new set or weapon, and once you overcame those challenges there wasn’t that much to do in the endgame except hunting talismans for RNG upgrades. Compared to Wilds, you might even say old gen had little to do post credits. But the point is that getting to the endgame was the grind, and in newer titles the endgame is the grind
Or so it’s supposed to be
In old titles, hunts were much more methodical and slow, rewarding good positioning and monster knowledge
Grinding was slower and you could not steamroll the mandatory quests / story. Monsters like FU Tigrex being a pain in the ass without flash bombs, Gravios is insufferable without bombs to break its stomach, Diablos has always been annoying without sonic bombs, Plesioth is terrible but has low hp, you could bring traps to kill it quickly or bring a weapon with a shield for that fight, Rajang would DESTROY you unless you were constantly positioning yourself to his right to avoid its Zig Zag attack, I could go on
These hurdles were taken into account by the team. The old games did not rush you to the final boss, you had to take your time progressing through the game, you had to properly prepare for difficult quests. The amount of things you need to do to unlock the 3 G rank Fatalis in FU is not a problem to be solved or a design philosophy to be fixed. That process is the game
Newer entries instead choose to streamline that process to make it more accessible to new players, and oldheads have been complaining about it since World. But it doesn’t have to be a bad thing, as long as the endgame does have those elements. Wilds doesn’t have that

[h1] Endgame [/h1]
It sucks. To me, endgames in base versions have never been that important
I never really liked talisman grind, and World’s tempered elder farming was bad, however I would expect the MH team to learn and improve on those systems. I cannot believe they looked at Iceborne’s Safi/Kulve weapons, and Sunbreak’s Qurious crafting and decided to double down and make us waste our time playing Menuing Hunter all over again.They could’ve taken notes on how Qurious crafting at least had us targeting every monster in the roster to farm for specific parts, but they didn’t even bother doing that
Instead, we got Gogmazios, an insanely scripted fight where you spend half the quest running and the other half shooting at it instead of actually fighting the monster head on. All of that so you could add more layers of RNG and menuing to the Artian system while being bombarded by tutorials that you cannot disable while doing the quest thanks to tourists crying with Alatreon,when all you needed to do was read the tutorial they throw right to your face, spend some time preparing, and learn the fight

[h1] Conclusion [/h1]
I’ve tried hard to like this game-And for a time, I did
Lagiacrus’s return brought back memories from my introduction to the series on the Wii
Omega made me properly prepare like I used to do on the PSP
Arch-tempered Arkveld felt like the properly challenging endgame hunt this game was desperately missing
Proof of a hero being a quest that actually took time and effort to complete

But then I boot up the game to do the final quest in the game
I load into a completely grey map
I ride my unengaging Uber and wait for it to take me to the monster
I start getting the worst stutters I’ve ever gotten playing this game and remember this game is still insanely unoptimized
I use my focus strike to do the pretty animation and get a completely free opening

If they manage to fix this mess of a game with a DLC, I might even consider playing it- After they release the final TU and the early access ends, that is

In the meantime, I will pray Capcom and the other team are taking notes so they can release a finished MH game I can enjoy

Its been over a year since the game came out and oh boy did Capcom mess up...

The Good ;
- Gameplay is still solid
- Best weapon design of any of the Monster Hunter games, each weapon feels better than ever and the wound system is fun and super rewarding. An extreme step in the right direction on this front.
- Automatic part drop pick up, god this is amazing and also bypassed the artificial drop limit of previous games. There is a cap on how many drops you can get but it still requires you to constantly make and break wounds but its so rewarding and fun.


The Bad ; (Basically everything else)

- Poor Optimization that breaks the game with every other update to it on both console and PC regardless of low end or high end specs, most likely almost every single player has run into an issue of some kind. Not only are the crashing and graphical issues know about and extremely common they still can't fix them.
- In built DRM that literally breaks the game to this day and doesnt actually stop players from editing their game files in the first place to say give them infinite levels and money or give them all the weapon unlocks as you can just edit your save game data. Still see a player or two literally just one shot a monster in multiplayer, its rare but it happens.
- Constant weird graphical bugs render characters/monsters into random polygons and it takes them a while to get their model loaded in an the textures correctly placed. But god its funny going from 2025 era graphics to ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ PS1 after one load screen.
- $70 price tag, while this game is still receiving active updates as all recent titles he amount of content doesnt justify this especially in the economy. Are you gonna get your money out of it if you like MH? Of course, is it worth it? No.
- Story is rather hamfisted and seemingly an afterthought, its not the worst but its certainly not good. I get wanting to add a narrative to the game to push things along and introduce new zones. The gameplay loop has remained the same since the very beginning of the series, its a main drive and why the series has stood the test of time. We as players wanna bonk big monsters
- Progression seemed rather clunky compared to previous titles, I rarely had to upgrade anything except when going into Arch-Tempered. The game sent from a flat curve in difficulty to a rather sharp spike, which happens when getting into the tougher sections of the game but the drop rates for items seemed far more forgiving and upgrades themselves felt rather meaningless. You got materials either too early or too late compared to their point in the upgrade progression. Some drop rates just completely overdid other chances like Gems/Plates sharing the same table but one was basically guaranteed over the other based on the level of the monster so farming something lower was actually more beneficial. Also whatever the ♥♥♥♥ they did to the armor sphere system was really dumb
- The lack of actual weaponry across the board was supplemented by the Artian system, which was good on paper. Get the element and the stat type you want. You give up the innate skill for more raw power, but once you start farming those materials you basically only use Artian weapons as they are just better stat wise and nearly fully customize-able. They dont fill the gaps, they replace everything and make basically every monster drop obsolete the moment you can make Rarity 8s and can just farm the parts.
- The jewel system seemed to get a rather heavy downgrade, there is too many skills now and they made a hard split between weapon and armor jeweling making it near impossible to hit certain thresholds or go into something like a full crit build. Its not impossible, just harder to min/max. If I wanna go glass cannon just let me do it instead of artificially restricting me on top of needing to craft/earn many many many more jewels because of the dual skill system making certain tier 3 jewels required for your builds (and bow/ammunition gems seemed way more common than others, could just be RNG bias speaking)
- Pop-up camp system is a direct downgrade to previous camp systems. Having to pick an choose where to put camps has never been an issue as they are clearly laid out in previous titles but in this game you dont just put up camps in safe areas like normal there are risky camps that can literally be destroyed by any passing monster or a random turf war you didnt see while on the other side of the map. There are still highly safe camps that are untouchable but the other roughly half of the camps can be wrecked wasting points and time. Customizing them is neat though.



I enjoy the Monster Hunter series a lot, been playing since 3 and even went back to play 2 and 1 just to see how the game has changed over the years. Ive got several thousands of hours in the series and want to see this game succeed. I cant call this a bad Monster Hunter game as WHEN IT WORKS the game is fun and probably the best by far gameplay wise there is in the series. However the rest of the game outside of the actual gameplay loop is either a side-grade or downgrade to previous versions.

I cannot in good faith recommend this game to anyone mainly because of the price tag and the serious issues with the game crashing constantly. Trying to hard push the game engine and graphics to the absolute limit was an extreme mistake and not taking the time to actually fix the ♥♥♥♥ and optimize it before launch was an even bigger one. The forced Anti-cheat (that literally doesnt work) was dumb. The "pay-to-win" aspect where owning all the DLC made your game load faster was insane (that got fixed thankfully). They put both the mobile and main team on this project and yet still rushed them to push out a product instead of giving it some time to cook. There was good ideas here but it feels like they just didnt have time to actually finish and polish the game in the slightest.

Save your money, but Rise/World instead as people still play them, they are 'completed' and arent a technological nightmare to your system.

Let me preface, I absolutely love Monster Hunter.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a fun time, especially if you’re already into the Monster Hunter games. The world feels bigger and more alive than before, with dynamic environments and monsters that actually interact with each other in cool ways. Hunting is still super satisfying. Tracking something down, learning its patterns, and finally taking it out never really gets old. When everything clicks, it’s great.

That said, the game is WAY easier than its previous entries. Performance can be hit or miss too, which kind of pulls you out of the experience, although they have made MAJOR improvements to it. It’s still a solid game and worth playing if you like the series, but it doesn’t feel like a huge leap forward. Overall, I’d give it a 7/10.

Monster Hunter Wilds still runs like garbage on PC even after a year, and the grind just feels repetitive.

Meanwhile, Elden Ring Nightreign is better in literally every way — more chaotic, co-op is actually addictive instead of repetitive, and somehow it’s more fun getting absolutely destroyed together.

Monster Hunter Wilds delivers a very enjoyable experience, especially for fans of the series. The story is surprisingly engaging and adds more depth to the overall adventure, making your journey feel more meaningful.

The combat is absolutely fantastic. Fights are intense, well-designed, and incredibly satisfying. Each encounter feels unique, and mastering different weapons and strategies keeps the gameplay fresh and exciting.

Another great aspect of the game is the constant updates. The developers continue to support the game with new content, which keeps players coming back and ensures there’s always something new to experience.

Performance on PC is also very good. The game runs smoothly, providing a stable and polished experience that enhances both exploration and combat.

Overall, Monster Hunter Wilds is a great game with an engaging story, amazing combat, ongoing support, and strong performance on PC.

Absolute potential

Fully recommend this game. Right now it’s in a really good state, and the devs did a great job with optimization.

It feels like an upgraded version of World where almost all the monsters feel unique and fun to hunt. The maps are really immersive, and it’s a big jump compared to World/Rise.

But… even though all that is true, the game also has some downsides. At least for me (LS main), the game still feels clunky in a way, similar to how World did. The combos are boring and it often feels like you’re just spamming the same attack over and over. Also, the autopilot of the Seikret can ruin the exploration since it's a no-brainer mechanic. It’s a shame that, with such complex and well-designed maps, they ruin it like that.

It doesn’t have the same level of dynamism that Rise has to offer with LS, where you have a lot of combos to do, skills to choose from, and more entertaining gameplay overall. Of course, this applies mainly to LS and maybe some other weapons (I don’t know, since I haven’t played any others), but it could be the opposite for some players.

I have a friend who mains DBs, and he tells me the opposite: in Rise he spams the same attack, while in Wilds he does crazy combos and the gameplay feels much more dynamic. I know Capcom can’t make everyone happy or make all 14 weapons perfect, but I hope this gets improved in a DLC.

Other than that, the fights are pretty good and enjoyable. Maybe there are some monsters that spam attacks that one-shot you, but that’s just bad design and it only happens in the endgame. About 95% of the fights are really good.

Overall, I recommend this game to both new players and veterans. It will only get better over time with updates and DLC, and it has the potential to become the best MH ever.

I do not recommend this game not because the graphics quality and watered down gameplay which a lot of people already mentioned. My main disatisfiers are (1) Wasteful vast world design and (2) Forced story. 

Wilds environment is really huge, I won't deny that. But all of the vastness are just "extended distance/Highways" so Seikret can fit the gameplay. The environment design is also bland, especially wyveria as the "forgotten ancient civilization." Personally, I feel more awe with old gen "Ancient Tower." You don't need high end graphics to depict a long vanished ancient civilization and a forced storyline, just use the "show don't tell" technique and NPCs' speculations.

Personally, I feel that the worldbuilding doesn't fit Monster Hunter. I thought "The Everstream" in World was absurd but this "Dragontorch" and "Wylk" is even more absurd. Maybe future DLC(s?) will uncover this narrative problem. 

With that "Wylk," "Dragontorch," and "Everforge" not even a single tribe know how to make a weapon, defensive structures, even a counter-attack strategy against all those dangerous monster? Human is not that naive. In an environment that harsh, they are more likely to be aggressive and unforgiving. They can even wage war against each other. Just take a look on history of civilizations and religions: Brutality united in separated Identities. Maybe the Monster Hunter want to write Human as a benevolent race. If that's true, then what is the point of writing "Wyveria's Sin (Zoh Shia)" if not of the innate brutal nature of human race as animal (or Monster in this game's context... maybe)?

The storyline is even more absurd. That kid MC... after he had witnessed his tribe almost being annihilated by Arkveld and how brutal Arkveld was, he still persisted on letting Arkveld live freely and "Be Strong"??? This is top tier naivety. How do these tribes even survived prior to the expedition teams (The Hunter) arrival?

More Personal and Biased Comment: Arkveld design is bad, Rey Dau fits more as the primary flagship monster. Some OSTs are decent, but not that memorable. Character creation is much better than World but what's with that "Body Type" nonsense when we are playing a fantasy game with big consideration of Biology?? Just say it as plain as Male and Female. Gameplay 5/7

My biggest gripe is the lack of gated progress from Low Rank to High Rank, the game is not teaching "bads"/pugs how to play and they are relying far too much own meta speed run builds when they are clearly not competent enough.

Add in Player Best Times, DPS Meters, and ELO. Greatly reduce the need for HR### or add proper skill gates, I had near perfect gear in HR400s and have pugged with HR900+ that are terrible.

UI 2/7
Menu design and hotkeys are pretty bad. The inherited lag is noticeable in menus too. Pretty poor menu design and interactivity overall. You can test this yourself  when the ship arrives: F > D > F as fast you can, you'll notice it just doesn't respond.

Music 4/7
When it hits it hits, as with most games, just listen to your own playlist.

Story 3/7
It's a monster hunter game, save the world by eliminating ecological outliers

OVERALL 4.2/7 (weighted)
Gameplay/combat will always be king. The game is still better than most soulslikes - if you disagree, get good at street fighter first.

Combat 10/10

After the updates this game is my favorite in the series besides for GU. They really nailed the combat in this one although I do miss the verticality and wirebug mechanics from rise, but it's monster hunter so i appreciate them trying new things in each installation. With the recent addition of the 10 star monsters the difficulty has spiked by a lot so it gives me hope for the expansion and future of the game. 

Story 3/10

They need to just make the story focused on the hunter and not some reject from a PETA ad campaign.

Positives:

-Fighting is better than MHWorld, tho it is not as fluid and fun as Rise
-Weapon and Armor designs is as good if not better as one knows it from MH


Negatives:

-Runs incredibly bad, needed Frame Generation to make it playable (RTX 3060 Ti)
-Most Monster designs are not memorable, especially early Monster have no real character
-Progression, especially in the late game feels incredibly bad, since you can easily craft the highest rank weapons, but Armor spheres are very scarce, which makes later fights where higher Defense is necessary to make a fight playable gatekept unless you have the right weapon matchup / are willing to die dozens of times to learn the moveset perfectly
-Story is even worse than old MH titles
-Multiiplayer is a huge setback from Rise and even worse than world which is absolutely crazy to say
-Charms AND Decorations are randomized, which makes people who want to go for perfect builds depend on pure luck for the perfect equipment pieces
-Environments are uninteresting and there is no incentive to go exploring
-The mount is very out of place since most maps are more cramped than Rise, where the dog was very nice to have for mobility. The Seitret often times just limits your ability to move
-Lots of systems are now compromised like Palico gathering and cooking meals
-etc. i could continue this list but i will stop now

All in all most Monster fights are fun and feel nice to play. If that's what you want, you get it.
 
If you want anything else from this game like:
Exploration, good perforamce, good graphics (looks a LOT worse than world and in my opinion even worse than Rise which enitially released on the Nintendo Switch), and play the story together with your friends - then this is not your game and I highly suggest getting MHRise or MHWorld as they are better games.

I didn't want to like this game and I do have some design gripes:
~I like the idea of memorizing the maps and researching the quarry. I don't like the idea of your new mount, the seikret, ferrying you everywhere on an automated path. You don't even have to worry when the monster changes zones because you can just whistle, leave to refill your coffee. No need to figure out where the monster sleeps or what it eats- you just stay on it's tail.
~I think there maybe are a few too many tools for the hunters to use. I don't really like the wound system. It takes you out of regular gameplay to jiggle the camera around and find glowing red dots. Simple weapons got a bit more complicated. I'm used to 1 gauge with switch-axe but there's two now. (Kind of.) 
~It seems like there are are mechanics that are completely useless that they were obliged to carry over from previous games. Having the psychic knowledge of knowing where every monster is on the map is overwhelming means the research-to-tracking system obsolete. You can still analyze the occasional footprint for a measly 10 ecology points. There's no point in crafting items in preparation for a hunt anymore. Hungry? Pull a bug off the wall to eat. Low on health? Pull a green bug off the wall to eat. Cold? Pull a red bug off the wall to eat. Hot? Pull a blue bug off the wall to eat. At this point why have survival elements in the game at all? It over-complicates things.
~I think the game looks bad. The sudo-hyperrealism is a stark change from previous monster hunter games. The game isn't nearly as colorful as it used to be and it's strictly an art-style thing. It's a shame because I thought World looked fine and Rise was a nice stet back into the previous monster-hunter art style.

...however...

Despite my complaints there is still a *lot* to like about this game:
~The new open-world design of the game lends itself to the ecological aspect of the game. Small and large monsters migrate as they fight over territory. The did a great job making the terrain readable on the map despite the three-dimensional layout of it.
~Despite all the tools the game is still challenging. Capcom giving players more tools means they can give different kinds of obstacles. The story doesn't warn you about the next monster you'll be fighting so general preparedness is all the more important. I found myself switching to my sword and shield to cover more elemental options and having a block against hyper-aggressive monsters. I was glad that I had some tinged-meat in my inventory when a Rathalos crossed my path. This let to a lot of interesting moments to me I wouldn't have had otherwise.
~Story is good. It continues the theme of mankind needing to harmonize with nature. Characters are good. I was surprised by all the MH4 references and continuing continuity for those in the know. Dialogue and voice acting is fine. Some people have gripes about it but I don't.
~Character creation whips ass. My cat looks good as hell. My hunter looks a little less cool but that's a personal skill issue. I like that you need to earn the layered armor and that you can look cute without having to compromise stats.
~None of my gripes about graphics apply to the food visuals good god how did they render this food to look this tantalizing what the hell


All in all, despite my gripes about all the new stuff, it's still Monster Hunter. It's a good game that keeps to the theme and can still flatten me like a pancake at high rank. I hope they learn from the mistakes in this one so they can make it better in the next one.

The game has been running great since release on my rig, even moreso since they've implemented the performance improvements. Honestly a great game, as always capcom. i've played world, rise and now wilds (duh) and it's great. wilds takes all the good aspects of the two previous games, and makes it into one gorgeous game. so far no bugs. just beautiful.

With wilds they took the hunt out of monster hunt but honestly that is okay. Coming from over 200 hours on world/iceborne across steam/xbox, wilds is a more streamlined experience. With the added mount system you can traverse maps quicker and fight your target quicker. There really is no more hunting and tracking. The mount system also adds a safety net when your in a dire situation so take that as you may. Some like it some think it makes it less dangerous. You will still get totally rocked so be prepared to get carted a lot. Combat is much more fluid than previous titles and the added wound system is a nice feature. I have tried all weapons and most of them have drastically improved. Some are a bit clunky on fluidity, looking at you charged blade, but overall all the weapons can be viable just need a good build and hours of practice. The lineup of monsters is good and excited to see more when the DLC is released. For me the elder dragon fights are the weakest part of the game. Don't get me wrong they are enjoyable, but can seem too cinematic, Capcom needs to keep it to an epic fight between you and the dragon and not a script based movie fight. The build diversity is decent, once you get to endgame, the diversity lacks a bit. I hope they improve upon that in the DLC and add more (maybe more weapons?!). The transmog system (layered armor/weapons) is a nice feature, there are some great looking armor but also some terrible looking armor. Same goes for Weapon skins. Also please CAPCOM let us re color all armor pieces, some armor piece colors can't be augmented unfortunately. The story is meh but I am really not a story focused player so it doesn't bother me too much. I just want to fight monsters with cool weapons. There are areas to improve the experience for players but overall its a fun game and again more streamlined to go fight a monster or 2 and then log off. Much more friendly to a player like me with less time to play games day to day.

After hours of enduring the game's abysmal performance, I've finally given up.

I've done every fix imaginable to get this absolutely terrible, lazy, unoptimized PC port to work. Give rolled back to AMD 25.3.1, edited the registry to try and delay GPU Pipeline timeouts, disabled overlays, tried full screen, windowed and windowed fullscreen, move the game files to another SSD, checked the game files half a dozen times, and more...

The game itself is a good instalment to the franchise, overall. But my god, it should be scrapped and rebuilt for PC because this god awful port DOES NOT WORK.

The fact Capcom have acknowledged the issue and basically said "Yeah, we'll see what we can do but no guarantees" just shows how lazy and customer-averse they are.

The Pros: This game has massive improvements to the flow of combat compared to Rise and World, with far more monster variety with unique looks and attacks, more frequent unique looking weapons (compared to World), and allows for many more cool mechanics like having a secondary weapon and an improved wound system. Likewise, collecting resources is a lot faster now due to the QOL added so you can collect resources at range. The tracks and research aspect from World didn't make a return sadly, you just kind of find a monster once and then it's always discovered; however, that works in the favor of this game because the maps are huge and could take a while to find where a monster otherwise. Though few monsters are called "Elder dragon" they have numerous monsters that are the same quality, spectacle, and feel of elder dragons from previous games.
The Cons: Most of the cons are small things and not make or break reasons on whether or not to get the game. It still has some optimization issues though nowhere near as bad as before. Switching weapons can become a forgettable mechanic due to you needing to go on your mount to swap them. The game feels a lot easier compared to World and Rise due to the wound system, improved mechanics of weapons, focus system, and ability to call your mount at almost anytime to get you out of danger; though this does make it more accessible to new players, once any player starts getting a grasp of the game, it will be very easy and require little to no prep to take on each monster up until Gog and Omega -- which will hopefully change with the DLC adding harder enemies. The palico consistently likes to heal me after I've taken a health potion and healed near full: there are thankfully an equivalent to palico gadgets you can earn, but the vigorwasp delivery specifically is the bane of my existence; I add that in part because I wasn't even aware that I was able to change the actions of my palico until recently and believed that I was stuck with only the vigorwasp delivery. I'm not sure if I missed some section talking about the ability to change palico actions but I was unaware of it throughout the main story.

A fun addition to the Monster Hunter series, and many of the performance issues have smoothed out.

I say 'smoothed out' and not 'runs well': I have a 1080Ti and this game barely does a consistent 30 fps on the lowest settings, but at least it's a consistent framerate.  For the graphical fidelity of the game, however, I'd expect the resting framerate to be much higher.  Probably best if you have a 20- series or better nVidia card before picking this up.

Wow, this is a polarizing game. I think Monster Hunter Wilds suffers from so many problems, more so than any other game in this series.  Pretty much the only bright spots in this game are the gameplay and certain design elements. There is no expansion yet, so the game ends in High Rank and that's all I can review. I've finished all title update fights so I can speak on them all.

Taking notes from World, Wilds prides itself on full immersion. This time it's actually to the detriment of the game and it's reputation. Each map is a fully simulated environment where monsters can turf war, get shocked and stunned, or fight small monsters off screen. Why? This serves no purpose besides giving modders something to showcase on Youtube for clicks. In game it tanks performance and interactions not seen by the player are a waste of time. These environments, simulated all the way down to the smallest monsters, are novel but made performance on PC so bad on launch and for 8 months after that it killed the reputation of this game. Now they optimized the game a lot better, but I don't think sacrificing performance for these meaningless additions added to the game.

Gameplay wise, just like World stuff is grounded without flashy wirebugs or super attacks, although some weapons actually got some. The single biggest additions in this game are the wound system and focus mode, both of which are terrible additions and should not have even been considered for this series. The wound system is a disaster, you strike a monster's body and create spots that allow you to do special attacks with your weapon on that deal a lot of damage and usually trigger knockdowns. This is a problem because creating wounds and attacking them knocks down a monster leading to more wounds and an endless cycle of destroying the monster. Basically the entire game before updates was easy to beat and monsters got steamrolled because of this. Later on the mechanic was so bad they just made monsters stop getting wounds as often and heal themselves quicker. Terrible design concept. Next feature is focus mode, which lets you directly aim any weapon in the game. This basically kills the need for good positioning near a monster because you can hold focus mode and adjust yourself mid swing. Besides these two mechanics the game at it's core is monster hunter so it's still fun and weapons feel good to use.

Next thing I want to touch on is the new mount, the Seikret. I need to make an entire section discussing this because everything about the Seikret just sucks. The concept and the execution are just the antithesis to everything this series stands for. The Seikret can just auto navigate you to monsters. So why is there the need to make elaborate and pretty maps in the first place? You can let the Seikret walk and go on your phone. It's disengaging and a horrible idea. Another is you can use the Seikret to save you from monster attacks. However, unlike wirebugs it's frustrating and inconsistent and will get you killed a lot of the times because the damn bird runs towards the monster randomly. Awful awful awful.

Now monster roster, even this disappointed me to be honest. I think of any of the new monsters only 3 of them even impressed me and gave me a fun fight: Xu Wu, Rey Dau, and Doshaguma. The rest are either a complete mess, or uninteresting. For the updates, Mizutsune is good, Seregios is a fun dance when not tempered, and Lagiacrus for being so hyped is just not very fun and had very questionable hip check hitboxes and a atrocious underwater section. Omega is a fight I actually really enjoy, it's very technical and brutal to learn and master. Lastly, Gogmazios. It's a fun spectacle but a very boring fight.

So overall, do I think Monster Hunter Wilds is a disaster? Yes. Do I still have enjoyment of the game because it's Monster Hunter? Also yes. Do not ever pay full price for this game, get it on a cheap sale or wait for the expansion. It's just not worth it.

TL;DR: It's... a game. I can't really recommend it to newer players when other Monster Hunter games exist that I think excel in almost every way, but if you're a series veteran who wants a fresh experience it's not the worst thing ever created.

[i]"The sunk cost fallacy is our tendency to follow through with something that we’ve already invested heavily in (be it time, money, effort, or emotional energy), even when giving up is clearly a better idea."[/i] 

Monster Hunter Wilds was launched on the 28th of February 2025, a little over a year ago as of posting this review. I have waited for so long to review this game because I really just wanted to believe Capcom would lock in and get this game into a state where I could at least say that overall it's a positive experience and just leave it on a negative review purely because of the awful performance...

[b]But that day never came.[/b] 

I want to start off just quickly mentioning that when I originally drafted this review it was over 4 pages long and roughly 2000 words. I have a lot to say about this game and unfortunately it is mostly negative but I think I’ve done a good job compressing my points here.

First of all the combat. As with every other game in the franchise it is very fun and addictive to learn and master. There are a variety of new and returning tools given to players such as perfect guards, perfect evades, offsets (basically certain heavy attacks have the ability to interrupt monster actions and send them reeling backwards) and focus mode which makes aiming attacks a lot easier for melee weapons. Most of the 14 weapon types are in a pretty good spot right now, although a few of them do feel like pretty significant downgrades from previous iterations which I hope are improved upon in the expansion.

Next the campaign. This is without question the worst campaign in the franchise… A needlessly drawn out story that interrupts the action at every opportunity, long on-rails dialogue segments and pretty poor balancing that feels like it doesn’t really prepare the player for the sudden spike in difficulty at endgame. Also if you are planning on playing through this game with friends the multiplayer story experience is awful, despite Capcom claiming pre-release that they had made changes from World to ensure it was fluid… Thanks Capcom <3

The endgame itself is… interesting? Skill decorations are randomised again despite literally no-one asking for that to return, but the drop rates are far more relaxed than in World so it's not the worst thing ever. The Artian weapon system absolutely sucked at launch and is honestly even worse now, but the new Gogmazios upgrades added in Title Update 4 at least allow for some cool build variety with all the different set bonuses you can mix together. There are also randomised talismans to grind out if you are a really dedicated player, but they have very little effect on your build overall so that whole system is kind of just there.

The monsters are fantastic. A bunch of really cool new concepts like Rey Dau whose head transforms into a railgun or Jin Dahaad who is literally just a giant walking heat sink. Lots of the returning monsters also got amazing treatment with their fights in this game and it was really great to see Lagiacrus return after so long and come back so strong! (although the underwater section gets old fast…). And the “final” monster in this game, Arch Tempered Arkveld, is one of the greatest fights in the entire series. They honestly did a great job with them and I’m excited to see what they cook up for the expansion!

Finally the performance…

[b][u]It still kinda sucks…[/u][/b] 

And honestly, regardless of how much it’s been improved upon and may be improved upon in the future, the game's state at launch was atrocious and for that alone this game deserves a negative review.

Overall, I was just left feeling incredibly disappointed with this game from the moment I finished its campaign over a year ago now, and even after all the updates, events and balance adjustments the game still felt messy and unsatisfying for the vast majority of the time I spent playing it.

Despite all the negative things I have said about this game, if you have played previous entries in the series and enjoyed them, but have maybe run out of things to do or gotten burnt out, this game does have a sizable chunk of content and there is… [i]something…[/i] in between all of the awful decisions. If you are a new player interested in the series, I would wholeheartedly recommend looking into both Rise and World and deciding which one looks more your style because both are spectacular games and have even greater expansions waiting for you if you enjoy them!

Thank you for reading! :D

Overall I can give this game a solid recommendation for anyone interested in the franchise. Unlike many of it's predecessors, Wilds actually had an endgame at launch, and Capcom has done a great job expanding it. It has hundreds of hours of content with solid combat and great monster designs.

The performance used to be poor for most folk, I never had a lot of issues personally but after a recent patch I'm seeing noticeably higher and consistent framerates which I can imagine is especially fantastic for lower end machines. Capcom was committed to fixing the performance issues and I commend them for that, even if it took them around half a year.

Now for my negatives. This is not a monster hunter game made for previous fans. Wilds is 110% made to widen the series' appeal. This on it's own is not a bad thing, but to get new players they have to shave off some of the rough edges that made some of the older games special. I think MH:World and it's expansion did a much better job of walking the line between not having too many QoL improvements, and having so many QoL improvements that the series loses it's unique approach to gameplay. Wilds just shaves off a little too much and has some redundant design because of it. (The chillmantle and heatmantle bugs completely negate the need to ever bring cold and hot drinks along on a hunt for example)

I also feel Wilds is missing some of the wonder that previous games had. World's hubs Astera and Seliana felt so much more alive than any area in Wilds. They were colorful and every shop you interacted with had it's own huge area with a unique look. Even the older games hubs areas like Kokoto village or Pokke village felt much better to be in than the base camps we get as our hubs in Wilds. Every NPC we interact with is stuck in a tent. I miss that theme park like experience of going around Astera or Seliana and having each area feel like it has a purpose. In Wilds, the base camps are thrown together and talking to the smithy feels less engaging when I'm just talking to a girl in a tent. The Gathering Hub slightly makes up for this but then it goes against the design of being able to freely go out on any hunt from the base camp which the game toted in it's marketing. (Thats a whole other thing because that mechanic is completely ignored almost immediately) The Gathering Hub also just has bizarro versions of previous games' mechanics when they don't fit Wilds at all. In previous games, eating before a hunt cost Zenny, and buffs would be lost when you died but thats besides the point. In Wilds, the main way you eat is from getting ingredients and cooking on a grill, or having an NPC invite you over for a meal. The Hub has a big roundtable not dissimilar to previous game's canteens, but instead of paying with money you pay with tickets, and the meals you get are just DoorDashed from the villages they belong to. It's a way to implement old mechanics into this new system, but to me it just feels clunky and I wish it was just how it was in World with the Meowscular Chef and Grandmeowster Chef.

I admit a lot of this review is negative, and all of it is from personal preference for what I personally think a Monster Hunter game should be, and I know that for some this review will come off with "old woman yells at cloud" energy. But I think that in a vacuum, Wilds is not a horribly offensive game. It has some especially smoothed over edges but new players won't care or notice and most returning players will probably appreciate it. For me though, I see these QoL changes to be a step in the wrong direction, and all I can hope is that Capcom doesn't push the franchise down those steps for the sake of mass market appeal for future mainline games. Monster Hunter is really something special, and seeing it lean away from the things that I personally love about it is hard to watch.

Nothing personal, but i don't think this game is fun, sorry. Go play GU or WI instead. In more details: 

The campaign is really ez until the last boss but sincerelly this is the only thing i really don't really care. Dahaad is really funny because he plays like a meh siege boss but they throw him at you like he is nothing.

Moster design is 'ok' to 'meh' specially in the monsters from other games, who have *exactly* the same moveset from the games before world plus 1 or 2 new attacks to poke you off guard. Thats a bummer comming from world and rise balancing phylosophy. The last boss is also peak lazy design after you know his only trick: it's just like a nakarkos but with less unique things to say. Just don't ask me about the mizutsune sans ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ they done; i find it very amusing to see how lazy they could get to then throw me the old music from 3, 4 and GU like they are doing something. 
In the endgame, also, a LOT of attacks are made to ♥♥♥♥ normal parries like lagi and duna where you *can* parry any attacks, but you will hear the sheen and then proceed take 3/4 of the damage from the ground part of the attack animation plus you're given hyperarmor to kill you, so like, '♥♥♥♥ LS and DB players'. Due to that unfun design i probably will be skipping the endgame until at least the DLC as i'm not really inclined to like that game design school.

Weapon design is good despite of that but what gets what is a greater question, with weapons such Lance and DB gaining a lot of the good things from GU where others like LS and CB gaining nothing except what can be seen as nerfs with CB being stripped of it's savage axe mode until exploding a mark (and the savage axe is less savage than you would expect) and LS with it's now "worse than nothing" (as said before) parries and, while not in red-bar, "even worse" parries.

The endgame farm also is a step back from the other more rng-light grinding from WI, now the rng weapon grind is ♥♥♥♥ (worse than safi and kulve, maybe even to 4's expedition fiesta, you have to grind materials, grind upgrades, pray for better artian atributes and save-reload-toss artian parts for better 6 slot atributes and then grind gog for the rng upgrade), the deco grind is *still* ♥♥♥♥ (maybe a little better than WI) and now you *ALSO* have to grind for good collars as they are now another thing to grind your day off. You know, i like the *idea*, but i also value my time and my sanity (e.g. in gog tries, omega tries and archtempered tries, to then be contemplated with ♥♥♥♥ artifacts or ♥♥♥♥ gogmart upgrades).
I literally started playing MH to get off genshin, i don't want more ♥♥♥♥♥-♥♥♥♥♥♥-go-die-grinding mechanics.

Also, the game runs like ♥♥♥♥. FSR shouldn't be the default option.
