This game is really bad. The graphics look like they’re from 1999 and the gameplay is very boring. I honestly wouldn’t recommend buying it. If you want to try something similar, you might as well play a game on Roblox because the graphics and overall experience feel about the same.

Cons

• Designed with controllers in mind. Mouse and keyboard take some getting used to, but they are serviceable.
• Some texture pop-ins after first loading up the game, but they smooth out over time. There are also low-res textures on a lot of objects for some reason, especially on architecture.
• Hair, scarves, and cloth clip through armour a lot. Kind of distracting, but this game is not the only sinner in this regard.
• The UI is a convoluted mess. There, I said it.
• Much too easy in the first half of the game.
• The story is pure tripe. The villagers don't know what a "weapon" is, but in the very next scene, the elder cuts the bread with a knife. Christ, who wrote this?!
• The intro (i.e., the first 8-10 hours) is a railroad. I almost quit the game because of handholding and endless cutscenes. Listen: We don't want long-winded, spoon-feeding intros. We want to play the game! Don't insult our intelligence by holding our hands through everything. Show, don't tell.

Pros

• The animations, design, and behavior of the monsters are top tier. They really seem alive with unique behaviors and movesets. Great stuff. I sometimes feel bad for killing them.
• I like the feeling of being inside a functioning ecosystem.
• You can use your slinger to grab stuff from afar and manipulate the environment.
• Like other MH games, each weapon makes this feel like a different game.
• Plenty of ways to approach the game, which makes for great replay value.

Conclusion: Why in the world would developers create these fantastic biomes, elegant and diverse monsters, and magnificent environments just to NOT LET THE PLAYER explore them? It is unfathomable to me. A huge misstep on the director's part. However, if you can stomach the story railroad, the game mechanics themselves are very good.

Context: 
I have played every single Monster Hunter all the way back to the PS1 and PSP.
I have watched the series evolve tremendously over the years and make leaps and bounds towards becoming a stronger hunter by utilizing new tools such as wire bugs, clutch claws, mounts, the mounting mechanics etc. 
Each Monster Hunter has had an identity that was unique to it with a dedicated flagship, or on occasion flagships, that built the story to be interesting and captivating. 

With MHWorld, every event quest had something either fun, goofy, or challenging that made them enjoyable. With MHWilds, I cannot say the same. There are some goofy quests such as the recently added slime farming quest but aside from that they are all just standard hunts for, at best, mediocre armor that can't be mixed well into any set without significant sacrifice or even layering. 

Now to talk about the risk vs reward aspect of the hunts. I truly believe that Monster Hunter Wilds has some of the worst design philosophies when it comes to rewarding the player for arising to meet a higher challenge. Of note, grinding a perfect set to minimize your time on a hunt of an apex monster to only receive a slightly cooler pendant  is insulting at best. When compared to the previous titles, it appears as though CAPCOM are just pushing out trinkets without putting any thought into the personality or the essence of what the monster is. Take AT Jin Dahaad as an example, for his armor it is simply the frosted version of his base armor. Comparing that to AT Arkveld there is no contest as to which armor has a closer affinity to the monster it represents. The rewards for rising to the challenge of a 10 star GOG; a title banner.... a 10 star Zoh Shia? literally nothing extra. Fighting 4 AT apexes? A title banner. This is pathetic. 

The 10 stars. I am personally tired of seeing the same 4 monsters getting a new star just because. There are many other monsters in the game I would love to see variants or subspecies of and THEN get those as 10 stars. Now with the precedent being set that the apex monster act as the elder dragons for late game, looking at armor sets here, and then to have Gogmazious come in and have multiple passives, every elder would now have to have multiple passives. The overarching problem with MHWilds is that they are only using the same resources already in the game rather than try to incorporate monsters that we have seen and loved from previous titles because they had a poor launch. They are letting down the community and should really reevaluate what it is that players want. 

TLDR; CAPCOM is getting lazy with the rewards for players who rise to meet the challenge of a higher difficulty quest and Monster Hunter is going downhill if they keep this up.

It’s finally playable. I’ve waited for almost a year to play Monster Hunter Wilds after the miserable performance of the beta. It runs relatively well now. Everything on Ultra with Raytracing at 1080p stays around 70 to 80 FPS using Frame Gen 99% of the time with some drops to 55 or less in more demanding zones. For a build with an RTX 3060 8GB, an AMD Ryzen 5 5500, and 16GB of RAM, it performs great especially for a more budget friendly setup.

Even without a Master Rank expansion, there is a very good amount of playability for the price. The grind is much more bearable because Investigations let you get specific items like Plates and Gems as guaranteed rewards. The new Wound System also feels great. It gives the fights more depth and also guarantees a drop which helps the grind. The weapons feel great as usual. The story is about as good as Monster Hunter gets. Let's be real, it is not Oscar worthy by any means, but it is enough to keep you going through the story.

Co-op is also a big step up from World since I do not have to watch a cinematic before people can join me. I played through every main and subquest solo except the Final Fantasy collaboration. ♥♥♥♥ Omega Planetes. That quest frustrated me to no end, but otherwise the game is very friendly to solo players.

The Seikret is this game's version of a Palamute. It can auto walk to your target and carries your secondary weapon which you can switch to with the press of a button while mounted. This allows you to carry two different elemental weapons of the same type for example or really any weapon combination you want. It deepens the gameplay, especially on hunts with multiple monsters, by enabling you to keep going without returning to camp to switch your weapon. The Seikret also carries your quest supplies like First Aid and traps. This means you can access them from anywhere even if you forgot to take them at the start since there is no supply chest anymore.

The environments feel populated and the map is seamless except for changing between the overall biomes. For example, the desert is a separate open world map from the forest, but they are both big seamless maps on their own.

As with many others, the performance of this game on launch was terrible. Whilst I personally was at "playable" performance at 1440p, many struggled with playing at an enjoyable experience. As a long time MH franchise player I usually play the games extensively after finishing the story but that wasn't the case after this one. I love having high frame rate in all my games so I ended up resorting to 3rd party frame gen at the time like lossless scaling and optimization mods from nexus. It was tedious so after the first playthrough I ended up just putting the game down until they get some optimization updates going.

Although it took a while, they eventually did it and it's been amazing! While I don't speak for all rigs, it worked wonders on mine and the game feels and plays like all the previous gen MH games that I fell in love with. I don't use anymore of my previous optimizations and they finally added DLSS for the older RTX cards which didn't work if you had anything older than a 40 series on launch. Unfortunately, my experience still isn't relatable to alot of people with older rigs... Hopefully in the future they can further optimize it to the point that lower end PC's can fully experience the game smoothly.

If you have a fairly new or decent PC I 100% recommend this game to anyone that enjoyed world, rise, or any previous MH. Otherwise if you have something older or weaker than the latter I'd tread carefully.

After 100% completion, I'd recommend this to anyone who's a fan of the series, late game since launch is a lot grindier and harder which imo is a positive as in base you'd be done with endgame in 60 hours including main + side quests, but the title updates have redeemed this game completely with monsters like Planetes, Gogmazios, and ofc all the AT monsters.
Anyone who might be looking to make this their first entry into the series, you'd be glad to know this might be the most beginner friendly MH title I've seen (Rise/Sunbreak excluded), if you have friends to play with, it's genuinely one of the most enjoyable experiences I've had with a game. 
As far as performance goes, my specs are listed below and I've locked my fps to 90 with dlss 3 on and ray tracing and runs just fine, my old rig with a 3060ti and ryzen 7 5700x3d would only hit up to 70 fps with occasional drops with fsr3 and no ray tracing just to compare.
Looking towards the future of this game, I am wholeheartedly optimistic and believe Capcom, with how they've handled their disastrous launch and a full year of negativity surrounding the game, have shown how dedicated they are to their fan base and truly do want to deliver a great game. It might not be there yet, but the best days are ahead of us for sure.

I cannot recommend this game. It is by far the worst Monster Hunter to date.

1st: Performance

It is completely indefensible how badly optimized this game is. It looks worse than World and runs insanely bad. Without FrameGen and Mid to High settings (raytracing off) I don't even get stable 60 fps. I recently started playing again after stopping when Lagiacrus released and Youtubers said they "fixed" the performance, but they did not. It runs like ♥♥♥♥ and looks like ♥♥♥♥ and this will never get fixed, because the RE Engine is not made for grand scale games like this. But it probably still runs better than if it were using UE5. 

2nd: Pacing

The game progression to Endgame somehow manages to both feel rushed and way too slow at the same time. How? You have 15 minute story segments where nothing happens, then you get to fight a monster for 5-10 minutes followed by another 15 minute story segment following another 5-10 minute hunt and after that you are immediately rushed to the next biome. Since the story is really bad, those railroaded sequences suck all life joy out of you and the hunts in LR are so fast that the joy doesn't return before the next story sequence. 

3rd: Optional quests/side content

The optional quests are completely useless, since they are just the same monsters you already killed, with nothing to differentiate these quests from the normal ones. There are absolutely no gathering quests... WHY??? I know maybe not many people enjoyed them, but they definitely gave you some time to breathe and learn the new maps, find some shortcuts between areas. None of that is here anymore. World had the best implementation of this system with optional quest chains that rewarded you with important gear (mantles) and upgrades (canteen, plant growth). They also visually upgraded your hub area, which let those quests feel important. Even if you do all the optional quests you get nothing. No visual upgrades to the hub, not even an indicator in the quest menu that you finished all the optional stuff. Nothing. 

4th: UI

The UI for this game is a step down from World, which was already confusing for first time players. 

5th: New monsters:

The new apex monsters for each biome are all enjoyable to fight. Doshaguma is easily the worst new monster, as it is not only visually repulsive, but incredibly annoying to fight too. The new pack mechanic sucks and I seriously hope they discontinue that ♥♥♥♥, because if I have to deal with 3-4 monsters at the same time in G-Rank I'm going to ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ explode. Don't come at me with dung bombs, as this does not help your argument. When the response to packs is to break them up and just fight the monster solo then what was the reason for implementing the pack mechanic. Annoy you? Guardian Doshaguma is immediately more enjoyable to fight, because it doesn't have a pack mechanic. The rest of the normal new monsters are mostly forgettable. Some highlights are Ajarakan as he is simply fun to fight and Quematrice as an enjoyable early monster. Balahara is annoying with Bowguns. The returning monsters are very well done and enjoyable as always. The post game additions (Mizutsune, Lagiacrus and Seregios) are also really enjoyable. I love Mizutsune. Unfortunately, they fumbled with his theme in this game. His music was way better in every other title. I cannot comment on anything after Lagiacrus since I stopped playing then. But I do not give a rats ass about the other content added after Lagi since they are all designed to be fought as a Group and I'm a solo player and since I have constant server disconnections in this game (every 15 - 20 minutes) I will not even try the multiplayer stuff so I won't destroy my set-up out of sheer frustration and anger. And no, this is not a me problem, I do not have these issues with any other game, just the Monster Hunter series and since I've been playing since Freedom Unite I know that the Capcom servers are ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. In World I could not finish even one Safi'Jiva quest without disconnecting. 

6th: Handholding

My God Capcom, I am a grown adult, I do not need to know where all the monsters are on the map at all times, I can go out in the world and find my own iron ore for upgrading my basic gear and what reason is there to fight the same monster multiple times anymore, when new gear and weapons need so few parts from the monsters that I can craft everything after killing the same monster 3 times. Wtf? 

7th: Artian weapons

Why? They suck visually, they are an annoying chore to upgrade while being complete RNG if you made a good weapon. The normal weapons should have received an extra upgrade and be the best in slot weapons until the Post Game additions dropped. 

8th: Conclusion

Some of these issues can be remedied with the G-Rank Expansion, but the core problems will still be there. There are numerous other things that can be critiqued about this game, but this is already getting way too long. The new maps are all a convoluted mess. Even after 65 hours I get lost easily in some areas. The fact that I only have 65 hours in this game is extremely sad, but the ♥♥♥♥ performance just killed all enjoyment I could've had with this game, so I burnt out fast. In every other game I have at least 100 hours, mostly 200-300. World was such a great game, while not being perfect, but giving you a really immersive new experience in the series and Rise and now Wilds completely killed my hope for the mainline games. Stop taking every bit of friction out of the game just so newcomers can perfectly optimize their fastest way to the Endboss. The story sucks ass too. The focus of "here, look at these ethnic minorities with their vibrant ethnic culture and their ethnic minority food" just feels super weird, as if it was written by some fat white women. Also ♥♥♥♥ Nata. 

Will I try out the expansion? Yes. Will I enjoy it? No, probably not. 

I hope Stories 3 delivers.

Have a good day!

MONSTER HUNTER WILDS IN 2026

You could not believe how excited I am to tell you all about Monster Hunter Wilds. A game that needs little introduction, I’ve been eagerly waiting for its release and… well, I got the miraculous chance to play it early. And after completing the main campaign, I can safely say this is an incredible experience that fans of the franchise will love. So… let’s get this quest started!

The story takes place in a new region called the Forbidden Lands, an Eastern area of the world that the Hunter's Guild has never even set foot in, filled with strange monsters and unique environments. It is also the home of Nata, a young boy who managed to survive the destruction of his village caused by a monster named the “White Wraith”. Now, as a hunter of the Guild sent on this expedition, it's your unit's job to find the Keepers, hunt down the White Wraith, and solve the mysteries of the Forbidden Lands.

I think the story is actually fairly well done, and that's a serious shock. I’m not saying it’s an absolute masterpiece, and I'm always a sucker for learning about monster ecology, but I never expected to be invested in the actual narrative of the game. I loved the characters and how they help out and grow, all while delivering a ton of great fights and cinematic moments. It actually has an Aesop other than “Nature is awesome and we should protect it with everything we have.”

As for the graphics, it’s really pushing what the RE Engine can do. I don't have the best computer, but I'd say that the game on High settings looks just as good as Monster Hunter: World, which still holds up graphically today. The characters and monsters are nicely done, but the real star of the show is the Forbidden Lands. The locales featured can be awe-inspiring. Each map has three states: Fallow where it's lifeless, Inclemency where the environment is actively hostile and dangerous, and Plenty where life blooms. All states feature different endemic life, gathering spots, and monsters so be prepared for surprises when the weather changes. It can turn from a wasteland to a flourishing ecosystem in a snap, and it’s exciting to see and provides some twists to your quests.

Character creation is also freakishly detailed with many more options to choose from. Like, you can edit your teeth and how nasty your dental hygiene can be. Everything about your face can be adjusted so you can create your ideal self… or a horrific monster that makes you wonder why hunters aren’t going after them. And thanks to the elimination of gendered sets, you can look however you want to. Female hunters can now wear helmets and hide their thighs and guys can go out in their underwear and show off their chests. It is truly the best change.

Once you’re done with the initial character creation, however, you are very limited in what you can change afterwards (hair, voice, and make-up). If you want to physically change your character, you need Character Edit Vouchers, which I hate with a passion. It is a holdover from Monster Hunter Rise and it is extra frustrating here now that my hunter actually talks in cutscenes. I wanted to change the voice after hearing them talk for the first time, but all you can do without a voucher is change the pitch. If you don't have any, you're out of luck.

You can get one free voucher for both Hunter and Palico via DLC, but you'll need to shell out real money for extras. I'll happily pay for layered armour but not for editing my character, which is free in other games. By the way, if you care about this, pronouns are tied to your voice type (which only comes up in two cutscenes). What’s worse is that you can save your appearances in a baffling amount of slots you might never use. I love the character creation, but it still irks me that this is still a thing.

But enough about my ramblings on terrible microtransactions, let’s get hunting! If you’ve played Monster Hunter World, you should find yourself in familiar territory, with intuitive weapon controls and a Slinger to pelt rocks and dung at monsters or set off environmental traps with its new Hook function. All the weapons have some new moves and combos to learn, as well as new opportunities to hit ‘em where it hurts through Focus Mode and targeting their wounds with Focus Strikes, counter monsters using Offset Attacks and engage them in Power Clashes, or even get a leg up on your prey with Sneak Attacks. Not every weapon has access to these moves, but that’s why you have a Seikret, a bird-like mount that, not only is it transportation to objectives and waypoints, it also acts as your item box as it can deliver supply items and even a second weapon for you to switch to in the middle of a hunt. Although I am a Great Sword main, I decided to give Dual Blades a shot and I loved it. One for damage, the other for element and status ailments. There aren't any restrictions on the weapons you bring, and weapons now have skills, so go nuts with your builds.

I can't really judge difficulty accurately due to… well, actually having too much experience thanks to World and Rise. Low Rank isn't meant to be hard, but it isn’t too easy either. If you’re trying to blow through the story, you might find yourself struggling in the later fights. The real challenge comes in at High Rank, and the challenges there can seriously test your skills, but will be greatly rewarded in return. Tempered monsters, Investigations, Artian weapons, and so much more.

I know I’m likely to be really biased here, but I think Monster Hunter Wilds is just really damn good. It improves on a lot of aspects of the series and creates an incredible time that will make anyone sink hundreds of hours into it. With a story that actually has substance, a seamless gameplay loop, and an impressive world to explore and fight in, I highly recommend anyone interested to get it. It’s Monster Hunter at its finest. Just make sure you have the specs to run it properly and gather as much as you can.

I'll confess that when Capcom announced its multi-patch performance improvement plan for Monster Hunter Wilds, I was skeptical about just how much it would accomplish—especially when the first of the regimen's updates delivered the same mixed results as Capcom's previous attempts to address Wilds' technical woes. The early details for this month's PC-specific follow-up patch, which touted additional graphics and CPU options alongside the vague promise of yet more "CPU/GPU optimization," didn't inspire confidence either.

I'm pleased to say that, for once, I was wrong to doubt.

"After almost a year I can finally recommend this game after today's performance patch," says another reviewer with a staggering 1,042 hours of Wilds playtime. "We all expected this update to be a nothing burger but it's actually really good."

Performance and Stability (2026 Status)
Major Improvements: After months of performance issues at launch, updates by early 2026 have stabilized the game, allowing many players to reach 60+ FPS on capable hardware without heavy reliance on frame generation.

Hardware Scaling: While high-end machines (e.g., RTX 4070) can now run the game smoothly, base console performance and lower-end PCs can still face challenges, although the experience is significantly better than at launch.

Steam Deck: The game has seen improvements on the Steam Deck, with stable 30+ FPS achievable. 

9/10 Verdict
Monster Hunter Wilds in 2026 is considered a polished Must-play, and expansive hunting experience that has overcome its rocky launch to become a top-tier title in the franchise.

Context: Just hit HR 100 and cleared the last optional quest.

Monster Hunter Wilds is a very fun game. I love how they basically brought back the Guiding Lands concept from World and expanded it to make the world feel more immersive. The downside, though, is the whole “passing the day” mechanic to refresh monsters so you can get the ones you want and save them as investigations.

Personally, I’m not a fan of doing quests with randos unless it’s a dedicated hunting party formed through the official Discord, besides that, I’m mostly a solo hunter, so you can imagine how thrilled I was when I discovered the Support Hunter system, calling in NPC hunters during quests with actually decent AI is a huge win for solo players.

With the introduction of the SF6 and FFXIV collab, Capcom kind of introduce specs to the game in a form of Akuma and the blackest night which I'm all in for, it shows that they’re putting more thought into their collaborations, and I hope we see even more creative ones in the expansion.

I started playing when they announced the final update for the base game, and I can say for sure that the game is FINALLY optimized with no lag whatsoever (high settings on a 3070).

My main issue with Wilds is how easy the early game is. Monsters go down way too quickly, and you don’t really feel that classic “Monster Hunter” challenge until around HR50+. The main story also ends very quickly as you could finish it in under three hours if you speedrun it.

I can definitely understand the criticism the game received at launch, especially when it had abysmal optimization and very little endgame content before the title updates. But now that the base game has reached its last and final update, I can confidently say that Monster Hunter really shines once all the content or DLC have been released.

I’m really looking forward to the expansion, and I hope more people give this game a try.

For some context: this is my first MH game. I tried World when it came out but I didn't play long before refunding it.
Wilds has good things, but also quite a few bad ones. If you're looking for a boss simulator, you found it. If you're looking for a story rich game, you did not find it.
Why do I play it? Fashion hunter basically. 

At just over 200 hours, I seriously considered why I was still playing because I was just fighting the same monsters over and over for materials and gear upgrades. Just so I could fight the same monster with a higher difficulty. This might be appealing to some players, but I need a goal outside of this. It's not like I can change my hunter's personality for a different narrative (no dialogue choices), just the appearance.

Speaking of narrative, the main story and the characters are incredibly dry. Cringy even at some points. I feel absolutely nothing for the story and the only character I would enjoy more of is Erik. And maybe Olivia. This is not a story game. It is a fight to get stronger game.

My biggest complaint: graphics. Outside of cinematics, this game looks like it came out 10 years ago.
We all know Wilds had absolutely horrible performance issues at launch. Capcom really has made efforts to improve it, but it's still rough for me at least. I have to sit there for quite a while in a new area for character/monster models to load or it's just polygons. And I mean BASIC polygons. I could attempt to fight the monster before it fully loads, but I'm gonna get smacked by the invisible claw that hasn't loaded yet.
The textures are bad. They just are. I audibly complain every time I play and none of my settings are set to high or ultra (mostly low). I don't dare go down that path if low is struggling. Tried the better textures 'DLC' and I didn't notice a difference.

You'll also see complaints about the UI and menu navigation, and it's very true. 
Very little gets explained and you often just get thrown into a mission with no idea what you're supposed to do. Prime example is the introduction of captures. You don't encounter the concept until later in the story and it literally just throws you into a mission with an NPC saying to do something. And these monsters are the most annoying Fers for a capture. I didn't know I was supposed to open a different window to grab items I've never used before. And of course all my inventory slots of my hotbar were taken (why wouldn't they be) so I had to throw two item stacks away to get those items needed. All while running around with these 3 MFers running wild like toddlers in a small area.
I've had to look up quite a few things online to understand what they even do. Game says 'armor spheres make your armor stronger' is actually 'armor spheres increase the amount of times you can upgrade to make your armor stronger.' 

All these negatives, yet a positive review. I might just be hyper fixated, but I really like the battles and weapons. Charge Blade chainsaw >:) I improve my positioning, find new ways to use the environment, and naming my build 'Move B*tch' with full evade because it's my biggest flaw. It does get a bit monotonous trying to get a particular drop so try to change it up every now and then. As much as I complained, I just can't give a negative recommendation.

But I can't believe this game is still $70. Buy it on a sale, cuz it ain't worth that price.

[h1]Comprehnsive, spoiler-free review of Monster Hunter Wilds (MHWilds):[/h1]

[b]Net Positive[/b] - Monster Hunter (MH) games once they "click" are fantasic, MHWilds is no exception. 
Now that all Monsters for base Game are relased (TU4+) I can [u]recommend this game[/u].

[h2]Preface:[/h2]
MH:World (MHW) was my first entry into the MH Series, where the game only clicked for me when trying out the Gunlance, after almost dropping it playing with Dualblades. This as a whole feels like a common theme with people who drop the game, usually because they didn't click with a weapon.

[h2]Gameplay:[/h2]
Fighting monsters is very fun, and after completing all base game achievements, I [u]still want to keep playing[/u]. The gameplay experience is based around 14 different weapons, which act like "Classes" in a traditional RPG. All weapons have different moves, systems, and learning curves. The gameplay loop boils down to Hunt a Monster, carve it for parts to make better weapons and armour, repeat.

[h2]Difficulty:[/h2]
Variable - a vast range of low to high - [u]recommended for all[/u].
As a person who has played at least one Previous Title, the game [u]starts off very easy[/u]. There are no intro "Small Monster" practice hunts, You are immmediately tasked with a hunting a Large Monster with very low health. The game does pick up the difficulty later on, but in my playthrough of Low Rank (Story) with Sword and Shield (SnS) and Gunlance GL I fainted once, to an attack I didn't read the tutorial message on.

In High Rank (Post-Story) the [u]difficulty picks up slightly[/u], and the game opens up into levelling for progression, with a few Monsters that are only fightable in High Rank, and the Tempered system opens up - increasing health, attack rate, attack power, and on occasion, new moves for Monsters previously fought.

End Game and Title Updates (TU) improved the experience tremendously. The Tempered Monsters feel like where the base Monsters could have been, though for a new player experiece I can appreciate the lower difficulty. Higher Star (9*, 10*) and Arch-Tempered (AT) Monsters [u]can feel like a good challenge[/u], with the End Game Monsters often requiring many attempts and build tweaks to get an initial clear.

[h2]Versus other Entries:[/h2]
Variable - [u]pros and cons[/u].
MHW had a sense of exploration that is lost slightly by MHWilds' streamlining of the gameplay experience - essentially letting the player press a button to be on-rails delivered to a target. 
The environments in MHWilds feel alive, like MHW, and the graphical fidelity has increased, as you would expect from a newer entry. 
The "Gimmick" of MHWilds - akin to the Clutch Claw from MHW: Iceborne (MHW:I) - are the "Wound" and "Focus Mode" systems, which are heavily intertwined.  Wounds - at least in earlier parts of the game are very strong at disrupting Monsters. Focus Mode - essentially lowers the skill ceiling for ensuring attacks hit their target, but also provides access to additional moves not in previous titles.
Fishing, capturing endemic life, and environmental looting (Mining Ores / Bone piles / Plants / Fungus) are all still present, with varying degrees of importance to the gameplay loop.

[h2]Story:[/h2]
Variable - [u]Not important to me[/u], but more in the spotlight than previous titles.
The Story in MHWilds is much more cohesive than MHW in my opinion, though this doesn't affect my enjoyment of either game, as most of my playtime takes place outside of Story quests.

[h2]Technical:[/h2]
[u]Needs a decent system to run[/u].
The launch was rough for this game, performance to graphical quality ratio was not accpetable - most issues [i]seemed[/i] unrelated to graphics. Post TU4 the game runs much better, though on my system (i7-10700K - RTX 3070Ti - 64GB RAM) I maintained 60fps with Medium/Custom Settings. 
I recommed playing on Controller/Gamepad - personally have been using an XBox Series Controller.

[h3]Importance of Categories to me:[/h3] 
[u]Gameplay/Difficulty > VS Previous > Story[/u]. Technical becomes most important in most negative reviews.

[h2]Overall, I recommend MHWilds.[/h2]

I've decided long ago that I wouldn't type my review until the expansion was out, but as of today I feel like I can't disregard the elephant in the room anymore.

90% of my playtime I had fun playing despite its bad performance on my old rig (10580k 32GB RAM and a 3060Ti). 

I felt that the game disincentivized trying out different builds because once you unlocked Arkveld's armor you pretty much could steamroll anything without much trouble and it was honestly pointless to try to mix stuff up if every monster was dead under 8 mins. 
At this point I started geting bored so I chose to stop playing and wait for more updates after completing TU1 in one brief session. I just wanted to have more things to do before diving back in. 

TU4 comes by and I have a new rig now (AMD 7800x3d, 9070xt, 32GB of overpriced DDR5 RAM) and it's going ok: I'm having fun with end game content, finally I'm on my toes again with monsters that punish you hard for not paying attention, I'm starting to think about my build. 

And then a new patch comes out. Suddenly I couldn't play anymore. Constant crashes, weird glitches, system reboots.

I've tried everything the guy with the green pfp tells you to do on the steam forum (you know who it is), I've tried also to effectively nerf my card to be able to play but the game still reboots my pc when I hit "save and exit" after finally being able to play for 1 hr straight. 

Then I swapped the 9070xt putting in the 3060Ti once again and the game worked flawlessly.
I'm so frustrated. I think the new 10 stars are a good addition and I was having fun, but I can't push myself to turn a blind eye on all the problems the game has if you sport an AMD configuration. 

This is a bad review solely because of the compatibility problems. If they manage to fix in the future, or if AMD does something about it with its Drivers I will change it.

I genuinely love this game. Fighting the monsters is low-key addictive and every hunt feels intense. The monster designs are awesome and some of them are seriously tough, which makes every victory feel super satisfying.

The multiplayer feature is also really nice. You can squad up with friends to take down stronger monsters and it makes the grind way easier. The devs definitely cooked with the co-op system.

But honestly? I still prefer playing solo. There’s just something different about facing a powerful monster alone and actually beating it by yourself. No teammates, no backup, just you and the monster. When you finally win, it hits different. No cap, it feels way more rewarding.

Another thing I really enjoy is crafting armor after defeating monsters. Turning your hard-earned monster parts into cool gear just gives the game that perfect progression loop. Hunt → win → craft → look badass → repeat.

After playing the game since day 1 as a gift from my dad, I have to say that everything about this game besides its optimization in the first year is absolutely amazing. I’m not a Monster Hunter veteran, but having played Monster Hunter World before, this game proves itself to be a very good successor and also a way better entry point for new players due to its new mechanics and gameplay pace; it’s just an overall improvement.

The optimization issue affected the game for about a year. I have to say that it didn’t stop me from enjoying the game above 30fps on max settings since the beginning on hardware that can be considered midrange, and for me, a game as such it’s plenty enjoyable on 30fps although running it above 60fps all the time now does make it better of course.

It has way more good points than the few issues you may find in the immense content that it offers WITHOUT PAYING beyond the base game, and after putting into it almost 700 hours and even getting all its achievements, it still has things to offer me.

100% recommend playing with friends or alone if you prefer, and getting it on sale makes it a 10/10.

Overhaul, it's a great game. The combat is fluid, the monsters are amazing and the graphics are beautiful, although the story is lacking, you don't play monster hunter for the story, but to hunt monsters.

But there it's where is the problem: hunting. The game makes it too easy to engage in combat. You don't have to follow the monster's trail or use other tools to track or bait him, as it doesn't reward that type of gameplay, although it's in the game. Most of the late game monsters are arena fights. Amazing fights. But more like an mmo fight than anything else.

The biggest problem is the artesian weapon. The system came to aid certain weapons that lack an element or status in their trees. The problem is that, they are too strong and makes the actual trees irrelevant, specially when the whole end game grind is for them. After you reach endgame, you don't want to hunt lesser monsters for their material and that made me hunt the same 4 or 5 monsters again and again and again and the fact that to upgrade it is "ramdom" (it uses a pool of abilities and status) is completely dumb and I hope they don't make the end game in expansion like this.

My way to make the artisans better, would be to be able to upgrade the monsters weapon with better augments and give them their respective monster's armour set ability. We have 2-4 slots to activate a set ability, 6 pieces of equipment but only 5 actually count? With 5, we have a piece not being used or kbly counting for group set with that's possible.

Difficulty? The endgame is a great middle finger to players complaining at launch about it. Gog and omega and now arch monsters add a great sense of danger that the base game lacked. They are not perfect! Status didn't keep up with the new gigantic monsters health and many of them move like they weigh like feathers and stun lock you, but nothing impossible when you know what you are doing.

Performance? I have a quite weak laptop and it's working fine. Some dips where the action is very strong, but it got much better lately. At least I don't have to mod to get a proper performance!
I recommend the game, but they still have to work somethings here and there in the gameplay loop

Ps: many of the jewels in the game are completely useless or redundant right now like wind resistance,  many monsters use it but you probably never was hit by it if compared to world for example (rathalos wind pressure while flying was somewhat a problem there and here it doesn't affect your character); tremor resistance, the game right now game has NO monster that makes you need the whole 3 levels, not even gog with its humangus size; flinch free and shock absorber and jump master, they pretty much do the same thing but flinch free do what jump do and you still can hit players, unlike shock abs; climbing and any stealth ability really, since you have a mount and always are hitting the monster and not hiding from it and antismell. Just one monster uses it and thr monster is not that dangerous to waste jewel slot for that

EDIT: I can't in good conscience recommend this game. Even post-optmization patch, it is not release-ready even if framerates are acceptable. I want to play it, but the hardware issues it's causing are past the point of ridiculous and I'm sincerely concerned continuing to play it will cause damage to my computer whether on the hardware level or software level. I've never seen anything like this. I'm just gonna revert my BIOS/everything else settings back to what was stable on every other game, and then stop playing this one. When a single piece of software forces you to power limit and negative frequency offset your GPU, downclock your RAM, artificially limit monitor refresh rate, disable CPU features, and offload displays just to prevent Kernel Power 41 hardware shutdowns, the "fun" is officially gone. This game doesn't actually work. It should never have been released on this engine. This is a paid virus that handles resources so poorly and irresponsibly it triggers EMERGENCY STOPS to protect your computer from MH Wilds. Is my multiasking and 7680x2160@240hz alongside two other monitors contributing to edge case instability? Probably. But other decently coded software handles that just fine, taking a minor framerate hit when it becomes a problem, without trying to spike beyond the available resources and killing itself, GPU drivers, CPU, RAM, or just the whole computer.

Buyer Beware. Expect *serious* problems, but a game that is fun.

The game is playable after optimization patch in 2026, and gets decent framerates (still doesn't look or play much better than MH World) but while every other game runs fine on my system, MH wilds is definitely creating GPU and CPU instability to the point of kernel power shutdowns.

It's absolutely ridiculous for me to - for a single game, when everything else is fine - be disabling PBO, Expo, lowering texture resolution and anything to do with VRAM, playing windowed mode at lower resolution, changing the actual refresh rate of my monitor, moving side-monitors from GPU to IGPU - all to avoid various software level crashes - and still having it actually power down my computer to the MH WILDLY UNOPTIMIZED stuff going on. 

From a "game" perspective it's a great game. And while it appears to run better than it did before, it kind of doesn't. The game really can't be trusted to manage a computer's resources, and will self-destruct trying to grab more. Changing some VRAM-settings in config on top of the previous things could be what finally solves the resource-usage spikes shutting down GPU/computer.... let's see, I guess.

The monster line-up is decent but in order to progress endgame you will be grinding only about 3 of the monsters in the game, and the rest is sort of redundant sideshow stuff - which is a little disappointing compared to World - not for rare drops but to throw incredible amounts of materials into a slot machine. It's still a good foundation for a new generation of Monster Hunter, and I really like the new implementation of wounds/focus mechanics. It's a lot better than any other gimmicks they've introduced to the series over the years.

Using this engine for a game like this was a monumental mistake and I hope they can fix it.

I want to recommend this game, but I can't honestly do so in any sensible degree. 

The performance is STILL terrible. I run this game on some high end stuff, by no means should I be getting the severe amount of lag and frame drops and stutters that I do. I've tried running this with and without frame gen, turning down graphic settings, etc etc etc. Still stutters and freezes, and considering the modest difficult of the end game, this will literally ruin my hunts. Nothing is more irritating than the game itself prevent me from playing it.

I do like this game a lot. I enjoy the combat and the monsters, but jesus christ does the performance problems, input delay, input detection and overall just terrible optimization of hardware utilization in this game absolutely kill me love and enjoyment of this game as a franchise.

And this is just ignoring the slew of general problems with the combat with things feeling sluggish for no reason, having confusing as design (explain to me which idiot thought making greatsword due a shuffle after guarding was a good idea?) or in general being so inferior to other options it feels more daunting to convince myself to use hammer than it does to spam dual blade dodges. Seriously the dodge mechanic is completely broken, its better than the long swords special sheath because at least during the dodge, you can negate ALL forms of damage. The special sheath still takes damage through the fat fk known as Uth Duna. I hate that fish.

Frankly, if they want people to stick around for G/Master Rank, they need to balance the weapons to be as actually good as the best options (that being bow and dual blades for aforementioned reasons) and speed things up a bit with weapon animations and remove so many unnecessary shuffles from perfect guards. Playing this game against the 10 star AT's and NOT being a speedrunner is geniunly a miserable experience for so many weapons that I've entirely dropped some. I had done this during Savage Omega and now I have to do it with just the games overall design. This is ultimately unrelated rambling but uhhh 

TL;DR:

Optimize the game. ffs. I shouldn't be stuttering and lagging and having my inputs ate or outright ignored when im running a 4090 and 9900X. 

Balance the weapons in the game to be as useful or have as useful tech as the discerning dodges. 10 star monsters make most other weapons a slog to actually get proficient in. This was a problem with the 9 stars and STILL is. Stop being scared of strong weapons, theres better ways to make monsters challenging instead of forcing you to sit around and do nothing because of chip damage, perfect guards being nearly useless if not outright redundant or outright being slow. Greatsword tackle shouldn't be better than a PERFECT GUARD. Don't make "vital" mechanic and proceed to make every challenging monster completely negate its benefits on everything that doesn't slot Guard/G. Up. Especially since slotting that will shaft most weapons damage and make the hunts even MORE sloggish.

After playing this game since launch i can say that it has come a long way and it was not without issues but is an absolute solid MH to play after all the patches and TU`s, The starter Roster is really solid i personally have no issues with the current monsters that we have 

The Good:
-The Arch Tempered Monsters are a blast to fight, challenging you with new moves from the apexes 
 or Absolute relentless strikes from AT Arkveld.

-Layered armor system is also way more accessible compared to world which i do like as you can hide 
 some of the armor that does not look that great very easy or dress to an absolute unmatched fit to 
 intimidate the monsters 

-Focus mode is Amazing in my opinion as it allows you to hit the monsters when it otherwise would have 
 been a miss thought it kind of removes the penalty for not learning the monsters pattern which is the 
 core of MH but i am not complaining about it (we wilds players are spoiled with focus mode) 

-Seikrets not being a instand get out of jail free card unlike rise`s emergency move (forgot the name) as 
 the seikrets take time until they get to you dependent on how far away they are and after that take a 
 little time picking you up

-most of the performance issues are gone with a crash being super rare now and performance running 
 rather stable 

The Bad:

First is the Artian weapon system where after getting to Gogmazios, you are grinding for the perfect or decent stats on a weapon/weapons as literally any other weapon from the smithy is utterly useless or inferior to use which sucks, the only reason to craft smithy weapons at the moment is to make layered weapons (as regular artian weapons are kind of ugly, gog weapons are fine imo). which is kinda like WOW that weapon from X monster looks so cool i bet its gonna help in my hunts, NAH grind gog like 17 times in a row for a Chance at a decent weapon and then do it another Dozen times if you want different elements which can get stale very fast. 

Second is more a personal gripe but still a major let down in my eyes that being the Coloration of the world as it appears SUPER washed which is an absolute bummer even though it makes sense in the world building sense like the windward plains being a desert that frequently gets hit by massive sandstorm or Scarlet forest getting hit by massive floods and rain (absolute bummer as the scarlet forest during the Plenty is absolutely gorgeous) 

Third is the Ice shard cliffs which is easily the most boring locale in MH wilds it just feels super bland and the claustrophobic fighting in thin corridors against for example a Gore Magala does not feel great at all. its an absolute let down when compared to the Hoarfrost reach from world (but that locale came with Iceborn and wasn`t  base game so its kind of unknown what an wilds expansion has up its sleeve)

That being said though if you got a decent Rig that can Run this game i do totally recommend you play this one, even though my negatives outweigh the positives i absolutely love MH wilds and am exited what capcom is going to cook up for an expansion

MH Wilds = MH World Slop Edition. At this point, the team responsible for MH should just declare creative bankruptcy and do something else. 4/10

Wilds is a lazy copy of World and is a downgrade in almost every aspect. The camera is still trash, the menus are a cluttered mess and the new "Focus Mode" does more bad than good. Wilds has also managed to make an even more RNG end-game grind than World (how is that even possible).

The biomes/locales in Wilds are a MASSIVE downgrade from World. Akward map layout with too much verticality and cluttered visuals make for an awful experience. Compared to World, the navigation feels awkward and the maps don't have a natural flow or cohesive design to them. The Wyveria map is especially bad.

Then there's the monster roster; besides the Arachnid and Cephalopod variants most of the "new" monsters in Wilds are a copy pasta of a monster from World but worse:

Arkveld < Nergigante (even the lore is almost the same)
Zoh Shia < Xeno Jiiva
Gogmazio < Safi Jiiva
Jin Dahaad < Behemoth
Uth Duna < Namielle
etc.

The best thing about the monsters in Wilds are the bad ones from World that didn't make into Wilds (yet) like the Piscine Wyverns and Alatreon.

Last but not least, there's the unacceptable PC performance at launch that took a whole year to "fix". 

In closing, I remember reading a Wilds review on Steam a couple months back that simply read "Just play MH World". After completing Wilds, I could not agree more.

After 200+ on both SlopStation 5 and PC and after a few updates Wilds is a good game just sucked Capcom didn't delay the pc version so that they could iron out the performance hiccups Wilds faced at launch. My main grips with Wilds mainly the difficulty and some of the accessibility features. For the difficulty I have no problem with low rank being a walk in the park since it seems like they want low rank to be more story driven that is fine but high rank was just a joke up until title update 2 when they introduced seregios and lagiacrus. What also affected the difficulty was the new wound mechanic while I think it's a nice addition the wound system also made monsters topple more frequent and monsters being slower, less aggressive, and smaller health pools (unless you're goremagala) it brought the game down a bit until the recent title updates. The accessibility feature more so stuff like being able to fast travel to camps while in combat this is something I hope they never bring back we already have farcaster for that! also not a big fan of being able to buy monster materials or the weapons it felt like they were removing a big part of monster hunter's gameplay loop, grinding, I only ever use the support ships for consumable items but otherwise it just feels like such a pointless addition it works fine for a mmo like frontier but it shouldn't be in the mainline games. Now for the good stuff the roster of new monsters the monster hunter team keeping up a good job at doing variety and not just dragons and dinosaurs like in world it was also nice to finally see the likes of Lagiacrus and Gog comeback after so long. The new weapon movesets and focus mode while yes I did wish we got a new weapon in Wilds and I did criticize the it to an extent because of the wound mechanic it's a double edge sword because it's a fun edition just needs more fine tuning the focus strikes make the already existing weapons feel refreshing enough along with the new moves. Wilds might have my favorite iteration of the Insect Glaive it's so much fun to use it was already fun to use in world but now it's peak, Gunlance and charge blade got some neat additions too but so far I'd say Insect Glaive is the mvp this time around. While Wilds has it's flaws it's still a good game one I'll be grinding at after I'm done with all of the content for both the base game and the upcoming expansion I'm also glad the mh devs could turn things around for the game's end game and performance the start to the 6th gen of Monster Hunter may have had a rocky but things are starting to look bright I give Wilds a 8/10.

And honestly, that makes this journey feel special in its own way.

I recommend this game because, in spite of its issues, it's still extremely enjoyable and expansive in terms of content and things to do. There are many positives I can think of, as well as negatives.

The performance, after a few updates attempting to improve it, HAS gotten better... but still nowhere near that good.  The recommended specs for this game feels like an actual lie. I have a 3080 ti as well as a 5800x, and I'm barely pulling an average of 60 fps on Medium settings in some areas. As someone had said in another review, it's like going from ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ yourself to now pissing yourself. I feel as though there are still MANY areas that performance can still improve, and I REALLY hope they keep that flow, but I doubt it. 

The implementation of the wound and focus systems, while good and fun ideas, have led to a very core issue with the gameplay itself. For the base game, excluding the 10 and 9 star monsters, it has led to a very ridiculous easing of the difficulty. I should not be able to kill a 8 star Guardian Rathalos in 3-4 minutes through the sheer power of stun-locking and wound exploiting. While this problem has been addressed with the harder 9 and 10 star quests, I believe, if not balanced properly for the inevitable expansion, it will present another massive issue in terms of difficulty. As stated, the newer higher tier of monsters have at-least addressed this issue... through the usage of one-shots and ketamine, but it is what it is. Another thing that bothers me is the changes to some of the weapons, as they had to make a-lot of changes to match the new systems. Furthermore, I'm a bit iffy on them only letting you put combat decorations on the weapons themselves. I miss the variety I had in previous titles.

Next would be the dulling of mechanics that made the Monster Hunter franchise what it was. This game gets rid of having to search for the monster, knowing your environment to get to areas quicker, overall knowledge like where the monster typically spawns or areas that it often roams, etc. While perhaps not an issue for many people, for me the smaller mechanics such as that is at-least what made World for me. I believe the world itself is detailed and gorgeous, but learning the map through gameplay to better your hunts is gone. At most, you auto-ride your bird until it takes you where you need to go and memorize the environmental stuff like boulders. Perhaps its a sacrifice you have to make to make the world as big as it is (traversing the desert on foot would be abysmal), but I'm not sure I'd take larger maps over automating what was once a big part of the gameplay for me. Perhaps what Rise did with the Palamutes would be a better implementation other than 'Press 'button' to auto-path to the monster'. There are a ton of small things as well, like the chef cats and the quest descriptions being funny or unique, 

I think that, in other areas, the game itself is excellent. It looks nice (imo), the gameplay is extremely fun, there is a ton of content and replayability here, the late game difficulty is fun (also frustrating), the animations are amazing... however, I wish they didn't take away things the old titles had. If they kept the changes, balanced them well, and didn't remove what made the old games what they were, it would be perfect. 
Regardless, I'll recommend this because, in spite of these issues, I still play this game and enjoy it a ton.

At first, I never heard good things about MH Wilds. After they released the performance patch (granted, sometimes when I boot up the game, it does stutter a LOT), I gave the game a try and I was in for a treat. As someone who's experience with the MH series is MH3U (the 3DS version), a little bit of 4U and Frontier Z, this game takes MH Wilds to the next level. I love how the game feels cinematic. This was an interesting turn for the series but a welcomed one! This may not appeal to fans from older gens of MH, but this is an amazing approach from the series!

It's been long road since launch. Multiple performance updates, the return of fan-favorite monsters, and an ever-evolving gear progression have elevated the base game Wilds to a more compelling form than the last 2 Monster Hunter entries (for their base games). Between focus mode, mix-and-match set bonus weapons, Qol improvements, and easy access to layered gear, Wilds has completely pulled ahead after a rocky start plagued by bad optimization.

After a whole YEAR since this game's release, I can finally recommend it to most people. Assuming they have a PC that can run the game (mine just barely can at medium-high graphics, admittedly). With Wilds now in a relatively stable state performance-wise, it lets me see how good this game is overall, arguably one of the best base-game/high rank experiences we've ever gotten. A solid roster of monsters and what might be the best hunt-by-hunt gameplay in the series, it might not be perfect, far from it, but I honestly consider Wilds a must play for any MH fans.

That being said, I can't help but be worried for the expansion. If this is how hard the base game is, I'm worried master rank will be filled with bloated HP and damage numbers, rather than actual difficult fights that require genuine strategy. We'll just have to wait and see.

[h1]Optimization[/h1]
For those concerned about optimization, I pre-ordered Monster Hunter Wilds on day one, and the launch experience was rough. The game was plagued by lag, making gameplay choppy and unsatisfying. I only managed about 30 hours before shelving it. However, after Title Update 4, news of major improvements caught my eye. I jumped back in and racked up another 60 hours over two weeks—with zero issues. It ran buttery smooth at high graphics settings, no crashes, lag, or interruptions, even during online hunts with other players. (For reference: RTX 4070 Super.) Optimization is now excellent.

[h1]Gameplay[/h1]
I've been hooked on the series since Monster Hunter World and Rise. Wilds brings meaningful upgrades like Focus Mode and monster wounds, which make it way easier for a noob like me to target weak points precisely. The new movesets are fantastic—especially the Long Sword's flashy finisher. Weapon designs are top-notch, and the Darkness Shatterseal Greatsword's effects are so cool they convinced me to main Great Sword.

[h1]Graphics[/h1]
Graphics aren't my top priority, but Wilds feels like a solid step up from World. Maps are massive, and new monsters like Rey Dau and Arkveld look incredible. (I won't compare it to Rise, which was built for Nintendo Switch with a different art style.)

[h1]Story[/h1]
Story has never been a draw for me in this series—no one plays Monster Hunter for the narrative. No complaints here.

[h1]Overall[/h1]
This is almost my favorite game of all time. Nothing beats the adrenaline rush of wielding an oversized weapon to perfectly parry a colossal monster's attack and turn the hunt in your favor. With optimization fixed post-Title Update 4, I highly recommend it. 9.5/10

so the base games now "feature complete" as its received its last content update prior to the expansion reveal (unveiling in the summer).  And oh my god the game doesn't run like doodoo anymore after the latest patch. So tldr on my feelings on this game are, There's a great game at the core of it but, even by monster hunter standards, you actually do have to push through a lot of really mediocority to get there. 

The low rank story has interesting world building but is railroading you on very slow walk and talk sections you can get through faster, not even diverting from the set path to pick up items at all. Feels more Monster Safari than Monster Hunter. Your hunter feels buttery smooth and great to control, however, literally the monsters are too weak to put up a challenge. They die so fast you barely get to see most of their gimmicks honestly. Only really feels like you put up a "real fight" when you go up against higher tier Tempered monsters. 

And I do have some grievances with the bird-mount you have in this game as it really, really does take out so much of the traversal and map learning of previous games whith just pressing up on the dpad to auto navigate. You can't just hoof it on foot cause theres several gaps and bridges that require the bird to travel through them. I'm not opposed to it but definitely feels like there should be a happy medium.

But man, having seen it through to the end, going against the toughest monsters really, reaaaally pushes you to use every mechanic, skill and equipment you've earned up to that point. Really can push you to the edge and it feels fantastic triumphing over that. There is a great great game at the end of the tunnel but damn, it took such a long journey to get there.

I'm giving the game a very generous 7/10 and I hope whatever expansion comes around really knocks it out of the park. Get it on sale and just prep yourself to chip away at it over time.

I pre-ordered this game and held off from writing a review earlier due to how awful the performance was, to the point where it overshadowed the very positive aspects this game truly has. 
Now that Capcom FINALLY fixed most of the issues this game had (performance-wise), I can finally say that I love this game for how massive and fun it truly is. 
Originally, I was very concerned with the lack of difficult monsters to fight against or the absence of any fun grinds. However, now that we received the final base game update, this is the best base-game Monster Hunter game in latest years. 
We have so many options for builds, fun fights like Arch Tempered monsters, Omega Planetes, 9 stars monsters and Gogmazios. 
If anything, I would say that the worst part of this game is how the mount completely nullifies the player's ability to learn the maps and streamlines the "hunting" process of this game. I miss how Monster Hunter World handled exploration, as in that game I was familiarized with all the maps after serval hunts, and I really missed that feeling in this game. 
However, it is still a must-play if you are a Monster Hunter fan or if you are just getting started with the franchise.

Now that all of the title updates are out and the base game of wilds is "complete" I feel comfortable leaving a review. I wish there was a middle thumb option, but overall I'll leave it as a positive review.

I never had any performance issues luckily, so I have no comments on that aspect of the game.

To make a long story short after ~200 hours I can say I really enjoy the time right after a title update and absolutely loathe the end game grind for this game. Especially with the new 10* AT that came out with title update 4.5. I have never enjoyed monsters that gain difficulty through bloated HP and one shot moves. I do not think it fits the MH franchise, but that is the state of the current end game. Monster hunter has always been about prepping items in advance and adapting your build to the target of your hunt, but for most of these 10* ATs any adaptation I made felt fruitless. It came down to just perfect dodging or guarding for the 15-20 minutes it takes until the monster is dead. Some find that to be fun, I found it to be incredibly boring and just unfair at time if you happen to slightly mis-time something and get one shot. It makes random support hunts nigh impossible (unless you use AI hunters) since people tend to get one shot in quick succession until quest fail. If that is the expectation for the hunt, I'd prefer they wouldn't also bloat the HP and make me sit even longer doing the same dodge / block sequences repetitively across multiple hunts to get the required mats for the armor I want.

I feel the same about Gogmazios and the Gogma Artian grind. I did not like the base Artian system, but at least I could farm many different monsters and get somewhat similar drop rates. The only real way to farm the mutated armaments is to fight Gogmazios repeatedly and hope you get good RNG or its back to the basin for more armaments. The first time I hunted Gogmazios I thought it was spectacular, after the 40th time I got quite sick of his mechanics (especially the third phase...).

The seasonal festivals were great, character customization was quite deep and satisfying, and the majority of hunts I enjoyed and would farm the full armor sets and weapon sets for my weapons of choice. It is just a shame that the end game grind is such a slog of one shot mechanics and gambling.

Another release of Monster Hunter. After World and Rise, it should be instant favourite and a banger of a game. Well, it is not. Even after performance patch, perfomarmance is... mediocre. Game doesnt look actually that good either, if you look carefully (maybe it does with HD textures, but no way I can run those). Especially when you compare to other PC releases. Rise looked questionably before Sunbreak was released, but it has distinct style, making its somewhat lackluster graphics look way better. Wilds went for realistic look, and failed. Game actually looks worse than World, works way, way worse than World. Sure, we get somewhat open zones, which is kinda interesting, but I dont think it is worth its outrageous requirements. But the worst part is probably combat. Wound system is atrocious. Additional controls required by that system are obnoxious. New "more dynamic" combat feels way worse than clunkier, but more responsive combat of World or Rise. New "moves" which are supposed to make weapons feel worse than ever before (weapons i actually used: Lance - which i main, Bow, Heavy Bowgun, Sword and shield). Lance, for me, feel especially atrocious. Monster hitboxes feel all over the place, which, paired with lowish fps (around 47-55) make perfect blocks nigh impossible, perfect counters needless risk (example: i counter jaw attack of the monster, that misses even though im not moving, and im getting hit by front leg, since it has active hitbox, but since perfect counter requires releasing blocking stance, im getting hit). TLDR: A fun experience held back by technical issues and grind.

Before anything, I want to say this is my first game in the Monster Hunter series, and it was a mixed bag.

The learning curve was tough. There was so much thrown at you at the start, and a lot of it wasn’t properly explained. I was still discovering mechanics and interactions 30–40 hours into the game. In a way, that made it more interesting, but it would have been better to have clearer explanations. It often feels like the game forces you to go to Reddit or YouTube to understand basic systems.

That said, I had a blast hunting different monsters and observing the ecosystems. Combat is fun and surprisingly deep. There are many weapons to choose from, and each one changes your playstyle significantly, which keeps things fresh. Progression felt rewarding and enjoyable.

The co-op system, however, is extremely confusing and has a long way to go. The fact that there’s a 20-minute guide on YouTube explaining how to properly play together says a lot.

Story-wise, there isn’t much to say. Some parts were interesting, like exploring new lands and learning about their ecology (I wish there were more missions focused on that). On the other hand, Nata was incredibly annoying I honestly wanted the character gone halfway through the game. I’m not even sure what the main theme of the story was. Growing up? Self-discovery? It felt unclear and poorly executed.

Technical issues were my biggest problem. The game kept crashing during one mission, completely blocking my progression. I had to reinstall the game, update drivers, and search Reddit for solutions before I could fix it despite the issue apparently being known for five months.

Overall, I’ll continue playing as new updates come out, and I would still recommend it to friends, just with some warnings.

[h1]Monster Hunter Wilds[/h1]
Hours Played: 400+ (and counting…)

The game definitely had some growing pains at launch, but most of the major issues, especially performance but that has been ironed out by now.
 A lot of genuinely good changes have been added over time, like permanent festivals, rotating events, and a solid pool of event quests that actually feel worth doing.

The core game was always good, but the updates really pushed it into “just one more hunt” territory. Most monsters are well designed, the gameplay systems and weapons feel great once you sink your teeth into them, and there’s a satisfying amount of depth to keep you labbing builds instead of sleeping.

Graphics, sound design, and music are all on point, it looks good, it feels good, and when a big monster theme kicks in, it really kicks in.

Right now I’d call it a very solid entry in the series. If they deliver on what they’ve promised with the upcoming DLC, this could easily end up as one of the standout titles in the entire Monster Hunter franchise.

I’ve already thrown 400 hours at it and fully intend to keep pumping those numbers up.

I put in three months of 24/7 grinding on the PS5 with a buddy. We cleared the campaign, hit High Rank, and hunted every living thing in the ecosystem, all while staring at a 30fps "blurry turd." It was playable, but it looked like a mess, even on Low-Medium settings.

Switching to PC feels like playing a completely different game. It’s honestly like a high-definition remake of whatever I was playing on console.

GPU: AMD Radeon RX 9070 XT
CPU: Ryzen 5 5600X
RAM: 32GB DDR4 3200mHz

The Secret Sauce: Enabled Frame Generation and AMD FSR Native AA.

Native AA is the game-changer here. It keeps the image stunningly sharp without the typical upscaling artifacts. On the RX 9070 XT, it plays like a dream, buttery smooth and visually crisp. If you have the hardware to push it, don't even look back at the console version.

Verdict: If you’re coming from PS5, the difference is night and day. It’s finally the "Wilds" experience CAPCOM promised.

I'm sorry but even though I love Monster Hunter, I found this game to be a major disappointment. Don't get me wrong: there are a lot of excellent qualities in this game and I will probably get back to it later. I just feel disappointed. 

I like that it's an ambitious title for trying to make MH almost completely open world. However, I hate the choices the developers took to get there. To me, MH should do what it does best: hunt Monsters without anything to intrude on that experience. Instead, I got a game that fell back on World's worst qualities: having overly intrusive tutorials, NPCs that will not leave you alone, and an in your face "cinematic" story. It was okay in World since it didn't take too much of your time and you could skip most of it. However, it takes up every waking moment of your experience with follow the NPC scenes that force me to listen to them talk. It's not a good sign that I was actively trying to rebel against these scenes and also found myself having to turn off the voice volume just to hear myself think. Oh yeah. I actually didn't notice that the game had music until I tried it again last night. I couldn't hear it partly because on default voice volumes, the music is quiet compared to everything else and partly because I felt that for a fast paced monster game, the music felt very bland. For me, the music was the best part. So, seeing that go was especially disappointing.

I play on a pretty decent machine with an AMD Ryzen 5 9600x, 32gb DDR5, and a Radeon RX 7800xt with 16gb of Vram. I had been playing this game on Linux Mint with Proton for 11 or 12 hours just fine... then the crashing started. 

First I looked up the problem, and followed the numerous guides to make the game stop crashing.

I lowered the resolution from 1440p to 1080p.

I disabled vsync, HDR, FSR... everything I could think that might be causing crashes. No dice.

I deleted the crash files as directed by the guides.

I deleted the shader cache as directed by the guides and Capcom themselves... nada.

Now, I can't even play for 5 minutes before a crash.

When I looked deeper into the performance patches, I found to my surprise that my exact graphics card family and the one two generations prior to it are named directly in the patch notes... 

I've wasted too much time trying to get this game running again... a game that, mind you, ran fine for 11 hours. Certainly outside of the return window...

The content of this hot mess is phenomenal, some of the tightest gameplay I've had the displeasure of falling in love with. The controls are great, the world building is great, the story is fun, traversal is awesome... 

I. am. heartbroken... I am beyond disappointed. 

Capcom, you want to charge $70 for a game a YEAR after release that is STILL this broken? Even if for a small portion of gamers. Shame on you. Please fix this.

Personally this game performance feels much better for me even before the recent optimization patch, but it's still quite demanding game and some of my friends are still having troubles with running it, so play it if you can meet its requirement.

Wilds is a great game, tho I cannot for certain say that it's the best MH game ever made, especially comparing with World experience, or Rise gameplay, or MH4U (cuz I havent played it lol). I'd certainly it's an overhyped massive blunder, and this game would hit the mainstream MUCH more if it wasnt for dogsh optimization.

A lot of mechanics that they promised feels really overhyped (like pop up camp, weapon switching, even the openworld itself). In concept, you could do some really cool things with them, but in reality, they dont really offer that much and it's just a normal Monster Hunter game thing.
However because of that, a lot of good QoL systems are removed to trade for immersion: you have worse material farm system so you have to "interact with the locals". I believe the weapon switching system also results in probably the WORST and most restrictive decoration system since World.
The graphic is also really bad. Because it relies on realism, you have to crank your graphic to the max to see what it really offers, which you cant most of the time, cuz high graphic is really demanding. Even so, the artstyle direction is to gray and washed out, so little color to pop out even at the most vibrant area, that everything seems so dark and gray and dirty (not in a good way) and everything just blends in together for a very poor visual clarity. (If you don't trust me, go and fight a Gore Magala in Iceshard Cliffs. You will barely able to see it even though it's right in front of you)
The armor for me personally are really nice in concept, but have a few details that makes them really hard to mix to anything outside to its relative set. This is made worse by the fact that some armor you cant edit a large chunk of their color, because "you cant dye scales"
And that we havent even address the absolute elephant in the room, that is the long ahh page of hundred of DLC microtransactions on steam page.

But after all that one thing still remains is the core gameplay is really good, and Focus mode is a really nice addition to Monster Hunter that makes more clunky and passive weapons (especially GS) feels much more reactive. 
The story I think it's also best since World, with a lot of memorable characters with depth and interesting story to tell rather than just "monster act weird, go hunt monster", tho that's a good or bad thing really depends on what kind of player you are.
I think just the fight experience alone is the pros that outweight the cons.

In all fairness, MHWilds is a reall good game at core, tho I only recommend it when it's on sales or if you can run it. It's a shame that the game launch was such a disaster, but really 1-2 years from now everyone will realize that it's just another normal Monster Hunter title that ever existed.
I hope Capcom learns from this and do better in future Monster Hunter games.

50 hours into Monster Hunter Wilds and I’ve finally realized what it reminds me of: Dauntless and Wildheart… if both went through puberty, joined a biker gang, and started lifting monsters for fun.
As someone new to the MH series, I expected to get folded like a wet napkin. Instead, the gameplay loop grabbed me by the throat (lovingly) and dragged me into hunt after hunt like a toxic relationship I just keep going back to. The combat is addictive, the worldbuilding is weird in a “I probably shouldn’t drink the water here” kind of way, and every monster looks like it wants my insurance details.

The story though? Ehhh. It feels a bit “discount lore pack.” Not awful, just… there. Like the quiet kid in class who watches everything but contributes nothing.
Still, if you like big creatures, bigger weapons, and the emotional damage that comes from carting at 1% boss HP, this game is absolutely worth playing. 10/10 would get bodied again.

Do I wish the game had launched in this state? Absolutely. But you’ve got to give Capcom props for sticking with it and improving the experience in a big way.

Capcom has dragged me back into the building again. What notes do I have on Monster Hunter? 

Let's start with gameplay: You use combos to smack monsters and take them down. I wasn't expecting the combos at first, but it is Capcom and they're very well known for their fighting games here. Do note that the controls MAY take some getting used to. Don't be afraid to move around your buttons! I know I haven't... 

Next! Design! Each monster has their unique background fight song, most have their own unique armor you can make and SOME monsters have their own weapons too. I appreciate MH for allowing people to have the protective armor or the slightly slutty armor no matter what gender you are. Equal opportunistic sexiness! I think this is pretty cool :) Capcom and co really likes to get down with the art so expect a LOT. 

I haven't interacted much with the community, but there's people from every corner from the globe. A lot of hunters to do hunting! Rolls into my last point, hunting with friends can be loads of fun. You can call upon SOS flares to get folks to help you out too. Your never truly alone in the hunting! 

If you decide to get the game, I wish you luck! May the hunts forever be in your favor
