I’ve been playing Monster Hunter Wilds since release, and honestly, it’s been a ride. At launch, the game was gorgeous but super demanding — my old rig with an RTX 3070 could barely push 30 FPS. I ended up upgrading my PC just to enjoy it properly, and I’m glad I did because the visuals are stunning.

The early game felt pretty easy, and compared to Monster Hunter: World, the weapon and armor skills seemed limited. But I figured that was just because it was the start of the game. What really blew me away was the aesthetics — every map is beautiful, and the customization options are insane. You can tweak your hunter, your Seikrets, camps, even NPCs. It makes the world feel alive and personal.

Fast forward a year, and the game has improved a ton. Performance is way better (I’d say 20–26% smoother than launch), and the gameplay has gotten harder thanks to new monsters and events. Hunts feel more intense now, and I love that challenge. The best part is how the devs are clearly listening to feedback. They’ve been refining the game nonstop, and it shows. Credit where credit’s due — Capcom nailed the updates.

Now I’m just hyped for the upcoming DLC. If it keeps up the same level of polish and improvement, this game could easily surpass World in the long run.

[h1]Monster Hunter Wilds TU4.5 Review[/h1]

[b]TLDR:[/b] It has been a year since its release, and the game is great and now highly playable. There is so much content you can do in the game. Even if you are new to the series or a veteran try it out. If you played during launch and dropped it because of the performance issues give it a second chance because you might really like it now.

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Introduction & The State of the Game[/h2]
* [b]The Performance Turnaround:[/b] Launch performance was completely abysmal and left a horrible taste in many players mouths. It really should have been in a good state from the beginning. Thankfully after multiple optimization updates leading up to TU4.5 I can finally say the game runs smoothly and is very playable now.
* [b]Value:[/b] The content is so abundant it is almost overwhelming. No matter what price you buy this game at it is an absolute bang for your buck.

[h2]The Good: Combat, Monsters, and Story[/h2]
* [b]The MH Identity & Combat:[/b] The core gameplay has changed so much since MHP3rd but the overall combat is still as satisfying as ever. Optimized gameplay is buttery smooth and the combat is chef's kiss. It is a great evolution for the traditional MH Hack&Slash. Some veteran purists might prefer the original pacing and combo commitment but all I can say is try it and if it clicks then great.
* [b]Phenomenal Music:[/b] The music is phenomenal and fits every situation and environment. The music for each major monster is different and unique making it quite the spectacle.
* [b]Monster Roster & Story:[/b] The roster has amazing variety making it incredibly fun to fight new and returning monsters. The harder endgame bosses are just PEAK—wonderful cinematic and challenging fights blending set pieces and raw gameplay. There is more story now to progress the narrative and introduce the roster, and older fans will definitely love the characterization of returning characters.

[h2]Hunters, Gear, & QoL[/h2]
* [b]The Core Loop & Grind:[/b] The core loop of hunting monsters is satisfying and the endgame respects your time. You can avoid the RNG gear mechanics entirely and still clear the game. The base craftable Artian set and craftable charms are great upgrades if you do not want to farm for god roll talismans. Grinding for perfect stats is not for everyone but it is still a fun goal.
* [b]Equipment Freedom:[/b] There is so much cosplay and cool fashion available. It is a great blend of cool, cute, and silly/funny perfect for different tastes. Color customization is flexible and you are no longer restricted by gendered armor sets. If you want a fully armored gal or a sexy muscle man now you can.
* [b]Character Customization:[/b] The creator is incredibly detailed for making characters from fiction or reality. Even if you are not creative you can just search online for peoples character codes.
* [b]Support & QoL:[/b] You can have NPC support hunters help in battle and their carts do not count towards the quest failure. QoL changes make gameplay less tedious like easily reposting quests. Some might be divided on changes like Focus strike. Some love that you can adjust yourself during combos while others don't like it because it makes it easier to fight monsters.
* [b]Bonus Charm:[/b] The devs added small hidden things like the grandhub diva who gives a small buff after her performance or the secret bird's nest on the grandhub that gives you stuff from time to time.

[h2]The Bad: UI/UX, Network, & Nitpicks[/h2]
* [b]UI/UX Negative:[/b] The menus are confusing to navigate. It might be a skill issue in navigating the ancient forest on my part but it needs a touch up to be more digestible.
* [b]Cluttered Pop ups:[/b] Notification messages are messy. I want to know if I have a specific buff activated but it gets convoluted by notifications for things I don't care about right now. A filter would be great.
* [b]Default Controls:[/b] The default auto run for the Seikret and radial pop up for items feel clunky and need changing. Sometimes you do not even know there are much better settings because the UI/UX is convoluted, and I only knew about it because of youtube shorts on my feed. 
* [b]Investigation Quests:[/b] Great for saving world encounters but the limit of 50 is too small. Getting other players quests is pure RNG making it hard to get specific duo monster arena fights that you want to get. I hope we get a Custom Quest Maker.
* [b]Gathering and Delivery Quests:[/b] These shouldn't have been mostly removed as they gave players a breather to enjoy the environments. MHRise style event quests exploring the map would give more excuses to go straight from the Windward Plains to the Scarlet Forest without needing to fast travel.
* [b]Network Issues:[/b] I am not sure if it is because of crossplay or just server stability but I sometimes get disconnected suddenly leading to carts. We have decent internet and this doesn't happen in other games.
* [b]Missing Personal Rooms:[/b] I loved room customization in MHWorld and MHRise. Now it sucks that catching rare endemic life is just a number and a picture on my profile instead of something to display.

[h2]The Ugly: DLC & Monetization[/h2]
* [b]Overpriced DLC:[/b] Optional cosmetics are great but prices are a bit much. It also did not help that cosmetic DLC were released before the optimization updates which annoyed a lot of people.
* [b]Lore Behind Paywalls:[/b] Items like Alma's table give unique lore dialogue. It sucks that you have to pay to learn this lore. The basic version should have been free.
* [b]Character Edit Vouchers:[/b] Limiting edits to a maximum of 3 times and hiding further changes behind a paywall is very scummy. This should have been a completely free feature.

[hr][/hr]

[b]The Ultimate Verdict:[/b] Despite the issues and nitpicks I have this is a wonderful experience to pick up. Monster Hunter Wilds is a great game held back due to its rough performance and available content on release. The game was clearly rushed but as you can see with the constant updates the devs poured a lot of care and passion into this game. This is my biased view as a fan of this series since I was a kid, but I encourage you to try out the game. If you dropped it during launch because of the performance issues give it another chance to shine as you might just really enjoy it now.

(Edited for some Spelling Mistakes)

     Have you ever had the experience of trying a new recipe for a dish you love that promises to take the dishes best part and further elevate that dish above and beyond what you thought was possible? Some added garnishes you haven't tried before, but have seen or heard of before, an interesting blend of spices you didn't think to throw together before. That one ingredient that you normally wouldn't go out of your way to grab because it either doesn't really go into many other dishes, or due to it's higher quailty and/or it's limited supply, costs much more than what your day to day budget allows, but you think to yourself, "just this once I'll splurge and try it, I really want to try this dish".
    And then when it finally comes together, after simmering your dish for 3 hours more than you expected to spend in the ktichen, there were a few bumps here and there in the process, you were worried you might have messed the wholee thing up. 
     But you pushed your way through it and finally, that long awaited dish is right in front of you, and you can't beleive it. Look at it, it looks gorgeous, you take a step back, did YOU really make this? This looks like something a Michelin chef  would serve at their restaurant. You take some pictures, post them online. "Hey everyone, lok what I did!" You can't wait to try it, your friends are all amazed at the outcome, look how good it looks, YOU did that? 
Smiling, you finally take a bite of the dish.

It taste like Noodles in Tomato sauce.

     This can't be right? Sure, you can vaguely taste the extra bitter crispiness of the fancy Garnish, and that super pricey ingredient is pretty floral, (Now that you mention it, is floral really what you wanted to have with noodles?)

No, it's only the first bite, your overthinking. 

You have your second bite

Noodles in Tomato sauce.  It could use salt.

What went wrong? You followed the recipe, you spent all that extra money on those ingredients, you poured your entire afternoon into this??

Noodles in Tomato Sauce.

You keep eating, surely there's SOMETHING magnificent hiding, maybe you just have to get to the end.
The plate is empty. Your full, as sustenance it did it's job. But your left wanting. How could this be?
Was it really just that mediocre? Even with all the bells and whistles, and all that effort and time you put into it, is it possible that the dish itself was nothing more than

Noodles in Tomato Sauce.

Wilds is a dish that is simpley just, Noodles in Tomato Sauce.

It's more monster hunter.... but is it? The main story questline is pretty easy, so you rush straight through to high rank. You might get stuck and need to hunt a previous monster a couple of times to craft armor with poison or fire resistance ONCE or twice, then you'll get through.

Then once you get to high rank the progression system changes and you don't care about hunting specific monsters anymore. You craft the best weapons from "broken blade" and "cracked tube" or whatever. Not from monster teeth. So the Carve, Craft, Repeat loop is gone.

Plus the fake Guardian monsters are not part of the ecosystem which feels lame.

The game is pretty, the combat is fun, but imo they're close to forgetting what makes monster hunter special.
It has its flaws, but overall I really love this game.

I have played every Monster Hunter games since tri. I love Monster Hunter games, I love the challenge, I love seeing the monsters "live" in the enviroments, I love the weapons and the combat, the cooking, the online community. We had wholesome and hard hunts in mh4u, even when none of us could talk to eachother, cause no one in the lobby could speak english.

MH: Wilds is by far the worst and least enjoyable MH game I have ever played. MH: Online was better than this, And I can't read chinese. I didn't know what was happening half the time.

The combat is great. IMO the graphics are really nice. I think the Seikret is a good addition and the areas are great.

But this is not a true MH game. It feels like Final Fantasy, painted in MH colours. I don't want to disable items, I want to throw them out of the inventory! The story is incredibly annoying. We lost the MH language and the palico can SHUT UP for all I care.
And the worst of it is the Gogamizos fight. It's an easy battle, but the dps check is completely unnecessary. Yes, we had DPS checks in all games. Yes its an established thing in the MH games, but it was always something special. In mh4u, you could at least scale the walls during the ultimate attack, you got cannons and railways and it felt like something was at stake.

And worst of all, this game runs badly. If you want an MH game to play, go play World.

The game runs and looks like ♥♥♥♥, and somehow looks worse than Rise. The textures look like they were smeared with vaseline, while struggling to hit 70fps at 1440p with a 4090.

Base game was almost braindead easy, and they overcorrected the difficulty so most hunts after rank 100 cart you in one or two hits with sometimes broken hitboxes (Uth Duna bellyflop, Arkveld tail stab). Personally I'm okay with the difficulty spike, because most players going past rank 100 are going to be the experienced players anyways, though it can be a bit much sometimes.

The wound system is kind of interesting, but overpowered in the base game. I think it's good wounds are much more rare in higher ranked hunts.

The focus system completely invalidates any challenge the base game could have had by making positioning meaningless, something that was a big part of every prevoius entry.

The maps don't really feel like they've been handcrafted anymore, which can be a good or bad thing depending on who you ask. Personally I'm not a fan of any of the maps.

Seikrets I really do not like in their current form, Palamutes were better in every way. The one thing I do not like about Seikrets are the fact that they can autopilot to your destination, which discourages learning the maps, and just makes this entry of monster hunter feel like it was made for people who can't stop looking at social media. Monster went to another area? Better hop on my Seikret and look at a few tweets before I automatically reach the monster.
I think they should have brought back the tracking system, and tie it to the Seikret, and if you maxed out the research, then your Seikret can autopilot you to the monster.

Capcom continues their shady dlc practices in this game, and are still selling character edit vouchers for actual money. They've also started selling quest completion animations because people will probably buy them.

Despite everything I said, this is probably the best Monster Hunter game for any newcomers to the series because of how brandead easy the base game is.

If you're a veteran, then I can't recommend this game, unless it's at least 50% off. There's no way this game is worth $90, just wait for them to fix the expansion when it enevitably releases broken, just like this game.

I've tried and failed to get into the monster hunter games about 3-4 times before playing wilds, worlds and rise were my introduction into the series and I never found much enjoyment but there's something about this game that has kept me hooked for days on end. It's such a pleasant time killer if I have an extra few hours before/ after work, if I have plans later in the day I can go on a few hunts, cancel said plans and then continue going on hunts and I will be enjoying every second.

Grindy as hell? absolutely but that's one of the charms. I hate finishing a game and not being able to find any replay value so it just gains dust in my library until I decide to play it again, but this is borderline endless. Bigger and harder monsters, multiple weapon and armour upgrades, multiple weapons with unique combos and play styles, what a dream come true. Along with some amazing monster designs and sound design with most monsters having some connection with real world animals, insects and reptiles.

Now, I'll admit I am pretty bias because I haven't played many other monster hunter games to this extent but I believe that alone shows how much praise this game deserves. It isn't perfect don't get me wrong, if you don't enjoy grinding the same monsters for materials or aren't a fan of repetitive gameplay you may want to sit this one out. But if grinding is your cup of tea with a dash of sugar made from the scales of Ratholos, you. will. fall. in. love. xxxxx
